topics.title: Introduction and Basic Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction and Basic Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fluid Mechanics** is the branch of engineering science that deals with the behavior of fluids (liquids and gases) at rest (Fluid Statics) and in motion (Fluid Kinematics and Fluid Dynamics), together with the interaction of fluids with solid boundaries and other fluids.

> [!NOTE]
> **Hydraulics** is a sub-discipline of fluid mechanics that deals specifically with the practical engineering applications of liquids (mainly water) in pipelines, open channels, hydraulic machinery, and hydraulic structures.

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) introduces the foundational vocabulary and physical properties that govern all fluid behavior. Mastering this module is **non-negotiable** because every subsequent module — from manometry to boundary layer theory to open channel flow — relies on the definitions established here.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a beach. The **sand particles** on the shore represent a *solid* — they hold their shape when you cup them in your hand. Now scoop up a handful of **seawater** and try the same thing — it slips through your fingers, taking the shape of whatever contains it. This is the defining nature of a *fluid*: a substance that **continuously deforms (flows)** when subjected to a shear stress, no matter how small.

Think of fluid mechanics like **traffic engineering on a highway**:
- **Fluid statics** = parked cars (no motion, but pressure exists on the road surface).
- **Fluid kinematics** = cars moving with different speeds (we study *how* they move without asking *why*).
- **Fluid dynamics** = cars moving with reasons — accelerators, brakes, road slopes (we study the *forces* causing the motion).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters

| Engineering Field | Direct Application |
|------------------|-------------------|
| **Civil Engineering** | Dam design, water supply networks, sewerage |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Turbines, pumps, HVAC systems |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Pipe flow, mixing, reactor design |
| **Aerospace Engineering** | Airfoil design, wind tunnel testing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to define fluid mechanics, distinguish it from hydraulics, and identify the broad branches of the subject. Direct **2-mark / 3-mark definition questions** are frequently asked from this introductory section.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Distinction: Fluid Mechanics vs. Hydraulics

The two terms are often used interchangeably, but KTU expects you to know the **scope and limitations** of each:

- **Fluid Mechanics** is **theoretical and analytical** — it uses differential equations, calculus, and physics to derive general laws of fluid behavior. It applies to both liquids and gases.
- **Hydraulics** is **empirical and applied** — it relies heavily on experimental data, charts, and practical formulas. It deals almost exclusively with water as the working fluid.

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU question: *"Hydraulics is a branch of Fluid Mechanics. Justify."* — Hydraulics applies the principles of fluid mechanics to real engineering problems using empirical coefficients and practical design rules.

## 2.2 The Continuum Hypothesis

At the molecular level, fluids are not continuous — they consist of discrete molecules separated by voids. However, in most engineering problems, the **length scale of interest** (e.g., a pipe diameter of 10 cm) is **vastly larger** than the molecular spacing (about $3 \times 10^{-10}$ m for water).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Continuum Hypothesis:** Fluid properties such as density, pressure, and velocity are assumed to vary continuously in space and time, and each infinitesimal fluid element contains an enormous number of molecules so that macroscopic properties are well-defined averages.

**Knudsen Number (Kn)** is used to validate this assumption:
$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$
where $\lambda$ = mean free path of molecules, $L$ = characteristic length.

- If $Kn < 0.001$, the continuum assumption is **valid**.
- If $Kn > 0.1$, the fluid must be treated as a **rarefied gas** (e.g., upper atmosphere, MEMS devices).

## 2.3 Fundamental Fluid Properties — The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **single most important revision asset** for Module 1. Memorize every entry.

| Property | Symbol | Definition | Formula | SI Unit |
|----------|--------|------------|---------|---------|
| Mass Density | $\rho$ | Mass per unit volume | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| Specific Weight | $\gamma$ | Weight per unit volume | $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| Specific Gravity | $S \text{ or } SG$ | Ratio of fluid density to water density at $4^\circ\text{C}$ | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Dynamic Viscosity | $\mu$ | Resistance to deformation (internal friction) | $\mu = \dfrac{\tau}{du/dy}$ | $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa·s}$ |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu$ | Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| Bulk Modulus of Elasticity | $K$ | Resistance to compression | $K = \dfrac{-dP}{dV/V}$ | $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$ |
| Surface Tension | $\sigma$ | Force per unit length along a free surface | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{L}$ | $\text{N/m}$ |
| Vapor Pressure | $P_v$ | Pressure at which liquid boils | — | $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$ |
| Capillarity | $h$ | Rise or fall in a narrow tube | $h = \dfrac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\gamma \cdot d}$ | $\text{m}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values for Water at 4°C:**
> - $\rho_{\text{water}} = \mathbf{1000 \; kg/m^3}$
> - $\gamma_{\text{water}} = \mathbf{9810 \; N/m^3}$ (often rounded to **9.81 kN/m³**)
> - $\mu_{\text{water}} \approx \mathbf{1.002 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s}$
> - $\nu_{\text{water}} \approx \mathbf{1.004 \times 10^{-6} \; m^2/s}$

## 2.4 The Two Faces of Viscosity — Newton's Insight

Isaac Newton postulated that shear stress in a fluid is **linearly proportional** to the rate of angular deformation (velocity gradient):

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy}$$

- Fluids obeying this linear law are called **Newtonian fluids** (e.g., water, air, gasoline, alcohol).
- Fluids that do **not** obey it are called **Non-Newtonian fluids** (e.g., blood, paint, mud, ketchup, toothpaste).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse the *cause* and the *effect* in Newton's law of viscosity. The velocity gradient $\frac{du}{dy}$ is the *cause*, the shear stress $\tau$ is the *effect*, and $\mu$ is the *property* that links them.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The properties defined here are used in:

- **Pipe flow design** — viscosity determines head loss (Darcy-Weisbach equation).
- **Pump selection** — vapor pressure limits suction lift (NPSH calculations).
- **Dam engineering** — bulk modulus is critical for water hammer analysis.
- **Hydraulic structures** — surface tension affects flow over sharp-crested weirs.
- **Groundwater flow** — capillarity governs the height of moisture rise in soils.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between Specific Weight and Mass Density

**Given:**
- Mass density $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$
- Specific weight $\gamma = \dfrac{W}{V}$ where $W$ = weight

**Step 1 — Express weight in terms of mass:**
From Newton's second law, the weight of a body is the force exerted by gravity on its mass:
$$W = m \cdot g$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $W = m \cdot g$ into the specific weight formula:**
$$\gamma = \frac{W}{V} = \frac{m \cdot g}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Recognize that $m/V$ is exactly the mass density $\rho$:**
$$\gamma = \frac{m}{V} \cdot g = \rho \cdot g$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{\gamma = \rho \cdot g}$$

This is the **most frequently tested** relationship in Module 1 numerical problems.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Specific Gravity in Terms of Specific Weight

**Given:** $S = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{fluid}}}{\rho_{\text{water at } 4^\circ\text{C}}}$

**Step 1 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $g$ (a constant):**
$$S = \frac{\rho_{\text{fluid}} \cdot g}{\rho_{\text{water}} \cdot g}$$

**Step 2 — Recognize that $\rho \cdot g = \gamma$:**
$$S = \frac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water}}}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{S = \frac{\gamma_{\text{fluid}}}{\gamma_{\text{water}}}}$$

This is convenient because in many practical problems, $\gamma$ is more readily known than $\rho$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Kinematic Viscosity from Dynamic Viscosity

**Step 1 — Start with definitions:**
- Dynamic viscosity: $\mu$ has units of $\text{Pa} \cdot \text{s} = \dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$
- Mass density: $\rho$ has units of $\dfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3}$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $\mu / \rho$ and simplify units:**
$$\frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{\text{kg} / (\text{m} \cdot \text{s})}{\text{kg} / \text{m}^3} = \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}} \cdot \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{kg}} = \frac{\text{m}^2}{\text{s}}$$

**Step 3 — Define the result as kinematic viscosity $\nu$:**
$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "kinematic" terminology arises because the units ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$) contain **only kinematic** quantities (length and time) — no force or mass appears explicitly.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Bulk Modulus of Elasticity

**Step 1 — Consider an elemental fluid volume $V$ subjected to an increase in pressure $dP$.** This causes a volume decrease $-dV$ (the minus sign indicates compression).

**Step 2 — Write the volumetric strain:**
$$\text{Volumetric Strain} = \frac{-dV}{V}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the generalized Hooke's law definition (stress / strain):**
$$K = \frac{\text{Increase in pressure}}{\text{Volumetric strain}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the expressions:**
$$\boxed{K = \frac{-dP}{dV/V} = -V \cdot \frac{dP}{dV}}$$

**Step 5 — Common alternative forms** used in KTU problems:
$$K = \rho \cdot \frac{dP}{d\rho} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{1}{K} = \frac{1}{\rho} \cdot \frac{d\rho}{dP}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A large $K$ means the fluid is nearly **incompressible**. For water, $K \approx \mathbf{2.1 \times 10^9 \; N/m^2}$, which is why we treat water as incompressible in most civil engineering applications.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Capillary Rise in a Tube

**Step 1 — Consider a vertical glass tube of internal diameter $d$ dipped in a liquid that wets the glass (e.g., water).** The liquid rises to a height $h$ above the free surface.

**Step 2 — Apply a force balance on the column of liquid of height $h$:**
- **Upward force** = surface tension force acting along the contact perimeter.
$$F_{\text{up}} = \sigma \cdot \pi d \cdot \cos\theta$$
where $\theta$ is the contact angle.

**Step 3 — Write the weight of the liquid column (downward force):**
$$F_{\text{down}} = (\text{Volume}) \cdot \rho \cdot g = \left(\frac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot h\right) \cdot \rho \cdot g = \frac{\pi d^2 h \gamma}{4}$$

**Step 4 — At equilibrium, set $F_{\text{up}} = F_{\text{down}}$:**
$$\sigma \cdot \pi d \cdot \cos\theta = \frac{\pi d^2 h \gamma}{4}$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $\pi d$ from both sides and solve for $h$:**
$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\gamma \cdot d}$$

**Final Result:**
$$\boxed{h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\gamma \cdot d}}$$

**Special case for water-glass interface** (assuming $\theta \approx 0$, so $\cos\theta = 1$):
$$h = \frac{4 \sigma}{\gamma \cdot d}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Property

| Engineering Scenario | Governing Property | Why? |
|---------------------|-------------------|------|
| Pump cavitation check | Vapor Pressure ($P_v$) | Suction pressure must exceed $P_v$ to prevent boiling |
| Water hammer in pipes | Bulk Modulus ($K$) | Determines pressure wave speed |
| Lubricant selection | Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$) | Resistance to shearing in bearings |
| Reynolds Number computation | Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$) | $Re = \dfrac{VD}{\nu}$ |
| Capillary water in soils | Surface Tension ($\sigma$) | Governs moisture rise in pores |
| Design of a settling tank | Mass Density ($\rho$) | Determines buoyant force on particles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Fluid Mechanics Branches

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID MECHANICS]:::root
    A --> B[FLUID STATICS]:::branch
    A --> C[FLUID KINEMATICS]:::branch
    A --> D[FLUID DYNAMICS]:::branch
    B --> B1[Pressure measurement]:::leaf
    B --> B2[Manometry]:::leaf
    B --> B3[Buoyancy and flotation]:::leaf
    C --> C1[Streamlines and streaklines]:::leaf
    C --> C2[Continuity equation]:::leaf
    C --> C3[Acceleration field]:::leaf
    D --> D1[Bernoulli equation]:::leaf
    D --> D2[Momentum equation]:::leaf
    D --> D3[Boundary layer theory]:::leaf
    A --> E[HYDRAULICS]:::branch
    E --> E1[Open channel flow]:::leaf
    E --> E2[Pipe flow and networks]:::leaf
    E --> E3[Hydraulic machinery]:::leaf

    classDef root fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef branch fill:#2c5f8d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef leaf fill:#e8f1f8,stroke:#2c5f8d,color:#1f3a5f
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **leftmost column** (Statics → Kinematics → Dynamics) represents the *theoretical* core, while **Hydraulics** is the applied sibling that depends on all three.

## 4.2 Continuum vs. Molecular View of Matter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph M[MOLECULAR VIEW]
        M1[Discrete molecules]:::mnode
        M2[Random voids and motion]:::mnode
        M3[Statistical description]:::mnode
    end
    subgraph C[CONTINUUM VIEW]
        C1[Continuous medium]:::cnode
        C2[Point-wise properties]:::cnode
        C3[Deterministic equations]:::cnode
    end
    M -- Kn less than 0.001 --> C
    C -- Engineering macroscale --> APP1[Pipe flow]:::app
    C -- Engineering macroscale --> APP2[Open channel flow]:::app
    C -- Engineering macroscale --> APP3[Aerodynamics]:::app

    classDef mnode fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#663300
    classDef cnode fill:#e6f4ea,stroke:#1e7a3a,color:#0d3d1d
    classDef app fill:#dde7f0,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#1f3a5f
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow: From Property Definition to Engineering Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Define Fluid Property]:::step
    P2[Identify Governing Equation]:::step
    P3[Establish Reference Values]:::step
    P4[Apply to Engineering System]:::step
    P5[Validate with Empirical Data]:::step
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6[Design Decision]:::out

    classDef step fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef out fill:#cc6600,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Schematic: Fluid Continuum and Infinitesimal Fluid Element

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EL[Infinitesimal Fluid Element dV]:::elem
    EL --> PR[Pressure P acts on all faces]:::face
    EL --> SH[Shear stress tau acts tangentially]:::face
    EL --> MS[Mass dm = rho times dV]:::face
    PR --> EQ1[Force balance: dF = grad P times dV]:::eq
    SH --> EQ2[Newton law of viscosity]:::eq
    MS --> EQ3[Newton second law: F = ma]:::eq
    EQ1 --> RES[Euler and Navier-Stokes equations]:::res
    EQ2 --> RES
    EQ3 --> RES

    classDef elem fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef face fill:#e8f1f8,stroke:#1f3a5f,color:#1f3a5f
    classDef eq fill:#fff4e6,stroke:#cc6600,color:#663300
    classDef res fill:#2c5f8d,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The infinitesimal fluid element shown above is the **fundamental building block** used to derive every conservation law in fluid mechanics (continuity, momentum, energy) starting in Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Define the following terms with one example each:**
(i) Fluid Mechanics, (ii) Hydraulics, (iii) Continuum.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Fluid Mechanics:** It is the branch of engineering science that deals with the behavior of fluids (liquids and gases) at rest and in motion, and their interaction with solid boundaries. *Example: Analysis of flow through a pipe.*

**(ii) Hydraulics:** It is the applied branch of fluid mechanics dealing with the practical engineering use of liquids, especially water, through empirical and semi-empirical methods. *Example: Design of a spillway or an open channel.*

**(iii) Continuum:** A fluid is treated as a continuous distribution of matter with well-defined point properties (density, pressure, velocity), neglecting its discrete molecular nature. *Example: Standard water flow in municipal pipes.*

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Expected Pattern]**
**Differentiate between Newtonian and Non-Newtonian fluids with two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|-----------|----------------|---------------------|
| Shear stress vs. velocity gradient | Linear relationship ($\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$) | Non-linear or time-dependent |
| Viscosity | Constant at a given temperature | Varies with shear rate or time |
| Examples | Water, air, gasoline, alcohol | Blood, toothpaste, paint, mud, ketchup |

Newtonian fluids obey **Newton's law of viscosity** strictly, whereas non-Newtonian fluids exhibit shear-thinning, shear-thickening, or viscoelastic behavior.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Define the following fluid properties with their SI units: (i) Mass density, (ii) Specific weight, (iii) Specific gravity, (iv) Dynamic viscosity, (v) Kinematic viscosity, (vi) Surface tension, (vii) Bulk modulus of elasticity, (viii) Vapor pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A liquid has a specific gravity of 0.85. Calculate its (i) mass density, (ii) specific weight, and (iii) the dynamic viscosity if its kinematic viscosity is $4 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Take $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

Refer to the **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet** in Section 2.3 of these notes. Each property must be defined in one or two sentences with its correct SI unit:

1. **Mass density** $\rho$: Mass per unit volume. Unit: $\text{kg/m}^3$.
2. **Specific weight** $\gamma$: Weight per unit volume. Unit: $\text{N/m}^3$.
3. **Specific gravity** $S$: Ratio of density of fluid to that of water at $4^\circ\text{C}$. Dimensionless.
4. **Dynamic viscosity** $\mu$: Ratio of shear stress to velocity gradient; resistance to deformation. Unit: $\text{N·s/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa·s}$.
5. **Kinematic viscosity** $\nu$: Ratio of dynamic viscosity to mass density. Unit: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
6. **Surface tension** $\sigma$: Force per unit length acting along a free liquid surface. Unit: $\text{N/m}$.
7. **Bulk modulus of elasticity** $K$: Ratio of change in pressure to volumetric strain. Unit: $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$.
8. **Vapor pressure** $P_v$: Pressure exerted by the vapor of a liquid in thermodynamic equilibrium with its liquid phase. Unit: $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $S = 0.85$, $\nu = 4 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{m}^2/\text{s}$, $g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 1 — Calculate mass density:**
$$\rho_{\text{liquid}} = S \cdot \rho_{\text{water}} = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850 \; \text{kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate specific weight:**
$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g = 850 \times 9.81 = 8338.5 \; \text{N/m}^3$$

Rounded: $\gamma \approx 8.34 \; \text{kN/m}^3$.

**Step 3 — Calculate dynamic viscosity from kinematic viscosity:**
$$\mu = \nu \cdot \rho = (4 \times 10^{-6}) \times 850 = 3.4 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{Pa·s}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $\rho = \mathbf{850 \; kg/m^3}$
- $\gamma = \mathbf{8338.5 \; N/m^3 \approx 8.34 \; kN/m^3}$
- $\mu = \mathbf{3.4 \times 10^{-3} \; Pa \cdot s}$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Option 2

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the continuum hypothesis of fluid mechanics. State the condition for the validity of this hypothesis in terms of the Knudsen number. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A clean glass tube of internal diameter $1.5 \; \text{mm}$ is inserted vertically into a trough of water at $20^\circ\text{C}$. Calculate the capillary rise. Given: $\sigma_{\text{water}} = 0.0728 \; \text{N/m}$, contact angle $\theta = 0^\circ$, $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 9.79 \; \text{kN/m}^3$. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

At the molecular level, fluids consist of discrete molecules with random voids and motion. However, in nearly all engineering applications, the length scale of interest (e.g., pipe diameter, channel width) is many orders of magnitude greater than the molecular mean free path.

**Continuum Hypothesis:** A fluid is assumed to be a continuous medium such that properties such as density, pressure, and velocity are defined at every point in space and vary continuously.

**Validity Condition (Knudsen Number):**
$$Kn = \frac{\lambda}{L}$$

where $\lambda$ = mean free path of molecules, $L$ = characteristic length of the problem.

- $Kn < 0.001$ → continuum assumption **valid** (most engineering cases).
- $0.001 < Kn < 0.1$ → **slip-flow regime**.
- $Kn > 0.1$ → **free-molecular / rarefied gas flow** (e.g., re-entry vehicles, MEMS).

**Justification with example:** For water at standard conditions, $\lambda \approx 3 \times 10^{-10}$ m. In a pipe of diameter 0.1 m, $Kn = 3 \times 10^{-9} \ll 0.001$, so the continuum assumption is overwhelmingly valid.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $d = 1.5 \; \text{mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \; \text{m}$, $\sigma = 0.0728 \; \text{N/m}$, $\theta = 0^\circ$ (so $\cos\theta = 1$), $\gamma = 9.79 \; \text{kN/m}^3 = 9790 \; \text{N/m}^3$.

**Step 1 — Write the capillary rise formula:**
$$h = \frac{4 \sigma \cos\theta}{\gamma \cdot d}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**
$$h = \frac{4 \times 0.0728 \times \cos(0^\circ)}{9790 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator:**
$$4 \times 0.0728 \times 1 = 0.2912 \; \text{N/m}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate denominator:**
$$9790 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} = 14.685 \; \text{N/m}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the ratio:**
$$h = \frac{0.2912}{14.685} = 0.01983 \; \text{m}$$

**Final Answer:**
$$\boxed{h \approx 19.83 \; \text{mm} \approx 1.98 \; \text{cm}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Module 1 Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mistakes in viscosity** — Writing $\mu$ in $\text{N/m}^2$ (which is pressure) instead of $\text{N·s/m}^2$. Always include the time unit.
> 2. **Specific gravity confusion** — Forgetting that $S$ uses water density at $4^\circ\text{C}$ ($1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$) as the reference, not at room temperature.
> 3. **Direction of capillary rise** — Mercury in a glass tube *falls* because its contact angle $\theta > 90^\circ$ (i.e., $\cos\theta$ is negative). Many students write $h$ as positive without considering the sign.
> 4. **Vapor pressure in pump problems** — A pump placed too high above a water surface causes the local pressure to drop below $P_v$, leading to **cavitation**. This is a frequent KTU short-answer trap.
> 5. **Specific weight vs. specific gravity** — Examiners deduct 1 mark if $S$ is reported with units. $S$ is **dimensionless**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Fluid** = substance that continuously deforms under applied shear stress (no matter how small).
- [ ] **Fluid Mechanics** = theoretical study; **Hydraulics** = applied/empirical study.
- [ ] **Continuum hypothesis** holds when **Kn = $\lambda / L$ < 0.001**.
- [ ] **Key relationships to memorize:**
  - $\gamma = \rho \cdot g$
  - $S = \rho / \rho_{\text{water}} = \gamma / \gamma_{\text{water}}$
  - $\nu = \mu / \rho$
  - $K = -V (dP / dV)$
  - $h = 4\sigma \cos\theta / (\gamma d)$ (capillarity)
- [ ] **Newton's law of viscosity:** $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$. Fluids obeying it = Newtonian.
- [ ] **Bulk modulus of water** $\approx 2.1 \times 10^9 \; \text{N/m}^2$ → water is treated as **incompressible**.
- [ ] **Vapor pressure** depends on temperature; it rises with temperature.
- [ ] **SI units quick reference:**
  - $\rho$: $\text{kg/m}^3$ | $\gamma$: $\text{N/m}^3$ | $\mu$: $\text{Pa·s}$ | $\nu$: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | $\sigma$: $\text{N/m}$ | $K$, $P_v$: $\text{Pa}$ or $\text{N/m}^2$.
- [ ] **Standard water properties** at 4°C: $\rho = 1000 \; \text{kg/m}^3$, $\gamma = 9.81 \; \text{kN/m}^3$.
- [ ] **Bulk Modulus** is large for liquids (incompressible) and small for gases (compressible).
- [ ] **Surface tension** decreases with temperature; pure water has higher $\sigma$ than seawater.
- [ ] In **capillarity**, wetting fluids (water in glass) rise; non-wetting fluids (mercury in glass) fall.
- [ ] For **KTU exam**, expect at least one 2-mark definition and one numerical problem (typically 7–8 marks) from this module in every University exam cycle.

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Properties of Fluids: Density, Specific Weight, Vapour Pressure, Viscosity, Newton's Law of Viscosity, Surface Tension and Capillarity, Ideal and Real Fluid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fluid?

A **fluid** is a substance that **continuously deforms (flows)** under the action of an applied shear stress (or tangential force). Unlike solids, fluids cannot sustain shear stress in static equilibrium. The branch of mechanics dealing with fluids at rest is *Fluid Statics* (Hydrostatics), while that dealing with fluids in motion is *Fluid Dynamics* (Hydrodynamics).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fluid properties form the **foundational bedrock** of the entire course. Every hydraulic engineering problem — from pipe flow to open channel design — derives directly from these primary properties. Expect at least **one direct 3-mark question** and a **sub-part in a 14-mark question** on this module in the End Semester Examination (ESE).

---

## 1.2 The Fluid Property Matrix — A First Look

Fluid properties are classified into:

- **Intensive Properties** — Independent of mass of the system (e.g., Pressure, Temperature, Density, Specific Weight, Viscosity).
- **Extensive Properties** — Dependent on mass/size of the system (e.g., Mass, Volume, Momentum).

> [!NOTE]
> Fluid mechanics fundamentally works only with **intensive properties** because they represent the *state* of the fluid at a point, not the bulk quantity.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Overview of Each Property

| Property | Plain-English Meaning | Real-World Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Density (ρ)** | How tightly packed matter is | A suitcase full of iron feels heavier than one full of cotton at the *same volume* |
| **Specific Weight (γ)** | How heavy a unit volume feels under gravity | The "heft" you feel when lifting a bucket of water vs. a bucket of oil |
| **Vapour Pressure** | How eagerly the fluid wants to evaporate | Perfume evaporates quickly (high vapour pressure); water slowly (lower) |
| **Viscosity (μ)** | Internal stickiness / resistance to flow | Honey flows slowly (high μ); water flows easily (low μ) |
| **Surface Tension (σ)** | "Skin-like" film on the liquid surface | A needle floats on water due to this skin effect |
| **Capillarity** | Spontaneous rise/fall in thin tubes | Kerosene rises in lamp wicks; mercury falls in a glass tube |

---

## 1.4 Ideal vs Real Fluid

**Ideal Fluid:** A hypothetical fluid that is **inviscid** (zero viscosity) and **incompressible**. It obeys Newton's laws perfectly and has no internal friction. Used in Euler's equations and Bernoulli's principle.

**Real Fluid:** Actual fluids which **possess viscosity** and exhibit shear resistance (internal friction). Examples: water, air, oil, blood, honey.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Exam Ready):**
> An **Ideal Fluid** is one which possesses no viscosity and surface tension and is incompressible.
> A **Real Fluid** is one which possesses viscosity, surface tension, and is compressible to some extent.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Density (Mass Density)

**Formal Definition:** Density is *mass per unit volume* of a fluid.

**SI Units:** $\text{kg/m}^3$

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\rho = \lim_{\Delta V \to 0} \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta V} = \frac{m}{V}$$

**Specific Volume (v):** Volume per unit mass, $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ ; Units: $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$

---

## 2.2 Specific Weight (Weight Density)

**Formal Definition:** Specific weight is the *weight per unit volume* of a fluid.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\gamma = \rho \cdot g$$

where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (standard gravitational acceleration in Kerala).

**SI Units:** $\text{N/m}^3$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both ρ and γ?** Density is used where mass-based analysis (e.g., Newton's 2nd law, continuity) is needed. Specific weight is used where gravitational force is dominant (e.g., hydrostatic pressure $P = \gamma h$).

---

## 2.3 Specific Gravity (Relative Density)

**Formal Definition:** The *ratio* of the density (or specific weight) of a fluid to that of a standard fluid (water at **4 °C**, where $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$).

$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}} = \frac{\gamma_{fluid}}{\gamma_{water}}$$

**It is dimensionless.** For water: $S = 1$. For mercury: $S = 13.6$. For sea water: $S = 1.025$.

---

## 2.4 Vapour Pressure

**Formal Definition:** The pressure exerted by a fluid in *vapour phase* in thermodynamic equilibrium with its *liquid phase* at a given temperature.

**Phenomenon — Cavitation:** When the local pressure in a flowing fluid drops **below** its vapour pressure, the fluid boils locally, forming vapour bubbles. These bubbles collapse violently when they reach higher-pressure regions, causing pitting damage to pump impellers, turbine blades, and pipes.

> [!WARNING]
> **Cavitation Threshold (KTU Favourite):** Cavitation is a recurring 7-mark question topic. The governing condition is: **Cavitation begins when local pressure ≤ Vapour Pressure.**

**Why Vapour Pressure Matters:**

- Determines the **boiling point** of a fluid (boiling occurs when $P_{local} = P_{vapour}$).
- Sets the **minimum operating pressure** in pumps and turbines (Net Positive Suction Head available, NPSH$_a$ > NPSH$_r$).
- Influences **barometric fluctuations** affecting fluid systems.

---

## 2.5 Viscosity — The Heart of Fluid Mechanics

**Formal Definition:** Viscosity is the property of a fluid that **resists relative motion** between its layers. It represents the internal friction of the fluid.

### 2.5.1 Dynamic Viscosity (μ)

From **Newton's Law of Viscosity**:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa or N/m²)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity (Pa·s or N·s/m²)
- $\dfrac{du}{dy}$ = Velocity gradient (rate of shear strain) — perpendicular to flow direction, in $\text{s}^{-1}$

**SI Unit of μ:** $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$
**CGS Unit:** *Poise*; $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$

### 2.5.2 Kinematic Viscosity (ν)

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho}$$

**SI Unit:** $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
**CGS Unit:** *Stoke*; $1 \text{ Stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

### 2.5.3 Newton's Law of Viscosity — Detailed Mechanics

Consider a fluid element between two parallel plates separated by distance $dy$. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate moves with velocity $u$:

- The fluid layer in contact with the **moving plate** moves with velocity $u$ (no-slip condition).
- The fluid layer in contact with the **stationary plate** has zero velocity.
- Velocity varies linearly from 0 to $u$ across the gap.
- A **shear stress** $\tau$ is required to maintain the motion of the upper plate against this viscous resistance.

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \lim_{\Delta y \to 0} \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta y} = \text{Velocity Gradient}$$

---

## 2.6 Classification of Fluids Based on Viscosity

| Fluid Type | Relation between τ and du/dy | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Newtonian Fluid** | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ (linear) | Water, Air, Mercury, Kerosene |
| **Non-Newtonian Fluid** | $\tau = \mu \left(\dfrac{du}{dy}\right)^n$, $n \neq 1$ | Blood, Paint, Toothpaste, Mud |
| **Ideal Plastic (Bingham)** | $\tau = \tau_y + \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | Drilling mud, Sewage sludge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Newtonian Fluid Definition:** A fluid that obeys Newton's law of viscosity with a *constant coefficient of viscosity* is called a Newtonian fluid. If the viscosity *changes* with rate of shear, it is *Non-Newtonian*.

---

## 2.7 Surface Tension

**Formal Definition:** The property of a liquid surface to behave like a **stretched elastic membrane** that tends to contract to **minimize surface area**.

**Mechanism:** Molecules inside the liquid experience balanced cohesive forces. Molecules at the surface experience a net inward pull, creating tension along the surface.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\sigma = \frac{F}{L}$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Surface tension (N/m)
- $F$ = Force on the imaginary line drawn on the liquid surface (N)
- $L$ = Length of that line (m)

**Pressure difference across a curved liquid interface (Young-Laplace equation):**

$$\Delta P = \sigma \left( \frac{1}{R_1} + \frac{1}{R_2} \right)$$

For a spherical droplet (single radius R): $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\sigma}{R}$
For a soap bubble (two surfaces): $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\sigma}{R}$

---

## 2.8 Capillarity

**Formal Definition:** The phenomenon of **spontaneous rise or fall** of a liquid in a small-diameter tube (or porous medium) due to surface tension.

**Jurin's Law (Capillary Rise Formula):**

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}$$

Where:
- $h$ = Capillary rise/fall (m)
- $\sigma$ = Surface tension (N/m)
- $\theta$ = Angle of contact between liquid and solid (degrees or radians)
- $\rho$ = Density of liquid (kg/m³)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (m/s²)
- $r$ = Radius of capillary tube (m); $d = 2r$

**Cases:**

- $\theta < 90°$ → *Wetting fluid* (e.g., water on clean glass) → **Capillary RISE**.
- $\theta > 90°$ → *Non-wetting fluid* (e.g., mercury on glass) → **Capillary DEPRESSION (Fall)**.
- $\theta = 90°$ → Flat meniscus, no capillarity (water on perfectly clean silver surface).

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Property | Real-World Engineering Application |
|---|---|
| Density & Specific Weight | Hydrostatic pressure calculations, dam design, ship buoyancy |
| Vapour Pressure | Cavitation prevention in turbines and pumps, refrigeration cycles |
| Viscosity | Lubricant design, pipeline pumping power, blood-flow modeling |
| Surface Tension | Detergents, inkjet printing, droplet formation in sprays |
| Capillarity | Soil moisture movement (hydrology), wick action in lamps, paper chromatography |

---

## 2.10 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This table is your **ready-reckoner** for the ESE. Memorise SI units alongside the symbols.

| # | Property | Formula | SI Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Density | $\rho = \dfrac{m}{V}$ | $\text{kg/m}^3$ |
| 2 | Specific Volume | $v = \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{kg}$ |
| 3 | Specific Weight | $\gamma = \rho g$ | $\text{N/m}^3$ |
| 4 | Specific Gravity | $S = \dfrac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Newton's Law of Viscosity | $\tau = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy}$ | $\tau$ in $\text{Pa}$; $\mu$ in $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ |
| 6 | Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ |
| 7 | Surface Tension | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{L}$ | $\text{N/m}$ |
| 8 | Pressure inside droplet | $\Delta P = \dfrac{2\sigma}{R}$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 9 | Pressure inside soap bubble | $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\sigma}{R}$ | $\text{Pa}$ |
| 10 | Capillary Rise / Fall | $h = \dfrac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$ | $\text{m}$ |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Newton's Law of Viscosity from Shear Force Balance

### Conceptual Setup

Consider a fluid layer of thickness $dy$ between two large parallel plates. Let:
- The lower plate be **fixed**.
- The upper plate moves with velocity $u$ under an applied tangential force $F$ per unit area $\tau$.
- The fluid adheres to both plates (the **No-Slip Condition**).

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Velocity variation across the gap.**

Because of the no-slip condition, the velocity varies linearly from $0$ at the bottom plate to $u$ at the top plate across the gap $dy$.

$$u(y) = u \cdot \frac{y}{dy}$$

**Step 2 — Define the velocity gradient.**

The rate of change of velocity in the $y$-direction is:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{u}{dy}$$

**Step 3 — Relate shear stress to velocity gradient (Newton's Hypothesis).**

Newton postulated that the shear stress is **directly proportional** to the velocity gradient, and the constant of proportionality is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:

$$\tau \propto \frac{du}{dy}$$

Introducing the proportionality constant:

$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 4 — Express total shear force.**

For an area $A$ on the upper plate, the total shear force is:

$$F = \tau \cdot A = \mu A \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Step 5 — Rearrange to express viscosity.**

This is the standard form used for viscosity measurement using a **rotational viscometer** or a **falling ball viscometer**:

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{\left( \dfrac{du}{dy} \right)} = \frac{F \cdot dy}{A \cdot u}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** Viscosity $\mu$ is the ratio of shear stress to the rate of shear strain. High $\mu$ → fluid resists deformation strongly. Zero $\mu$ → ideal (inviscid) fluid.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Capillary Rise (Jurin's Law)

### Conceptual Setup

A thin glass tube of radius $r$ is dipped into a liquid of density $\rho$ and surface tension $\sigma$. The liquid rises to a height $h$ inside the tube due to surface tension acting along the contact line.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the upward force due to surface tension.**

Surface tension $\sigma$ acts along the contact line of length equal to the circumference of the tube ($2\pi r$). At the contact angle $\theta$, the **vertical component** of the surface tension pulls the liquid up:

$$F_{up} = \sigma \cdot (2\pi r) \cdot \cos\theta$$

**Step 2 — Identify the downward force due to gravity.**

The weight of the liquid column of height $h$ is balanced against this surface tension force. Volume of liquid column $V = \pi r^2 h$. Hence the weight:

$$F_{down} = \rho \cdot g \cdot \pi r^2 \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium condition.**

At equilibrium, the upward surface tension force equals the downward weight of the liquid column:

$$\sigma (2\pi r) \cos\theta = \rho g \pi r^2 h$$

**Step 4 — Simplify.**

Divide both sides by $\pi r$:

$$2\sigma \cos\theta = \rho g r h$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $h$.**

$$\boxed{\,h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r} = \frac{4\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g d}\,}$$

**Special Cases:**

- $\theta = 0°$ (perfect wetting, e.g., water on clean glass): $h_{max} = \dfrac{2\sigma}{\rho g r}$
- $\theta = 180°$ (perfect non-wetting): $h_{max} = -\dfrac{2\sigma}{\rho g r}$ (capillary depression)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Pressure Inside a Liquid Droplet (Young-Laplace)

**Step 1 — Geometry.** Consider a spherical droplet of radius $R$ with internal pressure $P_i$ and external pressure $P_o$.

**Step 2 — Free body cut.** Imagine cutting the droplet into two equal hemispheres. The internal pressure acts over the projected circular area $\pi R^2$, pushing the two halves apart.

$$F_{pressure} = (P_i - P_o) \cdot \pi R^2 = \Delta P \cdot \pi R^2$$

**Step 3 — Surface tension along the cut.** Surface tension $\sigma$ acts along the cut circumference $2\pi R$, holding the two halves together:

$$F_{surface tension} = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 4 — Equilibrium.**

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 = \sigma \cdot 2\pi R$$

**Step 5 — Solve.**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta P = \frac{2\sigma}{R}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Soap bubble has two surfaces (inner and outer)**, so the force due to surface tension doubles → $\Delta P = \dfrac{4\sigma}{R}$. This distinction is a *guaranteed KTU question*.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ideal vs Real Fluid

| Parameter | Ideal Fluid | Real Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Viscosity (μ) | Zero (μ = 0) | Non-zero (μ > 0) |
| Surface Tension (σ) | Neglected | Considered |
| Compressibility | Incompressible | Slightly compressible |
| Shear Resistance under static condition | None | None at rest; finite when moving |
| Friction in flow | Zero | Finite head loss |
| Equations used | Euler's Equation | Navier-Stokes Equation |
| Examples | Hypothetical (vacuum-like) | Water, Oil, Air, Honey |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Pattern

**Problem (Model):** A Newtonian fluid fills the gap of 0.5 mm between a fixed lower plate and an upper plate of area $0.4 \text{ m}^2$. The upper plate moves at $0.25 \text{ m/s}$ and requires a force of 1.5 N to maintain this velocity. Determine: (a) the shear stress, (b) the dynamic viscosity, and (c) the kinematic viscosity if the fluid density is 850 kg/m³.

### Solution

**Given:**
- Gap $dy = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Area $A = 0.4 \text{ m}^2$
- Velocity $u = 0.25 \text{ m/s}$
- Force $F = 1.5 \text{ N}$
- Density $\rho = 850 \text{ kg/m}^3$

**(a) Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{1.5}{0.4} = 3.75 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**(b) Velocity Gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{0.25}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 500 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Dynamic Viscosity:**

$$\mu = \frac{\tau}{du/dy} = \frac{3.75}{500} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$$

**(c) Kinematic Viscosity:**

$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^{-3}}{850} = 8.824 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Final Answer:** $\tau = 3.75 \text{ Pa}$, $\mu = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$, $\nu = 8.824 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$.
