# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Q: Define Newton's law of viscosity. Differentiate between dynamic and kinematic viscosity.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Newton's Law of Viscosity** states that the shear stress $\tau$ developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain (velocity gradient) $\dfrac{du}{dy}$, and the proportionality constant is the dynamic viscosity $\mu$:
>
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$
>
> **Dynamic Viscosity ($\mu$):** It is the ratio of shear stress to velocity gradient and represents the fluid's internal resistance to flow. SI Unit: $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ or $\text{N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$.
>
> **Kinematic Viscosity ($\nu$):** It is the ratio of dynamic viscosity to the density of the fluid, $\nu = \mu / \rho$. SI Unit: $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. It represents the fluid's resistance to flow under the influence of gravity alone.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q: Explain the phenomenon of capillarity with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Capillarity** is the phenomenon of rise or fall of a liquid in a small-diameter tube (capillary) due to surface tension.
>
> When a capillary tube is dipped in water (a wetting fluid with contact angle $\theta < 90°$), the water **rises** inside the tube. For non-wetting fluids like mercury ($\theta > 90°$), the level **falls (depression)**.
>
> **Example:** Kerosene rises in the wick of a lamp; water rises in the roots of plants and tree trunks; mercury falls in a glass tube.
>
> The capillary rise $h$ is given by Jurin's Law:
>
> $$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme Part B questions offer an internal choice. Below are two **complete alternative questions (A & B)** with full model solutions. Examinees may answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### ❖ QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Define the following fluid properties with their SI units: (i) Density, (ii) Specific Weight, (iii) Specific Gravity, (iv) Dynamic Viscosity, (v) Kinematic Viscosity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12 mm diameter capillary tube is dipped into a liquid of surface tension 0.072 N/m and density 1000 kg/m³. If the contact angle is 15°, calculate the capillary rise. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Definitions:**

(i) **Density (ρ):** Mass per unit volume of the fluid.
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} \quad \text{; SI Unit: kg/m}^3$$

(ii) **Specific Weight (γ):** Weight per unit volume.
$$\gamma = \rho g \quad \text{; SI Unit: N/m}^3$$

(iii) **Specific Gravity (S):** Ratio of density of the fluid to the density of water at 4 °C.
$$S = \frac{\rho_{fluid}}{\rho_{water}} \quad \text{; Dimensionless}$$

(iv) **Dynamic Viscosity (μ):** The proportionality constant in Newton's law of viscosity.
$$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} \quad \text{; SI Unit: Pa}\cdot\text{s or N}\cdot\text{s/m}^2$$

(v) **Kinematic Viscosity (ν):** Ratio of dynamic viscosity to density.
$$\nu = \frac{\mu}{\rho} \quad \text{; SI Unit: m}^2/\text{s}$$

---

**Part (b) — Capillary Rise Calculation:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$ → Radius $r = 0.006 \text{ m}$
- Surface tension $\sigma = 0.072 \text{ N/m}$
- Density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Contact angle $\theta = 15°$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Apply Jurin's Law:**

$$h = \frac{2\sigma \cos\theta}{\rho g r}$$

**Substitute values:**

$$\cos(15°) = 0.9659$$

$$h = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times 0.9659}{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.006}$$

**Numerator:**

$$2 \times 0.072 \times 0.9659 = 0.1391$$

**Denominator:**

$$1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.006 = 58.86$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{h = \frac{0.1391}{58.86} = 2.363 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 2.363 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### ❖ QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** State and explain Newton's law of viscosity. A fluid of dynamic viscosity 0.9 Pa·s fills the gap of 1.5 mm between a fixed plate and a movable plate of area $0.6 \text{ m}^2$. Find the force required to drag the upper plate at a velocity of 1.2 m/s. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With neat sketches, explain the difference between a Newtonian and a Non-Newtonian fluid. Give two examples of each. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Newton's Law & Force Calculation:**

> **Newton's Law of Viscosity:** The shear stress developed in a fluid is directly proportional to the rate of shear strain, and the proportionality constant is the dynamic viscosity:
>
> $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$

**Given:**
- $\mu = 0.9 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$
- $dy = 1.5 \text{ mm} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- $A = 0.6 \text{ m}^2$
- $u = 1.2 \text{ m/s}$

**Step 1 — Velocity Gradient:**

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1.2}{1.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 800 \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Shear Stress:**

$$\tau = \mu \cdot \frac{du}{dy} = 0.9 \times 800 = 720 \text{ Pa}$$

**Step 3 — Required Force:**

$$F = \tau \times A = 720 \times 0.6 = 432 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F = 432 \text{ N}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Newtonian vs Non-Newtonian Fluids:**

| Feature | Newtonian Fluid | Non-Newtonian Fluid |
|---|---|---|
| Relation between $\tau$ and $du/dy$ | Linear; passes through origin | Non-linear; may not pass through origin |
| Viscosity | Constant with shear rate | Varies with shear rate |
| Examples | Water, Air, Mercury, Kerosene, Light oil | Blood, Paint, Toothpaste, Honey, Slurry |

> **Sketch of τ vs du/dy:**
>
> - **Newtonian fluid:** A straight line passing through the origin with slope $\mu$.
> - **Pseudoplastic (shear-thinning):** Curve concave to the du/dy axis (viscosity decreases with shear) — e.g., paint.
> - **Dilatant (shear-thickening):** Curve convex to the du/dy axis (viscosity increases with shear) — e.g., starch suspensions.
> - **Bingham plastic:** A line with a non-zero yield stress intercept $\tau_y$ — e.g., toothpaste.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on This Module:**
>
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Mixing up $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ (dynamic) with $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (kinematic) viscosity. Always write the unit explicitly.
> 2. **Soap Bubble vs Droplet:** Forgetting that a soap bubble has **two surfaces**, hence $\Delta P = 4\sigma/R$, not $2\sigma/R$.
> 3. **Capillary Sign:** Not specifying whether the liquid is **wetting ($\theta < 90°$, rises)** or **non-wetting ($\theta > 90°$, falls)**.
> 4. **Newton's Law Misstatement:** Writing "shear stress is proportional to velocity" instead of "shear stress is proportional to **velocity gradient**" — the gradient (spatial derivative) is critical.
> 5. **Specific Gravity ≠ Density:** Writing numerical values of density in the box for specific gravity. $S$ is *dimensionless*.
> 6. **Ideal Fluid Definition:** Stating "ideal fluid has no viscosity" — but examiners prefer the *complete* definition: **inviscid + incompressible**.
> 7. **Cavitation:** Forgetting to state that cavitation occurs when $P_{local} \le P_{vapour}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Density (ρ)** = mass/volume; SI: $\text{kg/m}^3$
- [x] **Specific Weight (γ)** = $\rho g$; SI: $\text{N/m}^3$
- [x] **Specific Gravity (S)** is **dimensionless**; reference is water at 4 °C ($\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$)
- [x] **Vapour Pressure** governs **cavitation** — when local pressure drops below it
- [x] **Newton's Law of Viscosity**: $\tau = \mu (du/dy)$ — valid **only for Newtonian fluids**
- [x] **Dynamic viscosity μ** has units $\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$; **Kinematic viscosity ν** has units $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- [x] **Newtonian fluids** have *linear* τ vs du/dy relationship (water, air, mercury)
- [x] **Non-Newtonian fluids** have *non-linear* τ vs du/dy (blood, paint, toothpaste)
- [x] **Surface tension σ** has units $\text{N/m}$; acts along the surface like a stretched membrane
- [x] **Pressure inside a droplet**: $\Delta P = 2\sigma/R$
- [x] **Pressure inside a soap bubble**: $\Delta P = 4\sigma/R$ (two surfaces!)
- [x] **Capillarity rise/fall** follows **Jurin's Law**: $h = (2\sigma \cos\theta)/(\rho g r)$
- [x] **Wetting fluids** (e.g., water-glass): $\theta < 90°$ → RISE
- [x] **Non-wetting fluids** (e.g., mercury-glass): $\theta > 90°$ → FALL
- [x] **Ideal Fluid**: Inviscid (μ = 0) + Incompressible — Euler's equation applies
- [x] **Real Fluid**: Has viscosity, surface tension, and may be compressible — Navier-Stokes applies
- [x] **No-slip condition**: Fluid layer in contact with a solid surface has the same velocity as the surface
- [x] **Standard values to memorise**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\sigma_{water} \approx 0.072 \text{ N/m}$
- [x] **Practice unit conversions**: $1 \text{ Poise} = 0.1 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$; $1 \text{ Stoke} = 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closing Note:** This module carries **15–20% of Module 1 marks**. Mastering these properties gives you a *guaranteed foundation* for all subsequent modules — fluid statics, kinematics, dynamics, and open channel flow all build directly on these primary properties. Revise the formulas before every test!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pressure and Its Measurement: Fluid Pressure at a Point, Pascal's Law, Pressure Variation in a Fluid, Gauge Pressure, Absolute Pressure, Vacuum Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Pressure and Its Measurement: Fluid Pressure at a Point, Pascal's Law, Pressure Variation in a Fluid, Gauge Pressure, Absolute Pressure, Vacuum Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Fluid Pressure — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Fluid Pressure** is defined as the **normal force per unit area** exerted by a fluid (liquid or gas) on a surface or at a point within the fluid. Mathematically:

$$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

where $P$ is pressure (in **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**), $\Delta F$ is the normal force acting on an infinitesimal area $\Delta A$.

In the **SI system**, the standard unit is **Pascal (Pa) = 1 N/m²**. In engineering practice, larger multiples are commonly used: **kPa**, **MPa**, and **bar** (1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa).

---

### 1.2 Fluid Pressure at a Point — The Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Fluid pressure at a point is a **scalar quantity** (despite force and area being vectors), because the pressure at a point in a static fluid acts equally in all directions.

This is a fundamental result derived from the equilibrium of an infinitesimal fluid element. The pressure at a point is **independent of the orientation** of the area on which it acts. If you tilt a small pressure sensor inside a fluid, the reading remains the same in every direction.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Underwater Balloon:**
Imagine a sealed, air-filled balloon submerged in a swimming pool. The water squeezes the balloon from **every side equally** — top, bottom, left, right, and even diagonally. A small pressure gauge placed anywhere on the balloon's surface would record the **same value**, regardless of which face it touches. This is exactly what "pressure at a point is isotropic" means.

---

### 1.3 Pascal's Law — Statement and Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law (KTU Board Definition):** *A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container.*

**Conceptual Analogy — The Hydraulic Car Jack:**
When you press down on the small piston of a hydraulic jack with a small force $F_1$, this creates a pressure $P = F_1/A_1$ in the oil. This same pressure is transmitted to the large piston (area $A_2$), producing an amplified force $F_2 = P \times A_2$. This is the operating principle behind **hydraulic lifts, hydraulic brakes, and earth-moving equipment**.

---

### 1.4 Types of Pressure — Gauge, Absolute, and Vacuum

| Type | Definition | Reference Point |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Absolute Pressure ($P_{abs}$)** | Total pressure measured from **perfect vacuum (zero)** reference | 0 Pa (vacuum) |
| **Gauge Pressure ($P_{gauge}$)** | Pressure measured **above atmospheric** pressure | Atmospheric ($P_{atm}$) |
| **Vacuum Pressure ($P_{vac}$)** | Pressure **below atmospheric** (negative gauge) | Atmospheric ($P_{atm}$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Relationship (must be memorized for KTU):**
> $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ and $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$

**Real-World Analogy — The Tyre Pressure Gauge:**
When you check your car tyres at a petrol pump, the gauge reads **2.4 bar** — this is the *gauge pressure* (above atmospheric). The *absolute pressure* inside the tyre is actually $2.4 + 1.013 = 3.413$ bar. If the gauge instead reads **-0.5 bar**, that means the system is under vacuum (suction).

---

### 1.5 Pressure Variation in a Fluid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a **static fluid**, pressure varies only with **depth (vertical height)**, not with the shape or cross-section of the container. This is famously demonstrated by the **hydrostatic paradox** — a tall, thin tube and a wide vessel containing the same fluid depth produce the same pressure at the bottom.

**Conceptual Analogy — Diving in a Swimming Pool:**
The deeper you swim, the more your ears hurt. This is because water pressure increases with depth. At **10 m** depth in freshwater, the pressure is already **double** the atmospheric pressure at the surface. Deep-sea submarines must be built as spheres (uniform stress) because pressure acts equally from all sides at great depths.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Properties of Fluid Pressure

**Why is fluid pressure a scalar?**
Consider a small triangular wedge of fluid at rest. Since the fluid is in static equilibrium, the net force on every face must be zero. Balancing the forces on three mutually perpendicular faces (x, y, z) yields the result that the **normal stress (pressure) is the same in all directions**.

**Bulleted Logic Steps — Pressure at a Point:**

1. **Step 1:** Imagine a small tetrahedral fluid element with three faces perpendicular to x, y, z axes and one inclined face of area $dA$.
2. **Step 2:** Let the inclined face have an outward normal $\vec{n}$ with direction cosines $l, m, n$.
3. **Step 3:** Areas of the perpendicular faces are $l \cdot dA$, $m \cdot dA$, $n \cdot dA$ (geometric projection).
4. **Step 4:** Apply equilibrium: $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum F_z = 0$, ignoring body forces (weight) as the element shrinks to a point.
5. **Step 5:** The result yields $P_x = P_y = P_z = P_n = P$ — pressure is the same in **all directions** at a point.

---

### 2.2 Pascal's Law — Mathematical Form

For a fluid in static equilibrium, the **pressure difference between two points** depends only on the **vertical elevation difference** and the **specific weight** of the fluid:

$$P_2 - P_1 = -\gamma (z_2 - z_1) = \gamma (z_1 - z_2)$$

where $\gamma = \rho g$ is the **specific weight** (N/m³), $\rho$ is density (kg/m³), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Hydraulic Press Equation (Application of Pascal's Law):**

$$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$

**Mechanical Advantage** = $A_2 / A_1$. A small input force on a small piston produces a large output force on a large piston.

---

### 2.3 Hydrostatic Pressure Variation (Equation Derivation)

The pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface of a liquid is:

$$P = P_0 + \rho g h = P_0 + \gamma h$$

where $P_0$ is the pressure acting on the free surface (usually atmospheric).

**Pressure Head (height of fluid column):**

$$h = \frac{P}{\gamma}$$

The dimension of $h$ is **metres (m) of fluid**. The same pressure corresponds to different heads in different fluids (e.g., 1 m of water = 0.0735 m of mercury).

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P = F/A$ | Basic pressure definition | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $P = P_0 + \gamma h$ | Hydrostatic pressure at depth $h$ | Pa |
| 3 | $dP/dz = -\gamma$ | Hydrostatic pressure gradient (vertical) | Pa/m |
| 4 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Gauge to absolute conversion | Pa |
| 5 | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ | Vacuum pressure (when $P_{abs} < P_{atm}$) | Pa |
| 6 | $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$ | Pascal's Law (hydraulic press) | — |
| 7 | $h = P/\gamma$ | Pressure head | m |
| 8 | $P = \rho g h$ | Pressure due to liquid column (gauge) | Pa |
| 9 | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa | Standard atmospheric pressure | kPa |
| 10 | $P_{atm} = 10.33$ m of water | Atmospheric head in water | m |
| 11 | $P_{atm} = 760$ mm of Hg | Atmospheric head in mercury | mm Hg |
| 12 | $\gamma_{water} = 9.81$ kN/m³ | Specific weight of water at 4°C | kN/m³ |
| 13 | $\gamma_{Hg} = 13.6 \times \gamma_{water}$ | Specific weight of mercury | kN/m³ |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Dam Design:** Hydrostatic pressure distribution on a vertical dam face increases linearly with depth. The resultant thrust acts at $H/3$ from the base, where $H$ is the depth of water. This is critical for stability analysis.
- **Submarine Hulls:** Must withstand crushing pressures at great depths using spherical/cylindrical pressure vessels.
- **Hydraulic Presses & Brakes:** All exploit Pascal's law to multiply force.
- **Barometers and Manometers:** Measure pressure using liquid column heights.
- **Water Supply Systems:** Pressure tanks and pumps must account for hydrostatic head to deliver water to elevated storage tanks.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Quick Tip:** Whenever you see a numerical problem involving "depth below free surface," immediately write $P = P_{atm} + \gamma h$ if the problem wants **absolute pressure**, or $P = \gamma h$ if it wants **gauge pressure**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrostatic Pressure Equation $P = P_0 + \gamma h$

Consider a static fluid in a container. Take a vertical column of fluid of cross-sectional area $A$ extending from the free surface (point 1) down to a depth $h$ (point 2).

**Forces Acting on the Column:**

- $P_1 \cdot A$ — downward force from the pressure on the free surface.
- $P_2 \cdot A$ — upward force from the fluid pressure at the bottom.
- $W$ — weight of the fluid column acting downward = $\rho g A h$.

**Step-by-Step Equilibrium Analysis:**

```
Step 1: Apply Newton's second law for static equilibrium.
        Net vertical force = 0
        (since the fluid is at rest, no acceleration)
```

$$\sum F_{vertical} = 0$$

```
Step 2: Sum the forces in the vertical direction.
        Upward forces: P_2 × A
        Downward forces: P_1 × A + W
```

$$P_2 \cdot A - P_1 \cdot A - W = 0$$

```
Step 3: Substitute the weight expression.
        W = mass × g = (ρ × V) × g = ρ × (A × h) × g = ρ g A h
```

$$P_2 \cdot A - P_1 \cdot A - \rho g A h = 0$$

```
Step 4: Divide throughout by the cross-sectional area A.
```

$$P_2 - P_1 - \rho g h = 0$$

```
Step 5: Rearrange to solve for P_2.
```

$$P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h$$

```
Step 6: Recognise that ρg = γ (specific weight), and P_1 = P_0 (free surface pressure).
        This gives the standard hydrostatic pressure equation.
```

$$\boxed{P = P_0 + \gamma h}$$

This is the **fundamental hydrostatic pressure equation** used in **every** KTU numerical on fluid statics.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Pressure is Isotropic at a Point

Consider a small triangular wedge of fluid in static equilibrium. Let the wedge have:
- Two faces perpendicular to x and y axes, with areas $dy \cdot dz$ and $dx \cdot dz$ (per unit length into the page).
- One inclined face (hypotenuse) of area $dA$, with outward normal $\vec{n}$.
- The angle of the inclined face with the x-axis is $\theta$.

**Direction Cosines of the Normal:**

$$l = \cos\theta, \quad m = \sin\theta, \quad n = 0 \text{ (2D analysis)}$$

**Geometric Projection of Areas:**

$$\text{Face perpendicular to x-axis: } dA_x = dA \cdot \cos\theta = dA \cdot l$$

$$\text{Face perpendicular to y-axis: } dA_y = dA \cdot \sin\theta = dA \cdot m$$

**Equilibrium in x-direction:**

```
Step 1: Sum horizontal forces and set to zero.
        Pressure on x-face pushes inward (positive x), pressure on inclined face has an x-component.
```

$$P_x \cdot dA \cdot l - P_n \cdot dA \cdot l = 0$$

```
Step 2: Solve for the relationship between pressures.
```

$$P_x = P_n$$

**Equilibrium in y-direction (similar analysis):**

$$P_y = P_n$$

```
Step 3: Combine both results.
```

$$\boxed{P_x = P_y = P_n = P}$$

As the element shrinks to a point, this holds for **all directions** — pressure at a point is the same regardless of the orientation of the surface. This proves Pascal's principle and the **scalar nature of pressure**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pressure Variation in a Compressible Fluid (Atmosphere)

For a compressible fluid like air, $\rho$ varies with elevation. From the hydrostatic equation:

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\rho g$$

For an isothermal atmosphere, using the ideal gas law $P = \rho R T$:

```
Step 1: Substitute ρ = P/(RT) into the hydrostatic equation.
```

$$\frac{dP}{dz} = -\frac{P g}{R T}$$

```
Step 2: Separate variables and integrate from z=0 (sea level) to z=h.
```

$$\int_{P_0}^{P} \frac{dP}{P} = -\int_0^h \frac{g}{R T} dz$$

```
Step 3: Assuming T = constant, perform the integration.
```

$$\ln\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{g h}{R T}$$

```
Step 4: Exponentiate to obtain the isothermal pressure variation.
```

$$P = P_0 \cdot e^{-g h / (R T)}$$

This shows that atmospheric pressure decays **exponentially** with altitude — a result you'll use in advanced fluid mechanics and gas dynamics courses.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples

**Example A (KTU Board Standard):**
A tank contains oil (specific gravity = 0.85) to a depth of 6 m above a water layer of depth 3 m. Find the gauge pressure at the bottom and the absolute pressure if the tank is open to atmosphere.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

```
Step 1: Identify the specific weights.
        γ_water = 9.81 kN/m³
        γ_oil = 0.85 × 9.81 = 8.3385 kN/m³
```

```
Step 2: Apply the hydrostatic pressure equation cumulatively.
        P_bottom = γ_oil × h_oil + γ_water × h_water
        (gauge, since atmospheric is excluded)
```

$$P_{bottom} = 8.3385 \times 6 + 9.81 \times 3$$

$$P_{bottom} = 50.031 + 29.43$$

$$P_{bottom} = 79.461 \text{ kPa (gauge)}$$

```
Step 3: Add atmospheric pressure for absolute value.
        P_abs = 79.461 + 101.325 = 180.786 kPa
```

$$\boxed{P_{gauge} = 79.46 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_{abs} = 180.79 \text{ kPa}}$$

**Example B (Pascal's Law Application):**
A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter 5 cm and a large piston of diameter 50 cm. A force of 200 N is applied on the small piston. Find the load that can be lifted.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

```
Step 1: Calculate the areas of both pistons.
        A_1 = π/4 × (0.05)² = 1.9635 × 10⁻³ m²
        A_2 = π/4 × (0.50)² = 0.19635 m²
```

```
Step 2: Apply Pascal's Law.
        F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2
        F_2 = F_1 × (A_2/A_1) = F_1 × (D_2/D_1)²
```

$$F_2 = 200 \times \left(\frac{50}{5}\right)^2 = 200 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{F_2 = 20{,}000 \text{ N} = 20 \text{ kN}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut:** For circular pistons, the force ratio simplifies to $(D_2/D_1)^2$ — no need to compute areas separately.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pressure Measurement Instruments — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fluid Under Test] --> B{Pressure Type?}
    B -- Above Atmospheric --> C[Gauge Pressure]
    B -- Below Atmospheric --> D[Vacuum Pressure]
    B -- Reference Vacuum --> E[Absolute Pressure]
    
    C --> F[Piezometer]
    C --> G[U-Tube Manometer]
    C --> H[Differential Manometer]
    C --> I[Bourdon Gauge]
    
    D --> J[Vacuum Gauge]
    D --> K[Barometer]
    
    E --> L[Absolute Pressure Transducer]
    E --> M[Mercury Barometer]
    
    F --> N[Output: Height h]
    G --> N
    H --> N
    I --> O[Output: Deflection]
    J --> O
    K --> N
    L --> O
    M --> N
    
    N --> P[Calculate: P = γh]
    O --> Q[Calibrated Scale Reading]
    P --> R[Final Pressure Value]
    Q --> R
```

**Interpretation:** The diagram maps the decision flow for selecting a pressure measurement device based on the **type of pressure** and the **desired output** (column height or mechanical deflection).

---

### 4.2 Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution on a Vertical Wall

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free Surface P_atm] --> B[Depth z = 0]
    B --> C[Depth z = h/2]
    C --> D[Depth z = h]
    
    A1[P = P_atm] -.-> B
    A2[P = P_atm + γh/2] -.-> C
    A3[P = P_atm + γh] -.-> D
    
    E[Hydrostatic Pressure] --> F[Triangular Distribution]
    F --> G[Resultant Force F_R = γh²·b/2]
    G --> H[Acts at h/3 from base]
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#ffe1e1
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style H fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Interpretation:** Shows how hydrostatic pressure increases **linearly** with depth, forming a triangular distribution. The **resultant force** acts at the **centroid** of the pressure prism, which is at $h/3$ from the bottom for a triangular distribution.

---

### 4.3 Pascal's Law — Hydraulic Press Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Force F1] --> B[Small Piston Area A1]
    B --> C{Pressure P = F1/A1}
    C --> D[Incompressible Fluid Column]
    D --> E[Transmitted Undiminished to Large Piston]
    E --> F[Large Piston Area A2]
    F --> G[Output Force F2 = P × A2]
    G --> H[Mechanical Advantage MA = A2/A1]
    
    H --> I[Used in: Hydraulic Jacks]
    H --> J[Used in: Hydraulic Brakes]
    H --> K[Used in: Hydraulic Lifts]
    H --> L[Used in: Earth Movers]
    
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#e1ffe1
    style H fill:#e1f5ff
```

**Interpretation:** Illustrates the force amplification mechanism of a hydraulic system, governed by Pascal's law. The mechanical advantage is the **ratio of piston areas**.

---

### 4.4 Absolute, Gauge, and Vacuum Pressure — Reference Scale

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[Perfect Vacuum 0 Pa] --> A1[Vacuum Region]
    A1 --> A2[Atmospheric Pressure 101.325 kPa]
    A2 --> A3[Positive Gauge Region]
    A3 --> A4[High Pressure Region]
    
    A0 -.->|Reference| B1[P_abs]
    A2 -.->|Reference| B2[P_gauge = 0 here]
    A2 -.->|Reference| B3[P_vac = 0 here]
    
    A1 -.-> B4[P_abs is small positive]
    A1 -.-> B5[P_gauge is NEGATIVE]
    A1 -.-> B6[P_vac is POSITIVE]
    
    A3 -.-> B7[P_abs > P_atm]
    A3 -.-> B8[P_gauge is POSITIVE]
    A3 -.-> B9[P_vac is NEGATIVE]
    
    style A0 fill:#1a1a1a,color:#fff
    style A2 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A1 fill:#ffe1e1
    style A3 fill:#e1ffe1
```

**Interpretation:** Shows the **three pressure regions** on a common reference scale and how the three pressure definitions relate to one another.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
*Define fluid pressure at a point. Why is pressure considered a scalar quantity even though force is a vector?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** Fluid pressure at a point is defined as the normal force per unit area acting on an infinitesimal surface element at that point:
> $$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0}\frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

> **Scalar Explanation (2 Marks):** Pressure is a scalar because at any point in a static fluid, the magnitude of the normal force per unit area is **the same in all directions**, regardless of the orientation of the area. This can be proved by considering a small tetrahedral fluid element in equilibrium and showing that $P_x = P_y = P_z = P_n$. Hence, pressure has magnitude only, not direction, making it a scalar.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
*Distinguish clearly between absolute pressure, gauge pressure, and vacuum pressure. State the equations relating them.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Type | Definition | Equation |
|------|-----------|----------|
| **Absolute Pressure** | Pressure measured from perfect vacuum (true zero) | $P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$ |
| **Gauge Pressure** | Pressure measured above atmospheric | $P_{gauge} = P_{abs} - P_{atm}$ |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | Pressure below atmospheric (suction) | $P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$ |

> **Key Insight (1 Mark):** Gauge and vacuum pressures are negative of each other when on opposite sides of atmospheric. A pressure below atmospheric has a **negative gauge** value and a **positive vacuum** value simultaneously.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024, Modified]*

**(a)** State and prove Pascal's law. Explain its application in a hydraulic press with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vertical tank contains water to a depth of 8 m, with a 3 m layer of oil (specific gravity = 0.8) on top. Find (i) the gauge pressure at the bottom of the tank, (ii) the absolute pressure at the bottom, and (iii) the location where the absolute pressure equals 150 kPa. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.A(a):**

**Pascal's Law Statement (2 Marks):**
> An external pressure applied to a confined fluid at rest is transmitted **equally and undiminished** in all directions throughout the fluid.

**Proof (3 Marks):**
Consider a fluid element in static equilibrium. From the isotropy of pressure at a point (already proved in Section 3.2), the pressure at any depth $h$ is:
$$P = P_0 + \gamma h$$
Since the change in pressure $dP = \gamma \, dh$ depends only on depth and not on horizontal position or container shape, a change in $P_0$ at the free surface produces an **identical change** at every point. This is the mathematical basis of Pascal's law.

**Application — Hydraulic Press (2 Marks):**
```
Input force F1 → Small piston A1 → Pressure P = F1/A1
       ↓
Transmitted through fluid
       ↓
Large piston A2 → Output force F2 = (F1/A1) × A2
       ↓
Mechanical Advantage = A2/A1
```
A small input force produces a large output force, enabling the lifting of heavy loads (e.g., cars, buses, heavy machinery).

---

**Solution to Q.A(b):**

**Given Data:**
- Depth of water, $h_w = 8$ m
- Depth of oil, $h_o = 3$ m
- Specific gravity of oil, $S_o = 0.8$
- $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa

**Specific Weights:**
$$\gamma_w = 9.81 \text{ kN/m}^3, \quad \gamma_o = 0.8 \times 9.81 = 7.848 \text{ kN/m}^3$$

**(i) Gauge Pressure at Bottom (2 Marks):**

$$P_{gauge} = \gamma_o \cdot h_o + \gamma_w \cdot h_w$$

$$P_{gauge} = 7.848 \times 3 + 9.81 \times 8$$

$$P_{gauge} = 23.544 + 78.48 = 102.024 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Absolute Pressure at Bottom (2 Marks):**

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge} = 101.325 + 102.024 = 203.349 \text{ kPa}$$

**(iii) Depth where $P_{abs} = 150$ kPa (3 Marks):**

This pressure must be located within the **water layer** (since oil layer gives max ~23.5 kPa, plus atmospheric is 124.8 kPa < 150 kPa).

Let $z$ be the depth into the water layer from the oil-water interface.

$$150 = P_{atm} + \gamma_o h_o + \gamma_w z$$

$$150 = 101.325 + 23.544 + 9.81 z$$

$$150 - 124.869 = 9.81 z$$

$$25.131 = 9.81 z$$

$$z = 2.562 \text{ m into the water layer}$$

Total depth from free surface = $3 + 2.562 = 5.562$ m.

$$\boxed{P_{gauge,bottom} = 102.02 \text{ kPa}, \quad P_{abs,bottom} = 203.35 \text{ kPa}, \quad \text{Depth} = 5.56 \text{ m}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]*

**(a)** Derive the hydrostatic pressure equation $P = P_0 + \gamma h$ for a static fluid column. List any two assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic press has a small piston of diameter 10 cm and a large piston of diameter 60 cm. An input force of 150 N is applied to the small piston. Calculate: (i) the pressure developed in the fluid, (ii) the load lifted by the large piston, and (iii) the mechanical advantage. If the small piston is pushed down by 20 cm, find the distance moved by the large piston (assuming incompressible fluid). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution to Q.B(a):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
1. Fluid is **static** (no motion, no shear stress).
2. Fluid is **incompressible** (constant density $\rho$).
3. Acceleration due to gravity $g$ is constant.

**Derivation (6 Marks):**

```
Step 1: Consider a vertical fluid column of cross-section A,
        extending from the free surface (point 1) to a depth h (point 2).
        
Step 2: Identify the forces:
        - P1·A acting downward on the top of the column
        - P2·A acting upward on the bottom of the column
        - W = ρgAh acting downward (weight of fluid)
        
Step 3: Apply equilibrium (static fluid, no acceleration):
        ΣF_vertical = 0
        P2·A - P1·A - W = 0
        P2·A - P1·A - ρgAh = 0
        
Step 4: Divide by A:
        P2 - P1 = ρgh
        
Step 5: Substitute γ = ρg and P1 = P0 (free surface pressure):
        P2 = P0 + γh
        
Final Result: P = P0 + γh
```

**Significance:** This equation is the foundation of manometry, dam design, and buoyancy calculations.

---

**Solution to Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 10$ cm = 0.10 m, $D_2 = 60$ cm = 0.60 m
- $F_1 = 150$ N
- $S_1 = 20$ cm = 0.20 m (small piston stroke)

**Areas:**
$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.10)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.60)^2 = 0.2827 \text{ m}^2$$

**(i) Pressure developed (2 Marks):**

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{150}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 19{,}098.6 \text{ Pa} \approx 19.1 \text{ kPa}$$

**(ii) Load lifted (2 Marks):**

$$F_2 = P \times A_2 = 19{,}098.6 \times 0.2827 = 5{,}399.2 \text{ N} \approx 5.4 \text{ kN}$$

Or using the ratio formula:
$$F_2 = F_1 \times \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = 150 \times (6)^2 = 150 \times 36 = 5400 \text{ N}$$

**(iii) Mechanical Advantage (1 Mark):**

$$MA = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{5400}{150} = 36 \quad \text{or}\quad MA = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2 = 36$$

**(iv) Distance moved by large piston (2 Marks):**

By **conservation of volume** (incompressible fluid):
$$A_1 \times S_1 = A_2 \times S_2$$

$$S_2 = \frac{A_1 \times S_1}{A_2} = \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20}{0.2827} = 5.556 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 5.56 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{P = 19.1 \text{ kPa}, \quad F_2 = 5.4 \text{ kN}, \quad MA = 36, \quad S_2 = 5.56 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks (KTU Board Exam):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to add atmospheric pressure** when the problem asks for *absolute pressure*. The examiner specifically tests whether you know $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$. (Lose up to **2 marks**.)
>
> 2. **Using gauge density instead of specific weight** or vice versa. The correct formula is $P = \gamma h$ where $\gamma = \rho g = SG \times 9.81$ kN/m³. Many students forget to multiply by $g$.
>
> 3. **Sign convention errors** in vacuum pressure. A pressure of "0.5 bar vacuum" is **NOT** $+0.5$ bar gauge — it is $-0.5$ bar gauge and $+0.5$ bar vacuum.
>
> 4. **Wrong units:** Writing pressure in $N/m^2$ instead of kPa, or omitting units entirely. Always write kPa or Pa explicitly.
>
> 5. **Forgetting the (D₂/D₁)² shortcut** in hydraulic press problems and computing the full area equation, leading to numerical errors.
>
> 6. **Mixing up the depth of liquid** in multi-layer problems (oil + water). Compute the pressure contribution of each layer **separately** and add.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

✅ **Pressure Definition:** $P = F/A$ (force per unit area, normal to surface). SI unit: **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**.

✅ **Pressure is a Scalar** at a point — same in all directions (isotropy property).

✅ **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to a confined static fluid is transmitted **equally in all directions** without loss.

✅ **Hydrostatic Equation:** $P = P_0 + \gamma h$ where $\gamma = \rho g$ is specific weight.

✅ **Three Pressure Types:**
   - Absolute = Atmospheric + Gauge
   - Gauge = Absolute − Atmospheric
   - Vacuum = Atmospheric − Absolute

✅ **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa = 760 mm Hg = 10.33 m of water = 1.01325 bar.

✅ **Specific Weight of Water:** $\gamma_w = 9.81$ kN/m³ (at 4°C, standard conditions).

✅ **Hydraulic Press:** Force amplification governed by $F_1/A_1 = F_2/A_2$. Mechanical advantage = $(D_2/D_1)^2$ for circular pistons.

✅ **Volume Conservation:** $A_1 S_1 = A_2 S_2$ (small piston moves farther, large piston moves shorter distance).

✅ **Pressure Head:** $h = P/\gamma$ — pressure can be expressed as a height of fluid column.

✅ **In multi-fluid problems**, calculate the pressure contribution of each layer separately using its own $\gamma$ and depth, then add them up cumulatively.

✅ **Always specify the pressure type** (gauge or absolute) in your final numerical answer — the KTU examiner deducts marks for ambiguity.

✅ **Units Check:** Pressure → Pa or kPa; Specific weight → kN/m³; Depth → m. Always verify dimensional consistency.

---

**End of Module 1 Topic Notes — Pressure and Its Measurement**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme — PCAGT303 Fluid Mechanics & Open Channel Hydraulics*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Pressure: Piezometer, Manometers (Simple U Tube, Differential and Single Column), and Mechanical Pressure Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of Pressure: Piezometer, Manometers (Simple U Tube, Differential and Single Column), and Mechanical Pressure Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Pressure? (Foundation Concept)

**Pressure ($P$)** is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on a surface (real or imaginary) in contact with it.

$$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A} = \frac{dF}{dA}$$

Where:
- $F$ = Normal force (in **Newtons, N**)
- $A$ = Area (in **square meters, m²**)
- $P$ = Pressure (in **Pascals, Pa = N/m²**)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In fluid statics, pressure always acts **normal** (perpendicular) to any surface it acts upon. This is because a fluid at rest cannot sustain shear stress. The standard SI unit of pressure is **Pascal (Pa) = 1 N/m²**, with practical units being **kPa, bar, and N/mm²**.

### Classification of Pressure

| Category | Reference Point | Symbol |
|----------|----------------|--------|
| **Absolute Pressure** | Perfect vacuum (zero) | $P_{abs}$ |
| **Gauge Pressure** | Local atmospheric pressure | $P_{gauge}$ |
| **Vacuum Pressure** | Below atmospheric (negative) | $P_{vac}$ |

The fundamental relationship is:

$$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + P_{gauge}$$

$$P_{vac} = P_{atm} - P_{abs}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Most engineering pressure measuring devices (except the barometer) measure **gauge pressure**, not absolute pressure. A negative gauge reading indicates a vacuum (partial) condition.

## 1.2 Why Measure Pressure? (Engineering Context)

Pressure measurement is **fundamental** in virtually every branch of engineering:
- 🏭 **Process Industries**: Monitoring boilers, reactors, distillation columns
- ✈️ **Aerospace**: Altitude measurement, cabin pressurization
- 🏥 **Medical**: Blood pressure, ventilators, syringe calibration
- 🏗️ **Civil/Hydraulic**: Dam pressure, pipe networks, pump performance testing
- 🚗 **Automotive**: Tyre pressure, fuel injection, oil circuits

## 1.3 Categories of Pressure Measuring Instruments

The instruments are broadly classified into **three** primary families:

### 1. Liquid Column Instruments (Manometric Devices)
These use the **hydrostatic principle** — the height of a liquid column is directly proportional to the pressure at its base.
- **Piezometer**
- **Simple U-tube Manometer**
- **Differential U-tube Manometer**
- **Inverted U-tube Manometer**
- **Single Column Manometer (Micromanometer)**

### 2. Mechanical / Elastic Pressure Gauges
These use the **elastic deformation** of a sensing element (tube, diaphragm, or bellows) under pressure.
- **Bourdon Tube Gauge**
- **Diaphragm Gauge**
- **Bellows Gauge**

### 3. Electrical / Electronic Pressure Transducers
Used for high-precision, dynamic, or remote measurements (Strain gauge, Capacitive, Piezoelectric — beyond KTU Module 1 scope).

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Piezometer):** Imagine drinking water through a transparent straw. The water rises in the straw to a height that exactly balances the pressure pushing it up. If you blow harder into the water, the water column rises higher. The taller the water column, the greater the pressure — this is exactly how a **piezometer** works!

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy (Manometer):** Picture a U-shaped transparent tube partially filled with mercury. If you connect one side to a pressurized water pipe and leave the other open to atmosphere, the mercury level on the pipe side will drop while the other side rises. The **difference in mercury levels** tells you exactly how much pressure is in the pipe. This is the classic **U-tube manometer**.

## 1.4 Static Fluid Principle (Foundation for All Devices)

For any incompressible static fluid, the **hydrostatic pressure equation** governs:

$$\frac{dP}{dh} = \rho g \implies P_2 - P_1 = \rho g (h_2 - h_1)$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $h$ = Vertical height difference (**m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This single equation is the **backbone** of every liquid column pressure-measuring device. Every manometer problem in KTU exams ultimately reduces to applying this equation along a continuous fluid path from point A to point B.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Piezometer (Simplest Pressure Measuring Device)

### Construction
A **piezometer** is a transparent vertical tube (glass or plastic) of small bore, connected directly to the vessel or pipe in which the pressure is to be measured. The open end of the tube is exposed to the atmosphere.

### Working Principle
The fluid from the system rises in the piezometer tube until the **weight of the fluid column** balances the pressure at the connection point.

### Governing Equation

For a piezometer measuring pressure at point A inside a pipe carrying a fluid of specific weight $w$:

$$P_A = w \cdot h = \rho g h$$

Where:
- $P_A$ = Gauge pressure at point A (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $h$ = Height of the fluid column above point A (in **m**)
- $\rho$ = Density of the fluid in the system (in **kg/m³**)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

### Why is the fluid column height in metres of fluid (not metres of mercury)?

The height is naturally expressed in **metres of the same fluid** because the equation involves the specific weight of that fluid. For example:
- Water in a piezometer for a water pipe: height in **metres of water (m H₂O)**
- For high pressures, the column would be impractically tall (e.g., 1 bar ≈ 10.2 m of water)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often forget that the **piezometer tube itself contains the same fluid** as the system. Do NOT use mercury's density here. The pressure equals $\rho_{system\,fluid} \times g \times h$.

### Limitations of a Piezometer
1. ❌ **Cannot measure high pressures** — the required column height becomes impractically large
2. ❌ **Cannot measure gas pressure** — gases do not form a stable, readable column in an open tube
3. ❌ **Cannot measure vacuum or negative gauge pressures**
4. ❌ **Cannot be used where the system fluid is highly viscous, corrosive, or opaque**

## 2.2 The U-Tube Manometer (Most Common Liquid Column Device)

### Simple U-Tube Manometer
A **U-tube** is bent into a "U" shape and partially filled with a **manometric fluid** (mercury for water/air systems, or a heavier/denser fluid). The two ends are connected to:
- One limb → the point of unknown pressure $P_A$
- Other limb → open atmosphere (or another known pressure)

### Pressure Equation Derivation (Water-Mercury System)
Let:
- $P_A$ = Unknown pressure at point A (system fluid = water)
- $h$ = Difference in mercury levels between the two limbs (in **m**)
- $\rho_w$ = Density of water (**1000 kg/m³**)
- $\rho_{Hg}$ = Density of mercury (**13600 kg/m³**)

Starting from point A and moving up through the water, then down through mercury to the open end:

$$P_A = \rho_w g (h_1) + \rho_{Hg} g (h) - \rho_{Hg} g (0)$$

Where $h_1$ is the distance from A up to the mercury surface in the system limb.

For a more generalized case where the manometer fluid is heavier than the system fluid:

$$\boxed{P_A = \rho_{system} g h_{system} + \rho_{manometer} g h_{manometer}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The general rule is: **add** terms when moving **downward** in a fluid, and **subtract** terms when moving **upward**. This is the single most important rule for KTU manometer problems.

### Differential U-Tube Manometer
Used to measure the **pressure difference** between two points (A and B) in a system. Both limbs are connected to the system — neither is open to atmosphere.

For a typical differential U-tube with mercury, measuring $\Delta P = P_A - P_B$:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \left(\rho_{Hg} - \rho_{system}\right) g \cdot h}$$

Where $h$ is the difference in mercury levels in the two limbs.

### Inverted U-Tube Manometer
- The U-tube is **inverted** (open ends facing down into the system fluid)
- The manometric fluid is **lighter** than the system fluid (typically oil or air)
- Used to measure pressure differences in **water pipelines** where the differential is small
- The lighter fluid sits in the inverted section; the heavier system fluid fills the two legs from below

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \left(\rho_{system} - \rho_{manometer}\right) g \cdot h}$$

## 2.3 Single Column Manometer (Micromanometer)

### Purpose
A **single column manometer** is essentially a refinement of the U-tube manometer designed to:
- ✅ Provide **higher accuracy** for small pressure measurements
- ✅ **Reduce** the size and weight of the instrument
- ✅ **Eliminate** parallax and measurement errors

### Construction
One limb of the U-tube is replaced with a **large reservoir (well)** of much greater cross-sectional area, while the other limb remains a **narrow vertical tube** (a "single column"). A vernier scale is fitted for precision reading.

### Theory of the Reservoir Correction
Because the reservoir has a much larger area ($A_1$) than the tube ($A_2$), when fluid is pushed into the tube, the level in the reservoir **drops slightly** — this drop must be accounted for.

By **conservation of volume** (volume displaced in reservoir = volume gained in tube):

$$A_1 \cdot \Delta h_1 = A_2 \cdot \Delta h_2$$

Since the change in reservoir level is negligible ($\Delta h_1 \approx 0$) when $A_1 \gg A_2$:

$$\Delta h_1 = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \Delta h_2$$

The **effective height** of the manometer fluid is:

$$h_{effective} = h \left(1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$

The pressure equation becomes:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \rho_{manometer} \cdot g \cdot h \left(1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)}$$

Where:
- $h$ = Observed rise in the narrow column
- $A_1$ = Cross-sectional area of the reservoir (well)
- $A_2$ = Cross-sectional area of the narrow tube
- $\rho_{manometer}$ = Density of the manometric fluid

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When $A_1 \gg A_2$ (typical design ratio is **200:1** or higher), the correction factor $(1 + A_2/A_1) \approx 1$, and the equation simplifies to $P_A - P_B = \rho g h$. This is why single column manometers are considered highly accurate.

## 2.4 Mechanical Pressure Gauges (Elastic Deformation Type)

These devices convert pressure into a **measurable mechanical displacement** using the elastic deformation of a curved metallic element.

### 2.4.1 Bourdon Tube Pressure Gauge (Most Widely Used Industrial Gauge)

**Construction:**
- A **flattened metal tube** (typically phosphor bronze, beryllium copper, or stainless steel) bent into a **C-shape** or **spiral**
- One end is **fixed and sealed** to the pressure inlet
- The other end is **free** and connected to a mechanical linkage (gear-sector and pinion) that drives a pointer over a calibrated dial

**Working Principle:**
When pressure enters the tube, it tends to **straighten out** (the cross-section tries to become circular). This slight straightening motion is mechanically amplified through the linkage to rotate the pointer.

**Pressure Range:** From **0.6 bar to 7000 bar** (special designs go higher)

**Advantages:**
- ✅ Simple, robust, inexpensive
- ✅ Wide pressure range
- ✅ Direct local reading
- ✅ Good accuracy (typically ±0.5% to ±2% of full scale)

**Disadvantages:**
- ❌ Sensitive to vibration and temperature
- ❌ Hysteresis errors
- ❌ Cannot measure rapidly fluctuating pressures well

### 2.4.2 Diaphragm Pressure Gauge

**Construction:**
- A **flexible circular diaphragm** (metallic or non-metallic) with concentric corrugations
- The diaphragm is clamped between two flanges
- The pressure to be measured acts on one side; the other side is open to atmosphere (gauge type) or a reference pressure (differential type)

**Working Principle:**
The pressure causes the diaphragm to **deflect**. The deflection is transmitted via a mechanical linkage to a pointer on a calibrated dial.

**Pressure Range:** From **very low pressures (mbar)** to about **25 bar**

**Best Suited For:**
- ✅ Low pressure measurement
- ✅ Corrosive fluids (when fitted with chemical-resistant diaphragms)
- ✅ Slurries and viscous fluids
- ✅ Pressure measurement where hygiene is critical (food, pharma)

### 2.4.3 Bellows Pressure Gauge

**Construction:**
- A **metallic bellows** (like a small accordion) — a series of concentric corrugations formed from a thin metal tube
- One end is sealed; pressure acts on the other
- The free end is linked to a mechanical pointer assembly

**Working Principle:**
Pressure causes the bellows to **axially compress or extend**. This linear motion is converted to a rotary pointer movement.

**Pressure Range:** **Low to medium** pressures (typically up to 4 bar)

### 2.4.4 Dead Weight Pressure Gauge (Pressure Tester / Calibrator)

This is **NOT a measuring device for routine use** — it is the **primary standard** used to calibrate all other pressure gauges.

**Construction:**
- A precision-machined **piston-cylinder assembly**
- Known **calibrated weights** placed on the piston
- The fluid (usually oil) pressure is applied to the base of the piston

**Working Principle:**
The applied pressure supports the piston and the loaded weights in equilibrium. At equilibrium:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{m \cdot g}{A}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Pressure being generated (Pa)
- $W$ = Total weight on the piston (N)
- $m$ = Mass of the piston + weights (kg)
- $A$ = Effective cross-sectional area of the piston (m²)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²

**Why is it the standard?**
- Accuracy achievable: **±0.01% to ±0.05%** of the reading
- It is **traced to fundamental SI units** (mass, length, time) — no calibration drift
- Used by national metrology institutes (NPL, NIST) as the ultimate reference

## 2.5 📋 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| **Instrument** | **Pressure Equation** | **When to Use** |
|----------------|----------------------|-----------------|
| **Piezometer** | $P_A = \rho_{sys} \cdot g \cdot h$ | Same fluid, moderate pressure |
| **Simple U-Tube** | $P_A = \rho_{sys} g h_{sys} + \rho_{man} g h_{man}$ | Different manometric fluid |
| **Differential U-Tube** | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_{man} - \rho_{sys}) g h$ | Pressure difference |
| **Inverted U-Tube** | $P_A - P_B = (\rho_{sys} - \rho_{man}) g h$ | Light manometric fluid |
| **Single Column** | $P_A - P_B = \rho_{man} g h (1 + A_2/A_1)$ | High-accuracy low ΔP |
| **Bourdon Gauge** | Calibration-based (empirical) | Industrial, general purpose |
| **Dead Weight Tester** | $P = W/A$ | Calibration standard |

### Important Physical Constants (For KTU Numerical Problems)

| **Quantity** | **Value** |
|--------------|-----------|
| Density of water | $\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Density of mercury | $\rho_{Hg} = 13600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ |
| Specific weight of water | $\gamma_w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$ |
| Specific weight of mercury | $\gamma_{Hg} = 133416 \text{ N/m}^3$ |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ |
| Standard atmospheric pressure | $P_{atm} = 101325 \text{ Pa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$ |
| 1 bar | $= 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ kPa}$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Piezometers** are still used in **soil mechanics** to measure pore water pressure in earth dams and slopes
> - **U-tube manometers** are standard in **HVAC** systems to measure duct pressure and filter ΔP
> - **Bourdon gauges** are on every **gas cylinder, compressor, and steam boiler**
> - **Dead weight testers** are in every **calibration laboratory** worldwide
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Piezometer Equation

### Setup
Consider a closed pipe containing a fluid of density $\rho$ under pressure $P_A$ at a point A. A vertical piezometer tube of small bore is connected at A and open to atmosphere at the top.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the system**
- The fluid rises in the piezometer to a height $h$ above point A
- The top of the column is exposed to atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$
- The bottom of the column (point A) is at pressure $P_A$

**Step 2: Apply the hydrostatic pressure equation**
For a vertical column of static fluid:

$$P_{top} - P_{bottom} = -\rho g h$$

The minus sign appears because pressure **decreases** as we move **upward** in a static fluid.

**Step 3: Substitute the known values**
- $P_{top} = P_{atm}$ (open to atmosphere)
- $P_{bottom} = P_A$ (point A in the pipe)
- $h$ = height of the column

$$P_{atm} - P_A = -\rho g h$$

**Step 4: Solve for $P_A$ in terms of absolute pressure**

$$P_{A,abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$

**Step 5: Express the gauge pressure**
Gauge pressure is measured relative to atmospheric pressure:

$$P_{A,gauge} = P_{A,abs} - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

$$\boxed{P_{A,gauge} = \rho g h}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Simple U-Tube Manometer Equation

### Setup
A pipe carrying water (density $\rho_w$) has a U-tube manometer connected at point A. The manometer uses mercury (density $\rho_{Hg}$) as the manometric fluid. The other end of the U-tube is **open to atmosphere**.

**Given:**
- $P_A$ = Unknown pressure at point A (gauge)
- $h_1$ = Vertical distance from point A to the mercury level in the left limb
- $h$ = Difference in mercury levels between the two limbs (right limb is higher)
- The right limb is open to atmosphere

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the path and reference point**
We move from point A (where pressure is $P_A$) along the fluid to the open end. We will use the principle of **pressure addition** (downward) and **pressure subtraction** (upward) at each fluid interface.

**Step 2: Start at point A and move up through water**

$$P_{\text{left-mercury-surface}} = P_A - \rho_w g h_1$$

(Moving upward by $h_1$ in water, so we subtract the hydrostatic head.)

**Step 3: At the left mercury surface, pressure is continuous into mercury**
The pressure at the top of mercury in the left limb is the same on both sides of the mercury-water interface:

$$P_{\text{left-mercury-surface}} = P_A - \rho_w g h_1$$

**Step 4: Move down through mercury in the left limb, then up through mercury in the right limb**

The pressure at the bottom of the U-bend (or any common level) must be the same when calculated from either limb.

Let us equate the pressures at the **lower mercury level** (the right limb's mercury surface) from both sides:

**From the left limb (moving down by $h$ through mercury):**

$$P_{\text{right-mercury-surface}} = P_A - \rho_w g h_1 + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

**From the right limb (this is the open end, so pressure = $P_{atm}$):**

$$P_{\text{right-mercury-surface}} = P_{atm}$$

**Step 5: Equate the two expressions**

$$P_A - \rho_w g h_1 + \rho_{Hg} g h = P_{atm}$$

**Step 6: Solve for $P_A$**

$$P_A = P_{atm} + \rho_{Hg} g h - \rho_w g h_1$$

**Step 7: Express as gauge pressure**
Gauge pressure is absolute minus atmospheric:

$$P_{A,gauge} = P_A - P_{atm} = \rho_{Hg} g h - \rho_w g h_1$$

$$\boxed{P_{A,gauge} = \rho_{Hg} g h - \rho_w g h_1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> If point A is at the **same level** as the left mercury surface (i.e., $h_1 = 0$), the equation simplifies to:
> $$P_{A,gauge} = \rho_{Hg} g h$$
> This is the most common form in KTU textbook problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Differential U-Tube Manometer Equation

### Setup
Two pipes A and B carry water and are connected by a U-tube manometer using mercury. We need to find the pressure difference $P_A - P_B$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Consider the lower mercury level as the reference (call it point M)**
The pressure at point M must be the same whether calculated from limb A or limb B.

**Step 2: From limb A**

$$P_M = P_A + \rho_w g \cdot h_{A,down}$$

Where $h_{A,down}$ is the vertical distance from A down to M through water, then through mercury on the A-side.

If A is at the same level as the **higher** mercury surface (say, on the B-side), then:

$$P_M = P_A + \rho_w g h_1 + \rho_{Hg} g h$$

(We go down by $h_1$ through water from A, then down by $h$ through mercury to M)

**Step 3: From limb B**

$$P_M = P_B + \rho_w g h_2$$

(We go down by $h_2$ through water from B directly to M, if B is at the same level as M)

**Step 4: Equate and solve for $P_A - P_B$**

$$P_A + \rho_w g h_1 + \rho_{Hg} g h = P_B + \rho_w g h_2$$

$$P_A - P_B = \rho_w g (h_2 - h_1) - \rho_{Hg} g h$$

**Step 5: If A and B are at the same level ($h_1 = h_2$)**

$$P_A - P_B = -\rho_{Hg} g h$$

The negative sign appears because, with the mercury on the A-side pushed down, B has higher pressure.

**Step 6: General case where A and B are at the same horizontal level**

For the typical KTU exam setup where the two pipe centerlines are at the same elevation, with the manometer fluid being heavier than the system fluid:

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = (\rho_{man} - \rho_{sys}) \cdot g \cdot h}$$

This is the **most commonly tested form** in KTU examinations.

> [!TIP]
> **Sign Convention Mastery:**
> - If the manometer fluid on side A is **lower** than on side B, then $P_A > P_B$
> - If the manometer fluid on side A is **higher** than on side B, then $P_A < P_B$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Single Column Manometer with Reservoir Correction

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Initial condition (no pressure applied)**
- Mercury (or any manometric fluid) sits at the same level in both the reservoir and the tube
- Let reservoir area = $A_1$, tube area = $A_2$
- Initial levels: $y_0$ in reservoir, $y_0$ in tube

**Step 2: Apply pressure difference $\Delta P = P_A - P_B$**
- Fluid is pushed from the reservoir into the tube
- Reservoir level **drops** by $\Delta y_1$
- Tube level **rises** by $\Delta y_2$

**Step 3: Volume conservation (incompressibility)**
The volume of fluid leaving the reservoir equals the volume entering the tube:

$$A_1 \cdot \Delta y_1 = A_2 \cdot \Delta y_2$$

**Step 4: Express the observed column height**
The observer reads the rise in the tube, $h = \Delta y_2$. So:

$$\Delta y_2 = h$$

$$\Delta y_1 = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot h$$

**Step 5: Calculate the true (effective) manometer height**
The true height difference between the two fluid surfaces is:

$$h_{eff} = \Delta y_1 + \Delta y_2 = \left(\frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) h + h = h \left(1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$

**Step 6: Apply the basic manometer pressure equation**

$$\Delta P = \rho \cdot g \cdot h_{eff} = \rho g h \left(1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{P_A - P_B = \rho_{man} g h \left(1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight:** For $A_1/A_2 = 200$ (a typical industrial design), the correction factor is $1 + 1/200 = 1.005$, meaning a mere 0.5% correction. This is why the single column manometer is preferred for precision work.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Dead Weight Pressure Tester Equation

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Equilibrium of the piston**
The piston rests on a thin film of oil. For it to be in equilibrium, the upward force from the oil pressure must balance the downward force from gravity (piston weight + applied standard weights).

**Step 2: Force balance equation**

$$P \cdot A = W_{piston} + W_{weights}$$

**Step 3: Express weights in terms of mass**

$$W_{piston} = m_{piston} \cdot g$$

$$W_{weights} = m_{weights} \cdot g$$

**Step 4: Combine and solve for pressure**

$$P \cdot A = (m_{piston} + m_{weights}) \cdot g$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{(m_{piston} + m_{weights}) \cdot g}{A}}$$

**Step 5: Practical example calculation**
Suppose $m_{piston} = 0.5$ kg, $m_{weights} = 5$ kg, $A = 1 \times 10^{-4}$ m² (1 cm²), $g = 9.81$ m/s²:

$$P = \frac{(0.5 + 5)(9.81)}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{5.5 \times 9.81}{10^{-4}} = 5.4 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 5.4 \text{ bar}$$

---

## 3.6 📊 KTU Analytical Matrix: "Which Device When?"

| **Scenario** | **Recommended Device** | **Reason** |
|--------------|------------------------|------------|
| Measuring pressure in a domestic water tap | Bourdon Gauge | Cheap, robust, direct reading |
| Calibrating a pressure gauge in a lab | Dead Weight Tester | Highest accuracy, primary standard |
| Measuring low ΔP across an orifice plate | Differential U-tube or Single column | High sensitivity, liquid column suited for low ΔP |
| Measuring blood pressure | Sphygmomanometer (diaphragm-based) | Safe, low pressure, portable |
| Measuring pore water pressure in a dam | Piezometer | Static, low pressure, water is the same fluid |
| Measuring pressure of a corrosive acid | Diaphragm Gauge with PTFE lining | Isolates corrosive fluid from instrument body |
| Measuring gas pressure in a closed tank | Bourdon Gauge | Wide range, robust for gases |
| Field measurement of a small pressure difference | Single column manometer | High accuracy with portable form factor |
| Continuous industrial monitoring with data logging | Strain gauge or Capacitive transducer | Electrical output for automation |

---

## 3.7 Comprehensive Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:**
A pipe carrying oil of specific gravity 0.85 is connected to a U-tube manometer. The manometer contains mercury (specific gravity 13.6). The mercury level in the limb connected to the pipe is 0.4 m below the pipe centerline, and the mercury level in the open limb is 0.6 m below the pipe centerline. The oil-mercury interface in the pipe-connected limb is 0.3 m above the lower mercury level. Find the gauge pressure at the pipe centerline.

### Solution

**Step 1: List given data**
- Specific gravity of oil: $S_{oil} = 0.85 \implies \rho_{oil} = 850$ kg/m³
- Specific gravity of mercury: $S_{Hg} = 13.6 \implies \rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³
- Distance from pipe centerline to oil-mercury interface (in left limb): $h_1 = 0.3$ m
- Distance from oil-mercury interface (left limb) to top mercury level (left limb): let's call this $h_2 = 0.4 - 0.3 = 0.1$ m below the interface? Let us re-read carefully.

**Re-interpretation of the problem statement:**
- Pipe centerline is at the top reference (0 m).
- Mercury level in the **left limb** (connected to pipe) is **0.4 m below** the pipe centerline.
- Mercury level in the **right limb** (open to atmosphere) is **0.6 m below** the pipe centerline.
- So the **right limb's mercury is 0.2 m lower** than the left limb's mercury.
- The oil-mercury interface in the left limb is 0.3 m above the **lower mercury level** — but since the right limb's mercury is lower, the oil-mercury interface in the left limb is at: $0.6 + 0.3 = 0.9$ m below the pipe centerline.
- The distance from the pipe centerline to the left mercury surface (oil-mercury interface) = 0.9 m
- The mercury height difference between the two limbs = 0.6 - 0.4 = 0.2 m

**Step 2: Walk from pipe centerline (A) to open end**

Path: From A → down through oil by 0.9 m → reach oil-mercury interface in left limb → down through mercury by 0.2 m → reach the right limb's mercury surface (which is the lower one) → then up to the open end (atmosphere).

Wait — to use the equal-pressure-at-same-level method, we equate pressure at the **lower mercury level** (right limb's mercury surface).

**Step 3: Pressure at the right mercury surface (from right limb)**
The right limb is open to atmosphere:

$$P_{\text{right-mercury-surface}} = P_{atm}$$

**Step 4: Pressure at the right mercury surface (from left limb)**

$$P_{\text{right-mercury-surface}} = P_A + \rho_{oil} g (0.9) + \rho_{Hg} g (0.2)$$

(We go down through oil by 0.9 m, then down through mercury by 0.2 m)

**Step 5: Equate and solve**

$$P_A + \rho_{oil} g (0.9) + \rho_{Hg} g (0.2) = P_{atm}$$

$$P_A = P_{atm} - \rho_{oil} g (0.9) - \rho_{Hg} g (0.2)$$

**Step 6: Compute gauge pressure**

$$P_{A,gauge} = P_A - P_{atm} = - \rho_{oil} g (0.9) - \rho_{Hg} g (0.2)$$

$$P_{A,gauge} = -(850)(9.81)(0.9) - (13600)(9.81)(0.2)$$

$$P_{A,gauge} = -7502.9 - 26683.2 = -34186.1 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P_{A,gauge} \approx -34.19 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **negative gauge pressure** indicates a **vacuum condition** at the pipe centerline. The pressure is 34.19 kPa **below** atmospheric.

---

## 3.8 Numerical Example: Single Column Manometer

**Problem:**
A single column manometer has a reservoir of area $A_1 = 80$ cm² and a tube of area $A_2 = 0.5$ cm². The manometric fluid is mercury. When connected to a gas line, the mercury in the tube rises by 40 cm. Calculate the gas pressure (gauge) in the line.

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the data**
- $A_1 = 80$ cm²
- $A_2 = 0.5$ cm²
- $h = 40$ cm = 0.40 m
- $\rho_{Hg} = 13600$ kg/m³
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2: Compute the correction factor**

$$1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 1 + \frac{0.5}{80} = 1 + 0.00625 = 1.00625$$

**Step 3: Apply the single column manometer equation**

$$P = \rho_{Hg} \cdot g \cdot h \cdot \left(1 + \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$$

$$P = (13600)(9.81)(0.40)(1.00625)$$

$$P = 13600 \times 9.81 \times 0.40 \times 1.00625$$

$$P = 53366.4 \times 1.00625 = 53700 \text{ Pa (approximately)}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 53.7 \text{ kPa (gauge)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Without the reservoir correction, $P = 53366.4$ Pa. With the correction, $P = 53700$ Pa. The correction accounts for the **0.63% error** that would otherwise creep in.
