> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches: Types of Fluid Flow (1-D, 2-D, 3-D), Steady, Unsteady, Uniform, Non-uniform, Laminar, Turbulent, Rotational, Irrotational Flows, Translation, Rotation, Circulation and Vorticity, Vortex Motion (Free Vortex and Forced Vortex)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Eulerian and Lagrangian Approaches to Fluid Flow

### Lagrangian Approach
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Lagrangian Approach):** A method of describing fluid motion by tracking the **history of individual fluid particles** as they move through space and time. The observer follows a specific particle, recording its velocity, acceleration, pressure, and density at every instant.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are a **wildlife biologist tracking a single tagged elephant** in a forest. You record its position, speed, and direction at every second of the day. You are *with* the elephant, gathering its personal history. This is the **Lagrangian approach** — you "ride" a fluid particle and report what *it* experiences.

**Mathematical Form:**
A fluid particle is identified by its initial (reference) position at time $t = t_0$:

$$
\vec{r} = \vec{r}(a, b, c, t)
$$

Where $a$, $b$, $c$ are the **Lagrangian coordinates** (initial coordinates of the particle), and $t$ is time.

The **Lagrangian velocity** is the total time derivative of position:

$$
\vec{V}_L = \frac{\partial \vec{r}}{\partial t}
$$

---

### Eulerian Approach
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Eulerian Approach):** A method of describing fluid motion by specifying the **field variables (velocity, pressure, density)** as functions of space and time at **fixed points** in the flow domain. The observer remains stationary and records what passes by.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Now imagine a **traffic camera mounted on a traffic signal pole**. The camera does not move. It observes the speed, type, and number of vehicles passing *through* its field of view. It does not follow any particular car. This is the **Eulerian approach** — you sit at a fixed point in space and describe what flows past you.

**Mathematical Form:**
Field variables are expressed as functions of spatial coordinates $(x, y, z)$ and time $t$:

$$
\vec{V}_E = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t)
$$

For a general scalar field $\phi$:

$$
\phi = \phi(x, y, z, t)
$$

---

### Comparison Table

| Feature | Lagrangian | Eulerian |
|---|---|---|
| **Observer** | Moves with the fluid particle | Fixed in space |
| **Coordinates** | Material/Lagrangian $(a,b,c)$ | Spatial/Eulerian $(x,y,z)$ |
| **Variables** | $f(a,b,c,t)$ | $F(x,y,z,t)$ |
| **Use Case** | Tracking droplets, pollution plumes, blood cells | Pipelines, rivers, wind tunnels (most engineering) |
| **Difficulty** | Mathematically intensive | Practically preferred |
| **Famous Use** | Molecular dynamics | CFD, Navier-Stokes equations |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The Eulerian approach is the **foundation of modern fluid mechanics**. The famous continuity equation, Euler's equation, Bernoulli's equation, and Navier-Stokes equations are all derived using the **Eulerian framework**.

---

## 1.2 Material Derivative (Substantial Derivative)

The bridge between the two approaches is the **Material Derivative** $\dfrac{D}{Dt}$. It tells us the rate of change of a property as experienced by a moving fluid particle (Lagrangian view), expressed in Eulerian variables.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Material Derivative):** The time rate of change of any fluid property as seen by an observer moving with the fluid. It relates the local and convective changes to the total (substantial) change.

For a scalar $\phi(x,y,z,t)$:

$$
\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local Rate of Change}} + \underbrace{u\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}}_{\text{Convective Rate of Change}}
$$

For the **velocity vector** $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$:

$$
\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}
$$

In expanded form:

$$
a_x = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}
$$

$$
a_y = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}
$$

$$
a_z = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Meaning:** The local derivative $\partial/\partial t$ captures *unsteadiness* at a fixed point. The convective term $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ captures the change due to the fluid's motion from one point to another.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Fluid Flow

### A. Dimensional Classification (Based on Variation of Flow Parameters in Space)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (One-Dimensional Flow):** Flow in which the flow parameters (velocity, pressure, density) vary in **only one spatial direction** and are functions of that single coordinate and time.

**Analogy:** Flow through a long straight pipe of constant cross-section where velocity is assumed uniform across the section. Engineers often use 1-D flow as a simplification to compute average velocity $V = Q/A$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Two-Dimensional Flow):** Flow in which flow parameters vary in **two spatial directions** and are uniform in the third (often the depth). Flow patterns are identical in parallel planes.

**Analogy:** Flow between two infinite parallel plates — the velocity profile $u(z)$ is the same at every cross-section along the flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Three-Dimensional Flow):** Flow in which flow parameters vary in all three spatial directions. This is the most general and physically accurate description.

**Analogy:** Flow around a bridge pier in a river — velocity varies in streamwise, vertical, and lateral directions.

---

### B. Temporal Classification (Steady vs. Unsteady)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Steady Flow):** A flow in which the fluid properties at **every point in the flow field do not change with time**.

Mathematically: $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = 0$ at every point. Therefore, $\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \phi$.

**Analogy:** Water flowing at constant rate from a fully opened tap into a sink with a regular outlet. The depth in the basin stays constant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Unsteady Flow):** A flow in which fluid properties **change with time at fixed points** in space.

Mathematically: $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \neq 0$.

**Analogy:** Water draining from a bathtub — the depth in the tub decreases with time.

---

### C. Spatial Uniformity (Uniform vs. Non-Uniform)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Uniform Flow):** A flow in which the **velocity (in magnitude and direction) remains constant at every instant along the streamline** (i.e., along the flow direction).

Mathematically: $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$ along a streamline, where $s$ is the distance along the streamline.

**Analogy:** Flow in a long straight pipe of constant diameter at constant discharge.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Non-Uniform Flow):** A flow in which velocity **changes along the direction of flow**.

**Analogy:** Flow in a pipe with a converging section (nozzle) — velocity increases as the cross-section decreases.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** *Steady vs Unsteady* depends on **time**, while *Uniform vs Non-uniform* depends on **space**. A flow can be steady-uniform, steady non-uniform, unsteady uniform (rare), or unsteady non-uniform.

---

### D. Laminar vs. Turbulent Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Laminar Flow):** A flow in which fluid particles move in **smooth, parallel layers (laminae) without mixing** between adjacent layers. The flow is highly ordered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Turbulent Flow):** A flow characterized by **chaotic, irregular fluctuations** of fluid particles, with significant mixing and eddies.

**Criterion — Reynolds Number ($Re$):**
$$
Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}
$$

Where $\rho$ is density, $V$ is velocity, $D$ is characteristic length (pipe diameter), $\mu$ is dynamic viscosity, and $\nu$ is kinematic viscosity.

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number (Pipe) |
|---|---|
| **Laminar** | $Re < 2000$ |
| **Transitional** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ |
| **Turbulent** | $Re > 4000$ |

**Analogy:** Laminar flow is like a **calm parade where everyone walks in neat rows**. Turbulent flow is like a **crowded marketplace where people bump and swirl**.

---

### E. Rotational vs. Irrotational Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Irrotational Flow):** A flow in which **no fluid element rotates about its own axis** as it moves. There is **no angular velocity** of a fluid particle about its center of mass.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Rotational Flow):** A flow in which fluid elements **rotate about their own mass centers** as they translate, even though the bulk flow may appear smooth.

**Mathematical Criterion:**
A flow is irrotational if and only if the **vorticity vector** $\vec{\omega}$ is identically zero:

$$
\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0
$$

$$
\vec{\omega} = \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}\right)\hat{i} + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)\hat{j} + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\right)\hat{k} = 0
$$

This implies the partial derivatives are individually zero:

$$
\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial z}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}
$$

**Analogy:** Imagine a tiny paddle wheel immersed in the flow. In **irrotational flow** the paddle wheel does **not spin** as it moves with the fluid. In **rotational flow** the paddle wheel **spins** because of shear within the fluid.

---

## 1.4 Translation, Rotation, Circulation, and Vorticity

### Translation
Pure linear displacement of a fluid element from one point to another, **without** rotation of the element about its own centroid. The element's orientation is preserved.

### Rotation
The **angular motion** of a fluid element about an axis passing through its **own center of mass**, in addition to its translation. Quantified by the angular velocity vector.

### Circulation ($\Gamma$)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Circulation):** The **line integral of the velocity vector** along a closed curve in the flow field. It is a macroscopic measure of the "swirl" of a region of fluid.

Mathematically:

$$
\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}
$$

Where $C$ is a closed curve and $d\vec{s}$ is the differential element of the curve.

**Units:** $m^2/s$.

### Vorticity ($\vec{\omega}$)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Vorticity):** The **local, pointwise measure of rotation** of a fluid element. It is the **curl of the velocity vector field**.

$$
\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}
$$

**Units:** $rad/s$ or $s^{-1}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Important Distinction:** Circulation is a **global (integral)** quantity over a closed curve, whereas vorticity is a **local (pointwise)** quantity. They are linked via **Stokes' Theorem**.

---

## 1.5 Vortex Motion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Vortex):** A flow region where fluid particles rotate around a common axis (the vortex axis). The motion is concentrated near the axis and diminishes (or grows) with radial distance.

### Free Vortex (Irrotational Vortex)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Free Vortex):** A vortex motion in which the **vorticity is zero everywhere** (except possibly at the singular axis). The tangential velocity is **inversely proportional** to the radial distance from the axis.

Mathematically:

$$
v_\theta = \frac{K}{r} = \frac{C}{r} \quad \text{(Free Vortex Equation)}
$$

Where $K$ (or $C$) is a constant depending on the strength of the vortex, with units of $m^2/s$.

**Examples:** Whirlpool in a bathtub, water draining from a sink, flow around a sharp bend in a river.

### Forced Vortex (Rotational Vortex)
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Forced Vortex):** A vortex motion in which fluid particles rotate as a **rigid body** with constant angular velocity $\omega$. The tangential velocity is **directly proportional** to the radial distance.

Mathematically:

$$
v_\theta = \omega r \quad \text{(Forced Vortex Equation)}
$$

**Examples:** Liquid in a rotating tank, water inside a centrifugal pump impeller, rotating tea in a stirred cup.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction:** In a *free* vortex, the vorticity is **zero** (irrotational motion with curved streamlines). In a *forced* vortex, the vorticity is **non-zero and uniform** (rotational rigid-body motion). This is a classic KTU counter-intuitive question.

---

## 1.6 Pathline, Streakline, and Streamline

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Pathline):** The **trajectory** of a single fluid particle over a period of time (Lagrangian concept).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Streakline):** The **locus of all particles that have previously passed through a given point** (e.g., the dye line in a flow visualization experiment).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Streamline):** A curve that is **everywhere tangent to the instantaneous velocity vector**. No fluid crosses a streamline (in steady flow, streamlines = pathlines = streaklines).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In *steady flow*, pathlines, streaklines, and streamlines **coincide**. In *unsteady flow*, they are distinct.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Analysis of the Eulerian vs Lagrangian Approach

### Why Does the Eulerian Approach Dominate Engineering?

In real engineering problems (e.g., flow over an aircraft wing, flow in a penstock, flow in a spillway), the fluid is composed of an **astronomical number of particles** (on the order of Avogadro's number). Tracking each particle individually is mathematically impossible. Therefore, the **field description** (Eulerian) — where we record properties at fixed spatial points — is far more practical.

**However**, in many physical situations, we are interested in *how much* a property changes as seen by a moving particle. The material derivative is the mathematical bridge that allows us to convert Eulerian field information into Lagrangian particle information.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Material Derivative

1. **Local Change ($\partial \phi / \partial t$):** Imagine a stationary weather vane. It records how wind speed changes *with time* at that fixed location. This is the local derivative.

2. **Convective Change ($\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \phi$):** As the fluid flows past the weather vane, it carries fluid with different $\phi$ values. The change due to the fluid *moving into* regions of different $\phi$ is the convective term.

3. **Total Change ($D\phi / Dt$):** The sum of both effects is what a *moving fluid particle* actually experiences. This is the **material derivative**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Utility:** The material derivative is the cornerstone of deriving the **continuity equation** (mass conservation), **Navier-Stokes equations** (momentum conservation), and **energy equation** in fluid mechanics.

---

## 2.2 Theoretical Analysis of Vortex Motion

### Detailed Study of Free Vortex

For a free vortex, $v_\theta = K/r$. The flow is **irrotational** everywhere except at the axis ($r = 0$). Let us verify this:

In polar coordinates, for purely circumferential motion $(v_r = 0, v_\theta = K/r)$:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}
$$

Substituting $v_\theta = K/r$ and $v_r = 0$:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (K)}{\partial r} - 0 = 0
$$

**Physical Implications of Free Vortex:**
- Pressure variation in the radial direction (Bernoulli in radial form):

$$
\frac{dp}{\rho} = \frac{v_\theta^2}{r} dr = \frac{K^2}{r^3} dr
$$

- Integration gives: $p = p_0 - \dfrac{\rho K^2}{2 r^2}$ (pressure decreases as $r$ decreases; this is why a free vortex has a **pressure dip at the core**, sometimes called the "bathtub vortex").

- Total head: $H = p/\gamma + v_\theta^2/(2g) = $ constant (along any streamline).

- **Vorticity is zero**, but **circulation is non-zero**. The circulation around any closed curve enclosing the axis is:

$$
\Gamma = \oint \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} = 2 \pi r \cdot v_\theta = 2 \pi r \cdot \frac{K}{r} = 2\pi K
$$

- The constant $K$ is called the **strength of the vortex**: $K = \Gamma / (2\pi)$.

### Detailed Study of Forced Vortex

For a forced vortex, $v_\theta = \omega r$, where $\omega$ is the constant angular velocity. The fluid rotates as a **rigid body**.

- The vorticity is **uniform and non-zero**:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (\omega r^2)}{\partial r} = 2\omega
$$

- The pressure distribution in the radial direction (in a rotating tank):

$$
\frac{dp}{dr} = \rho \omega^2 r
$$

Integrating:

$$
p = p_0 + \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2}
$$

- **Total head is NOT constant** along radial direction in a forced vortex. Pressure increases with $r^2$ due to centrifugal action. The free surface in a rotating tank is a **paraboloid of revolution**:

$$
z = \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}
$$

### Comparison: Free Vortex vs Forced Vortex

| Property | Free Vortex | Forced Vortex |
|---|---|---|
| **Velocity Law** | $v_\theta r = $ const $\;$ ($K$) | $v_\theta / r = $ const $\;$ ($\omega$) |
| **Vorticity** | $\omega = 0$ (irrotational) | $\omega = 2\omega_0$ (rotational) |
| **Circulation** | $\Gamma = 2\pi K = $ const | $\Gamma = 2\pi \omega r^2$ (variable) |
| **Streamlines** | Concentric circles | Concentric circles |
| **Pressure Variation** | $p = p_0 - \rho K^2/(2r^2)$ | $p = p_0 + \rho \omega^2 r^2 / 2$ |
| **Total Head** | Constant along streamlines | Not constant |
| **Example** | Whirlpool, drain vortex | Rotating tank, centrifuge |

---

## 2.3 Stream Function and Velocity Potential (Brief Overview)

For 2-D incompressible flow, two scalar functions simplify analysis:
- **Stream function $\psi(x,y)$:** $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$. Automatically satisfies continuity.
- **Velocity potential $\phi(x,y)$:** For irrotational flow, $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$, $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$. A flow that has both is a **potential flow** (continuity + irrotationality $\Rightarrow$ Laplace equation $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Lagrangian Position** | $\vec{r} = \vec{r}(a,b,c,t)$ | Particle location at time $t$ |
| 2 | **Eulerian Velocity Field** | $\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ | Velocity at fixed point |
| 3 | **Material Derivative (scalar)** | $\dfrac{D\phi}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}$ | Total change following particle |
| 4 | **Material Derivative (vector)** | $\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Acceleration of fluid particle |
| 5 | **Reynolds Number** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | Inertia / viscous ratio |
| 6 | **Laminar Criterion** | $Re < 2000$ (pipe) | Smooth layered flow |
| 7 | **Turbulent Criterion** | $Re > 4000$ (pipe) | Chaotic, mixed flow |
| 8 | **Vorticity (Cartesian)** | $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ | Local rotation measure |
| 9 | **Vorticity $x$-component** | $\omega_x = \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} - \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z}$ | Rotation about $x$-axis |
| 10 | **Vorticity $y$-component** | $\omega_y = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z} - \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x}$ | Rotation about $y$-axis |
| 11 | **Vorticity $z$-component** | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | Rotation about $z$-axis |
| 12 | **Irrotationality Condition** | $\vec{\omega} = 0$ (everywhere) | $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ |
| 13 | **Circulation** | $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}$ | Line integral over closed loop |
| 14 | **Stokes' Theorem** | $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot \hat{n} \, dS = \int_S \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{S}$ | Links circulation & vorticity |
| 15 | **Free Vortex Velocity** | $v_\theta = \dfrac{K}{r}$ | Tangential velocity |
| 16 | **Free Vortex Pressure** | $p = p_0 - \dfrac{\rho K^2}{2 r^2}$ | Pressure rises with $r$ |
| 17 | **Forced Vortex Velocity** | $v_\theta = \omega r$ | Tangential velocity |
| 18 | **Forced Vortex Pressure** | $p = p_0 + \dfrac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2}$ | Pressure rises with $r^2$ |
| 19 | **Forced Vortex Surface Profile** | $z = \dfrac{\omega^2 r^2}{2 g}$ | Paraboloid of revolution |
| 20 | **Stream Function (2-D)** | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Continuity satisfied |
| 21 | **Velocity Potential (2-D, irrotational)** | $u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \quad v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$ | Irrotationality satisfied |
| 22 | **Laplace Equation** | $\nabla^2 \phi = 0$ | Combined continuity + irrotationality |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The free vortex governs the design of **tangential flow inlets, vortex chambers, and spillway buckets**. The forced vortex governs **centrifugal pumps, rotating machinery, and settling tanks**.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Examples

| Application | Flow Type |
|---|---|
| Steady flow in a long pipe at constant discharge | Steady, Uniform, 1-D |
| Flow under a sluice gate | Steady, Non-uniform, 2-D |
| Surge in a pipeline after valve closure | Unsteady, Non-uniform |
| Honey flowing slowly | Laminar, Viscous |
| Flow past an aircraft wing at high $Re$ | Turbulent, 3-D, Rotational |
| Ideal flow around a cylinder | Irrotational, 2-D, Potential flow |
| Whirlpool in a dam reservoir | Free vortex |
| Liquid in a rotating tank (sludge digester) | Forced vortex |
| Centrifugal pump impeller | Forced vortex region + Free vortex region |
| Tornado / Cyclone | Combination of free and forced vortex |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Material Derivative (Acceleration in Eulerian Framework)

**Statement to prove:** The acceleration of a fluid particle in an Eulerian velocity field $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t)$ is given by:

$$
\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}
$$

**Step 1 — Lagrangian Position Definition.**
A fluid particle's position vector is a function of its initial coordinates and time:

$$
\vec{r} = \vec{r}(a, b, c, t) = x(a,b,c,t)\hat{i} + y(a,b,c,t)\hat{j} + z(a,b,c,t)\hat{k}
$$

**Step 2 — Lagrangian Velocity.**
The velocity of this specific particle is the time derivative of its position (keeping $a, b, c$ fixed):

$$
\vec{V}_L = \frac{\partial \vec{r}}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial x}{\partial t}\hat{i} + \frac{\partial y}{\partial t}\hat{j} + \frac{\partial z}{\partial t}\hat{k} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Eulerian Field View.**
In the Eulerian framework, the same velocity components are field variables:

$$
u = u(x, y, z, t), \quad v = v(x, y, z, t), \quad w = w(x, y, z, t)
$$

**Step 4 — Total Differential of Velocity.**
To find the *total* acceleration of the particle, we take the time derivative of $\vec{V}$. By the **chain rule of multivariable calculus**, since $x, y, z$ all change with time as the particle moves:

$$
\frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the Particle's Velocity.**
Since $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$ (the particle moves with the local fluid velocity):

$$
\frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 6 — Component Form for All Three Axes.**
Similarly:

$$
\frac{Dv}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}
$$

$$
\frac{Dw}{Dt} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

**Step 7 — Compact Vector Form.**
Combining the three scalar equations, the acceleration vector is:

$$
\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \underbrace{\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}}_{\text{Local}} + \underbrace{(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}_{\text{Convective}}
$$

**Result verified.**

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Vorticity in Cylindrical Coordinates

**Statement to prove:** For purely circumferential flow $v_r = 0$ and $v_\theta = v_\theta(r, \theta, t)$, the vorticity (only the $z$-component) is:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}
$$

**Step 1 — Vorticity Definition.**
By definition:

$$
\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}
$$

**Step 2 — Curl in Cylindrical Coordinates.**
The curl operator in cylindrical $(r, \theta, z)$ coordinates gives:

$$
\nabla \times \vec{V} = \left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_z}{\partial \theta} - \frac{\partial v_\theta}{\partial z}\right]\hat{e}_r + \left[\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial z} - \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial r}\right]\hat{e}_\theta + \left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial v_r}{\partial \theta}\right]\hat{e}_z
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Conditions $v_r = 0$ and $v_z = 0$ (no radial or axial flow).**
Substituting $v_r = 0$ and $v_z = 0$:

$$
\vec{\omega} = 0\hat{e}_r + 0\hat{e}_\theta + \left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} - 0\right]\hat{e}_z
$$

**Step 4 — Final Expression.**

$$
\boxed{\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r}}
$$

**Result verified.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Verification of Irrotationality of a Free Vortex

**Given:** Free vortex: $v_\theta = K/r$, $v_r = 0$, $v_z = 0$.

**To prove:** $\omega_z = 0$ everywhere (except at the singular axis $r = 0$).

**Step 1 — Use the result of §3.2.**

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $v_\theta = K/r$.**

$$
r v_\theta = r \cdot \frac{K}{r} = K
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to $r$.**

$$
\frac{\partial (r v_\theta)}{\partial r} = \frac{\partial (K)}{\partial r} = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Therefore:**

$$
\omega_z = \frac{1}{r}(0) = 0
$$

**Conclusion:** A free vortex is **irrotational** at all points except $r = 0$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Counter-intuitive KTU Fact:** Although fluid particles travel along **curved** (circular) paths in a free vortex, the fluid element itself **does not rotate** about its own centroid. Curved streamlines do not automatically mean rotational flow.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Pressure Distribution in a Free Vortex

**Given:** Free vortex $v_\theta = K/r$, steady, incompressible, inviscid.

**Step 1 — Apply Euler's Equation in Radial Direction.**

$$
\rho \frac{D v_r}{Dt} - \frac{\rho v_\theta^2}{r} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}
$$

For purely circumferential flow, $v_r = 0$, so the left side simplifies to:

$$
-\frac{\rho v_\theta^2}{r} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $v_\theta = K/r$.**

$$
-\frac{\rho (K/r)^2}{r} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{\rho K^2}{r^3}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate with respect to $r$.**

$$
p = \int \frac{\rho K^2}{r^3} dr = -\frac{\rho K^2}{2 r^2} + C
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Condition.**
At $r = \infty$ (or some reference $r_0$): $p = p_\infty$, so $C = p_\infty$:

$$
\boxed{p(r) = p_\infty - \frac{\rho K^2}{2 r^2}}
$$

**Pressure Interpretation:** As $r \to 0$ (toward the vortex core), pressure drops, creating a low-pressure "eye" of the vortex.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Pressure Distribution in a Forced Vortex

**Given:** Forced vortex $v_\theta = \omega r$, steady, incompressible.

**Step 1 — Apply Euler's Equation in Radial Direction (rotating reference frame equivalent).**
In the rotating frame, there is a centrifugal body force $\rho \omega^2 r$ outward. The radial equilibrium is:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \rho \omega^2 r
$$

(Equivalently, this is obtained by adding the centrifugal acceleration to the Euler equation.)

**Step 2 — Integrate.**

$$
p = \int \rho \omega^2 r \, dr = \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} + C
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Boundary Condition.**
At $r = 0$ (axis of rotation): $p = p_0$, so $C = p_0$:

$$
\boxed{p(r) = p_0 + \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Free Surface Profile.**
At the free surface, $p = p_{atm}$ everywhere. The elevation $z$ of the free surface satisfies:

$$
p_0 + \frac{\rho \omega^2 r^2}{2} + \rho g z = \text{const}
$$

Setting $z = 0$ at $r = 0$ and $z = z_s$ at radius $r$:

$$
z_s = \frac{\omega^2 r^2}{2g}
$$

**Result:** A **paraboloid of revolution** — the free surface of a rotating liquid is a paraboloid.

---

## 3.6 Derivation: Circulation Around a Free Vortex Core

**Given:** Free vortex $v_\theta = K/r$.

**Step 1 — Consider a closed circular path of radius $r$ centered on the axis.**

**Step 2 — Compute the circulation.**

$$
\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}
$$

Along the circle, $d\vec{s}$ is tangential and $v_r = 0$, so $\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} = v_\theta \, ds$:

$$
\Gamma = v_\theta \cdot (2\pi r) = \frac{K}{r} \cdot 2\pi r = 2\pi K
$$

**Step 3 — Result.**

$$
\boxed{\Gamma = 2\pi K \quad \text{(independent of } r\text{)}}
$$

**Interpretation:** The circulation around *any* closed loop enclosing the vortex axis is the same constant $2\pi K$. This is the **strength of the vortex**. By Stokes' theorem, this also equals the flux of vorticity through the enclosed area — but since vorticity is zero except at the axis, the entire circulation comes from a **delta-function singularity** at $r = 0$.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Reynolds Number Classification for Different Geometries

| Geometry | Characteristic Length | Laminar Range | Turbulent Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pipe flow** | Diameter $D$ | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| **Flow between parallel plates** | Gap $h$ | $Re < 1400$ | $Re > 1400$ (approx.) |
| **Boundary layer on flat plate** | Distance $x$ from leading edge | $Re_x < 5 \times 10^5$ | $Re_x > 5 \times 10^5$ |
| **Flow around sphere** | Diameter $D$ | $Re < 1$ | $Re > 10^4$ |
| **Open channel flow** | Hydraulic depth $D_h$ | $Re < 500$ | $Re > 2000$ |

---

## 3.8 Combined Continuity Equation in Cylindrical Coordinates (for Vortex Flow)

For an incompressible steady axisymmetric flow with $v_r = v_r(r, z)$, $v_\theta = v_\theta(r, z)$, $v_z = v_z(r, z)$:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (r v_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial v_z}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Special Case — Free Vortex with $v_r = 0$ and $v_z = 0$:** Continuity is **automatically satisfied**:

$$
\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial (0)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial (0)}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

**Special Case — Forced Vortex with $v_r = 0$ and $v_z = 0$:** Continuity is **also satisfied**, but vorticity is non-zero. This reinforces that **continuity alone does not guarantee irrotationality**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the following terms with suitable examples:**
(a) Steady and unsteady flow
(b) Uniform and non-uniform flow
(c) Laminar and turbulent flow

### Model Answer (Question 1):

**(a) Steady and Unsteady Flow:**

A flow is called **steady** if the fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) at every point in the flow field do not change with time. Mathematically, $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} = 0$. *Example:* Flow through a pipe at constant discharge.

A flow is called **unsteady** if the fluid properties at a point change with time. Mathematically, $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} \neq 0$. *Example:* Flow during the emptying of a tank.

**(b) Uniform and Non-Uniform Flow:**

A flow is **uniform** when the velocity (magnitude and direction) remains constant at every instant along the direction of flow. Mathematically, $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s} = 0$. *Example:* Flow in a long straight pipe of constant diameter.

A flow is **non-uniform** when the velocity changes along the direction of flow. Mathematically, $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial s} \neq 0$. *Example:* Flow through a pipe with a converging nozzle.

**(c) Laminar and Turbulent Flow:**

A flow is **laminar** if fluid particles move in smooth parallel layers (laminae) with no macroscopic mixing between layers. It occurs at low Reynolds numbers ($Re < 2000$ for pipe flow). *Example:* Flow of oil at low velocity in a small-diameter pipe.

A flow is **turbulent** if fluid particles move in a chaotic, random manner with eddies and intense mixing. It occurs at high Reynolds numbers ($Re > 4000$ for pipe flow). *Example:* Flow of water at high velocity in a large pipe.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between:**
(i) Rotational and irrotational flow
(ii) Free vortex and forced vortex
(iii) Streamline, pathline, and streakline

### Model Answer (Question 2):

**(i) Rotational and Irrotational Flow:**

A flow is **irrotational** if every fluid element translates and deforms but does **not rotate** about its own center of mass. Mathematically, vorticity $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ everywhere. *Example:* Ideal flow past a streamlined body.

A flow is **rotational** if fluid elements **rotate** about their own mass centers as they move. Mathematically, $\vec{\omega} \neq 0$ at some points. *Example:* Flow with viscous shear near a solid boundary.

**(ii) Free Vortex and Forced Vortex:**

A **free vortex** is one in which the tangential velocity is **inversely proportional** to the radius ($v_\theta = K/r$). The flow is irrotational except at the axis. *Example:* Whirlpool, drain vortex.

A **forced vortex** is one in which the fluid rotates as a **rigid body** with constant angular velocity. The tangential velocity is **directly proportional** to the radius ($v_\theta = \omega r$). *Example:* Rotating cylindrical tank, centrifuge.

**(iii) Streamline, Pathline, and Streakline:**

A **streamline** is an imaginary curve that is everywhere tangent to the instantaneous velocity vector. It indicates the direction of flow at a given instant.

A **pathline** is the actual trajectory traced by a single fluid particle over a period of time (Lagrangian concept).

A **streakline** is the locus of all fluid particles that have previously passed through a given fixed point (visualized by dye injection).

In **steady flow**, all three coincide. In **unsteady flow**, they are distinct.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **Eulerian and Lagrangian methods** of describing fluid flow. Derive an expression for the **acceleration of a fluid particle** in the Eulerian description. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **vorticity** and **circulation**. Using **Stokes' theorem**, establish the relationship between them. A free vortex has a tangential velocity distribution $v_\theta = K/r$. Find (i) the **circulation** around a circle of radius $R$ centered on the vortex axis, and (ii) the **pressure distribution** if the pressure at infinity is $p_\infty$. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Eulerian & Lagrangian Descriptions and Acceleration (7 marks)

**Eulerian Method:**
In the Eulerian method, the flow is described by specifying fluid properties (velocity, pressure, density) as **functions of space and time at fixed points**. The observer is **stationary**.

**Lagrangian Method:**
In the Lagrangian method, the observer **follows an individual fluid particle** and describes how its properties change with time. Each particle is tagged by its **initial coordinates** $(a, b, c)$ at time $t = 0$.

**Derivation of Acceleration:**

The position of a fluid particle in Lagrangian form is:

$$
\vec{r} = \vec{r}(a, b, c, t) = x(a,b,c,t)\hat{i} + y(a,b,c,t)\hat{j} + z(a,b,c,t)\hat{k}
$$

The Lagrangian velocity is:

$$
\vec{V}_L = \frac{\partial \vec{r}}{\partial t}
$$

In the Eulerian framework, the velocity is a field:

$$
\vec{V} = \vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}
$$

Taking the total time derivative (chain rule) and noting that the particle's position coordinates $x, y, z$ are themselves time-dependent:

$$
\frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}
$$

Since $dx/dt = u$, $dy/dt = v$, $dz/dt = w$ (the particle moves with the local fluid velocity):

$$
\boxed{\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}}
$$

The first term $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$ is the **local acceleration** and $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ is the **convective acceleration**.

#### Part (b) — Vorticity, Circulation, Stokes' Theorem & Free Vortex (7 marks)

**Definitions:**
- **Vorticity $\vec{\omega}$** is the curl of the velocity field: $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$. It is a local, pointwise measure of fluid element rotation.
- **Circulation $\Gamma$** is the line integral of velocity around a closed curve: $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}$. It is a global measure of rotation.

**Stokes' Theorem:**

Stokes' theorem converts the line integral around a closed curve $C$ to a surface integral of the curl over the surface $S$ bounded by $C$:

$$
\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot \hat{n} \, dS = \int_S \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{S}
$$

**(i) Circulation for a free vortex:**

Choose $C$ as a circle of radius $R$ centered on the vortex axis. Tangential velocity is $v_\theta = K/r$. On the circle, $d\vec{s}$ is tangential with magnitude $r\,d\theta$. The dot product $\vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s} = v_\theta \, r\, d\theta$:

$$
\Gamma = \int_0^{2\pi} v_\theta \cdot R \, d\theta = \int_0^{2\pi} \frac{K}{R} \cdot R \, d\theta = \int_0^{2\pi} K \, d\theta = 2\pi K
$$

$$
\boxed{\Gamma = 2\pi K \quad \text{(independent of } R\text{)}}
$$

**(ii) Pressure distribution:**

Apply Euler's equation in the radial direction for steady, inviscid, incompressible flow:

$$
-\frac{\rho v_\theta^2}{r} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial r}
$$

Substituting $v_\theta = K/r$:

$$
\frac{\partial p}{\partial r} = \frac{\rho K^2}{r^3}
$$

Integrating from $r$ to $\infty$ (where $p = p_\infty$):

$$
\int_{p(r)}^{p_\infty} dp = \int_r^\infty \frac{\rho K^2}{r^3} dr
$$

$$
p_\infty - p(r) = \rho K^2 \left[-\frac{1}{2r^2}\right]_r^\infty = \frac{\rho K^2}{2r^2}
$$

$$
\boxed{p(r) = p_\infty - \frac{\rho K^2}{2 r^2}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Define a **vortex**. Differentiate between a **free vortex** and a **forced vortex** with neat velocity profiles and pressure distribution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **forced vortex** is created in a cylindrical tank of radius $0.6$ m rotating at $120$ rpm. Determine:
(i) The **angular velocity** in rad/s.
(ii) The **tangential velocity** at the edge of the tank.
(iii) The **pressure rise** from the axis to the edge, assuming the tank is filled with water ($\rho = 1000$ kg/m³).
(iv) The **height of the paraboloid** formed by the free surface at the edge. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution:

#### Part (a) — Free vs Forced Vortex Comparison (7 marks)

A **vortex** is a flow region in which fluid particles rotate about a common axis.

**Free Vortex:**
- Velocity distribution: $v_\theta = K/r$ (inversely proportional to radius)
- Vorticity: $\omega = 0$ (irrotational)
- Pressure distribution: $p = p_\infty - \rho K^2/(2r^2)$ — pressure **decreases** toward the axis
- Total head is **constant** along streamlines
- Example: Whirlpool in a draining bathtub, flow around a sharp bend

**Forced Vortex:**
- Velocity distribution: $v_\theta = \omega r$ (linearly proportional to radius)
- Vorticity: $\omega = 2\omega_0$ (rotational, uniform)
- Pressure distribution: $p = p_0 + \rho \omega^2 r^2 / 2$ — pressure **increases** outward (parabolic)
- Free surface is a paraboloid: $z = \omega^2 r^2 / (2g)$
- Example: Liquid in a rotating tank, centrifuge

**Velocity Profile Sketch:**

```
FREE VORTEX:  v_theta                FORCED VORTEX: v_theta
                                         /|
                                        / |
                                       /  |
                                      /   |
                                     /    |
                                    /     |
                                   /______|
              r                       r
              (steep near axis,    (linear increase
               flat far away)       with r)
```

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem on Forced Vortex (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Radius of tank: $R = 0.6$ m
- Rotational speed: $N = 120$ rpm
- Density of water: $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³

**(i) Angular Velocity:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 120}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2}{1} = 4\pi \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{\omega = 12.566 \text{ rad/s}}
$$

**(ii) Tangential Velocity at the Edge:**

For a forced vortex, $v_\theta = \omega r$:

$$
v_{\theta, R} = \omega R = 4\pi \times 0.6 = 2.4\pi
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{\theta, R} = 7.540 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**(iii) Pressure Rise from Axis to Edge:**

For a forced vortex, $p(r) = p_0 + \rho \omega^2 r^2/2$. The pressure rise from $r = 0$ to $r = R$ is:

$$
\Delta p = p(R) - p(0) = \frac{\rho \omega^2 R^2}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta p = \frac{1000 \times (4\pi)^2 \times (0.6)^2}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta p = \frac{1000 \times 16\pi^2 \times 0.36}{2}
$$

$$
\Delta p = \frac{5760 \pi^2}{2} = 2880 \pi^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta p = 28,420.7 \text{ Pa} \approx 28.42 \text{ kPa}}
$$

**(iv) Height of Paraboloid at the Edge:**

For a rotating fluid, the free surface profile is a paraboloid:

$$
z_s = \frac{\omega^2 R^2}{2g}
$$

$$
z_s = \frac{(4\pi)^2 \times (0.6)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = \frac{16\pi^2 \times 0.36}{19.62}
$$

$$
z_s = \frac{5.76 \pi^2}{19.62} = \frac{56.85}{19.62}
$$

$$
\boxed{z_s = 2.898 \text{ m}}
$$

**Note:** If the tank is not tall enough, the rotation will cause spillage. The volume of fluid must be such that the paraboloid fits within the tank.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Uniform and Steady:** Many students interchange these. *Steady* is **time-based**; *uniform* is **space-based**.
> 2. **Free Vortex is Irrotational:** A free vortex has **zero vorticity**. Do not mark "rotational" just because streamlines are curved. Curved streamlines ≠ rotational flow.
> 3. **Units of Vorticity:** Vorticity is in $s^{-1}$ (or rad/s), **not** $m^2/s$. Circulation is in $m^2/s$. Do not interchange.
> 4. **Forced Vortex Pressure Variation:** Many students write $p = \rho \omega^2 r^2 / 2$ but forget the reference pressure $p_0$.
> 5. **Stokes' Theorem Direction:** The sign convention of the unit normal $\hat{n}$ must follow the right-hand rule with the direction of integration of the closed curve. KTU evaluators deduct marks if this is wrong.
> 6. **Reynolds Number:** Forget to convert cm to m in characteristic length — this leads to wrong order of magnitude in $Re$.
> 7. **Material Derivative vs Partial Derivative:** In the acceleration equation, do not write only $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t$; the convective term is **mandatory** in the general (unsteady, non-uniform) case.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 2):**

- ☐ **Eulerian vs Lagrangian:** Eulerian is field-based (fixed observer); Lagrangian is particle-based (moving observer). Material derivative bridges the two.
- ☐ **Material Derivative:** $D\phi/Dt = \partial\phi/\partial t + u\,\partial\phi/\partial x + v\,\partial\phi/\partial y + w\,\partial\phi/\partial z$.
- ☐ **Steady Flow:** $\partial\phi/\partial t = 0$ (time-invariant at a point).
- ☐ **Uniform Flow:** $\partial V/\partial s = 0$ (space-invariant along flow).
- ☐ **Laminar vs Turbulent:** $Re < 2000$ = laminar; $Re > 4000$ = turbulent (in pipe flow). $Re = \rho V D / \mu = V D / \nu$.
- ☐ **1-D, 2-D, 3-D Flow:** Count the number of spatial coordinates in which flow properties vary.
- ☐ **Irrotational Flow:** $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$ — no rotation of fluid element about its own center.
- ☐ **Rotational Flow:** $\nabla \times \vec{V} \neq 0$ — fluid elements rotate.
- ☐ **Vorticity:** $\vec{\omega} = \nabla \times \vec{V}$ — local rotation measure; in Cartesian, $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y$.
- ☐ **Circulation:** $\Gamma = \oint_C \vec{V} \cdot d\vec{s}$ — global integral measure; in $m^2/s$.
- ☐ **Stokes' Theorem:** $\Gamma = \int_S (\nabla \times \vec{V}) \cdot d\vec{S} = \int_S \vec{\omega} \cdot d\vec{S}$.
- ☐ **Free Vortex:** $v_\theta = K/r$, irrotational ($\omega = 0$), $p = p_\infty - \rho K^2/(2r^2)$, total head constant.
- ☐ **Forced Vortex:** $v_\theta = \omega r$, rotational ($\omega = 2\omega_0$), $p = p_0 + \rho \omega^2 r^2/2$, free surface is a paraboloid $z = \omega^2 r^2/(2g)$.
- ☐ **Pathline = Streamline = Streakline** in steady flow only.
- ☐ **Irrotational does NOT mean streamline is straight.** Free vortex has curved (circular) streamlines but is irrotational.
- ☐ **Continuity does NOT imply irrotationality.** Forced vortex satisfies continuity but has non-zero vorticity.
- ☐ **Rotational flow does NOT mean the flow is turbulent.** Rotation refers to angular motion of fluid elements; turbulence refers to chaotic fluctuations.
- ☐ **Engineering Applications:** Forced vortex → centrifugal pumps, rotating tanks. Free vortex → spillways, vortex chambers, drain designs. Irrotational flow → ideal flow theory, potential flow, aerodynamics.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Fluid Flow Classification, Vorticity, Circulation & Vortex Motion**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Continuity Equation: Continuity Equation in Three Dimensions, Velocity and Acceleration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Continuity Equation: Continuity Equation in Three Dimensions, Velocity and Acceleration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Continuity Equation — Formal Definition

The **Continuity Equation** in fluid mechanics is the mathematical statement of the **principle of conservation of mass** applied to a fluid in motion. In three-dimensional (3-D) Cartesian coordinates, it governs how fluid density and velocity fields must vary in space and time for mass to be neither created nor destroyed within any infinitesimal fluid element or control volume.

The general vector form of the continuity equation is:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

where $\rho$ is the fluid density and $\vec{V} = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ is the velocity vector field.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The continuity equation in 3-D is one of the **fundamental governing equations** of fluid mechanics (along with the Navier–Stokes and energy equations). It must be mastered thoroughly as it forms the basis for deriving Bernoulli's equation, Euler's equation, and the Navier–Stokes equations in later modules.

For an **incompressible fluid** (where $\rho$ = **constant** and density does not change with time or position, e.g., water under normal conditions), the continuity equation simplifies to the celebrated form:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0
$$

This expresses that the **divergence of the velocity field is zero**, meaning the net volumetric flow into any elemental volume is zero.

### 1.2 Velocity and Acceleration — Formal Definitions

**Velocity Field**: A fluid in motion is described by a velocity vector at every point in space and at every instant of time. This is called a **Eulerian description** of flow:

$$
\vec{V}(x, y, z, t) = u(x, y, z, t)\hat{i} + v(x, y, z, t)\hat{j} + w(x, y, z, t)\hat{k}
$$

**Acceleration of a Fluid Particle**: A fluid particle moves with the flow and experiences acceleration due to two distinct physical mechanisms:

- **Local (Temporal / Unsteady) Acceleration**: $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$ — caused by the flow being unsteady at a fixed point.
- **Convective (Advective / Spatial) Acceleration**: $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ — caused by the fluid particle moving from one location to another where the velocity is different.

The **total (material) acceleration** is therefore:

$$
\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}
$$

The operator $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ is known as the **Material Derivative** (or substantial / hydrodynamic derivative).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — Real-World Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Garden Hose Mental Model**
> Imagine squeezing a flexible garden hose while water flows through it. As the cross-section narrows, you instinctively see that the velocity of water must **increase** to push the same volume of water out the other end. This is exactly what the continuity equation captures — for an incompressible fluid, when the cross-sectional area decreases, velocity must increase so that the volumetric flow rate ($Q = A \cdot V$) remains **constant**.
>
> In 3-D, this idea extends to every infinitesimal fluid parcel: the rate at which mass flows **into** the parcel must equal the rate at which mass flows **out plus accumulation** inside it. If a stream is "spreading out" (divergence > 0), the fluid must be **compressible** or there must be a **source**; if it is "converging" (divergence < 0), there must be a **sink**.
>
> For acceleration, think of a leaf floating on a river: even if the river's speed at any single point is steady (no local acceleration), the leaf accelerates when it drifts into faster-moving regions of the river — this is **convective acceleration**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Density of water** at $4^{\circ}\text{C}$: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Standard acceleration due to gravity**: $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Kinematic viscosity of water** at $20^{\circ}\text{C}$: $\nu = 1.004 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Incompressibility Assumption** in this module is valid for liquids when the Mach number $M < 0.3$, pressure variations are small, and temperature is approximately uniform.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Foundation — Why the Continuity Equation Exists

The continuity equation is **not** an empirical law — it is a direct consequence of two foundational principles:

1. **Conservation of Mass**: Mass can neither be created nor destroyed within a closed system (a consequence of the classical conservation laws).
2. **Continuum Hypothesis**: Fluid properties (density, velocity, pressure) vary continuously in space and time, allowing the use of differential calculus.

When applied to an infinitesimal control volume, these principles yield a **partial differential equation** that must be satisfied by every physically admissible flow field.

### 2.2 Structural Breakdown — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Set up the control volume**: Consider an infinitesimal rectangular fluid element (parallelepiped) with sides $\delta x$, $\delta y$, $\delta z$, located at point $(x, y, z)$ at time $t$.

**Step 2 — Compute mass fluxes through each face**:
- Mass entering through the left face (at $x$) per unit time: $(\rho u)\,\delta y\,\delta z$
- Mass leaving through the right face (at $x + \delta x$) per unit time: $\left[\rho u + \dfrac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\delta x\right]\delta y\,\delta z$

**Step 3 — Net outflow in x-direction**: Using Taylor series expansion to first order:

$$
\text{Net mass outflow in } x = \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\delta x\,\delta y\,\delta z
$$

**Step 4 — Repeat for y and z directions**: Similar expressions arise from the y-faces and z-faces.

**Step 5 — Apply conservation of mass**: The rate of mass accumulation inside the element must equal the negative of the net outflow:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\delta x\,\delta y\,\delta z = -\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right]\delta x\,\delta y\,\delta z
$$

**Step 6 — Divide by the elemental volume** $\delta x\,\delta y\,\delta z \neq 0$ to obtain the differential form.

**Step 7 — Specialize for incompressible flow**: Set $\rho$ = constant, eliminating $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} = 0$ and $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial y} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial z} = 0$.

### 2.3 Decomposition of the Continuity Equation

The continuity equation can be expanded in two equivalent forms:

**Form 1 — Vector / Conservation Form**:
$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

**Form 2 — Expanded / Non-Conservation Form** (using product rule):
$$
\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho(\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0
$$

This tells us that the **rate of change of density following a fluid particle** plus the **density times divergence of velocity** equals zero.

For **incompressible flow** ($D\rho/Dt = 0$):
$$
\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Special Condition / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General 3-D Continuity (Vector) | $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Valid for compressible & incompressible flow |
| 2 | Continuity (Expanded) | $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho \left(\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right) = 0$ | Material derivative form |
| 3 | Continuity for Incompressible Flow | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | $\rho$ = constant (e.g., water) |
| 4 | Continuity in Cylindrical Coordinates | $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial(r u_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial u_z}{\partial z} = 0$ | For axisymmetric incompressible flow |
| 5 | Velocity Field Definition | $\vec{V}(x,y,z,t) = u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k}$ | Eulerian description |
| 6 | Material Derivative Operator | $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial}{\partial z}$ | Tracks property following fluid particle |
| 7 | Total Acceleration (Vector) | $\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Sum of local + convective |
| 8 | Local (Unsteady) Acceleration | $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} = \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t}\hat{i} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t}\hat{j} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t}\hat{k}$ | Vanishes for steady flow |
| 9 | Convective Acceleration | $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$ | Vanishes for uniform flow |
| 10 | Acceleration Component $a_x$ | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial z}$ | $x$-component |
| 11 | Acceleration Component $a_y$ | $\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial v}{\partial z}$ | $y$-component |
| 12 | Acceleration Component $a_z$ | $\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z}$ | $z$-component |
| 13 | Stream Function (2-D Incompressible) | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y},\quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Automatically satisfies 2-D continuity |
| 14 | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q = \displaystyle\iint_S \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}\, dA$ | Through any cross-section |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydraulic Engineering**: Designing pipelines, penstocks, and open channels by enforcing mass conservation between sections.
- **Aerospace Engineering**: Computing mass flow rates through jet engines, rocket nozzles, and wind tunnels.
- **Meteorology & Oceanography**: Continuity equations govern atmospheric moisture transport and ocean current modelling.
- **Biomedical Engineering**: Blood flow modelling through arteries and veins (treated as incompressible in most cases).
- **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)**: The continuity equation is one of the **Navier–Stokes equations solved numerically** by software like ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, and COMSOL.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the 3-D Continuity Equation (Control Volume Approach)

**Step 1 — Choose an infinitesimal control volume**:

Consider an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped fluid element fixed in space with its center at $(x, y, z)$ and sides of length $\delta x$, $\delta y$, $\delta z$ aligned with the coordinate axes.

**Step 2 — Mass contained inside the control volume at time $t$**:

$$
\delta m = \rho \cdot \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 3 — Rate of change of mass inside the control volume**:

By partial differentiation with respect to time (since $\delta x$, $\delta y$, $\delta z$ are fixed for a Eulerian CV):

$$
\frac{\partial (\delta m)}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate through the face at $x$ (left face, area $\delta y \, \delta z$)**:

Fluid enters with $x$-component of velocity $u$:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{in},\,x} = (\rho u)\, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow rate through the face at $x + \delta x$ (right face)**:

Using a Taylor series expansion to first order in $\delta x$:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{out},\,x} = \left[\rho u + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\delta x\right]\delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 6 — Net outflow of mass in the $x$-direction**:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{net},\,x} = \dot{m}_{\text{out},\,x} - \dot{m}_{\text{in},\,x} = \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\, \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 7 — Net outflows in the $y$ and $z$ directions** (by symmetry of the argument):

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{net},\,y} = \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y}\, \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{net},\,z} = \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\, \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 8 — Total net mass outflow from the control volume**:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{net,total}} = \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right]\delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 9 — Apply Conservation of Mass**:

$$
\text{(Rate of mass accumulation)} = - \text{(Net mass outflow)}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z = -\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right]\delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

**Step 10 — Cancel the non-zero elemental volume**:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0\;}
$$

**Step 11 — Vector notation compact form**:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0
$$

**Step 12 — Expand the divergence using the product rule**:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0
$$

Recognizing that $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \rho = \dfrac{D\rho}{Dt}$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0\;}
$$

**Step 13 — Specialize for incompressible flow**:

For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ = constant, so $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} = 0$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0\;}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of Acceleration Components (Material Derivative Expansion)

**Step 1 — Total acceleration in vector form**:

$$
\vec{a} = \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \frac{D}{Dt}(u\hat{i} + v\hat{j} + w\hat{k})
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the material derivative operator**:

Since $\hat{i}$, $\hat{j}$, $\hat{k}$ are constant unit vectors in Cartesian coordinates:

$$
\vec{a} = \frac{Du}{Dt}\hat{i} + \frac{Dv}{Dt}\hat{j} + \frac{Dw}{Dt}\hat{k}
$$

**Step 3 — Expand $Du/Dt$ using the definition of material derivative**:

The material derivative of any scalar property $f(x, y, z, t)$ is:

$$
\frac{Df}{Dt} = \frac{\partial f}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{dt} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial y}\frac{dy}{dt} + \frac{\partial f}{\partial z}\frac{dz}{dt}
$$

Since the particle moves with the fluid, $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = u$, $\dfrac{dy}{dt} = v$, $\dfrac{dz}{dt} = w$.

**Step 4 — Apply to $u$, $v$, $w$**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} &= \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \\
a_y = \frac{Dv}{Dt} &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} \\
a_z = \frac{Dw}{Dt} &= \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compact operator form**:

$$
\vec{a} = \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}
$$

where $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ is the **convective operator**:

$$
\vec{V} \cdot \nabla = u\frac{\partial}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial}{\partial z}
$$

### 3.3 Alternative Derivation via Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)

**Step 1 — State the Reynolds Transport Theorem for mass**:

$$
\frac{D}{Dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, d\forall = \int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}\, dA
$$

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass**: Mass within a system is conserved, so:

$$
\frac{D}{Dt}\int_{CV} \rho \, d\forall = 0
$$

Therefore:

$$
\int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}\, dA = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Divergence (Gauss) Theorem** to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$
\int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}\, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})\, d\forall
$$

**Step 4 — Combine**:

$$
\int_{CV} \left[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})\right] d\forall = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Since the integral must vanish for any arbitrary control volume, the integrand itself must vanish**:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0\;}
$$

This is identical to the control-volume derivation, confirming the result.

### 3.4 Analytical Solution — Worked Example

**Problem** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]: A velocity field is given by $\vec{V} = (3x + 2y)\hat{i} + (2xz - y)\hat{j} + (z^2 - x)\hat{k}$. Verify whether this flow is physically possible for an incompressible fluid, and determine the acceleration at the point $(1, 1, 2)$.

**Solution**:

**Step 1 — Identify velocity components**:
$u = 3x + 2y$, $v = 2xz - y$, $w = z^2 - x$.

**Step 2 — Compute partial derivatives for continuity**:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -1, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 2z
$$

**Step 3 — Sum them up**:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 3 + (-1) + 2z = 2 + 2z
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion on continuity**:

Since the sum equals $2 + 2z$ (which is generally **not zero**), the flow is **NOT** physically possible for an incompressible fluid.

**Step 5 — Compute acceleration components** (still useful pedagogically):

First, all required partial derivatives:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 3, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0 \\
\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2z, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -1, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 2x \\
\frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = -1, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 2z
\end{aligned}
$$

At point $(1, 1, 2)$: $u = 3(1) + 2(1) = 5$, $v = 2(1)(2) - 1 = 3$, $w = 2^2 - 1 = 3$.

**Step 6 — Acceleration components**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_x &= \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \\
&= 0 + 5(3) + 3(2) + 3(0) = 15 + 6 + 0 = 21 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_y &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} \\
&= 0 + 5(2 \cdot 2) + 3(-1) + 3(2 \cdot 1) = 0 + 20 - 3 + 6 = 23 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_z &= \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z} \\
&= 0 + 5(-1) + 3(0) + 3(2 \cdot 2) = -5 + 0 + 12 = 7 \text{ m/s}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Magnitude of acceleration**:

$$
\vert \vec{a} \vert = \sqrt{a_x^2 + a_y^2 + a_z^2} = \sqrt{21^2 + 23^2 + 7^2} = \sqrt{441 + 529 + 49} = \sqrt{1019} \approx 31.92 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Final Answer**: The flow is not physically possible for an incompressible fluid since $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} \neq 0$. The acceleration at $(1, 1, 2)$ is $\vec{a} = 21\hat{i} + 23\hat{j} + 7\hat{k} \text{ m/s}^2$, with magnitude $\approx 31.92 \text{ m/s}^2$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Flow Field Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FLUID FLOW ANALYSIS] --> B[Description Methods]
    A --> C[Governing Equations]
    A --> D[Kinematic Quantities]

    B --> B1[Eulerian<br/>Fixed Coordinates]
    B --> B2[Lagrangian<br/>Following Particle]

    C --> C1[Continuity Equation<br/>Mass Conservation]
    C --> C2[Momentum Equation<br/>Newtons Second Law]
    C --> C3[Energy Equation<br/>First Law of Thermodynamics]

    C1 --> C1a[General 3D Form]
    C1 --> C1b[Incompressible Form]

    C1a --> C1a1[drho/dt + div rhoV = 0]
    C1b --> C1b1[du/dx + dv/dy + dw/dz = 0]

    D --> D1[Velocity Field V]
    D --> D2[Acceleration Field a]

    D2 --> D2a[Local Acceleration<br/>dV/dt]
    D2 --> D2b[Convective Acceleration<br/>V dot grad V]

    D2a --> D2c[Material Derivative<br/>DV/Dt]
    D2b --> D2c

    D1 --> E[VELOCITY COMPONENTS]
    E --> E1[u in x direction]
    E --> E2[v in y direction]
    E --> E3[w in z direction]

    style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D2c fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Control Volume Mass Flux Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CV[Infinitesimal Control Volume<br/>delta_x times delta_y times delta_z] --> F1[Face at x<br/>rho times u]
    CV --> F2[Face at x+delta_x<br/>rho times u + d by dx of rho times u times delta_x]
    CV --> F3[Face at y<br/>rho times v]
    CV --> F4[Face at y+delta_y<br/>rho times v + d by dy of rho times v times delta_y]
    CV --> F5[Face at z<br/>rho times w]
    CV --> F6[Face at z+delta_z<br/>rho times w + d by dz of rho times w times delta_z]

    F1 --> SUM[Net Outflow Computation]
    F2 --> SUM
    F3 --> SUM
    F4 --> SUM
    F5 --> SUM
    F6 --> SUM

    SUM --> ACC[Mass Accumulation<br/>d rho by dt times delta_x times delta_y times delta_z]

    ACC --> CONS[CONSERVATION OF MASS<br/>Accumulation equals minus Net Outflow]
    CONS --> RES[Result<br/>drho/dt + div rhoV equals 0]

    RES --> INC[Incompressible Special Case<br/>div V equals 0]

    style CV fill:#ffd966,stroke:#000
    style CONS fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style RES fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style INC fill:#0b5394,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Decomposition of Total Acceleration

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOT[TOTAL ACCELERATION<br/>DV by Dt] --> L[LOCAL ACCELERATION<br/>dV by dt]
    TOT --> C[CONVECTIVE ACCELERATION<br/>V dot grad V]

    L --> L1[Causes Unsteadiness at Fixed Point]
    L --> L2[Vanishes for Steady Flow]
    L --> L3[Mathematical Expression<br/>du by dt i + dv by dt j + dw by dt k]

    C --> C1[Caused by Particle Moving to New Location]
    C --> C2[Vanishes for Uniform Flow]
    C --> C3[Mathematical Expression<br/>u du/dx + v du/dy + w du/dz in x dir]

    TOT --> COMP[COMPONENT FORM]
    COMP --> AX[a_x equals du/dt plus u du/dx plus v du/dy plus w du/dz]
    COMP --> AY[a_y equals dv/dt plus u dv/dx plus v dv/dy plus w dv/dz]
    COMP --> AZ[a_z equals dw/dt plus u dw/dx plus v dw/dy plus w dw/dz]

    style TOT fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L fill:#c00000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```
