
> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note**: The Mermaid block diagrams above are designed to render reliably in any Markdown-compatible viewer (GitHub, VS Code, Obsidian). They abstract the physical flow geometry into a logical architecture that captures the relationships between mass fluxes, conservation laws, and acceleration components — essential for understanding the topic without ambiguity.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
State the continuity equation in three dimensions for (i) a compressible fluid and (ii) an incompressible fluid. What physical principle does it represent?

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

(i) **Compressible fluid**:
$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0
$$

(ii) **Incompressible fluid**:
$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

**Physical Principle**: The continuity equation is the mathematical statement of the **conservation of mass** applied to a fluid flow — mass can neither be created nor destroyed within a fluid element.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Define (i) local acceleration and (ii) convective acceleration. When does each of these vanish?

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

(i) **Local Acceleration**: The rate of change of velocity at a fixed point in space with respect to time, $\dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t}$. It vanishes when the flow is **steady**.

(ii) **Convective Acceleration**: The acceleration experienced by a fluid particle as it moves to a new location where the velocity is different, $(\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$. It vanishes when the flow is **uniform** (velocity does not change with position).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QN]`

**(a)** Derive the three-dimensional continuity equation for a compressible fluid using an elemental control volume. State its simplified form for an incompressible fluid with physical justification. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the **material derivative** and obtain the components of acceleration of a fluid particle in $x$, $y$, $z$ directions. A fluid flows steadily with velocity components $u = x^2 + y^2$, $v = 2xy$, $w = 0$. Verify whether the continuity equation is satisfied. Find the acceleration components. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) Derivation (7 marks)**:

Consider an infinitesimal rectangular parallelepiped fluid element fixed in space with center at $(x, y, z)$ and dimensions $\delta x$, $\delta y$, $\delta z$.

*Mass inside CV* at time $t$: $\delta m = \rho \, \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z$

*Rate of accumulation of mass*:
$$
\frac{\partial (\delta m)}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

*Mass flow rate through x-face at $x$* (entering): $(\rho u) \, \delta y \, \delta z$

*Mass flow rate through x-face at $x + \delta x$* (leaving): Using Taylor expansion:
$$
\left[\rho u + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} \delta x\right] \delta y \, \delta z
$$

*Net outflow in x-direction*:
$$
\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

Similarly for y and z directions:
$$
\frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

*Total net mass outflow*:
$$
\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z \quad \text{...(2)}
$$

*Conservation of Mass*:
$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z = -\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] \delta x \, \delta y \, \delta z
$$

*After cancelling the elemental volume*:
$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0\;}
$$

**Incompressible simplification**: For an incompressible fluid, $\rho$ = constant, hence all density derivatives vanish. We obtain:
$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0\;}
$$
This implies the divergence of the velocity field is zero — the volumetric flow is conserved.

**(b) Material Derivative and Acceleration (7 marks)**:

The material derivative (also called substantial derivative) of a property $\phi(x,y,z,t)$ is the rate of change of $\phi$ **following a fluid particle**:

$$
\frac{D\phi}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z} = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\phi
$$

Applying to the velocity components:

$$
a_x = \frac{Du}{Dt} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}
$$

$$
a_y = \frac{Dv}{Dt} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial v}{\partial z}
$$

$$
a_z = \frac{Dw}{Dt} = \frac{\partial w}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial w}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}
$$

**Continuity check** for $u = x^2 + y^2$, $v = 2xy$, $w = 0$:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2x, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2x, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 2x + 2x + 0 = 4x \neq 0
$$

Hence the continuity equation is **NOT satisfied**. The flow is not physically possible for an incompressible fluid.

**Acceleration components** (still computed for completeness):

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2x, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2y, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 2y, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2x, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial z} = 0
$$

$$
\frac{\partial w}{\partial t} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial y} = 0, \quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0
$$

Since the flow is steady, all time derivatives vanish. Acceleration components:

$$
a_x = u(2x) + v(2y) + w(0) = (x^2+y^2)(2x) + (2xy)(2y) = 2x^3 + 2xy^2 + 4xy^2 = 2x^3 + 6xy^2
$$

$$
a_y = u(2y) + v(2x) + w(0) = (x^2+y^2)(2y) + (2xy)(2x) = 2x^2y + 2y^3 + 4x^2y = 6x^2y + 2y^3
$$

$$
a_z = 0
$$

So $\vec{a} = (2x^3 + 6xy^2)\hat{i} + (6x^2y + 2y^3)\hat{j}$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model QN]`

**(a)** Starting from the **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)**, derive the three-dimensional continuity equation in differential form. Show two equivalent forms and explain the physical meaning of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-dimensional incompressible flow has velocity components $u = 2x - y$ and $v = x + 3y$. Verify whether the continuity equation is satisfied. Compute the convective acceleration components $a_x$ and $a_y$ at the point $(2, 1)$. State whether the flow is rotational or irrotational. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**(a) RTT-Based Derivation (7 marks)**:

The **Reynolds Transport Theorem** for any extensive property $B$ relates the time rate of change of $B$ in a system to the control volume formulation:

$$
\frac{DB_{sys}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \, \beta \, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho \, \beta \, (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA
$$

where $\beta = B/m$ is the intensive property (per unit mass) and $\vec{V}$ is the velocity of the fluid relative to the control surface.

**Apply to mass conservation** ($B = m$, $\beta = 1$):

Since the total mass of a system is conserved, $\dfrac{Dm_{sys}}{Dt} = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \, d\forall + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA \quad \text{...(1)}
$$

**Apply Gauss Divergence Theorem** to convert the surface integral to a volume integral:

$$
\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, d\forall
$$

Substituting back into (1):

$$
\int_{CV} \left[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})\right] d\forall = 0
$$

Since this integral must vanish for **any arbitrary** control volume (in particular for an infinitesimal one), the integrand itself must vanish identically:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0\;} \quad \text{...(Form 1: Conservation Form)}
$$

**Form 2 (Non-Conservation Form)** — Expand the divergence using product rule:

$$
\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = \rho(\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) + \vec{V} \cdot (\nabla \rho)
$$

Substituting and rearranging:

$$
\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot (\nabla \rho) + \rho (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0
$$

Recognizing that $\dfrac{D\rho}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\rho$:

$$
\boxed{\;\frac{D\rho}{Dt} + \rho(\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) = 0\;} \quad \text{...(Form 2: Material / Lagrangian Form)}
$$

**Physical meaning**:
- **Form 1** states that the local rate of change of density at a point plus the net outflow of mass per unit volume from that point equals zero — an **Eulerian** statement.
- **Form 2** states that the rate of change of density **following a fluid particle** plus the rate of volumetric dilatation equals zero — a **Lagrangian** statement.

For an incompressible fluid ($\rho$ = constant, $D\rho/Dt = 0$): both forms reduce to $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.

**(b) Verification and Convective Acceleration (7 marks)**:

Given: $u = 2x - y$, $v = x + 3y$, $w = 0$.

**Continuity check** (for 2-D incompressible flow, $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$):

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 3
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2 + 3 = 5 \neq 0
$$

**Continuity equation is NOT satisfied** — the flow is physically impossible for an incompressible fluid.

**Convective Acceleration** (steady flow, so local acceleration = 0):

Partial derivatives required:

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 2, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = -1
$$

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} = 1, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 3
$$

Velocity components at $(2, 1)$:
$u = 2(2) - 1 = 3$, $v = 2 + 3(1) = 5$.

**$x$-component of acceleration**:
$$
a_x = u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 3(2) + 5(-1) = 6 - 5 = 1 \text{ unit/s}^2
$$

**$y$-component of acceleration**:
$$
a_y = u\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 3(1) + 5(3) = 3 + 15 = 18 \text{ unit/s}^2
$$

**Rotationality check** — The z-component of vorticity is:

$$
\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 1 - (-1) = 2 \neq 0
$$

Since $\omega_z \neq 0$, the flow is **rotational** (and hence contains vorticity).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to expand the divergence** when asked to write in "expanded form" — students often write only the vector form $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$, losing **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing local with convective acceleration** — local has $\partial/\partial t$ only; convective has spatial derivatives multiplied by velocity components. Marks are deducted when these are interchanged.
> 3. **Skipping the Taylor series step** in the control-volume derivation — examiners specifically look for the phrase "Taylor series expansion to first order" or equivalent. Omitting it costs **at least 1 mark**.
> 4. **Sign errors in continuity check problems** — students often drop signs when computing $\partial v/\partial y$. Always re-verify signs by re-substituting the function.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption** of an incompressible fluid when simplifying — failing to mention $\rho$ = constant costs easy marks.
> 6. **In cylindrical coordinates**, students frequently forget the factor of $r$ in the radial derivative term $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial(r u_r)}{\partial r}$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Continuity Equation, Velocity & Acceleration**

- **Continuity equation in 3-D** is the differential statement of **conservation of mass** applied to a fluid element.
- **General vector form**: $\dfrac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$
- **Incompressible simplified form**: $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$
- **Material derivative**: $\dfrac{D}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)$ — tracks property **following a fluid particle**.
- **Total acceleration** = Local + Convective: $\vec{a} = \dfrac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = \dfrac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla)\vec{V}$
- **Local acceleration vanishes** for **steady flow**.
- **Convective acceleration vanishes** for **uniform flow** (spatially constant velocity).
- **Velocity field** is an **Eulerian** concept — $\vec{V}(x, y, z, t)$ — defined at fixed points in space.
- **Continuity derivation methods** (both must be known): (i) Control volume + Taylor series, (ii) Reynolds Transport Theorem + Gauss Divergence Theorem.
- **Verification problems**: substitute velocity components into $\nabla \cdot \vec{V}$ and check if it equals zero.
- **Acceleration components** (Cartesian): each is $\partial u_i/\partial t + u \, \partial u_i/\partial x + v \, \partial u_i/\partial y + w \, \partial u_i/\partial z$.
- **Stream function** $\psi(x, y)$ exists only for **2-D incompressible flow** and automatically satisfies continuity.
- **Density** $\rho$ is **constant** for liquids under normal conditions → use the simplified incompressible form.
- **For gases** moving at low speeds (Mach < 0.3), incompressible approximation is often still valid.
- **Cylindrical form**: $\dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial(r u_r)}{\partial r} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial u_\theta}{\partial \theta} + \dfrac{\partial u_z}{\partial z} = 0$ (incompressible).
- **Continuity is a kinematic condition** — independent of forces, pressures, or fluid properties (only mass conservation matters).
- **Flow is irrotational** if $\nabla \times \vec{V} = 0$; **rotational** otherwise — determined separately from continuity.
- **Always check units** of acceleration components: must be in $\text{m/s}^2$ (or consistent).
- **KTU frequently asked**: derive continuity, simplify for incompressible, verify given velocity field, and compute acceleration — practice all four.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Streamlines, Path Lines, Streak Lines, Stream Function, Velocity Potential and Flow Net
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Streamlines, Path Lines, Streak Lines, Stream Function, Velocity Potential and Flow Net

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Streamlines

A **streamline** is an imaginary curve drawn through a flowing fluid such that the **velocity vector is tangent to the curve at every point** at a given instant. Physically, no fluid particle can cross a streamline; the fluid flows *along* it. In steady flow, streamlines remain fixed in space and form the familiar "flow pattern" of the fluid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** A streamline is a continuous line in a fluid flow field such that the tangent to the line at any point gives the direction of the instantaneous velocity vector at that point.

**Mathematical representation:**
For a 2D flow with velocity components $(u, v)$, a streamline satisfies:

$$\frac{dx}{u} = \frac{dy}{v} = \frac{dz}{w}$$

### 1.2 Path Lines

A **path line** is the **actual trajectory** traced by a single fluid particle as it moves through space over a period of time. It is the route a tagged particle follows, like tracking a single leaf floating down a river.

> [!NOTE]
> In **steady flow**, streamlines and path lines are **identical**. In **unsteady flow**, they differ.

### 1.3 Streak Lines

A **streak line** (or *filament line*) is the **locus of all fluid particles** that have *previously passed through a given fixed point* in the flow field. Think of it as the visible dye trail released continuously from a fixed point — every particle in the dye trail passed through that point at *some* earlier time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For **steady flow**: Streamlines ≡ Path lines ≡ Streak lines. For **unsteady flow**: All three are different curves.

### 1.4 Stream Function (ψ)

The **stream function** $\psi(x, y, t)$ is a scalar function whose partial derivatives give the velocity components in 2D incompressible flow:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

The stream function **automatically satisfies the continuity equation** for incompressible flow, and lines of constant $\psi$ are streamlines.

### 1.5 Velocity Potential (φ)

The **velocity potential** $\phi(x, y, t)$ is a scalar function whose gradient equals the velocity vector. It exists only if the flow is **irrotational** (i.e., the curl of velocity is zero):

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \qquad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

### 1.6 Flow Net

A **flow net** is an orthogonal curvilinear grid formed by a family of **streamlines** and a family of **equipotential lines** (lines of constant $\phi$). It is a graphical technique to solve 2D Laplace-equation problems such as seepage under dams, flow through earth dams, and confined/unconfined groundwater flow.

### 1.7 Conceptual Analogy — "Three Ways to Visualize a Crowd"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Imagine a stadium crowd moving toward exit gates:**
> - **Streamline** = A *map line* drawn right now, showing the direction each person is heading *at this instant*.
> - **Path line** = The *footprints* of a single tracked person from the moment they entered the stadium.
> - **Streak line** = The *smell trail* (e.g., a perfume released at one spot) — every person who walked through that spot in the past is contributing to the visible streak.
> - **Stream function** = A *topographic map* of the crowd, where contour lines show flow direction.
> - **Velocity potential** = A *pressure map*; lines of equal potential are perpendicular to the streamlines.
> - **Flow net** = The *intersecting contour + pressure* map of the stadium — the engineer's go-to visualization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Comparison of the Three Flow Lines

| Feature | Streamline | Pathline | Streakline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Tangent to velocity at an instant | Trajectory of a single particle | Locus of all particles that passed a fixed point |
| Time involved | One instant (snapshot) | Period of time | All past time |
| Useful for | Steady flow visualization | Particle tracking (Lagrangian view) | Experimental dye/ smoke visualization (Eulerian) |
| Coincide in | Steady flow | Steady flow | Steady flow |

### 2.2 Why Stream Function and Velocity Potential Matter

- **Stream function** $\psi$ exists for any 2D incompressible flow (rotational or irrotational).
- **Velocity potential** $\phi$ exists **only** for irrotational flow.
- When **both** exist (2D incompressible, irrotational), they satisfy the **Cauchy–Riemann equations** and are conjugate harmonic functions. Both then satisfy **Laplace's equation**.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this formula sheet — these equations are the bread-and-butter of KTU 2D fluid mechanics problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Conditions / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Streamline (2D) | $\dfrac{dx}{u} = \dfrac{dy}{v}$ | At a given instant |
| 2 | Stream function definition | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y},\ v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | 2D, incompressible |
| 3 | Velocity potential definition | $u = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x},\ v = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$ | Irrotational flow |
| 4 | Continuity (auto by $\psi$) | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$ | Incompressible |
| 5 | Irrotationality condition | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$ | 2D |
| 6 | Laplace equation for $\phi$ | $\nabla^{2}\phi = \dfrac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$ | Irrotational + incompressible |
| 7 | Laplace equation for $\psi$ | $\nabla^{2}\psi = \dfrac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$ | Irrotational + incompressible |
| 8 | Cauchy–Riemann equations | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y},\quad \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Both $\phi$ and $\psi$ exist |
| 9 | Volumetric flow rate between streamlines | $Q = \psi_{2} - \psi_{1}$ | Per unit depth, $\mathrm{m^{2}/s}$ |
| 10 | Circulation | $\Gamma = \oint \vec{V}\cdot d\vec{r}$ | $\mathrm{m^{2}/s}$ |
| 11 | Vorticity (2D) | $\omega_z = \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial y}$ | $\mathrm{s^{-1}}$ |
| 12 | Stream function — polar | $V_r = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta},\ V_\theta = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial r}$ | 2D polar |
| 13 | Velocity potential — polar | $V_r = \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial r},\ V_\theta = \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial \theta}$ | 2D polar |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** Stream function and flow net are indispensable in designing **earth dams, weirs, sheet pile walls, drainage systems, and groundwater models** — the entire branch of *seepage analysis* relies on these tools.

### 2.4 Flow Net — Properties and Construction Rules

A flow net consists of:
- **Streamlines** $\psi = $ const
- **Equipotential lines** $\phi = $ const

Key properties:
1. Streamlines and equipotentials **intersect orthogonally** (at right angles).
2. The flow region is divided into **curvilinear squares** (each "square" has nearly equal length and width, and diagonals meet at ~$90^{\circ}$).
3. Discharge per unit width through any flow channel is constant.
4. Equipotential drops are equal between consecutive equipotentials.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Stream Function from Continuity

**Given:** 2D incompressible flow with velocity components $u(x,y,t)$ and $v(x,y,t)$.

**Continuity equation for incompressible 2D flow:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{...(1)}$$

We seek a function $\psi(x,y,t)$ such that:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \text{...(2)}$$

**Substituting (2) into (1):**

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}\right) = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^{2} \psi}{\partial y\,\partial x} = 0$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2} \psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^{2} \psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} = 0 \equiv 0$$

This is **identically true**, so the stream function is a valid mathematical construct that **automatically guarantees mass conservation** for any 2D incompressible flow.

### 3.2 Derivation — Flow Rate Between Two Streamlines

Consider two streamlines $\psi_1$ and $\psi_2$ separated by a small distance $dn$ (along the direction normal to the streamline). The discharge per unit depth crossing the strip is:

$$dQ = V \cdot dn$$

But $V$ has components $u$ and $v$, and the normal direction to a streamline $\psi = $ const has components $(\partial \psi/\partial y, -\partial \psi/\partial x)$ normalized. By elementary vector calculus:

$$Q = \int_{\psi_1}^{\psi_2} d\psi = \psi_2 - \psi_1$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The discharge per unit depth between two streamlines equals the **difference in their stream-function values**. This is *the* most important property of $\psi$ in engineering practice.

### 3.3 Derivation — Velocity Potential from Irrotationality

**Irrotationality condition in 2D:**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0$$

If a scalar function $\phi(x,y,t)$ exists with:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}, \qquad v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

Then:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) = \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x\partial y} - \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial y\partial x} = 0$$

Hence irrotationality is automatically satisfied.

### 3.4 Derivation — Laplace Equation for $\phi$ and $\psi$

**Step 1:** Differentiate $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} \quad \text{...(a)}$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$ with respect to $y$:

$$\frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial y^{2}} \quad \text{...(b)}$$

**Step 3:** Add (a) and (b) and apply continuity $\partial u/\partial x + \partial v/\partial y = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial y^{2}} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \nabla^{2}\phi = 0$$

**Step 4:** Similarly for $\psi$. Use the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y}, \quad \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial y\,\partial x}$$

Add and apply continuity:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \nabla^{2}\psi = 0$$

### 3.5 Derivation — Cauchy–Riemann Equations

When **both** $\phi$ and $\psi$ exist (i.e., 2D, incompressible, irrotational), equate the expressions for $u$ and $v$:

From $\phi$: $\quad u = \dfrac{\partial\phi}{\partial x}$

From $\psi$: $\quad u = \dfrac{\partial\psi}{\partial y}$

Equating:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \text{(C-R #1)}$$

Similarly for $v$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \text{(C-R #2)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> These are identical in form to the Cauchy–Riemann equations in complex-variable theory, which is why flows with $\phi$ and $\psi$ can be elegantly described by the complex potential $\Omega(z) = \phi + i\psi$.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example: Uniform Flow

A **uniform flow** has $u = U$ (constant), $v = 0$. Verify all the conditions.

**Step 1 — Stream function:**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = U \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \psi = Uy + f(x)$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -f'(x) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f(x) = \text{const}$$

So $\psi = Uy$. Streamlines are $y = $ const — horizontal lines. ✓

**Step 2 — Velocity potential:**

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = U \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \phi = Ux + g(y)$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = g'(y) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; g(y) = \text{const}$$

So $\phi = Ux$. Equipotentials are $x = $ const — vertical lines. ✓

**Step 3 — Verify C-R:**

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = U = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \quad \checkmark \qquad \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = 0 = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \quad \checkmark$$

Streamlines and equipotentials are perpendicular. ✓

**Step 4 — Vorticity check:**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 0 - 0 = 0 \quad \text{(irrotational)} \checkmark$$

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Worked Example: Source/Sink

A 2D radial source at origin has $V_r = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi r},\ V_\theta = 0$.

**Stream function (polar form):**

$$V_\theta = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial r} = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \psi = \psi(\theta)$$

$$V_r = \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = \frac{Q}{2\pi r} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial \theta} = \frac{Q}{2\pi}$$

$$\psi = \frac{Q}{2\pi}\theta + \text{const}$$

Streamlines are radial lines $\theta = $ const. ✓

**Velocity potential:**

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial r} = \frac{Q}{2\pi r} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \phi = \frac{Q}{2\pi}\ln r + \text{const}$$

Equipotentials are concentric circles $r = $ const. ✓

**Orthogonality:** Radial lines and concentric circles are perpendicular at every intersection. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — Distinguishing the Three Flow Lines

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Fluid Flow Visualisation] --> B{Is the flow steady?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Streamlines = Pathlines = Streaklines]
    B -- No --> D[Three distinct curves exist]
    D --> E[Streamline: tangent to V at one instant]
    D --> F[Pathline: trajectory of one particle over time]
    D --> G[Streakline: locus of particles from one fixed point]
    E --> H[Engineering use: snapshot of flow]
    F --> I[Engineering use: Lagrangian particle tracking]
    G --> J[Engineering use: dye or smoke visualisation]
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Relationship Between Stream Function and Velocity Potential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[2D Incompressible Flow] --> B{Is flow irrotational?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Stream function psi exists]
    B -- Yes --> D[Velocity potential phi exists]
    C --> E[Cauchy-Riemann Equations]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Both satisfy Laplace equation]
    F --> G[Equipotential lines phi = const]
    F --> H[Streamlines psi = const]
    G --> I[Orthogonal Flow Net]
    H --> I
    B -- No --> J[Only psi exists]
    J --> K[Streamlines still defined]
    J --> L[No velocity potential]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Flow Net Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Input: Boundary geometry of flow domain] --> P2[Identify inlet and outlet boundaries]
    P2 --> P3[Sketch initial streamlines along physical walls]
    P3 --> P4[Sketch perpendicular equipotential lines]
    P4 --> P5{Quality check: curvilinear squares?}
    P5 -- No --> P6[Adjust streamlines to equalise cell sizes]
    P6 --> P4
    P5 -- Yes --> P7[Count number of flow channels Nf]
    P5 -- Yes --> P8[Count number of equipotential drops Nd]
    P7 --> P9[Compute discharge Q using flow net formula]
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10[Output: seepage discharge, velocity, pressure head]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Streamline Pattern Around a Cylinder (Conceptual)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Flow["Steady uniform flow from left to right around cylinder"]
        A1[Inlet streamline at top] --> B1[Curves over cylinder]
        B1 --> C1[Reattaches downstream]
        A2[Inlet streamline at bottom] --> B2[Curves under cylinder]
        B2 --> C2[Reattaches downstream]
    end
    D[Central body: solid cylinder] -.Tangential.-> B1
    D -.Tangential.-> B2
```

### 4.5 Schematic — Orthogonal Nature of a Flow Net

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Streamline psi equals constant 1] --- S2[Streamline psi equals constant 2] --- S3[Streamline psi equals constant 3]
    E1[Equipotential phi equals constant 1] -.Perpendicular.-> S1
    E1 -.intersects.-> S2
    E1 -.intersects.-> S3
    E2[Equipotential phi equals constant 2] -.Perpendicular.-> S1
    E2 -.intersects.-> S2
    E2 -.intersects.-> S3
    E3[Equipotential phi equals constant 3] -.Perpendicular.-> S1
    E3 -.intersects.-> S2
    E3 -.intersects.-> S3
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between streamlines, path lines and streak lines. When do they coincide?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Streamline | Pathline | Streakline |
|---|---|---|---|
| Definition | Curve tangent to velocity vector at a given instant | Trajectory of a single fluid particle over time | Locus of all fluid particles that have passed through a fixed point |
| Time basis | One instant | Time interval | All past time |
| Particle count | All particles on it move along it | One particle tracked | All particles that ever passed through the marked point |

**Coincidence condition:** All three are **identical** when the flow is **steady** (i.e., when $\partial \vec{V}/\partial t = 0$). For unsteady flow, all three are distinct curves.

#### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Define stream function and velocity potential. State the conditions under which each exists.**

**Model Answer:**

**Stream function $\psi(x, y, t)$:** A scalar function whose partial derivatives yield the velocity components in 2D incompressible flow:

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \qquad v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$$

**Existence conditions for $\psi$:**
- Flow must be **2-dimensional** (or axisymmetric 2D).
- Fluid must be **incompressible** (so that continuity holds).
- Streamlines are lines of constant $\psi$.

**Velocity potential $\phi(x, y, t)$:** A scalar function whose gradient equals the velocity vector:

$$\vec{V} = \nabla \phi, \quad \text{i.e.,} \quad u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x},\ v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}$$

**Existence conditions for $\phi$:**
- Flow must be **irrotational** (vorticity $\omega_z = 0$).
- The region must be **simply connected** (no holes).
- Fluid is usually incompressible, so $\phi$ satisfies Laplace's equation.

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module 2 — Internal Choice Pattern**
> Answer **any one** of the following:

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the Cauchy–Riemann equations for a 2D, irrotational, incompressible flow. Hence show that both the stream function and the velocity potential satisfy Laplace's equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a 2D flow, the stream function is given by $\psi = 3xy$. Determine:
- (i) The velocity components $u$ and $v$.
- (ii) The velocity at the point $(1, 2)$.
- (iii) Whether the flow is rotational or irrotational.
- (iv) The value of the velocity potential, if it exists. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Definitions:**

For 2D incompressible irrotational flow, both $\phi(x,y)$ and $\psi(x,y)$ exist:

$$u = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} \qquad (1)$$

$$v = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} \qquad (2)$$

These two pairs of equalities are the **Cauchy–Riemann equations**.

**Step 2 — Laplace equation for $\phi$:**

Differentiate (1) w.r.t. $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} = \frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y}$$

Differentiate (2) w.r.t. $y$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial y^{2}} = -\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial y\,\partial x}$$

Add both:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial y^{2}} = \frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} - \frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x\,\partial y} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\nabla^{2}\phi = 0\;}$$

**Step 3 — Laplace equation for $\psi$:**

Differentiate (1) w.r.t. $y$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial y\,\partial x} = \frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial y^{2}}$$

Differentiate (2) w.r.t. $x$:

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\phi}{\partial x\,\partial y} = -\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x^{2}}$$

Equate (mixed partials are equal):

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial y^{2}} = -\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x^{2}}$$

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial x^{2}} + \frac{\partial^{2}\psi}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$$

$$\boxed{\;\nabla^{2}\psi = 0\;}$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given $\psi = 3xy$.

**(i) Velocity components:**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 3x$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -3y$$

**(ii) Velocity at (1, 2):**

$$u = 3(1) = 3, \quad v = -3(2) = -6$$

$$\vec{V} = 3\hat{i} - 6\hat{j}\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

$$|\vec{V}| = \sqrt{3^{2} + (-6)^{2}} = \sqrt{9 + 36} = \sqrt{45} \approx 6.71\ \mathrm{m/s}$$

**(iii) Rotational or irrotational?**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = \frac{\partial(-3y)}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial(3x)}{\partial y} = 0 - 0 = 0$$

Since $\omega_z = 0$, the flow is **irrotational**. ✓

**(iv) Velocity potential:**

$$d\phi = \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}dx + \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}dy = u\,dx + v\,dy = 3x\,dx - 3y\,dy$$

Integrating:

$$\phi = \frac{3x^{2}}{2} - \frac{3y^{2}}{2} + C = \frac{3}{2}(x^{2} - y^{2}) + C$$

**Check C–R:** $\partial\phi/\partial x = 3x = u$ ✓, $\partial\phi/\partial y = -3y = v$ ✓.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]*

**(a)** Explain the construction and properties of a flow net. Mention its applications in civil engineering. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2D incompressible flow is described by the velocity field:
$$u = 2xy, \qquad v = x^{2} - y^{2}$$
Determine the stream function and the velocity potential (if it exists). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Definition of a Flow Net:**

A flow net is a graphical representation of a 2D steady irrotational flow consisting of two orthogonal families of curves:
- **Streamlines** (lines of constant $\psi$)
- **Equipotential lines** (lines of constant $\phi$)

**Construction steps:**

1. Draw the flow region to scale, marking all impermeable boundaries (which are themselves streamlines) and entry/exit surfaces (equipotentials).
2. Sketch 3–4 streamlines beginning from the upstream boundary and ending at the downstream boundary, following the contour of solid walls.
3. Sketch equipotential lines **perpendicular** to streamlines, beginning from the upstream face and ending at the downstream face.
4. Adjust the curves so that the network consists of **curvilinear squares** (length ≈ width, diagonals nearly perpendicular).
5. Count the number of flow channels $N_f$ (between adjacent streamlines) and equipotential drops $N_d$ (between adjacent equipotentials).

**Properties of a Flow Net:**

1. Streamlines and equipotentials are **mutually orthogonal** at every intersection.
2. The quantity of flow in each flow channel is the same.
3. The head loss between any two adjacent equipotentials is the same: $\Delta h = h_{total}/N_d$.
4. The flow net consists of curvilinear "squares" if properly drawn.
5. Boundary conditions are automatically satisfied: impermeable walls are streamlines; entry/exit faces are equipotentials.

**Applications in Civil Engineering:**

- Seepage analysis under **hydraulic structures** (dams, weirs, barrages).
- Seepage through **earth dams** and **embankments**.
- Flow around **sheet pile walls** in cofferdams.
- Design of **drainage blankets** and **filter layers**.
- Groundwater flow toward **wells** and **drainage trenches**.
- Seepage pressures and uplift forces beneath concrete dams.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

Given: $u = 2xy,\ v = x^{2} - y^{2}$.

**Step 1 — Verify continuity (must hold for $\psi$ to exist):**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 2y + (-2y) = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

Good, the flow is incompressible (2D).

**Step 2 — Determine $\psi$:**

$$u = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 2xy \;\Rightarrow\; \psi = xy^{2} + f(x)$$

$$v = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -y^{2} - f'(x) = x^{2} - y^{2}$$

So $f'(x) = -x^{2}$, hence $f(x) = -\dfrac{x^{3}}{3} + C$.

$$\boxed{\;\psi = xy^{2} - \frac{x^{3}}{3} + C\;}$$

**Step 3 — Check irrotationality:**

$$\omega_z = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} = 2x - 2x = 0 \quad \checkmark$$

So the flow **is** irrotational, and the velocity potential exists.

**Step 4 — Determine $\phi$:**

$$d\phi = u\,dx + v\,dy = 2xy\,dx + (x^{2} - y^{2})\,dy$$

Exact differential test: $\partial u/\partial y = 2x = \partial v/\partial x$ ✓ (so $\phi$ exists).

Integrate $u$ w.r.t. $x$:

$$\phi = x^{2}y + g(y)$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $y$ and equate to $v$:

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = x^{2} + g'(y) = x^{2} - y^{2}$$

$$g'(y) = -y^{2} \;\Rightarrow\; g(y) = -\frac{y^{3}}{3} + K$$

$$\boxed{\;\phi = x^{2}y - \frac{y^{3}}{3} + K\;}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Cauchy–Riemann:**

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x} = 2xy = \frac{\partial \psi}{\partial y} = 2xy \quad \checkmark$$

$$\frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y} = x^{2} - y^{2} = -\frac{\partial \psi}{\partial x} = -(-x^{2}) = x^{2} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Always check continuity first** before claiming that $\psi$ exists. Students often blindly integrate $u$ w.r.t. $y$ without verifying incompressibility.
> 2. **Verify C–R equations** at the end. A common mistake is missing the **negative sign** in $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.
> 3. For **velocity potential**, perform the **exact-differential check** $\partial u/\partial y = \partial v/\partial x$ to confirm integrability. Don't assume $\phi$ exists just because the question says so.
> 4. **Do not forget the constant of integration** $C$ (or $K$) — it carries a half-mark in KTU valuation.
> 5. In flow net problems, mark the **direction of flow** with arrows on streamlines and **head loss direction** with arrows on equipotentials. Omitting arrows usually costs one mark.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] A **streamline** is tangent to the velocity vector at a given instant; **pathline** is the trajectory of a particle; **streakline** is the locus of all particles through a fixed point.
- [x] In **steady flow**, all three coincide. In **unsteady flow**, they are different.
- [x] The **stream function** $\psi$ exists for any **2D incompressible** flow: $u = \partial\psi/\partial y$, $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$.
- [x] The **velocity potential** $\phi$ exists only for **irrotational** flow: $u = \partial\phi/\partial x$, $v = \partial\phi/\partial y$.
- [x] When both exist, they satisfy **Cauchy–Riemann equations** and **Laplace's equation**: $\nabla^{2}\phi = 0$, $\nabla^{2}\psi = 0$.
- [x] **Discharge per unit depth** between two streamlines = $\psi_2 - \psi_1$.
- [x] **Streamlines and equipotentials are orthogonal.**
- [x] **Irrotationality** (in 2D): $\omega_z = \partial v/\partial x - \partial u/\partial y = 0$.
- [x] A **flow net** is an orthogonal mesh of streamlines and equipotentials forming curvilinear squares; used in **seepage analysis** under dams, sheet piles, and through earth structures.
- [x] Always remember the **negative sign** in $v = -\partial\psi/\partial x$ — the most common error in KTU exams.
- [x] **Polar forms** of $\psi$ and $\phi$ are essential for source/sink, vortex, and doublet problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamics of Fluid Flow: Euler's and Bernoulli's Equation of Motion, Application of Bernoulli's Equation (Venturi Meter, Orifice Meter, Nozzle Meter and Pitot Tube)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Dynamics of Fluid Flow: Euler's and Bernoulli's Equation of Motion, Application of Bernoulli's Equation (Venturi Meter, Orifice Meter, Nozzle Meter and Pitot Tube)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Dynamics of Fluid Flow

**Fluid Dynamics** is the branch of fluid mechanics that studies fluids (liquids and gases) in motion. While **fluid statics** deals with fluids at rest, fluid dynamics focuses on the forces, velocities, accelerations, and energy transformations of moving fluid particles. In KTU 2024 terminology, this is termed the **"Dynamics of Fluid Flow"** — the study that links the pressure, velocity, and elevation of a fluid particle as it moves along a path.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The dynamics of fluid flow is governed by three landmark equations:
> 1. **Euler's Equation of Motion** (Differential form of energy along a streamline)
> 2. **Bernoulli's Equation** (Integrated form of Euler's equation for incompressible, steady, inviscid flow)
> 3. **Modified Bernoulli's Equation** (Real fluid form with hydraulic losses and energy additions)

---

## 1.2 Euler's Equation of Motion — Formal Definition

**Euler's Equation of Motion** is the fundamental differential equation that relates the **pressure gradient**, **gravity body force**, and **fluid acceleration** for a fluid particle moving along a **streamline**. It is derived by applying **Newton's Second Law of Motion** to a fluid element in an inviscid (frictionless) flow field.

For a fluid element moving along a streamline:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho g \sin\theta - \rho a_s$$

Where:
- $\frac{\partial P}{\partial s}$ = Pressure gradient along the streamline (Pa/m)
- $\rho$ = Mass density of the fluid (**kg/m³**)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity (**9.81 m/s²**)
- $\theta$ = Angle of the streamline with the horizontal
- $a_s$ = Acceleration of the fluid particle along the streamline (m/s²)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Intuition (Streamline Analogy):**
> Imagine you are a tiny "fluid ant" riding on a streamline (a path that a fluid particle follows). As you move along this curve, three things affect the **pressure change** you feel:
> 1. **Gravity** pulls you downward (acts like an elevator descending a slope).
> 2. **Acceleration** of the flow makes you feel heavier or lighter (Newton's law).
> 3. The **streamline slope** modifies the gravity effect.
>
> Euler's equation precisely balances these three effects at every instant!

---

## 1.3 Bernoulli's Equation — Formal Definition

**Bernoulli's Equation** is the **integrated form** of Euler's equation of motion, valid along a **single streamline** for:
- **Steady flow** (velocities do not change with time)
- **Incompressible flow** (constant density)
- **Inviscid flow** (no viscous friction)
- **Irrotational flow** (no net rotation of fluid particles)

Mathematically:

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \text{Constant (along a streamline)}}$$

Or in the **energy head form**:

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = H \text{ (Total Energy Head in metres)}}$$

Or in the **pressure form**:

$$\boxed{P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{Constant (in Pa or N/m²)}}$$

Where:
- $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ = **Pressure head** (m)
- $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ = **Velocity head** or **Kinetic energy head** (m)
- $z$ = **Elevation head** or **Potential energy head** (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Intuition (Water Slide Analogy):**
> Think of Bernoulli's equation like a **roller-coaster water slide** at a water park. As you slide down:
> - Your **height (z)** decreases, but your **speed (V)** increases.
> - The **pressure** in the slide also changes as the cross-section changes.
> - The **total energy** (height + speed-pressure energy) remains **constant** (ignoring friction)!
>
> Bernoulli's equation is the **"conservation of energy"** statement for ideal fluid flow.

---

## 1.4 Physical Significance of Each Term

| Term | Physical Meaning | Energy Form | Units |
|------|------------------|-------------|-------|
| $z$ | Height above datum | Potential energy per unit weight | m |
| $\frac{P}{\rho g}$ | Flow pressure | Pressure energy per unit weight | m |
| $\frac{V^2}{2g}$ | Flow velocity | Kinetic energy per unit weight | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** In KTU examination answers, you **MUST** clearly mention that Bernoulli's equation represents the **conservation of mechanical energy** of an ideal fluid particle along a streamline. Marks are specifically awarded for this conceptual statement.

---

## 1.5 Assumptions & Limitations of Bernoulli's Equation

| S.No. | Assumption | Reason |
|-------|------------|--------|
| 1 | **Steady flow** | $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ so flow is time-invariant |
| 2 | **Incompressible flow** | $\rho$ = constant, so integration is possible |
| 3 | **Inviscid (frictionless) flow** | $\mu = 0$, so no shear stresses |
| 4 | **Flow along a streamline** | Equation derived for a single fluid particle path |
| 5 | **No shaft work** | No pumps/turbines between sections |
| 6 | **No heat transfer** | Adiabatic process, energy is mechanical only |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Applying Bernoulli's equation to **turbulent flow in rough pipes** without accounting for head loss. The KTU 2024 paper has repeatedly asked about the **Modified Bernoulli's Equation** which includes head loss $(h_L)$ and pump head $(H_p)$:
> $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 + H_p = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

---

## 1.6 Real-World Applications Overview

Bernoulli's equation is the **workhorse** of fluid flow measurement devices. The four key KTU-relevant devices are:

| Device | Working Principle | Typical Use |
|--------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Venturi Meter** | Convergent-divergent throat creates pressure drop | Large pipe flow measurement (water supply) |
| **Orifice Meter** | Sudden constriction via a sharp-edged plate | Industrial flow measurement |
| **Nozzle Meter** | Convergent nozzle at the end of a pipe | High-velocity jet measurement |
| **Pitot Tube** | Stagnation pressure measurement | Point velocity measurement (aircraft, rivers) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These four devices appear in **almost every KTU university examination** in Module 2. A solid conceptual understanding of each is mandatory for scoring above 80% marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — From Euler to Bernoulli

### 2.1.1 Concept of Streamline, Pathline, and Streakline

Before deriving Euler's equation, it is essential to understand the **streamline** — the curve traced by a single fluid particle, or more precisely, a line whose tangent at every point is in the direction of the velocity vector at that instant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Properties of a Streamline:**
> 1. No fluid crosses a streamline.
> 2. Streamlines are **always parallel to the velocity vector**.
> 3. In **steady flow**, streamlines = pathlines = streaklines.
> 4. Streamlines can **never intersect** (intersection would imply two velocities at one point).

### 2.1.2 Acceleration Along a Streamline

The fluid particle accelerates as it moves along a curved, non-uniform streamline. The total acceleration has two components:

1. **Local (Temporal) Acceleration:** $\frac{\partial V}{\partial t}$ — Zero in steady flow.
2. **Convective (Spatial) Acceleration:** $V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$ — Always present in non-uniform flow.

Thus, the **total acceleration** along a streamline is:

$$a_s = V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

This is the cornerstone substitution used in deriving Euler's equation.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.2.1 Master Equations

| Equation Name | Formula | Validity | KTU Marks Weightage |
|---------------|---------|----------|---------------------|
| **Euler's Equation (General form)** | $\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} = \rho g \sin\theta - \rho a_s$ | All flows along streamline | 2 marks |
| **Euler's Equation (In terms of V)** | $\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = 0$ | Steady, incompressible | 4 marks |
| **Bernoulli's Equation (Head form)** | $\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = C$ | Steady, incompressible, inviscid, along streamline | 6 marks |
| **Bernoulli's Equation (Pressure form)** | $P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2 + \rho g z = C$ | Same as above | 4 marks |
| **Modified Bernoulli's Equation** | $\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$ | Real fluid flow | 4 marks |

### 2.2.2 Application Formula Sheet — Flow Measurement Devices

| Device | Discharge Equation | Coefficient of Discharge ($C_d$) Range | Typical Application |
|--------|---------------------|------------------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Venturi Meter** | $Q = C_d \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | $0.95$ to $0.99$ | Large pipes, water distribution |
| **Orifice Meter** | $Q = C_d \frac{A_0 A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_0^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | $0.60$ to $0.65$ | Industrial flow metering |
| **Nozzle Meter** | $Q = C_d \frac{A_n A_1}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_n^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$ | $0.95$ to $0.99$ | High-velocity jet applications |
| **Pitot Tube** | $V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$ | $0.98$ to $1.00$ | Point velocity measurement |

Where:
- $A_1$ = Cross-sectional area at the **inlet (upstream)**
- $A_2$, $A_0$, $A_n$ = Cross-sectional area at the **throat/orifice/nozzle**
- $h$ = Difference in piezometric head (m) = $\left(\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + z_1\right) - \left(\frac{P_2}{\rho g} + z_2\right)$
- $C_d$ = Coefficient of discharge (dimensionless)
- $C_v$ = Coefficient of velocity (dimensionless)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick — The Universal Discharge Formula:**
> All three (Venturi, Orifice, Nozzle) share the **same mathematical structure**:
> $$Q = C_d \frac{A_1 A_x}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_x^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$
> where $A_x$ is the **throat area**. The only difference is the numerical value of $C_d$!

### 2.2.3 Pitot Tube Specific Formulae

| Condition | Velocity Equation |
|-----------|-------------------|
| **Ideal Pitot Tube** ($C_v = 1$) | $V = \sqrt{2gh}$ |
| **Real Pitot Tube** | $V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$ |
| **Stagnation Pressure** | $P_0 = P + \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ |
| **Dynamic Pressure** | $q = \frac{1}{2}\rho V^2$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Pitot-static tube** measures both stagnation and static pressure. It is the standard instrument on **aircraft** (airspeed indicator), in **wind tunnels**, and in **open channel flow** (river velocity profiling).

---

## 2.3 Why Bernoulli's Equation is the Heart of Fluid Mechanics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Energy Triangle of Bernoulli's Equation:**
> Every fluid particle carries three types of mechanical energy. As the particle moves, these three energies **interconvert**, but their **sum remains constant** (in ideal flow).
>
> - **Pressure Energy** $\left(\frac{P}{\rho g}\right)$ → Energy due to molecular motion in a confined space
> - **Kinetic Energy** $\left(\frac{V^2}{2g}\right)$ → Energy due to bulk motion
> - **Potential Energy** $(z)$ → Energy due to elevation above a datum
>
> **KTU Real-World Applications:**
> 1. **Aerofoil lift** on aircraft wings (faster air on top = lower pressure = lift)
> 2. **Carburettor** in old cars (venturi effect draws fuel into the air stream)
> 3. **Chimney design** (shorter chimneys need wind-induced pressure differences)
> 4. **Bunsen burner** (gas pressure drops as it accelerates through the nozzle)
> 5. **Sailing boats** (Bernoulli effect on curved sails)
> 6. **Water supply lines** (Venturi meters for billing)

---

## 2.4 Logical Flow of the Theory

| Step | Concept | KTU Board "Why & How" |
|------|---------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Define streamline | Why: A streamline is the natural path of a fluid particle. How: Tangent at any point gives velocity direction. |
| 2 | Apply Newton's 2nd Law to fluid element | Why: We need to relate forces to acceleration. How: $\Sigma F = m \cdot a$ on a small fluid element. |
| 3 | Sum pressure + gravity forces | Why: Pressure acts on the surfaces; gravity acts on the body. How: Resolve into streamline direction. |
| 4 | Get Euler's equation | Why: This is the differential form of the momentum equation. How: Substitute $a_s = V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$. |
| 5 | Integrate along streamline | Why: Convert differential to usable form. How: Assume $\rho$ = constant, integrate term by term. |
| 6 | Get Bernoulli's equation | Why: The integrated form is the energy conservation statement. How: The constant of integration is the "total head". |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Equation of Motion

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Diagram of a Fluid Element

Consider a small fluid element of:
- Cross-sectional area = $dA$
- Length along streamline = $ds$
- Mass = $\rho \cdot dA \cdot ds$

The element is located at a streamline angle $\theta$ to the horizontal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Textual Setup:** Imagine a tiny cylindrical "fluid parcel" floating along the curved streamline. The angle $\theta$ is the slope of the streamline with respect to the horizontal at that instant.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify Forces on the Element**

Forces acting along the streamline direction ($s$-direction):

1. **Pressure force on the upstream face:** $+P \cdot dA$ (acts in the $+s$ direction, pushing the element forward)
2. **Pressure force on the downstream face:** $-\left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds\right) dA$ (acts in the $-s$ direction, opposing motion)
3. **Weight component along streamline:** The weight of the element is $W = \rho g \, dA \, ds$. The component along the streamline (acting downward along the slope) is $-\rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \sin\theta$.

**Step 2: Apply Newton's Second Law**

$$\Sigma F_s = m \cdot a_s$$

Substituting the forces:

$$P \, dA - \left(P + \frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds\right) dA - \rho g \, dA \, ds \cdot \sin\theta = (\rho \, dA \, ds) \cdot a_s$$

**Step 3: Simplify**

The $P \, dA$ terms cancel. Dividing throughout by $dA$:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds - \rho g \, ds \cdot \sin\theta = \rho \, ds \cdot a_s$$

Dividing throughout by $ds$:

$$-\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} - \rho g \sin\theta = \rho a_s$$

**Step 4: Rearrange to the Standard Form**

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho g \sin\theta + \rho a_s = 0}$$

This is **Euler's Equation of Motion** along a streamline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The negative signs indicate that pressure and gravity *oppose* the forward motion. The acceleration $a_s$ is a *resultant* effect on the right-hand side. When the element accelerates in the $+s$ direction, the net force must also be in the $+s$ direction, which is why we bring everything to the LHS.

### 3.1.3 Expressing in Terms of Velocity

Using the chain rule for the convective acceleration:

$$a_s = V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s}$$

Substituting in Euler's equation:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \sin\theta = 0$$

Now, from the geometry of the streamline:

$$\sin\theta = \frac{\partial z}{\partial s}$$

where $z$ is the elevation of the streamline element above a horizontal datum.

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = 0}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Bernoulli's Equation from Euler's Equation

### 3.2.1 The Integration Strategy

We begin with:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} = 0$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** This is a differential equation in $s$. To convert it to a usable algebraic equation, we **integrate** each term with respect to $s$ between two points (1) and (2) along the same streamline.

Multiplying both sides by $ds$:

$$\frac{\partial P}{\partial s} ds + \rho V \frac{\partial V}{\partial s} ds + \rho g \frac{\partial z}{\partial s} ds = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$dP + \rho V \, dV + \rho g \, dz = 0$$

### 3.2.2 Integration Step 1 — Assumption of Incompressibility

For **incompressible flow**, $\rho = \text{constant}$. Therefore $\rho$ can be taken outside the integration:

$$dP + \rho V \, dV + \rho g \, dz = 0$$

### 3.2.3 Integration Step 2 — Term-by-Term Integration

Integrate each term between section (1) and section (2) along the streamline:

$$\int_{P_1}^{P_2} dP + \rho \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V \, dV + \rho g \int_{z_1}^{z_2} dz = 0$$

**Evaluating each integral:**

$$\left[ P \right]_{P_1}^{P_2} + \rho \left[ \frac{V^2}{2} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} + \rho g \left[ z \right]_{z_1}^{z_2} = 0$$

**Substituting the limits:**

$$(P_2 - P_1) + \rho \left( \frac{V_2^2}{2} - \frac{V_1^2}{2} \right) + \rho g (z_2 - z_1) = 0$$

### 3.2.4 Integration Step 3 — Rearrange to the Standard Form

Rearranging (bringing all terms to the LHS):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_1^2 + \rho g z_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho V_2^2 + \rho g z_2$$

Or, expressing it as a single constant:

$$\boxed{P + \frac{1}{2} \rho V^2 + \rho g z = \text{Constant}}$$

### 3.2.5 The Head Form (Dividing by $\rho g$)

Dividing the entire equation by $\rho g$:

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = \frac{\text{Constant}}{\rho g} = H$$

$$\boxed{\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z = H \text{ (Total Head in metres)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** Always write the equation in **both** forms:
> 1. **Pressure form** (in Pa) for energy calculations
> 2. **Head form** (in m) for engineering practice (used in pipe flow, channels, etc.)

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Detailed Derivation of Application Equations

### 3.3.1 Venturi Meter — Complete Derivation

**Setup:** A Venturi meter consists of a **convergent section** (from $A_1$ to $A_2$) followed by a **divergent section** (back to $A_1$). A differential manometer measures the pressure drop between the inlet and the throat.

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation between Inlet (1) and Throat (2)**

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 2: Express Pressure Difference as Manometric Head $h$**

For a horizontal Venturi meter ($z_1 = z_2$):

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} = h \text{ (manometric head)}$$

**Step 3: Apply Continuity Equation**

$$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2 \implies V_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} V_2$$

**Step 4: Substitute and Simplify**

$$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{1}{2g} \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 V_2^2$$

$$h = \frac{V_2^2}{2g} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]$$

$$V_2^2 = \frac{2gh}{1 - (A_2/A_1)^2} = \frac{2gh \, A_1^2}{A_1^2 - A_2^2}$$

**Step 5: Solve for Theoretical Discharge**

$$Q_{th} = A_2 V_2 = A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2gh \, A_1^2}{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} = \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}$$

**Step 6: Introduce Coefficient of Discharge**

$$\boxed{Q_{actual} = C_d \frac{A_1 A_2}{\sqrt{A_1^2 - A_2^2}} \sqrt{2gh}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The derivation for **Orifice Meter** and **Nozzle Meter** follows the **exact same steps**, with only the area at section 2 changing ($A_0$ for orifice, $A_n$ for nozzle) and the value of $C_d$ changing!

### 3.3.2 Pitot Tube — Complete Derivation

**Setup:** A Pitot tube is a small open tube bent at 90°. One opening faces the flow (stagnation point), the other is on the side (static pressure point).

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation between the Free Stream and the Stagnation Point**

At the free stream (point 1): Pressure $= P$, Velocity $= V$
At the stagnation point (point 2): Pressure $= P_0$, Velocity $= 0$

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_0}{\rho g} + \frac{0^2}{2g} + z_2$$

**Step 2: Assume Horizontal Flow** ($z_1 = z_2$)

$$\frac{P}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0}{\rho g}$$

**Step 3: Solve for Velocity**

$$\frac{V^2}{2g} = \frac{P_0 - P}{\rho g} = h \text{ (manometric head)}$$

$$\boxed{V = \sqrt{2gh}}$$

**Step 4: Real Fluid Correction**

$$V = C_v \sqrt{2gh}$$

where $C_v$ = Coefficient of velocity (typically 0.98 to 1.00).

### 3.3.3 Key Difference Table — Why Each Device Has a Different $C_d$

| Device | Reason for Lower $C_d$ | Typical $C_d$ |
|--------|------------------------|---------------|
| **Venturi Meter** | Very gradual contraction, no separation, minimal losses | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| **Orifice Meter** | Sudden contraction causes flow separation and turbulence | 0.60 – 0.65 |
| **Nozzle Meter** | Smooth contraction, less separation than orifice | 0.95 – 0.99 |
| **Pitot Tube** | Frictionless stagnation, no vena contracta | 0.98 – 1.00 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Frequently Asked Question (FAQu):**
> *"Why does an orifice meter have a much lower coefficient of discharge than a Venturi meter?"*
>
> **Model Answer:** The orifice meter has a **sharp-edged plate** that causes **sudden contraction** of the flow. The fluid streamlines continue to contract **downstream of the orifice**, forming a **vena contracta**. This sudden geometric change results in significant **turbulent eddies and energy loss**. In contrast, the Venturi meter has a **smoothly curved convergent-divergent shape** that allows streamlines to contract gradually with **no flow separation**, hence minimal energy loss.

---

## 3.4 Boundary Conditions for the Derivations

| Derivation | Boundary Conditions Used |
|------------|---------------------------|
| **Euler's Equation** | (i) Inviscid flow (no shear stress), (ii) Steady flow (no local acceleration term) |
| **Bernoulli's Equation** | (i) All Euler's assumptions, (ii) Incompressible flow, (iii) Along a single streamline |
| **Venturi Meter** | (i) Steady flow, (ii) No friction loss, (iii) Horizontal meter, (iv) Incompressible fluid |
| **Orifice/Nozzle** | (i) Same as Venturi, (ii) Vena contracta effects captured by $C_d$ |
| **Pitot Tube** | (i) Incompressible, (ii) Stagnation point, (iii) Static and stagnation pressures measured |
