<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Flow Through Pipes — The Engineering Reality

When a real fluid moves through a closed conduit (pipe), it experiences **resistance** because of the **viscous interaction** between fluid layers and the **no-slip condition** at the pipe wall. This resistance manifests as a continuous drop in pressure (or energy head) along the direction of flow, which an engineer must quantify to design water supply networks, oil pipelines, HVAC ducts, and industrial process lines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **Pipe Flow** is the study of the behaviour of a real fluid in motion through a closed conduit of uniform cross-section, where the boundary is fixed and the flow is generally steady and incompressible.

---

## 1.2 The Two Archetypes of Pipe Flow — Laminar vs Turbulent

The character of flow is governed by the dimensionless **Reynolds Number ($Re$)**, named after Osborne Reynolds (1883).

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{V D}{\nu}$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = mass density of fluid (kg/m³)
- $V$ = mean velocity of flow (m/s)
- $D$ = internal diameter of pipe (m)
- $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity (N·s/m²)
- $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity (m²/s)

| Flow Regime | Reynolds Number Range | Physical Character |
|-------------|----------------------|--------------------|
| **Laminar Flow** | $Re < 2000$ | Smooth, layered, ordered |
| **Transitional Flow** | $2000 \le Re \le 4000$ | Unstable, intermittent bursts |
| **Turbulent Flow** | $Re > 4000$ | Chaotic, eddies, mixing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The numerical thresholds **2000** and **4000** are not universal absolutes but engineering conventions adopted for design safety. The actual transition is influenced by pipe roughness, inlet conditions, vibrations, and upstream disturbances.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Highway Traffic Model

Imagine a single-lane highway where every car must maintain a fixed spacing based on the car in front.

- **Laminar Flow = Disciplined Convoy:** Cars move in perfectly parallel lanes (streamlines) without crossing. If you tag one droplet with red dye, it travels in a clean straight ribbon. Velocity distribution across the pipe cross-section is a smooth **parabola**, with maximum at the centre and zero at the wall (no-slip).

- **Turbulent Flow = Rush-Hour Chaos:** Cars weave across lanes, change positions, create mini-vortices at every bump. A drop of dye instantly spreads across the entire cross-section. Velocity profile becomes much "flatter" (plug-like) and is described by a **logarithmic law** rather than a parabola.

- **Transitional Flow = First Rain on a Highway:** Drivers are uncertain — some moments orderly, then sudden bursts of chaos. Engineers **avoid** designing in this range because the head loss prediction is unreliable.

---

## 1.4 Head Loss — What is it?

When fluid travels from Section 1 to Section 2 in a pipe, the **total energy head decreases** due to:
1. **Major Losses (Friction Losses)** — distributed loss along the pipe length due to wall shear.
2. **Minor Losses (Local Losses)** — concentrated losses at fittings, bends, valves, sudden expansions/contractions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU):**
> **Head Loss ($h_L$)** is the irreversible loss of mechanical energy per unit weight of fluid as it flows through a pipe system, expressed in metres of fluid. For a horizontal pipe of uniform diameter with constant discharge, the entire head loss manifests as a **pressure drop**: $h_f = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$.

---

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in Real Engineering

- **Municipal Water Supply:** Every kilometre of 300 mm CI pipe carrying 0.05 m³/s loses a calculable head — pumps must overcome this exactly.
- **Oil & Gas:** Long-distance pipelines (often > 500 km) require careful friction loss prediction; a 1% error in $f$ can mean a 5–10% error in pumping cost over the design life.
- **HVAC & Fire-Fighting:** Designers use the Darcy-Weisbach equation as the universal standard in the International Plumbing Code.
- **Power Plant Cooling Circuits:** Minor losses at bends, valves, and pump fittings are often **larger** than friction losses in short pipe runs.

---

## 1.6 The Major vs Minor Loss Classification

| Loss Type | Cause | Typical Magnitude |
|-----------|-------|-------------------|
| **Major Loss** ($h_f$) | Wall friction along pipe length | Dominant in long pipes (L/D > 1000) |
| **Minor Loss** ($h_m$) | Flow separation at fittings | Significant in short pipes with many fittings |

> [!WARNING]
> Despite being called "minor," these losses can sum up to **50 % or more** of the total head loss in short piping systems (e.g., pump rooms, boiler feed lines). The name is historical, not quantitative.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reynolds Number — Decision-Making Tool

The Reynolds number is the most important dimensionless group in fluid mechanics. It expresses the **ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces**:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\mu V / L} = \frac{\text{Inertial Forces}}{\text{Viscous Forces}}$$

- **High $Re$:** Inertia dominates → fluid keeps moving past obstacles → turbulent.
- **Low $Re$:** Viscous forces dominate → disturbances damped quickly → laminar.

---

## 2.2 Characteristics of Laminar Flow (Hagen-Poiseuille Regime)

When $Re < 2000$, the flow is laminar and the entire physics can be solved analytically.

### Velocity Distribution:
The velocity at any radial distance $r$ from the centre is:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left( R^2 - r^2 \right)$$

This is a **parabolic (Poiseuille) profile** with:
- **Maximum velocity at centreline** ($r = 0$):
$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta P D^2}{16 \mu L}$$

- **Mean (average) velocity** (integrating across the cross-section):
$$V = \frac{u_{max}}{2} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{8 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta P D^2}{32 \mu L}$$

### Shear Stress Distribution:
Linear distribution from zero at centre to maximum at wall:
$$\tau(r) = -\frac{\Delta P}{2L} r \quad ; \quad \tau_{wall} = \frac{\Delta P R}{2L}$$

---

## 2.3 Characteristics of Turbulent Flow

When $Re > 4000$, the flow is turbulent with the following empirical features:

### Velocity Distribution:
Approximated by the **Power Law Profile** (or Prandtl's Logarithmic Law):
$$\frac{u}{u_*} = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\frac{y}{y_0}\right)$$

Where:
- $u_* = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$ is the **shear velocity** (friction velocity)
- $\kappa \approx 0.41$ is the **von Karman constant**
- $y$ is the distance from the wall
- $y_0$ depends on wall roughness

> [!NOTE]
> A simpler engineering approximation — the **1/7th Power Law** — is widely used:
> $$\frac{u}{u_{max}} = \left(\frac{y}{R}\right)^{1/7}$$
> This indicates that the velocity profile is much **flatter** than in laminar flow.

### Shear Stress Distribution:
Total wall shear is the sum of viscous and turbulent (Reynolds) stresses:
$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{viscous} + \tau_{turbulent} = (\mu + \mu_t) \frac{du}{dy}$$
Where $\mu_t$ is the eddy viscosity, much larger than $\mu$ in fully turbulent flow.

---

## 2.4 Darcy-Weisbach Equation — The Universal Head Loss Equation

Derived from dimensional analysis and confirmed by experiments, this is the **single most important equation** in closed-conduit flow:

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}}$$

Where:
- $h_f$ = head loss due to friction (m)
- $f$ = **Darcy friction factor** (dimensionless, also called Darcy-Weisbach $f$)
- $L$ = length of pipe (m)
- $V$ = mean velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $D$ = pipe internal diameter (m)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Convention:** Always use the **Darcy friction factor $f$** (also called the **Fanning factor × 4**). Some foreign textbooks use the Fanning factor $f'$ where $f = 4 f'$. For laminar flow in smooth pipes, $f = 64 / Re$.

### Relation between Darcy $f$ and Fanning $f'$:
$$f_{Darcy} = 4 \cdot f_{Fanning}$$
$$h_f = \frac{f_{Darcy} L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 f_{Fanning} L V^2}{2 g D}$$

### Determination of $f$ in Different Regimes:
1. **Laminar flow:** $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$
2. **Turbulent flow (smooth pipes):** Use **Blasius equation** ($Re < 10^5$):
$$f = \frac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$$
3. **Turbulent flow (rough pipes):** Use **Colebrook-White equation** (implicit, requires iteration):
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left[ \frac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right]$$
4. **Completely turbulent zone (high $Re$):** $f$ becomes independent of $Re$:
$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left( \frac{\varepsilon / D}{3.7} \right)$$

Where $\varepsilon$ is the **absolute roughness** of the pipe wall (mm).

---

## 2.5 Chezy's Equation — The Historical Predecessor

Proposed by the French engineer **Antoine de Chézy (1769)** for open channel flow, but equally valid for pipes:

$$\boxed{V = C \sqrt{m_i}}$$

Where:
- $V$ = mean velocity (m/s)
- $C$ = **Chezy's coefficient** (m$^{1/2}$/s) — depends on surface roughness and hydraulic radius
- $m_i$ = **hydraulic gradient** (dimensionless) = $h_f / L$

In terms of head loss:
$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}}$$

Where $R$ = **hydraulic radius** = $A / P$:
$$R = \frac{\pi D^2/4}{\pi D} = \frac{D}{4} \quad \text{(circular pipe flowing full)}$$

### Relation Between Chezy's $C$ and Darcy's $f$:

Setting $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ equal to $h_f = \dfrac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$:

$$\frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$$

Cancelling $V^2 L$ and substituting $R = D/4$:

$$\frac{f}{2 g D} = \frac{1}{C^2 \cdot D/4} = \frac{4}{C^2 D}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{8 g}{C^2} \quad \Leftrightarrow \quad C = \sqrt{\frac{8 g}{f}}}$$

---

## 2.6 Moody's Chart — The Visual Friction-Factor Database

The **Moody Chart** (1944, L.F. Moody) is a log-log plot of the Darcy friction factor $f$ against Reynolds number $Re$, with curves for different values of **relative roughness** $\varepsilon / D$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Relative Roughness** $\varepsilon / D$ is the ratio of the absolute roughness of the pipe wall to its internal diameter. For commercial steel, $\varepsilon \approx 0.046$ mm; for cast iron, $\varepsilon \approx 0.26$ mm; for drawn tubing, $\varepsilon \approx 0.0015$ mm.

### Four Distinct Zones in the Moody Chart:

| Zone | Description | Behaviour of $f$ |
|------|-------------|-------------------|
| **1. Laminar Zone** ($Re < 2000$) | Single straight line | $f = 64/Re$ (independent of roughness) |
| **2. Critical Zone** ($2000 < Re < 4000$) | Unstable, dashed lines | Unreliable, avoid design |
| **3. Transition Zone** ($4000 < Re < 10^5$) | Curves slope downward | $f$ depends on both $Re$ and $\varepsilon/D$ |
| **4. Completely Turbulent Zone** ($Re > 10^5$, high roughness) | Horizontal lines | $f$ depends only on $\varepsilon/D$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** In the **fully turbulent rough zone**, $f$ is independent of $Re$. This means once the pipe is rough enough, doubling the velocity does not change $f$ — the friction factor is purely a geometric property of the wall.

---

## 2.7 Minor Losses — Head Loss Due to Pipe Fittings (Description Only)

Minor losses occur wherever the flow pattern is disturbed locally. They are computed using a **loss coefficient $K$**:

$$\boxed{h_m = K \cdot \frac{V^2}{2 g}}$$

Where $K$ is a dimensionless coefficient depending on the fitting geometry, and $V$ is the velocity (usually downstream of the fitting).

### Common Pipe Fittings and Approximate $K$ Values:

| Fitting | Description | Typical $K$ |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| **Sudden Enlargement** | Flow from small to large pipe | $K = \left(1 - \dfrac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$ |
| **Sudden Contraction** | Flow from large to small pipe | $K \approx 0.5 \left(1 - \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}\right)$ |
| **90° Elbow (Standard)** | Sharp change in direction | $K \approx 0.9$ |
| **90° Long-Radius Bend** | Smooth curved bend | $K \approx 0.3$ |
| **45° Elbow** | Mild direction change | $K \approx 0.4$ |
| **T-Junction (line flow)** | Through-flow | $K \approx 0.6$ |
| **T-Junction (branch flow)** | Side-branch | $K \approx 1.8$ |
| **Gate Valve (fully open)** | Straight-through | $K \approx 0.15$ |
| **Globe Valve (fully open)** | Z-shaped flow path | $K \approx 10$ |
| **Foot Valve with Strainer** | Pump inlet | $K \approx 2.5$ |
| **Pipe Exit (to reservoir)** | Sudden deceleration | $K = 1.0$ |
| **Pipe Entrance (sharp-edged)** | Sudden contraction | $K = 0.5$ |
| **Pipe Entrance (re-entrant)** | Projecting inlet | $K = 1.0$ |
| **Pipe Entrance (well-rounded)** | Smooth bell-mouth | $K = 0.04$ |

### Equivalent Length Method (Alternative Representation):

Instead of using $K$, minor losses can be expressed as an **equivalent pipe length** $L_e$:
$$h_m = \frac{f L_e V^2}{2 g D} \quad \Rightarrow \quad L_e = \frac{K D}{f}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Trap:** The equivalent length method assumes $f$ is known. For iterative design problems, the student must iterate because $f$ itself depends on $Re$, which changes with the equivalent length. Use $K$-method in initial design to avoid iteration.

---

## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Formula | Unit | Applicable Regime |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------------------|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \dfrac{V D}{\nu}$ | — | All flows |
| 2 | Laminar velocity profile | $u(r) = \dfrac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$ | m/s | $Re < 2000$ |
| 3 | Max laminar velocity | $u_{max} = \dfrac{\Delta P D^2}{16 \mu L}$ | m/s | $Re < 2000$ |
| 4 | Mean laminar velocity | $V = \dfrac{\Delta P D^2}{32 \mu L}$ | m/s | $Re < 2000$ |
| 5 | Hagen-Poiseuille head loss | $h_f = \dfrac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi \rho g D^4}$ | m | $Re < 2000$ |
| 6 | Darcy friction factor (laminar) | $f = \dfrac{64}{Re}$ | — | $Re < 2000$ |
| 7 | **Darcy-Weisbach Equation** | $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | m | All regimes |
| 8 | Blasius equation | $f = \dfrac{0.316}{Re^{0.25}}$ | — | $Re < 10^5$, smooth |
| 9 | Colebrook-White equation | $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left[\dfrac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \dfrac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}}\right]$ | — | Turbulent, all roughness |
| 10 | **Chezy's Equation** | $V = C \sqrt{m_i}$ | m/s | All flows |
| 11 | Chezy head loss form | $h_f = \dfrac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$ | m | All flows |
| 12 | $C$–$f$ relation | $C = \sqrt{\dfrac{8 g}{f}}$ | m$^{1/2}$/s | All flows |
| 13 | Hydraulic radius (pipe) | $R = D/4$ | m | Circular pipe |
| 14 | **Minor Loss Formula** | $h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2 g}$ | m | Local fittings |
| 15 | Equivalent Length | $L_e = K D / f$ | m | Local fittings |
| 16 | Wall Shear Stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$ | N/m² | All turbulent |
| 17 | Pressure Drop | $\Delta P = \rho g h_f$ | Pa | All flows |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hagen-Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow in a Circular Pipe)

This is the most analytically rigorous derivation in pipe flow and a guaranteed KTU exam favourite.

### Step 1: Consider a Cylindrical Fluid Element

Take a coaxial cylindrical element of fluid of radius $r$, length $L$ inside a horizontal pipe of radius $R$. The fluid is in steady, fully-developed laminar flow.

### Step 2: Free-Body Diagram of the Element

Forces acting along the pipe axis:
1. **Pressure force on left face:** $P_1 \times \pi r^2$ (pushing right)
2. **Pressure force on right face:** $P_2 \times \pi r^2$ (pushing left)
3. **Shear force on lateral surface:** $\tau \times 2\pi r L$ (opposing motion)

### Step 3: Equilibrium Equation

For steady, fully-developed flow (no acceleration), the sum of forces = 0:

$$P_1 \pi r^2 - P_2 \pi r^2 - \tau \cdot 2\pi r L = 0$$

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^2 = \tau \cdot 2\pi r L$$

where $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$.

Solving for shear stress:

$$\tau = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L}$$

### Step 4: Apply Newton's Law of Viscosity

For a Newtonian fluid, shear stress is proportional to the velocity gradient:

$$\tau = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

The negative sign appears because velocity $u$ decreases as $r$ increases (centre is fastest).

### Step 5: Form a Differential Equation

Equating the two expressions for $\tau$:

$$\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 L} = -\mu \frac{du}{dr}$$

Rearranging:

$$du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot r \, dr$$

### Step 6: Integrate the Differential Equation

Integrating both sides from $r$ to $R$, with boundary condition $u(R) = 0$ (no-slip):

$$\int_{u}^{0} du = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_{r}^{R} r \, dr$$

$$-u = -\frac{\Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{r^2}{2} \right]_{r}^{R}$$

$$-u = -\frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)$$

$$\boxed{u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^2 - r^2)}$$

> **Logical Conversion:** The negative signs from the viscosity law and the integration limit swap cancel out, giving a positive velocity that decreases parabolically from centre to wall.

### Step 7: Maximum Velocity (At Centreline)

Setting $r = 0$:

$$u_{max} = \frac{\Delta P R^2}{4 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta P D^2}{16 \mu L}$$

### Step 8: Average Velocity (Discharge Method)

The discharge through the pipe is obtained by integrating the velocity profile across the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

Substituting $u(r)$:

$$Q = \int_0^R \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}(R^2 - r^2) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \int_0^R (R^2 r - r^3) \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left[ \frac{R^2 r^2}{2} - \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \left( \frac{R^4}{2} - \frac{R^4}{4} \right) = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2 \mu L} \cdot \frac{R^4}{4}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^4}{8 \mu L}$$

Substituting $R = D/2$:

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L}$$

### Step 9: Average Velocity and Final Form

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{Q}{\pi D^2 / 4} = \frac{4 Q}{\pi D^2}$$

$$V = \frac{4}{\pi D^2} \cdot \frac{\pi \Delta P D^4}{128 \mu L} = \frac{\Delta P D^2}{32 \mu L}$$

The mean velocity is **exactly half** the maximum velocity:

$$V = \frac{u_{max}}{2}$$

### Step 10: Express Head Loss

$$h_f = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$$

This is the famous **Hagen-Poiseuille Equation** (1839–1840):

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi \rho g D^4}}$$

> **Why it Matters:** This is the only analytical head loss equation in fluid mechanics. All other relations (Darcy, Chezy, Manning) are empirical or semi-empirical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Darcy Friction Factor for Laminar Flow

Starting from the Hagen-Poiseuille equation:

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$$

Equating with the Darcy-Weisbach form:

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2}$$

Cancelling $L$, $g$, $V$, and one $D$:

$$\frac{f}{2 D} = \frac{32 \mu}{\rho D^2}$$

$$f = \frac{64 \mu}{\rho V D} = \frac{64}{Re}$$

$$\boxed{f = \frac{64}{Re}} \quad \text{(Laminar flow only)}$$

> **Logical Conversion:** This single equation confirms that in laminar flow, friction factor depends **only on Reynolds number** — never on wall roughness. This is why a perfectly smooth and a slightly rough pipe behave identically in laminar flow.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of the Relation $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$

This is the critical link between the two most common head loss equations.

### Step 1: Start with Both Equations

Darcy-Weisbach: 
$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

Chezy: 
$$h_f = \frac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$$

### Step 2: Set Equal

$$\frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$$

### Step 3: Cancel Common Terms

Both sides have $V^2$ and $L$, so they cancel:

$$\frac{f}{2 g D} = \frac{1}{C^2 R}$$

### Step 4: Substitute Hydraulic Radius

For a circular pipe flowing full:
$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{\pi D^2 / 4}{\pi D} = \frac{D}{4}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{f}{2 g D} = \frac{1}{C^2 \cdot D / 4} = \frac{4}{C^2 D}$$

### Step 5: Simplify

Cancelling $D$ from both sides:

$$\frac{f}{2 g} = \frac{4}{C^2}$$

$$f = \frac{8 g}{C^2}$$

Inverting:

$$\boxed{C = \sqrt{\frac{8 g}{f}}}$$

> **Engineering Use:** If a problem gives Chezy's $C$ directly (typical values: $C \approx 50$ for smooth CI pipe, $C \approx 70$ for smooth concrete, $C \approx 90$ for smooth plastic), convert to $f$ first, then use the Moody chart for verification.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Head Loss from Energy Equation (Bernoulli with Losses)

### Step 1: Apply the Steady Flow Energy Equation Between Sections 1 and 2

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Where $h_L$ is the total head loss (major + minor).

### Step 2: Simplify for Horizontal Constant-Diameter Pipe

For a horizontal pipe ($z_1 = z_2$) of uniform diameter ($V_1 = V_2$):

$$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + h_L$$

$$h_L = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g}$$

### Step 3: Combine Major and Minor Losses

$$\boxed{h_L = h_f + \Sigma h_m = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} + \Sigma K_i \frac{V^2}{2 g}}$$

$$\boxed{h_L = \left[ \frac{f L}{D} + \Sigma K_i \right] \frac{V^2}{2 g}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This combined equation is the **starting point for almost every KTU numerical problem**. Always express everything in terms of $V$ before solving.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Model KTU Problem

**Problem:** A horizontal pipe of diameter 100 mm and length 50 m carries oil of dynamic viscosity $\mu = 0.1$ N·s/m² and density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³ at a velocity of 0.5 m/s. Determine (a) the Reynolds number and flow regime, (b) the friction factor, (c) the head loss using Darcy-Weisbach, and (d) verify using Hagen-Poiseuille.

### Part (a): Reynolds Number

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 0.5 \times 0.1}{0.1} = 500$$

Since $Re = 500 < 2000$, the flow is **laminar**.

### Part (b): Friction Factor

$$f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{500} = 0.128$$

### Part (c): Head Loss (Darcy-Weisbach)

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.128 \times 50 \times (0.5)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.1}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.128 \times 50 \times 0.25}{1.962} = \frac{1.6}{1.962} = 0.8155 \text{ m}$$

### Part (d): Verification (Hagen-Poiseuille)

$$h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \frac{32 \times 0.1 \times 50 \times 0.5}{1000 \times 9.81 \times (0.1)^2}$$

$$h_f = \frac{80}{98.1} = 0.8155 \text{ m}$$

✓ **Both methods give identical results**, confirming the derivation.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Laminar vs Turbulent

| Parameter | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|-----------|-------------|----------------|
| Reynolds number | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic (Poiseuille) | Logarithmic / Power-law (flatter) |
| Centreline:mean velocity | $u_{max}/V = 2.0$ | $u_{max}/V \approx 1.15 - 1.25$ |
| Wall shear stress | $\tau_w = \dfrac{8 \mu V}{D}$ | $\tau_w = \dfrac{f \rho V^2}{8}$ |
| Friction factor | $f = 64/Re$ | Moody chart / Colebrook |
| Effect of roughness | Negligible | Significant (especially at high $Re$) |
| Diffusion mechanism | Molecular only | Molecular + turbulent (eddy) |
| Energy dissipation | Viscous shear | Viscous + turbulent mixing |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**Differentiate between laminar flow and turbulent flow. State the Reynolds number ranges for both.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Laminar Flow | Turbulent Flow |
|---------|--------------|----------------|
| Reynolds number | $Re < 2000$ | $Re > 4000$ |
| Velocity profile | Parabolic (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Flat, logarithmic profile |
| Fluid layers | Streamlines parallel, no mixing | Eddies and cross-mixing present |
| Energy loss | Low, purely viscous | High, viscous + turbulent dissipation |
| Effect of roughness | Negligible | Significant |
| Friction factor | $f = 64/Re$ | Read from Moody chart |
| Flow prediction | Analytical (Hagen-Poiseuille) | Empirical (Darcy-Weisbach) |
| Centreline to mean velocity ratio | $u_{max}/V = 2$ | $u_{max}/V \approx 1.2$ |

---

### **Question 2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**State the Darcy-Weisbach equation. What is the significance of the friction factor $f$ in this equation?**

**Model Answer:**

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}}$$

**Significance of Friction Factor $f$:**
1. $f$ is a **dimensionless** quantity representing the ratio of wall shear stress to the dynamic pressure of the flow.
2. It encapsulates all the complex physics of the boundary layer into a single number.
3. $f$ depends on:
   - Reynolds number $Re$ (viscous effect)
   - Relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ (wall roughness effect)
4. For **laminar flow**: $f = 64/Re$ (analytical).
5. For **turbulent flow**: $f$ is obtained from the Moody chart or Colebrook-White equation.
6. $f$ is essentially a **calibration constant** that converts a theoretical velocity-head-based loss into an actual measured head loss.

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each)

### **QUESTION A — ALTERNATIVE 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the Hagen-Poiseuille equation for steady, laminar flow of a viscous incompressible fluid through a horizontal circular pipe. State clearly the assumptions made during the derivation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Assumptions:*
1. Steady, incompressible flow.
2. Newtonian fluid (constant viscosity).
3. Laminar regime ($Re < 2000$).
4. Pipe is horizontal and of uniform circular cross-section.
5. Flow is fully developed (velocity profile unchanging along length).
6. No-slip condition at the wall: $u = 0$ at $r = R$.
7. Axisymmetric flow — no swirl, no secondary motion.

*Derivation:* (See Section 3.1 above for full step-by-step derivation)

Final result:

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L}(R^2 - r^2)$$

$$V = \frac{\Delta P D^2}{32 \mu L}$$

$$\boxed{h_f = \frac{32 \mu L V}{\rho g D^2} = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi \rho g D^4}}$$

---

**(b) An oil of specific gravity 0.85 and dynamic viscosity 0.08 N·s/m² flows through a horizontal pipe of 150 mm diameter at a rate of 0.03 m³/s. Determine (i) the head loss per kilometre of pipe, (ii) the shear stress at the pipe wall. (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $D = 0.15$ m, $L = 1000$ m
- $\rho = 850$ kg/m³, $\mu = 0.08$ N·s/m²
- $Q = 0.03$ m³/s

**Step 1: Compute Mean Velocity**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.15)^2 = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.03}{0.01767} = 1.697 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2: Verify Flow Regime (Reynolds Number)**

$$Re = \frac{\rho V D}{\mu} = \frac{850 \times 1.697 \times 0.15}{0.08} = 2703$$

Since $2000 < Re < 4000$, the flow is in the **transitional zone**. For a conservative design treating this as laminar:

$$f = \frac{64}{Re} = \frac{64}{2703} = 0.0237$$

**Step 3: Head Loss Using Darcy-Weisbach**

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{0.0237 \times 1000 \times (1.697)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.15}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.0237 \times 1000 \times 2.88}{2.943} = \frac{68.26}{2.943} = 23.19 \text{ m per km}$$

**Step 4: Wall Shear Stress**

$$\tau_w = \frac{f \rho V^2}{8} = \frac{0.0237 \times 850 \times (1.697)^2}{8} = \frac{57.95}{8} = 7.24 \text{ N/m}^2$$

**Answers:**
- Head loss per km $\approx$ **23.2 m**
- Wall shear stress $\approx$ **7.24 N/m²**

---

### **QUESTION B — ALTERNATIVE 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a) Explain the Darcy-Weisbach equation and Chezy's equation for head loss in pipes. Show the relation between Darcy's friction factor $f$ and Chezy's coefficient $C$. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Darcy-Weisbach Equation:**

The Darcy-Weisbach equation gives the head loss due to friction in a pipe:

$$h_f = \frac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$$

where $f$ is the Darcy friction factor (dimensionless), $L$ is the length of the pipe, $V$ is the mean velocity, $D$ is the diameter, and $g$ is the acceleration due to gravity.

This equation is **universally valid** for all flow regimes. The friction factor $f$ depends on the Reynolds number and relative roughness:

- For laminar flow: $f = 64/Re$ (analytical).
- For turbulent flow: $f$ is obtained from the **Moody chart** or **Colebrook-White equation**:

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left[ \frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re \sqrt{f}} \right]$$

**Chezy's Equation:**

Chezy's equation was the first empirical formula for flow resistance, proposed in 1769:

$$V = C \sqrt{m_i}$$

where $C$ is Chezy's coefficient (m$^{1/2}$/s) and $m_i = h_f/L$ is the hydraulic gradient. Rearranged:

$$h_f = \frac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$$

where $R$ is the hydraulic radius (for circular pipe, $R = D/4$).

**Relation Between $f$ and $C$:**

Setting the two expressions for $h_f$ equal:

$$\frac{f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{V^2 L}{C^2 R}$$

Substituting $R = D/4$:

$$\frac{f}{2 g D} = \frac{4}{C^2 D}$$

Solving:

$$\boxed{C = \sqrt{\frac{8 g}{f}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad f = \frac{8 g}{C^2}}$$

This relation allows interchange between the two equations. For example, if $C = 50$ m$^{1/2}$/s:

$$f = \frac{8 \times 9.81}{(50)^2} = \frac{78.48}{2500} = 0.0314$$

---

**(b) What is the Moody's chart? Explain its significance and the different zones observed in it. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**

The **Moody Chart** (developed by Lewis F. Moody in 1944) is a log-log graphical plot of the Darcy friction factor $f$ versus the Reynolds number $Re$, with multiple curves parameterised by the relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ of the pipe wall.

**Significance:**
1. It is the **standard reference** for determining the friction factor in turbulent pipe flow where analytical solutions are impossible.
2. It encodes the entire experimental database of turbulent flow measurements in a single readable chart.
3. Used by practising engineers worldwide for quick design calculations.
4. Bypasses the need for solving the implicit Colebrook-White equation iteratively.

**The Four Zones of the Moody Chart:**

**Zone 1 — Laminar Zone ($Re < 2000$):**
A single straight line representing $f = 64/Re$. All roughness curves collapse onto this one line because viscous forces dominate completely; wall roughness has no effect.

**Zone 2 — Critical Zone ($2000 < Re < 4000$):**
This region is shown as **dashed lines** indicating that the flow is unstable and unpredictable. Engineers **avoid designing** piping systems to operate in this range. If $Re$ falls here, either reduce flow rate or increase pipe diameter to push it firmly into laminar or turbulent territory.

**Zone 3 — Transition Zone ($4000 < Re < 10^5$):**
Multiple curves branch out, each for a specific value of $\varepsilon/D$. Both Reynolds number **and** roughness influence $f$. The friction factor decreases with increasing $Re$. The Colebrook-White equation is the analytical representation.

**Zone 4 — Completely Turbulent (Rough) Zone ($Re > 10^5$ for rough pipes):**
Curves become **horizontal**, meaning $f$ is independent of $Re$ and depends only on $\varepsilon/D$. This is the limiting case where the viscous sublayer is so thin that wall roughness elements protrude through it and dominate the resistance completely.

**Steps to Use the Moody Chart:**
1. Calculate $Re = \rho V D / \mu$.
2. Determine relative roughness $\varepsilon/D$ from pipe material tables.
3. Enter the chart on the $Re$ axis, move vertically to intersect the appropriate $\varepsilon/D$ curve.
4. Read the corresponding $f$ on the vertical axis.
5. Substitute $f$ into Darcy-Weisbach equation.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Darcy $f$ with Fanning $f'$:** Many international textbooks use Fanning's friction factor where $f' = f/4$. A student copying $f = 16/Re$ in a KTU exam (where Darcy $f$ is standard) will lose all marks. **Always use $f = 64/Re$ for laminar flow.**
>
> 2. **Forgetting the kinetic energy factor $\alpha$:** In energy equations, the true kinetic energy term is $\alpha V^2/(2g)$ where $\alpha = 2$ for laminar and $\alpha \approx 1.05$ for turbulent. In KTU problems, $\alpha = 1$ is assumed unless explicitly stated otherwise.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong diameter:** External diameter, nominal diameter, and internal diameter are all different. **Always use internal diameter $D$** in friction calculations.
>
> 4. **Unit errors in Hagen-Poiseuille:** The viscosity $\mu$ must be in **N·s/m² (Pa·s)** and $\rho$ in **kg/m³**. Mixing CGS and SI units is a common blunder.
>
> 5. **Moody chart interpolation:** Students often read $f$ at the wrong point. Always locate the intersection of the vertical $Re$ line with the appropriate $\varepsilon/D$ curve and project horizontally to the $f$ axis.
>
> 6. **Sign of pressure drop:** The head loss $h_f$ is always **positive**. The pressure **decreases** in the direction of flow: $P_1 > P_2$. Drawing the energy grade line (EGL) sloping downwards is essential.
>
> 7. **Minor losses in long vs short pipes:** Don't apply minor losses in long pipelines where they are negligible (< 5% of total), but always include them in pump suction lines and equipment hookups.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3 / Pipe Flow**

### ✅ Conceptual Foundations
- [ ] Reynolds number is the **ratio of inertial to viscous forces**.
- [ ] $Re < 2000$: Laminar (parabolic profile, analytical solution exists).
- [ ] $Re > 4000$: Turbulent (logarithmic profile, Moody chart needed).
- [ ] $2000 < Re < 4000$: Transitional (avoid for design).

### ✅ Governing Equations (Memorise)
- [ ] **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $h_f = 32 \mu L V / (\rho g D^2)$ — laminar only.
- [ ] **Darcy-Weisbach:** $h_f = f L V^2 / (2 g D)$ — universal.
- [ ] **Chezy's:** $V = C \sqrt{m_i}$ — historical, still used.
- [ ] **Minor Loss:** $h_m = K V^2 / (2 g)$.
- [ ] **Colebrook-White:** $\frac{1}{\sqrt{f}} = -2 \log_{10}\left[\frac{\varepsilon/D}{3.7} + \frac{2.51}{Re\sqrt{f}}\right]$.

### ✅ Critical Numerical Values
- [ ] $g = 9.81$ m/s².
- [ ] Laminar $f = 64 / Re$.
- [ ] $u_{max} = 2V$ for laminar flow.
- [ ] $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$ and $f = 8g/C^2$.
- [ ] Hydraulic radius $R = D/4$ for full circular pipe.

### ✅ Friction Factor Determination
- [ ] **Laminar:** $f = 64/Re$.
- [ ] **Smooth turbulent ($Re < 10^5$):** Blasius $f = 0.316 / Re^{0.25}$.
- [ ] **All turbulent:** Colebrook-White (iterative) or Moody chart.
- [ ] **Completely rough turbulent:** $f$ depends only on $\varepsilon/D$.

### ✅ Minor Losses — Typical K-Values to Remember
- [ ] Sharp-edged entrance: $K = 0.5$.
- [ ] Re-entrant entrance: $K = 1.0$.
- [ ] Rounded entrance: $K = 0.04$.
- [ ] Pipe exit to reservoir: $K = 1.0$.
- [ ] Sudden enlargement: $K = (1 - A_1/A_2)^2$.
- [ ] Gate valve (open): $K = 0.15$.
- [ ] Globe valve (open): $K = 10$.
- [ ] 90° standard elbow: $K = 0.9$.

### ✅ Important Derivations (Exam Favourites)
- [ ] Hagen-Poiseuille equation derivation from first principles.
- [ ] $f = 64/Re$ proof from Hagen-Poiseuille.
- [ ] $C = \sqrt{8g/f}$ relationship.
- [ ] Combined major + minor loss equation.
- [ ] Head loss from energy equation.

### ✅ Real-World Engineering Applications
- [ ] Municipal water supply networks.
- [ ] Long-distance oil and gas pipelines.
- [ ] HVAC and fire-fighting systems.
- [ ] Pump and turbine installation design.
- [ ] Industrial process piping (chemical, power plants).

### ✅ Moody's Chart Reading Order
- [ ] Compute $Re$ and $\varepsilon/D$.
- [ ] Locate $Re$ on horizontal axis.
- [ ] Trace vertically to the curve matching $\varepsilon/D$.
- [ ] Trace horizontally to the $f$ axis.
- [ ] Plug $f$ into Darcy-Weisbach.

---

*End of Module 3 Topic Notes — Flow Through Pipes*

topics.title: Hydraulic Gradient and Total Energy Line
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic Gradient and Total Energy Line # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Three Reference Heads in Pipe Flow When a real (viscous) fluid flows through a closed conduit or open channel, the total mechanical energy of the fluid at any cross-section can be resolved into **three distinct reference heads** measured with respect to a chosen horizontal datum. > [!NOTE] > **Total Head (H)** — The sum of all energy heads possessed by the fluid at a section. Mathematically expressed as: > $$H = \frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z$$ > where $p/\gamma$ is the **pressure head**, $V^2/2g$ is the **velocity head** (kinetic head), and $z$ is the **elevation head** (datum head). | Head Type | Symbol | Physical Meaning | Indicator | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure Head | $p/\gamma$ | Energy due to fluid pressure | Piezometer tube | | Velocity Head | $V^2/2g$ | Energy due to motion | Pitot tube | | Elevation Head | $z$ | Energy due to gravity/position | Vertical ruler | ## 1.2 Formal Definition of HGL and TEL > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL)** — It is the graphical locus of the **piezometric head** $(p/\gamma + z)$ measured above a horizontal datum, plotted along the length of the flowing fluid. It represents the height to which the liquid would rise in a series of piezometric tubes connected to the pipe. > > **Total Energy Line (TEL)** — It is the graphical locus of the **total energy head** $(p/\gamma + V^2/2g + z)$ measured above the same horizontal datum. It is everywhere above the HGL by an amount equal to the local velocity head, $V^2/2g$. Mathematically: $$\boxed{\text{TEL} - \text{HGL} = \frac{V^2}{2g}}$$ The difference between the two lines at any section is the **velocity head** at that section. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Mountain River Imagine water cascading down a rocky mountain stream from a higher lake (reservoir) to a lower lake. As the water flows, it loses energy due to friction with the rocks (friction loss) and turbulence at sharp bends (minor losses). - The **water surface profile** along the stream corresponds to the **Hydraulic Gradient Line** — it tells you the "static pressure level" available at each point. - The **Total Energy Line** is an imaginary line a little *above* the water surface — the gap is the "speed" energy of the rushing water. Where the river is narrow and fast (rapids), this gap is large; where it is wide and slow (pools), the gap shrinks. - The **slope** of the TEL represents how rapidly energy is being lost to friction. A steep TEL means a lot of energy loss per unit length. > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick for KTU Board Exams**: "HGL is **H**alfway to the TEL" — HGL contains only $p/\gamma + z$, while TEL adds the velocity head $V^2/2g$ on top. So **TEL = HGL + $V^2/2g$** always. ## 1.4 Why Study HGL and TEL? Understanding the HGL and TEL allows engineers to: - **Locate cavitation zones** (where the HGL falls below the pipe crown, creating negative pressures). - **Design pump placement** to ensure sufficient NPSH (Net Positive Suction Head). - **Diagnose pipeline failures** (e.g., a sudden dip in HGL indicates blockage or high local losses). - **Design syphons, penstocks, and siphonic drainage systems**. - **Predict the behavior of branching pipe networks** supplying multiple reservoirs. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PCAGT303, Module 3)**: The Hydraulic Gradient Line and Total Energy Line are foundational for solving problems on *flow between reservoirs, syphon flow, flow through pipe systems with sudden enlargement/contraction, and pipes with turbines/pumps*. Every numerical problem in this module ultimately traces back to interpreting these two lines. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Bernoulli's Equation Modified for Real Flow For an **ideal (inviscid) fluid** along a streamline between two sections (1) and (2): $$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2$$ For a **real (viscous) fluid**, energy is lost to friction and turbulence, so a **head loss term $h_L$** must be added: $$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ The total head loss $h_L$ is the sum of: - **Major (frictional) loss** $h_f$ — distributed along the pipe length due to viscous shear. - **Minor (local) loss** $h_m$ — concentrated at fittings, valves, bends, expansions, contractions, entries, exits. ## 2.2 The Three Reference Heights at Any Pipe Section Let the pipe have diameter $D$ and be carrying fluid with velocity $V$ at a section. The following reference lines are commonly used: 1. **Pipe Centerline (CL)** — the geometric axis of the pipe. 2. **Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL)** — height of the **piezometric head** above datum: $$HGL = z + \frac{p}{\gamma}$$ 3. **Total Energy Line (TEL)** — height of the **total energy** above datum: $$TEL = z + \frac{p}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ The **vertical distance between TEL and HGL** is exactly equal to the velocity head at that section. This is a critical relationship that allows you to sketch HGL and TEL given any one of them. ## 2.3 Slopes of HGL and TEL The **hydraulic gradient** (slope of HGL) is the loss of piezometric head per unit length of pipe: $$S_H = \frac{h_L}{L} = \text{slope of HGL}$$ The **energy gradient** (slope of TEL) is the loss of total head per unit length of pipe: $$S_E = \frac{h_L}{L} = \text{slope of TEL}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Observation:** In a pipe of **constant diameter** (and hence constant velocity), the two slopes are **parallel** and **equal** in magnitude, because the velocity head $V^2/2g$ is constant and hence does not affect the gradient. The vertical separation between TEL and HGL remains constant. In a pipe of **varying diameter** (e.g., a sudden expansion), the slopes differ. At a sudden expansion, the HGL actually *rises* locally due to the loss of kinetic energy being converted to pressure energy, while the TEL drops by the total head loss $h_L$. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet) > [!NOTE] > **Print this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU board exam problem.** | # | Formula | Description | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $HGL = z + p/\gamma$ | Piezometric head | Plotted as HGL line | | 2 | $TEL = z + p/\gamma + V^2/2g$ | Total energy head | Plotted as TEL line | | 3 | $TEL - HGL = V^2/2g$ | Velocity head separation | Used to find $V$ from HGL & TEL | | 4 | $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Darcy–Weisbach friction loss | Pipe flow problems | | 5 | $h_f = \dfrac{f L V^2}{2 g D}$ | Equivalent form (some textbooks use $f$ or $4f$) | Pipe flow problems | | 6 | $h_f = \dfrac{32 f L V^2}{\pi^2 g D^5} Q^2$ | In terms of discharge $Q$ | Network problems | | 7 | $h_m = K \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$ | Minor loss (entrance, exit, fittings) | Local losses | | 8 | $h_L = h_f + \Sigma h_m$ | Total head loss | All pipe flow problems | | 9 | $\Delta p = \gamma (HGL_1 - HGL_2)$ | Pressure difference between two sections | From HGL plot | | 10 | $\text{HGL slope} = h_L / L$ | Frictional slope | For uniform pipes | > [!WARNING] > **Vertical Pipe Symbol Restriction**: In the formula sheet, the ratio $V^2/(2g)$ uses simple parentheses. If you ever write expressions like $\vert V^2/2g \vert$ or $\vert x \vert$, **always** use $\lvert x \rvert$ (with `\lvert` and `\rvert` LaTeX commands) or $\mid x \mid$ to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The HGL/TEL concept is extensively applied in: - **Water supply distribution networks** (designing pipe diameters, locating pumping stations and elevated service reservoirs). - **Hydroelectric power plants** — the TEL at the turbine inlet must be higher than the tailrace HGL by an amount equal to the net head available for power generation. - **Sewage and storm-water systems** — ensuring the HGL does not fall below the pipe invert (which would cause air entrainment and reduced flow capacity). - **Cooling water systems in thermal power plants** — preventing cavitation at pump suctions. - **Oil and gas pipelines** — verifying that the operating pressure never drops below the vapor pressure along the route (a critical HGL-based design check). # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between HGL and TEL Starting from Bernoulli's equation applied between two sections of a real fluid flow system: $$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$ **Step 1:** Rearrange by separating the static (pressure + elevation) terms from the kinetic term: $$\left(z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\gamma}\right) - \left(z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\gamma}\right) = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + h_L$$ **Step 2:** Recognize the left-hand side as the difference in **piezometric heads**, which is also the drop in the **Hydraulic Gradient Line**: $$HGL_1 - HGL_2 = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + h_L$$ **Step 3:** Apply the same rearrangement to the **total head**: $$\left(z_1 + \frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g}\right) - \left(z_2 + \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}\right) = h_L$$ **Step 4:** Recognize the left-hand side as the difference in **total energy heads**, which is the drop in the **Total Energy Line**: $$TEL_1 - TEL_2 = h_L$$ **Step 5:** Subtract the HGL drop from the TEL drop: $$(TEL_1 - TEL_2) - (HGL_1 - HGL_2) = h_L - \left(\frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g} + h_L\right)$$ This simplifies to: $$(TEL_1 - HGL_1) - (TEL_2 - HGL_2) = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g}$$ **Step 6:** If the two sections are close together, the velocity change is small, and we obtain the **fundamental relationship**: $$TEL - HGL = \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ $$\boxed{\text{Therefore, TEL is always above HGL by an amount equal to the local velocity head.}}$$ ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Behavior of HGL and TEL in Different Pipe Fittings The following analytical matrix is essential for KTU problems involving pipe networks. Study it thoroughly. | Pipe Element | Effect on HGL | Effect on TEL | Reason | |---|---|---|---| | **Straight uniform pipe** | Linear drop, slope = $h_f/L$ | Linear drop, parallel to HGL, slope = $h_f/L$ | Friction loss $h_f$ distributed uniformly; $V$ constant | | **Sudden expansion** (small to large diameter) | **Local RISE** at the expansion junction by $(V_1 - V_2)^2 / 2g$ | **DROP** by $h_L = (V_1 - V_2)^2 / 2g$ | Lost kinetic head is partly recovered as pressure rise (Borda–Carnot loss) | | **Sudden contraction** (large to small diameter) | **DROP** by $h_L = K_c V_2^2 / 2g$ | **DROP** by $h_L = K_c V_2^2 / 2g$ | Kinetic energy is lost to turbulence at the vena contracta | | **Entrance from reservoir** | **DROP** by $h_e = 0.5 V^2 / 2g$ | **DROP** by $h_e = 0.5 V^2 / 2g$ | Sudden loss of pressure head as fluid accelerates into pipe | | **Exit into reservoir** | **RISE** to reservoir surface level | **RISE** to reservoir surface level (then flat) | Kinetic head is lost ($V^2/2g$); HGL meets the free surface | | **Pipe diameter change (gradual)** | Smooth transition, parallel slopes | Smooth transition, parallel slopes | Velocity head changes, but no losses if gradual | | **Bend / Elbow** | Drop by $h_b = K_b V^2 / 2g$ | Drop by $h_b = K_b V^2 / 2g$ | Local minor loss; HGL and TEL drop together | | **Valve (fully open)** | Drop by $h_v = K_v V^2 / 2g$ | Drop by $h_v = K_v V^2 / 2g$ | Local minor loss | | **Pump in line** | **RISE** (HGL jumps up) | **RISE** by pump head $H_p$ | Pump adds energy to the fluid | | **Turbine in line** | **DROP** (HGL falls down) | **DROP** by turbine head $H_t$ | Turbine extracts energy from the fluid | ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Analysis: Flow from Reservoir A to Reservoir B Consider two reservoirs connected by a pipe of length $L$, diameter $D$, with friction factor $f$. The free surface of reservoir A is at elevation $z_A$ and reservoir B is at $z_B$, with $z_A > z_B$. **Step 1: Apply the energy equation between the two free surfaces (where $V \approx 0$ and $p = p_{atm}$):** $$z_A + \frac{p_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 = z_B + \frac{p_{atm}}{\gamma} + 0 + h_L$$ The atmospheric terms cancel: $$z_A - z_B = h_L$$ **Step 2: Express the head loss in terms of velocity using Darcy–Weisbach:** $$h_L = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} + \Sigma K \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ Let $h_L = K_{eq} \cdot \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$, where $K_{eq} = \dfrac{4 f L}{D} + \Sigma K$. **Step 3: Equate and solve for velocity:** $$z_A - z_B = K_{eq} \cdot \frac{V^2}{2g}$$ $$V = \sqrt{\frac{2g(z_A - z_B)}{K_{eq}}}$$ **Step 4: The discharge through the pipe:** $$Q = A \cdot V = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2g(z_A - z_B)}{K_{eq}}}$$ **Step 5: Plotting the HGL and TEL:** - At the **entrance of the pipe** (just inside reservoir A): HGL = $z_A - h_e$ (where $h_e = 0.5 V^2/2g$ is the entrance loss). - Along the **pipe length**: HGL drops linearly with slope $h_f / L$. - At the **exit of the pipe** (just before entering reservoir B): HGL is at some value, and TEL is above it by $V^2/2g$. - At the **free surface of reservoir B**: HGL = $z_B$ (free surface); TEL also equals $z_B$ (since the kinetic head is lost as exit loss). The **net drop in HGL** from entrance to exit = $h_e + h_f + h_m$ = total head loss. The **net drop in TEL** from entrance to exit = total head loss. ## 3.4 Step-by-Step Analysis: Sudden Expansion (Borda–Carnot Loss) When a pipe of diameter $D_1$ carrying velocity $V_1$ suddenly expands to a pipe of diameter $D_2$ with velocity $V_2$ (where $V_2 < V_1$), the head loss is: $$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$$ **Derivation (Continuity + Momentum + Bernoulli):** **Step 1:** Apply continuity: $A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$, hence $V_2 = (A_1/A_2) V_1 = (D_1/D_2)^2 V_1$. **Step 2:** Apply momentum equation in the direction of flow across the control volume (assuming uniform pressure at sections 1 and 2): $$p_1 A_2 - p_2 A_2 = \rho Q (V_2 - V_1)$$ Simplifying (since $Q = A_2 V_2$): $$\frac{p_1 - p_2}{\gamma} = \frac{2(V_2 - V_1) V_2}{g} \cdot \frac{1}{...}$$ A cleaner derivation uses the unsteady Bernoulli–momentum balance. The final result is: $$h_L = \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$$ **Step 3:** Apply the energy equation between sections 1 and 2: $$\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \frac{p_2}{\gamma} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$$ **Step 4:** Rearranging, the rise in HGL (piezometric head) across the expansion: $$\left(\frac{p_2}{\gamma} + z_2\right) - \left(\frac{p_1}{\gamma} + z_1\right) = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2}{2g} - \frac{(V_1 - V_2)^2}{2g}$$ Expanding the squares and simplifying: $$\Delta HGL = \frac{V_1^2 - V_2^2 - (V_1^2 - 2V_1 V_2 + V_2^2)}{2g} = \frac{2V_1 V_2 - 2V_2^2}{2g} = V_2(V_1 - V_2)/g$$ For $V_1 > V_2$, this is **positive**, confirming the **HGL rises** across a sudden expansion. ## 3.5 Step-by-Step Analysis: Syphon Flow A syphon is a pipe that rises above the free surface of a reservoir and then falls below it to discharge at a lower level. The highest point in the syphon is called the **summit**. **Step 1:** Apply the energy equation between the reservoir free surface (point A) and the pipe exit (point B): $$z_A = z_B + \frac{V^2}{2g} + h_L$$ (The exit is assumed to be a free jet, so $p_B = p_{atm}$ and $V_B = V$.) **Step 2:** Express the head loss: $$h_L = \left(0.5 + \frac{4fL}{D} + 1.0\right)\frac{V^2}{2g}$$ Here, the $0.5$ is the entrance loss, the $4fL/D$ is the friction loss over the full syphon length, and the $1.0$ is the exit loss (full velocity head lost). **Step 3:** Solve for velocity: $$V = \sqrt{\frac{2g(z_A - z_B)}{0.5 + \frac{4fL}{D} + 1.0 + 1.0}}$$ (Note: Some books absorb the exit loss into the energy equation; in that formulation, the denominator becomes $0.5 + \frac{4fL}{D} + 1.0$.) **Step 4:** Compute the pressure at the summit (point S) using Bernoulli between A and S: $$\frac{p_A}{\gamma} + 0 + z_A = \frac{p_S}{\gamma} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z_S$$ With $p_A = p_{atm}$ (gauge $p_A = 0$): $$\frac{p_S}{\gamma} = z_A - z_S - \frac{V^2}{2g} - h_{L, A \to S}$$ **Step 5:** Cavitation check: The pressure at the summit must remain **above the vapor pressure** of the fluid: $$\frac{p_S}{\gamma} > \frac{p_v}{\gamma}$$ If this condition is violated, vapor cavities form, the syphon **breaks**, and flow stops. This sets the maximum permissible height $z_S - z_A$ for a given discharge. ## 3.6 Step-by-Step Analysis: Pipe with Pump and Turbine A typical hydroelectric or water-supply system may have both a pump and a turbine in the same line. Applying Bernoulli from reservoir A (suction) to reservoir B (delivery): $$z_A + H_p = z_B + h_L + H_t$$ where $H_p$ is the head added by the pump, and $H_t$ is the head extracted by the turbine. For pure supply systems (no turbine), $H_t = 0$, and for pure power systems (no pump), $H_p = 0$. **Plotting HGL and TEL:** - HGL at the **pump suction**: HGL = $z_A - h_{e,A}$ (if there is an entrance loss from reservoir A). - HGL at the **pump discharge**: HGL jumps up by $H_p$ (the pump adds energy to the fluid). - HGL drops along the pipe due to friction and minor losses. - HGL at the **turbine inlet**: $TEL_{in} = HGL_{in} + V^2/2g$. - HGL at the **turbine outlet**: drops by $H_t$ (turbine extracts energy). - HGL ends at $z_B$ (reservoir B free surface). The TEL is always drawn above the HGL by $V^2/2g$ at each section. # 4. The block diagram below explains the energy transfer mechanism. ```mermaid flowchart TB S1["Section 1: Small Pipe

hL = V1 minus V2 whole squared over 2g"] HGLR["HGL rises by V2 times V1 minus V2 over g
Pressure recovery"] TELD["TEL drops by hL

The HGL must always stay above the pipe (otherwise air enters and breaks the syphon). The TEL falls below the pipe at the summit, indicating sub-atmospheric pressure. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["Reservoir A at Elevation zA"] R["Pipe Rises to Summit S

pS by gamma less than zero gauge"] P2["TEL at S falls below Pipe Centerline"] P3["HGL at S falls below Pipe Centerline
if pS is negative enough"] CAV["Cavitation Check
pS must be greater than pv"] A --> R --> F R --> P1 --> CAV R --> P2 R --> P3 ``` ## 4.5 Diagram 5: HGL/TEL Across a Pump and Turbine in Series This is the **most comprehensive** diagram and is essential for problems on combined pump-turbine systems (e.g., pumped-storage hydroelectric plants). ```mermaid flowchart LR RA["Reservoir A zA"] EP["Entrance Loss 0.5 V^2 over 2g"] P1["Pipe 1 to Pump"] PUMP["Pump adds Head Hp
