## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "Efficiency of Power Transmission" in a pipe and write the expression for overall efficiency.**

**Model Answer:**

> Efficiency of power transmission is the ratio of useful power delivered at the pipe outlet to the power supplied at the inlet.
>
> $$\eta = \frac{P_{\text{transmitted}}}{P_{\text{supplied}}}$$
>
> Substituting the power expressions:
>
> $$\eta = \frac{\rho g Q (H - h_f)}{\rho g Q H} = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H}$$
>
> It is a **dimensionless quantity** expressed as a percentage. A higher efficiency means less friction loss and lower pumping cost. For a long pipeline, typical efficiencies range from 85% to 95%.

---

#### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the condition for maximum power transmission through a pipe. What is the efficiency at this condition?**

**Model Answer:**

> The condition for maximum power transmission through a long pipe occurs when the **friction head loss equals one-third of the total available head**:
>
> $$\boxed{h_f = \frac{H}{3}}$$
>
> At this condition, the useful power $P_{\text{use}} = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$ is maximum, and the **efficiency is**:
>
> $$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{H - H/3}{H} = \frac{2}{3} = 66.67\%$$
>
> This is a **fixed constant** — independent of pipe length, diameter, fluid, or friction factor. It is a fundamental result in fluid power transmission.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3 Choice A]`

**A horizontal pipe of length 1500 m and diameter 200 mm carries water at a rate of 0.05 m³/s. The head at the inlet is 25 m. Take friction factor $f = 0.02$.**

**(a)** Calculate the head lost due to friction, the power transmitted, and the overall efficiency of transmission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the diameter of the pipe for maximum power transmission and the corresponding maximum power. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $L = 1500$ m, $D = 200$ mm $= 0.2$ m
- $Q = 0.05$ m³/s, $H = 25$ m
- $f = 0.02$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Calculate velocity:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.05}{\frac{\pi}{4}(0.2)^2} = \frac{0.05}{0.03142} = 1.5915 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate head loss (Darcy-Weisbach):**

$$h_f = \frac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D} = \frac{4 \times 0.02 \times 1500 \times (1.5915)^2}{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2}$$

$$h_f = \frac{0.08 \times 1500 \times 2.5329}{3.924} = \frac{303.948}{3.924} = 77.46 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate Power Supplied:**

$$P_{\text{sup}} = \rho g Q H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 25 = 12{,}262.5 \text{ W} = 12.26 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Power Lost:**

$$P_{\text{lost}} = \rho g Q h_f = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 77.46 = 37{,}994.4 \text{ W} = 37.99 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate Power Transmitted:**

$$P_{\text{use}} = P_{\text{sup}} - P_{\text{lost}} = 12.26 - 37.99 = -25.73 \text{ kW}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **NEGATIVE POWER!** This means the head loss **exceeds the available head** — the pipe cannot carry this discharge. The student must recognize this. Let me re-check the calculation.

> **Recheck Step 2:** $V^2 = (1.5915)^2 = 2.5329$ m²/s²
>
> $4fL = 4 \times 0.02 \times 1500 = 120$
>
> Numerator: $120 \times 2.5329 = 303.95$
>
> Denominator: $2gD = 2 \times 9.81 \times 0.2 = 3.924$
>
> $h_f = 303.95 / 3.924 = 77.46$ m ✓
>
> **Yes, $h_f = 77.46$ m >> $H = 25$ m.** The problem as stated is physically unrealistic. A **good student should note this in the answer**.

**Step 5 (Corrected) — Recognize Infeasibility:**

Since $h_f = 77.46$ m $\gg$ $H = 25$ m, the given discharge cannot flow through this pipe. The maximum discharge possible (when $h_f = H = 25$ m) is:

$$Q_{\max}^2 = \frac{\pi^2 g D^5 H}{8 f L} = \frac{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times (0.2)^5 \times 25}{8 \times 0.02 \times 1500}$$

$$Q_{\max}^2 = \frac{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 0.00032 \times 25}{240} = \frac{0.7747}{240} = 0.003228$$

$$Q_{\max} = 0.0568 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

So $Q = 0.05$ m³/s is just below the maximum, and the system is **operating near the maximum flow point** (not maximum power transmission). Let us use the actual $h_f = 25$ m (since the inlet head is only 25 m, the actual head loss cannot exceed this):

**Realistic Calculation (head loss is capped at available head):**

$$H_{\text{out}} = H - h_f = 25 - 25 = 0 \text{ m}$$

This means **no useful power** is transmitted — the fluid just barely trickles out with no pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** A well-designed KTU problem avoids this contradiction. The student should be marked for recognizing the contradiction and re-computing. In a real exam, always state the assumption clearly.

> **For academic demonstration, let's assume the problem meant $H = 80$ m (a typical value for such a long pipe):**
>
> $H = 80$ m
>
> $P_{\text{sup}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 80 = 39{,}240$ W $= 39.24$ kW
>
> $P_{\text{lost}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 77.46 = 37{,}994$ W $= 37.99$ kW
>
> $P_{\text{use}} = 39.24 - 37.99 = 1.25$ kW
>
> $\eta = 1.25 / 39.24 = 0.0318 = 3.18\%$

This confirms the pipe is poorly designed for this service.

**Step 6 — Efficiency (with corrected $H = 80$ m):**

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{80 - 77.46}{80} = \frac{2.54}{80} = 0.0318 = 3.18\%$$

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Apply Maximum Power Condition $h_f = H/3$:**

With $H = 80$ m (corrected):

$$h_f = \frac{80}{3} = 26.67 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Diameter:**

$$h_f = \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$$

$$D^5 = \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g h_f} = \frac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1500 \times (0.05)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 26.67}$$

$$D^5 = \frac{8 \times 0.02 \times 1500 \times 0.0025}{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 26.67} = \frac{0.6}{2581.5} = 2.324 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$D = (2.324 \times 10^{-4})^{1/5} = 0.2974 \text{ m} \approx 297.4 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3 — Maximum Power Transmitted:**

$$P_{\max} = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H = \frac{2}{3} \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 80$$

$$P_{\max} = \frac{2}{3} \times 39{,}240 = 26{,}160 \text{ W} = 26.16 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Verify Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{H - h_f}{H} = \frac{80 - 26.67}{80} = 0.6667 = 66.67\% \quad \checkmark$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3 Choice B]`

**A pipeline of length 3000 m connects two reservoirs having a difference in water surface elevation of 60 m. The pipe is to deliver 0.10 m³/s. Take $f = 0.025$.**

**(a)** Find the diameter of the pipe for maximum power transmission. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Find the diameter of the pipe for an efficiency of 90% in power transmission, and calculate the corresponding power transmitted. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Given:**
- $L = 3000$ m, $H = 60$ m, $Q = 0.10$ m³/s, $f = 0.025$

**Step 1 — Apply Maximum Power Condition:**

For maximum power transmission: $h_f = H/3 = 60/3 = 20$ m

**Step 2 — Use Darcy-Weisbach to Find $D$:**

$$D^5 = \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g h_f} = \frac{8 \times 0.025 \times 3000 \times (0.10)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 20}$$

$$D^5 = \frac{8 \times 0.025 \times 3000 \times 0.01}{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 20} = \frac{6.0}{1936.1} = 3.099 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$D = (3.099 \times 10^{-3})^{1/5}$$

Computing: $D^5 = 0.003099$. Take the 5th root:

$$D = 0.003099^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \ln(0.003099)} = e^{0.2 \times (-5.776)} = e^{-1.1552} = 0.315 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D = 0.315 \text{ m} = 315 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Verify Power Transmitted:**

$$P_{\max} = \frac{2}{3} \rho g Q H = \frac{2}{3} \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 \times 60 = 39{,}240 \text{ W} = 39.24 \text{ kW}$$

---

##### **Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Given Efficiency = 90%:**

$$\eta = 0.90 = 1 - \frac{h_f}{H} \quad \Rightarrow \quad h_f = 0.10 \times H = 0.10 \times 60 = 6 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for Diameter:**

$$D^5 = \frac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g h_f} = \frac{8 \times 0.025 \times 3000 \times (0.10)^2}{\pi^2 \times 9.81 \times 6}$$

$$D^5 = \frac{6.0}{9.8696 \times 9.81 \times 6} = \frac{6.0}{580.83} = 0.01033$$

$$D = 0.01033^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \times (-4.573)} = e^{-0.9146} = 0.401 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{D = 0.401 \text{ m} = 401 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Power Transmitted:**

$$P_{\text{use}} = \rho g Q (H - h_f) = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 \times (60 - 6)$$

$$P_{\text{use}} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 \times 54 = 52{,}974 \text{ W} \approx 52.97 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with Maximum Power Case:**

| Parameter | Max Power Case (a) | 90% Efficiency Case (b) |
|---|---|---|
| Diameter | 315 mm | 401 mm |
| $h_f$ | 20 m | 6 m |
| Power Transmitted | 39.24 kW | 52.97 kW |
| Efficiency | 66.67% | 90.00% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The 90% efficiency case transmits **more power** (52.97 kW > 39.24 kW) because the larger diameter reduces friction loss, allowing more head to reach the outlet. The "maximum power" condition is a *local* maximum, not a global one. For practical engineering, the **efficiency-based design is preferred** when pumping cost matters.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Maximum Power" with "Maximum Efficiency":**
>    - Maximum Power → $h_f = H/3$, $\eta = 66.67\%$
>    - Maximum Efficiency → $h_f \to 0$, but $Q \to 0$ (impractical)
>    - For practical "high efficiency" design, take $h_f = 0.05H$ to $0.10H$ (i.e., 90%–95% efficiency).
>
> 2. **Using Wrong Friction Factor Convention:**
>    - If your textbook uses **Fanning** $f$, the formula is $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$.
>    - If using **Darcy** $\lambda$, the formula is $h_f = \dfrac{\lambda L V^2}{2 g D}$.
>    - $\lambda = 4 f$ (Fanning). KTU typically uses the **Fanning factor** $f$ in $4fLV^2/(2gD)$ form, but verify with the data book.
>
> 3. **Unit Mismatches in Diameter Formulas:**
>    - $D = \left( \dfrac{24 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$ requires $D$ in **metres**, $Q$ in **m³/s**, $H$ in **m**, $L$ in **m**, $f$ dimensionless, $g$ in m/s².
>    - If you input $D$ in mm, the result will be off by a factor of $100^{1/5} = 2.512$.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert Q to Velocity or Vice-Versa:**
>    - $V = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ is the link. Many students use $V = Q/A$ with $A = \pi D^2$ (forgetting the 4) — instant zero.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Negative or Unrealistic Results:**
>    - If $h_f > H$, the problem is infeasible. A well-prepared student notes this and computes the **maximum possible discharge** for the given pipe.
>
> 6. **Confusing Hydraulic Gradient Line (HGL) with Total Energy Line (TEL):**
>    - HGL is at piezometric head level; TEL is HGL + velocity head.
>    - Both slope downward in flow direction, but TEL has a steeper drop if pipe diameter changes.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize Before Exam Day**

**Core Definitions:**
- ☑ **Power Supplied** $P_{\text{sup}} = \rho g Q H$ — energy entering pipe per second
- ☑ **Power Lost** $P_{\text{lost}} = \rho g Q h_f$ — energy dissipated as friction
- ☑ **Power Transmitted** $P_{\text{use}} = \rho g Q (H - h_f)$ — useful output
- ☑ **Efficiency** $\eta = (H - h_f)/H$ — fraction of input that becomes useful

**The Two Sacred Conditions:**
- ☑ **Maximum Power Transmission:** $h_f = H/3 \Rightarrow \eta = 2/3 = 66.67\%$
- ☑ **Maximum Efficiency Design:** Choose $h_f = 0.05H$ to $0.10H$ for $\eta = 90\%-95\%$

**Master Formula:**
- ☑ $D = \left( \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g h_f} \right)^{1/5}$ — generic diameter from friction loss
- ☑ $D_{\max P} = \left( \dfrac{24 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g H} \right)^{1/5}$ — diameter for max power
- ☑ $D_{\max \eta} = \left( \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g (0.10 H)} \right)^{1/5}$ — diameter for $\eta = 90\%$

**Darcy-Weisbach Forms (must know both):**
- ☑ Velocity form: $h_f = \dfrac{4 f L V^2}{2 g D}$
- ☑ Discharge form: $h_f = \dfrac{8 f L Q^2}{\pi^2 g D^5}$

**HGL/TEL Rules:**
- ☑ TEL is always **above** HGL by velocity head $\frac{V^2}{2g}$
- ☑ Both slope downward at slope $S_f = h_f/L$
- ☑ HGL must remain **above pipe profile** to avoid cavitation/negative pressure

**Quick-Reference Constants:**
- ☑ $\rho_{\text{water}} = 1000$ kg/m³, $\gamma_{\text{water}} = 9810$ N/m³
- ☑ $g = 9.81$ m/s²
- ☑ $1 \text{ kW} = 1000$ W

**Problem-Solving Workflow:**
1. Identify given $H$, $L$, $Q$, $D$, $f$.
2. Compute $V$ from $Q$ and $D$.
3. Compute $h_f$ from Darcy-Weisbach.
4. Compute $P_{\text{sup}}$, $P_{\text{lost}}$, $P_{\text{use}}$.
5. Compute $\eta$.
6. If asked for design diameter, use the appropriate formula.
7. Check physical feasibility ($h_f < H$).

**Common Pitfalls to Avoid:**
- ☑ Never use $|x|$ with vertical bars in markdown tables — use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.
- ☑ Always state units in the final answer.
- ☑ Verify that $\eta \leq 1$ (efficiency cannot exceed 100%).
- ☑ If the problem gives $\lambda$ (Darcy), convert to $f$ (Fanning) using $f = \lambda/4$ or vice versa as per textbook convention.

**Engineering Wisdom:**
> *"A larger diameter costs more in material but saves more in pumping. The art of pipe design is finding the diameter where the **annual cost of friction loss** equals the **annual capitalized cost of the pipe** — a true economic optimum. The 'maximum power' and 'maximum efficiency' conditions are theoretical limits that bracket this economic optimum."*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Siphon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Siphon (Definition):** A siphon is a long bent pipe or tube used to transfer liquid from a higher reservoir to a lower reservoir by passing over an intervening obstacle (hump), provided the inlet (summit) lies below the atmospheric pressure head equivalent of the liquid.

A siphon works on the principle of **gravity and atmospheric pressure**. The highest point in the siphon (called the *summit* or *crown*) must lie within a certain height above the free surface of the upper reservoir, otherwise the pressure at the summit will drop below the vapor pressure of the liquid and cause **cavitation**, which breaks the siphon column.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a siphon like drinking water from a glass through a straw, but instead of sucking, you let gravity do the work. You fill the tube with water, place one end in the upper tank, and let the other end hang below the water level — water magically flows upward and then downward, climbing over the rim.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Siphon Parameters:**
> - **Maximum theoretical height of summit:** $7.5 \text{ m}$ of water column (at sea level, since $p_{atm} = 10.3 \text{ m}$ of water minus losses).
> - **Net head available for flow:** Difference of water surface elevations between the two reservoirs.
> - **Priming required:** The pipe must be initially filled with water (or primed) before flow begins.

---

## 1.2 Water Hammer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Water Hammer (Definition):** Water hammer is a hydraulic transient phenomenon characterized by a sudden rise or fall in pressure inside a pipe caused by a rapid change in fluid velocity, typically due to sudden valve closure, pump stoppage, or rapid opening/closing of a control device.

When a moving column of liquid is brought to rest abruptly (e.g., by closing a valve), its **kinetic energy** is converted into **pressure energy** almost instantaneously. This produces a pressure wave that travels up the pipe at the **celerity of the pressure wave** (speed of sound in the fluid medium), often accompanied by a characteristic banging sound — hence the name "water hammer."

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine swinging a heavy garden hose filled with water and then quickly pinching the nozzle shut with your thumb. You'll feel a sudden jolt — that's water hammer. The water "slams" into the closed valve just like a train slamming into a stopped barrier.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Water Hammer Equations (Description Only):**
> - **Pressure rise due to sudden closure:**
> $$\Delta p = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta V$$
> where $\rho$ = density, $c$ = celerity of pressure wave, $\Delta V$ = change in velocity.
> - **Celerity (Joukowsky Equation):**
> $$c = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{\rho}} \Big/ \sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t}}$$
> where $K$ = bulk modulus, $E$ = pipe material modulus, $D$ = diameter, $t$ = pipe thickness.

---

## 1.3 Cavitation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cavitation (Definition):** Cavitation is the formation, growth, and subsequent collapse of vapor cavities (bubbles) within a liquid when the local static pressure drops below the vapor pressure of the liquid at the prevailing temperature.

When local pressure falls below vapor pressure, dissolved gases and liquid vapor form bubbles at the low-pressure zones (e.g., at the throat of a venturi, on the suction side of a pump, or at the summit of a siphon). When these bubbles are carried by the flow to regions of higher pressure, they collapse violently, generating extremely high local pressures, micro-jets, and intense noise.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of boiling water — but this "boiling" happens not because of heat, but because the pressure has dropped so low that the water "wants" to boil. When the bubbles move to higher-pressure regions, they implode like tiny depth-charges, hammering the pipe walls and producing a characteristic rattling noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Cavitation Damage Effects:**
> - **Pitting and erosion** of metal surfaces.
> - **Vibration and noise** in pumps and turbines.
> - **Reduction in efficiency** and performance of hydraulic machinery.
> - **Cavitation parameter (Thoma number):**
> $$\sigma = \dfrac{p - p_v}{\frac{1}{2}\rho V^2}$$

---

## 1.4 Open Channel Flow (OCF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Open Channel Flow (Definition):** Open channel flow is the flow of a liquid with a free surface (a surface exposed to atmospheric pressure) in a conduit where the driving force is essentially gravity. The flow cross-section is determined jointly by the geometry of the channel and the depth of liquid.

Unlike pipe flow, where the liquid completely fills the closed conduit under pressure, open channel flow has **one boundary (the free surface) that is deformable and unknown a priori**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a river flowing down a valley — the top surface is open to the air (free surface), and the water flows because of the slope of the ground (gravity). This is open channel flow. If we cover the river with a tight lid, it becomes pipe flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinctions — Pipe Flow vs. Open Channel Flow:**
>
> | Feature | Pipe Flow | Open Channel Flow |
> |---|---|---|
> | Driving force | Pressure difference | Gravity (bed slope) |
> | Free surface | Absent | Present |
> | Cross-section | Fixed | Variable with depth |
> | Energy equation | Includes only pressure head | Includes elevation head over free surface |
> | Hydraulic grade line | Coincides with pipe centre line | Coincides with free water surface |

---

## 1.5 Types of Flow in Open Channels

Open channel flows are classified based on **time-dependence**, **spatial variation**, **viscous effects**, and **Froude number**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Classification of Open Channel Flow:**
> 1. **Steady vs Unsteady Flow** — Based on time variation of depth/velocity.
> 2. **Uniform vs Non-Uniform (Varied) Flow** — Based on spatial variation.
> 3. **Laminar vs Turbulent Flow** — Based on Reynolds number.
> 4. **Subcritical, Critical, and Supercritical Flow** — Based on Froude number.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Imagine water flowing through a rectangular flume:
- If the depth remains constant along the flume, it's **uniform flow**.
- If the depth changes (like over a hump or through a constriction), it's **varied flow**.
- If you slowly pour more water in, it's **unsteady flow**.
- If the waves travel upstream (gentle slope, deep water), it's **subcritical**.
- If the waves get carried downstream (steep slope, shallow water), it's **supercritical**.

---

## 1.6 Velocity Distribution in Channels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Distribution (Definition):** The variation of flow velocity across the cross-section of an open channel. In turbulent flow, the velocity is zero at the boundaries (channel bed and walls due to no-slip) and maximum somewhere below the free surface.

Due to the presence of the **free surface** (where shear is zero) and the **rough solid boundary** (where velocity is zero due to no-slip condition), the velocity is **not maximum at the free surface** but slightly below it — a phenomenon known as the **velocity dip**.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Picture a slow-moving river. The water near the riverbed moves slowly (friction with rocks), the water at the very top also moves a bit slower (air resistance), but the fastest water is just an inch or two below the surface. That's the velocity dip.

---

## 1.7 Measurement of Velocity in Open Channels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Velocity Measurement Devices:**
> - **Current Meter** — A rotating-element device that measures point velocity.
> - **Parshall Flume** — A calibrated critical-depth flume used for discharge measurement.
> - **Cut-Throat Flume** — A flat-bottomed, narrow-throat flume (modified Parshall).
> - **Venturi Flume** — A flume with a constricted throat, similar to a pipe venturi.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
A current meter is like the speedometer of a boat placed in the water to measure local speed. A Parshall flume is like a deliberately built "speed bump" in the channel — by measuring the depth at a specific point, you can deduce the flow rate. A venturi flume squeezes the flow and uses the principle that faster flow means lower depth (Bernoulli's idea).

---

## 1.8 Chezy's Equation and Manning's Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Chezy's Equation:** An empirical equation for uniform flow velocity in an open channel:
> $$V = C \sqrt{R \cdot S}$$
> where $C$ = Chezy's coefficient, $R$ = hydraulic radius, $S$ = bed slope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manning's Equation:** A widely-used empirical formula (refinement of Chezy's) for uniform flow velocity:
> $$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$
> where $n$ = Manning's roughness coefficient.

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Chezy's equation is like saying, "Speed of a bicycle depends on the slope of the road and how rough the tires are." Manning's equation is a more refined version of the same idea, where the "tire roughness" is quantified by Manning's $n$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Siphon — Working Principle

### Operational Logic Steps:
1. **Initial priming** — The siphon pipe must be completely filled with liquid; otherwise, air pockets will break the siphon action.
2. **Pressure at the inlet (upper reservoir)** is atmospheric.
3. **Pressure at the summit** is the lowest point in the siphon. Applying Bernoulli's equation between the upper reservoir surface and the summit:
$$p_{atm}/\rho g + 0 + 0 = p_s/\rho g + V_s^2/2g + z_s$$
4. **Velocity at summit** $V_s$ equals velocity in the pipe (assuming constant cross-section):
$$V_s = \sqrt{2g \cdot (H_s)}$$
where $H_s$ = difference between upper water surface elevation and summit elevation minus losses.
5. **Pressure at summit** must remain above vapor pressure to prevent cavitation.

### Real-World Utility:
- Used in **irrigation systems** to transfer water across ridges.
- Used in **water supply** for hills and elevated areas.
- Used in **sewerage systems** to carry flow over obstacles.

---

## 2.2 Water Hammer — Theory of Pressure Wave

### Operational Logic Steps:
1. **Sudden valve closure** generates a pressure wave that propagates upstream at celerity $c$.
2. The wave travels from valve to reservoir, reflects, and oscillates.
3. **Joukowsky pressure rise:**
$$\Delta p = \rho \cdot c \cdot (V_0 - V_f)$$
where $V_0$ = initial velocity, $V_f$ = final velocity after closure.
4. For **sudden complete closure** ($V_f = 0$):
$$\Delta p = \rho \cdot c \cdot V_0$$
5. **Critical time** — time for pressure wave to travel to reservoir and return:
$$t_c = \dfrac{2L}{c}$$
If valve closure time $t < t_c$, it's **sudden closure** (full water hammer).
If $t > t_c$, it's **gradual closure** (reduced water hammer).

### Real-World Utility:
- **Design of surge tanks** in hydroelectric plants.
- **Specification of pipe thickness** and pressure ratings.
- **Selection of valves** (e.g., slow-closing valves) to mitigate water hammer.

---

## 2.3 Cavitation — Mechanism

### Operational Logic Steps:
1. Local pressure $p$ falls below vapor pressure $p_v$.
2. Liquid vaporizes, forming **cavities** (bubbles) at the low-pressure zone.
3. Bubbles are convected to higher-pressure regions.
4. Bubbles **collapse violently**, producing micro-jets (velocities up to **100 m/s**) and shock waves.
5. Repeated collapse erodes surfaces (especially metals), creating pits and roughness.

### Cavitation Avoidance:
- Keep **NPSH available** (Net Positive Suction Head available) > **NPSH required**.
- Limit pump suction lift to manufacturer-specified values.
- Avoid excessively high flow velocities.
- Avoid local pressure drops below $p_v$.

---

## 2.4 Open Channel Flow — Geometric Elements

For a channel cross-section:
- **Depth of flow:** $y$ — vertical distance from the lowest point of the section to the free surface.
- **Top width:** $T$ — width of the section at the free surface.
- **Area of flow:** $A$ — cross-sectional area of flow.
- **Wetted perimeter:** $P$ — length of the boundary in contact with the fluid.
- **Hydraulic radius:** $R = A / P$.
- **Hydraulic depth:** $D = A / T$.
- **Section factor (for critical flow):** $Z = A \cdot \sqrt{D}$.
- **Element of section (for uniform flow):** $A \cdot R^{2/3}$.

---

## 2.5 Types of Open Channel Flow — Detailed Classification

### (a) Steady vs Unsteady Flow
| Type | Condition |
|---|---|
| **Steady Flow** | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} = 0$ at any point |
| **Unsteady Flow** | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} \neq 0$ at any point |

### (b) Uniform vs Non-Uniform (Varied) Flow
| Type | Condition |
|---|---|
| **Uniform Flow** | Depth $y$ is constant along the channel length |
| **Varied Flow** | Depth $y$ changes along the channel |

Varied flow is further classified as:
- **Gradually Varied Flow (GVF)** — depth changes slowly (e.g., backwater curves).
- **Rapidly Varied Flow (RVF)** — depth changes abruptly (e.g., hydraulic jump, flow over weir).

### (c) Laminar vs Turbulent Flow
Reynolds Number for open channel flow:
$$Re = \dfrac{\rho V R}{\mu} = \dfrac{V R}{\nu}$$

| Type | Reynolds Number |
|---|---|
| Laminar | $Re < 500$ |
| Turbulent | $Re > 2000$ |
| Transitional | $500 \leq Re \leq 2000$ |

### (d) Subcritical, Critical, and Supercritical Flow
Froude Number:
$$Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gD}} = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{g \cdot A/T}}$$

| Flow Regime | Froude Number |
|---|---|
| Subcritical (Tranquil / Streaming) | $Fr < 1$ |
| Critical | $Fr = 1$ |
| Supercritical (Shooting / Rapid) | $Fr > 1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Depth Equation (Rectangular Channel):**
> $$y_c = \left(\dfrac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}$$
> where $q = Q/b$ = discharge per unit width.

---

## 2.6 Velocity Distribution — Theoretical Models

### (a) Logarithmic Distribution (Prandtl's Law)
$$\dfrac{u}{u_*} = \dfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln\left(\dfrac{y}{y_0}\right)$$
where $u_* = \sqrt{gRS}$ = shear velocity, $\kappa \approx 0.41$ = von Kármán constant.

### (b) Power-Law Distribution
$$\dfrac{u}{u_{max}} = \left(\dfrac{y}{y_{max}}\right)^{1/n}$$
where $n$ varies between **6 to 10** for turbulent flow in open channels.

### Velocity Dip:
The maximum velocity in a channel occurs at approximately **$0.05 y$ to $0.25 y$** below the free surface (where $y$ is total depth).

---

## 2.7 Measurement Devices

### (a) Current Meter
- **Components:** Rotor (cup-type or propeller-type), tail fin, suspension rod, electrical contact.
- **Principle:** Rotation speed of rotor is proportional to local velocity.
- **Equation:**
$$V = a \cdot N_s + b$$
where $N_s$ = revolutions per second, $a$ and $b$ are calibration constants.

### (b) Parshall Flume
- A **calibrated critical-depth flume** with three sections: **converging section, throat, and diverging section**.
- Bed of the throat is **level** (horizontal).
- Discharge is a function of the upstream depth $H_a$ only:
$$Q = C \cdot H_a^n$$
- $C$ and $n$ depend on the **flume size (throat width)**.
- For **standard Parshall flume with 3-inch to 8-ft throat:**
$$Q = 4 \cdot W \cdot H_a^{1.522 \cdot W^{0.026}}$$
(where $W$ = throat width in feet, $H_a$ in feet, $Q$ in cfs — for free flow).

### (c) Cut-Throat Flume
- A **flat-bottomed** flume (no hump) with a narrow throat.
- A modification of Parshall flume — the throat has no parallel length, only a converging and diverging section meeting at a single point (hence "cut throat").
- Used where bed slope is flat and head loss must be minimal.
- Discharge equation:
$$Q = C \cdot H_a^{n_1} \cdot W^{n_2}$$

### (d) Venturi Flume
- A flume with a constricted cross-section (similar to pipe venturi).
- Acts as a **broad-crested weir** at the throat.
- For a rectangular venturi flume:
$$Q = C_d \cdot b \cdot \sqrt{2g \cdot \Delta H^{3/2}}$$
where $b$ = throat width, $\Delta H$ = head difference.

---

> - Earth channel (weedy): $n = 0.030$
> - Natural stream (clean): $n = 0.030$
> - Natural stream (stony): $n = 0.040$
> - Floodplain (heavy brush): $n = 0.075$

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Chezy's Equation

### Concept Foundation:
Chezy's equation was developed by the French engineer **Antoine de Chézy in 1775** for uniform flow in open channels. The derivation is based on balancing the **gravitational force component** along the flow direction with the **frictional resistance** at the boundary.

### Assumptions:
1. Flow is **steady** and **uniform**.
2. Channel cross-section, roughness, and slope are **constant**.
3. The hydraulic gradient line, water surface, and channel bed are **parallel**.
4. **Forces acting on the fluid mass** are in **equilibrium**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Define the control volume.**
Consider a length $L$ of the channel with cross-sectional area $A$ and wetted perimeter $P$.

**Step 2: Compute the gravitational force component along the flow direction.**
Let $S$ = bed slope (sin $\theta \approx$ tan $\theta \approx \theta$ for small slopes).
The weight of the fluid in the control volume:
$$W = \rho g A L$$

The component of weight along the flow direction (driving force):
$$F_g = \rho g A L \sin\theta \approx \rho g A L \cdot S$$

**Step 3: Compute the frictional (resistive) force at the boundary.**
The shear stress $\tau_0$ acts on the wetted area $P \cdot L$. The total frictional force:
$$F_f = \tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L$$

**Step 4: Apply equilibrium of forces (uniform flow → no acceleration).**
$$F_g = F_f$$
$$\rho g A L \cdot S = \tau_0 \cdot P \cdot L$$

Cancel $L$ from both sides:
$$\rho g A \cdot S = \tau_0 \cdot P$$

**Step 5: Introduce hydraulic radius.**
Hydraulic radius: $R = A / P$, so $P = A/R$.
$$\rho g A \cdot S = \tau_0 \cdot \dfrac{A}{R}$$

Solve for $\tau_0$:
$$\tau_0 = \rho g R S$$

**Step 6: Apply Chezy's hypothesis on shear.**
Chezy proposed that the average shear stress is proportional to the square of the mean velocity:
$$\tau_0 = K \cdot V^2$$
where $K$ is a resistance coefficient (depends on roughness).

Equating both expressions of $\tau_0$:
$$K V^2 = \rho g R S$$

**Step 7: Solve for $V$.**
$$V^2 = \dfrac{\rho g}{K} \cdot R \cdot S$$

$$V = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho g}{K}} \cdot \sqrt{R S}$$

**Step 8: Define Chezy's coefficient $C$.**
$$C = \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho g}{K}}$$

Thus, **Chezy's Equation:**
$$\boxed{V = C \sqrt{R S}}$$

This is the famous uniform-flow velocity formula used for designing open channels.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Manning's Equation

### Concept Foundation:
Manning's equation is a refinement of Chezy's equation. It expresses Chezy's coefficient $C$ in terms of the **hydraulic radius $R$** and a **roughness coefficient $n$** that has been tabulated for many surfaces.

The Irish engineer **Robert Manning** presented this formula in **1889** based on observations from many rivers and channels.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1: Start with Chezy's equation.**
$$V = C \sqrt{R S}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Chezy's $C$ in terms of $R$ and $n$.**
Empirically (based on experiments), it has been found that:
$$C = \dfrac{1}{n} \cdot R^{1/6}$$

This is the famous **Gauckler–Manning relationship**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin of the 1/6 power:**
> It comes from extensive experiments on European rivers and canals. The exponent **1/6** best fits the observed data for the variation of $C$ with $R$.

**Step 3: Substitute into Chezy's equation.**
$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6} \cdot \sqrt{R S}$$

**Step 4: Simplify the expression.**
$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6} \cdot R^{1/2} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{(1/6 + 1/2)} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{(1/6 + 3/6)} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{4/6} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

**Step 5: Final simplified form — Manning's Equation (SI units).**
$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}}$$

### Unit Conversion (FPS Units):
In FPS units, the numerical constant changes from $1$ to $1.486$:
$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{1.486}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}}$$

### Discharge Form of Manning's Equation:
Multiplying both sides by area $A$:
$$Q = A V = \dfrac{A}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

---

## 3.3 Numerical Example — Manning's Equation

**Problem:** A trapezoidal channel has bottom width $b = 3 \text{ m}$, side slopes $1:1$ (horizontal:vertical), depth of flow $y = 2 \text{ m}$, bed slope $S = 0.001$, and Manning's $n = 0.025$. Calculate the discharge.

### Step 1: Compute the cross-sectional area $A$.
For a trapezoidal section:
$$A = (b + zy) \cdot y$$
where $z$ = horizontal component of side slope (here $z = 1$).
$$A = (3 + 1 \cdot 2) \cdot 2 = 5 \cdot 2 = 10 \text{ m}^2$$

### Step 2: Compute the wetted perimeter $P$.
$$P = b + 2y\sqrt{1 + z^2}$$
$$P = 3 + 2 \cdot 2 \cdot \sqrt{1 + 1^2} = 3 + 4\sqrt{2}$$
$$P = 3 + 5.6568 = 8.6568 \text{ m}$$

### Step 3: Compute the hydraulic radius $R$.
$$R = \dfrac{A}{P} = \dfrac{10}{8.6568} = 1.155 \text{ m}$$

### Step 4: Apply Manning's equation.
$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$
$$V = \dfrac{1}{0.025} \cdot (1.155)^{2/3} \cdot (0.001)^{1/2}$$
$$V = 40 \cdot 1.0995 \cdot 0.03162$$
$$V = 40 \cdot 0.03477 = 1.391 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 5: Compute the discharge.
$$Q = A \cdot V = 10 \cdot 1.391 = 13.91 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Answer:** $Q \approx 13.91 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ (commonly rounded to $13.9$ or $14 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Critical Depth Equation (Rectangular Channel)

### Step 1: Specific energy equation.
$$E = y + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} = y + \dfrac{Q^2}{2gA^2}$$

For rectangular channel: $A = b \cdot y$, so:
$$E = y + \dfrac{Q^2}{2g b^2 y^2}$$

Define discharge per unit width: $q = Q/b$.
$$E = y + \dfrac{q^2}{2g y^2}$$

### Step 2: Condition for critical flow.
At critical depth, specific energy is minimum:
$$\dfrac{dE}{dy} = 0$$
$$1 - \dfrac{q^2}{g y^3} = 0$$
$$\dfrac{q^2}{g y^3} = 1$$
$$y_c^3 = \dfrac{q^2}{g}$$

### Step 3: Solve for critical depth.
$$\boxed{y_c = \left(\dfrac{q^2}{g}\right)^{1/3}}$$

### Step 4: Critical velocity.
$$V_c = \dfrac{q}{y_c} = \sqrt{g \cdot y_c}$$

### Step 5: Froude number at critical flow.
$$Fr_c = \dfrac{V_c}{\sqrt{g y_c}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{g y_c}}{\sqrt{g y_c}} = 1$$

This confirms that critical flow corresponds to $Fr = 1$.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Chezy vs. Manning

| Parameter | Chezy | Manning |
|---|---|---|
| Year | 1775 | 1889 |
| Originator | Antoine de Chézy (France) | Robert Manning (Ireland) |
| Form | $V = C \sqrt{RS}$ | $V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ |
| Coefficient | $C$ (depends on $R$ and roughness) | $n$ (independent of $R$, only on roughness) |
| Ease of use | Moderate | High — extensively tabulated $n$ values |
| Conversion | $C = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{1/6}$ | $n = \dfrac{R^{1/6}}{C}$ |
