# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3 Mark Questions

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define the term "Water Hammer". What are its causes and effects?**

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** Water hammer is a hydraulic transient phenomenon in which a sudden change in fluid velocity (typically due to rapid valve closure or pump stoppage) generates a sharp pressure rise (or fall) in a pipe system, accompanied by a characteristic hammering sound.

**Causes:**
- Sudden closure of a valve.
- Sudden starting or stopping of a pump.
- Rapid opening or closing of a control device.
- Column separation in long pipelines.

**Effects:**
- **Pipe rupture** due to excessive pressure.
- **Vibration and noise.**
- **Damage to valves, fittings, and gauges.**
- **Fatigue failure** of pipe supports.
- **Cavitation** at low-pressure zones during the pressure cycle.

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is meant by "velocity dip" in open channel flow? Why does it occur?**

**Model Answer:**
**Definition:** The **velocity dip** refers to the phenomenon in which the maximum velocity in an open channel does **not occur at the free surface**, but at a depth of approximately **$0.05 y$ to $0.25 y$** below the free surface.

**Reason for Occurrence:**
- At the **free surface**, there is no shear stress (boundary condition with air), but secondary currents and air resistance reduce velocity.
- At the **channel bed**, the **no-slip condition** forces velocity to zero.
- Between these two extremes, the velocity reaches a maximum.
- The presence of **secondary currents** (helical motion) further redistributes velocity, pushing the maximum velocity downward.

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

> [KTU University Exam — Sample Model Question]

**a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **working principle of a Siphon**. Derive the expression for the maximum height of the siphon summit above the upper reservoir water surface. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A siphon of diameter **300 mm** is used to transfer water from a reservoir with water surface at elevation **+25.0 m** to another reservoir with water surface at elevation **+15.0 m**. The summit of the siphon lies at elevation **+30.0 m**. Assume Manning's $n = 0.012$ and total length of siphon $L = 200$ m. Calculate the discharge through the siphon and the pressure head at the summit. Take atmospheric pressure as **10.3 m of water**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Working Principle of a Siphon:**

A siphon is a bent pipe used to convey water from a **higher reservoir** to a **lower reservoir** over an intervening obstacle. It works on the combined principles of **gravity** and **atmospheric pressure**.

**Working Steps:**
1. The pipe is initially **primed** (filled with water) — air is removed.
2. Water surface of the upper reservoir is at **atmospheric pressure**.
3. The **summit** (highest point) of the siphon has the **lowest pressure** in the entire system.
4. The **driving head** is the difference between the water surfaces of the two reservoirs: $H = z_1 - z_2$.
5. Atmospheric pressure pushes water up through the inlet leg; gravity pulls water down through the outlet leg.

**Maximum Height Derivation:**
Applying Bernoulli's equation between the upper reservoir surface (point 1) and the summit (point 2):

$$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_1^2}{2g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V_2^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

At point 1: $p_1 = p_{atm}$, $V_1 \approx 0$, $z_1 = z_s - h_s$ (where $h_s$ is the height of summit above upper water surface).

At point 2 (summit): $p_2 = p_s$ (pressure at summit), $V_2 = V$ (velocity in pipe), $z_2 = z_s$.

$$\dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_s - h_s = \dfrac{p_s}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z_s + h_L$$

$$\dfrac{p_{atm} - p_s}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + h_s + h_L$$

For no cavitation, $p_s \geq p_v$ (vapor pressure). Taking $p_v = 0$ (theoretical limit):

$$\dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} = \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + h_s + h_L$$

Ignoring losses and velocity head (ideal case):
$$h_s = \dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} = 10.33 \text{ m}$$

Therefore, **maximum theoretical height of summit** above upper reservoir surface is **10.33 m of water**. In practice, with losses, it is about **7.5 m**.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $D = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $z_1 = 25.0$ m (upper), $z_2 = 15.0$ m (lower), $z_s = 30.0$ m (summit)
- $n = 0.012$, $L = 200$ m, $p_{atm}/\rho g = 10.3$ m

**Step 1: Compute driving head.**
$$H = z_1 - z_2 = 25 - 15 = 10 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Compute area and wetted perimeter of pipe.**
$$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.3)^2 = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2$$
$$P = \pi D = \pi (0.3) = 0.9425 \text{ m}$$
$$R = A/P = 0.075 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Apply Manning's equation for velocity.**
$$V = \dfrac{1}{n} R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

Note: For a pipe flowing full under gravity (acting as a siphon), the slope $S$ here corresponds to the **hydraulic gradient**, not the bed slope. The driving head provides $H$ over length $L$:
$$S = \dfrac{H}{L} = \dfrac{10}{200} = 0.05$$

$$V = \dfrac{1}{0.012} (0.075)^{2/3} (0.05)^{1/2}$$
$$V = 83.33 \cdot 0.1807 \cdot 0.2236$$
$$V = 3.37 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 4: Compute discharge.**
$$Q = A V = 0.0707 \cdot 3.37 = 0.238 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5: Compute pressure head at summit.**
Applying Bernoulli's equation between upper reservoir surface (1) and summit (2):
$$\dfrac{p_1}{\rho g} + z_1 = \dfrac{p_2}{\rho g} + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + z_2 + h_L$$

Losses $h_L$ can be neglected for this simplified problem.
$$\dfrac{p_s}{\rho g} = \dfrac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + z_1 - z_s - \dfrac{V^2}{2g}$$
$$\dfrac{p_s}{\rho g} = 10.3 + 25 - 30 - \dfrac{(3.37)^2}{2 \cdot 9.81}$$
$$\dfrac{p_s}{\rho g} = 10.3 - 5 - 0.579 = 4.72 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** $Q = 0.238 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, Pressure head at summit $= 4.72 \text{ m}$ (absolute). Since this is positive, **no cavitation occurs**.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**a)** Explain the **Parshall Flume** with a neat sketch. Derive its discharge equation. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A Parshall Flume with a throat width of **0.5 m** is installed in a rectangular channel of width **1.0 m**. The upstream depth (measured at the Ha gauge point) is **0.6 m**. Compute the discharge if the flow is **free** (i.e., not submerged). Use the standard Parshall Flume equation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Parshall Flume — Description and Theory:**

**Description:**
The **Parshall Flume** (also called the Parshall Measuring Flume) is a **calibrated critical-depth flume** developed by **Ralph L. Parshall in 1926**. It is used for measuring the discharge of water in open channels, irrigation canals, and wastewater treatment plants.

**Components (3 sections):**
1. **Converging (Inlet) Section:** Smoothly contracts from channel width to throat width.
2. **Throat Section:** A short parallel-sided narrow section where the bed is **flat (horizontal)** — this is where critical flow occurs.
3. **Diverging (Outlet) Section:** Gradually expands back to the original channel width; bed rises slightly.

**Gauge Points:**
- **Ha gauge point:** Located in the converging section (typically $\frac{2}{3}$ of the length from the inlet).
- **Hb gauge point:** Located in the diverging section.

**Working Principle:**
As water enters the flume, the cross-section contracts, accelerating the flow. At the throat, the depth equals **critical depth**, and the flow becomes critical. By measuring the upstream depth $H_a$, the discharge can be computed.

**Discharge Equation:**
For **free flow** (downstream depth does not affect upstream conditions):
$$Q = C \cdot H_a^n$$
where:
- $H_a$ = depth at Ha gauge point (m)
- $C, n$ = constants depending on throat width $W$

For a **standard Parshall Flume**, the empirical equation is:
$$Q = K \cdot W^{1.025} \cdot H_a^{1.55}$$
(where $W$ = throat width in m, $H_a$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s)

Or commonly expressed as:
$$Q = 4 \cdot W \cdot H_a^{1.522 W^{0.026}}$$
(when $W$ is in feet and $H_a$ in feet, $Q$ in cfs — older USBR formula).

**Conditions for Free Flow:**
For free flow:
$$\dfrac{H_b}{H_a} \leq 0.6 \text{ (for small flumes)}$$

If the ratio exceeds this limit, the flow becomes **submerged**, and corrections are required.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Throat width $W = 0.5 \text{ m}$
- Upstream depth $H_a = 0.6 \text{ m}$

**Step 1: Apply the standard Parshall Flume equation.**
For a throat width of 0.5 m, using the standard table:
- $C = 0.685 \cdot W$ (approximate coefficient for small flumes)
- $n = 1.55$

For a more precise computation:
$$Q = K \cdot W^{1.025} \cdot H_a^{1.55}$$

For $W = 0.5$ m, $K \approx 4.0$:
$$Q = 4.0 \cdot (0.5)^{1.025} \cdot (0.6)^{1.55}$$

**Step 2: Calculate each term.**
$$(0.5)^{1.025} = 0.4939$$
$$(0.6)^{1.55} = 0.6^{1.5} \cdot 0.6^{0.05}$$
$$0.6^{1.5} = 0.4648$$
$$0.6^{0.05} = 0.97 \text{ (approximately)}$$
$$(0.6)^{1.55} \approx 0.4648 \cdot 0.97 = 0.451$$

**Step 3: Compute discharge.**
$$Q = 4.0 \cdot 0.4939 \cdot 0.451 = 0.891 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** $Q \approx 0.89 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.

**Verification (using the ISO standard formula for $W$ in meters):**
$$Q = C_d \cdot W \cdot \sqrt{g} \cdot H_a^{3/2}$$
where $C_d \approx 0.65$ for Parshall flumes:
$$Q = 0.65 \cdot 0.5 \cdot \sqrt{9.81} \cdot (0.6)^{3/2}$$
$$Q = 0.325 \cdot 3.132 \cdot 0.465$$
$$Q = 0.473 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

(The two methods give different values because the empirical constants differ — students should use the equation prescribed in their course material.)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **In Chezy's derivation**, students often forget to convert $\rho g A L \sin\theta$ to $\rho g A L S$ (using $\sin\theta = S$) — leading to dimensional inconsistency.
> 2. **In Manning's equation**, the unit conversion factor ($1$ for SI vs $1.486$ for FPS) is **frequently missed** by students. Always check units of $R$ and $V$.
> 3. **For Siphon problems**, students often compute the pressure at the summit as **negative**, but if atmospheric pressure is given as absolute head, remember the answer is **gauge pressure** when reporting cavitation risk.
> 4. **In Parshall Flume**, do **NOT** use the discharge equation for submerged flow if only $H_a$ is given — verify that $H_b/H_a < 0.6$.
> 5. **In Critical Depth problems**, students often forget that the formula $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3}$ is **only for rectangular channels** — for trapezoidal or circular channels, the general equation $Q^2 T/(g A^3) = 1$ must be solved iteratively.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3**

### Siphon
- ✅ Definition: bent pipe that carries water over an obstacle using gravity and atmospheric pressure.
- ✅ Maximum theoretical height of summit: **10.33 m** of water (no losses).
- ✅ Practical maximum: **7.5 m** (with losses).
- ✅ Pressure at summit is the lowest — must stay **above vapor pressure**.

### Water Hammer
- ✅ Caused by sudden valve closure or pump stoppage.
- ✅ Joukowsky equation: $\Delta p = \rho c \Delta V$.
- ✅ Celerity $c$ depends on fluid and pipe elasticity.
- ✅ Critical time $t_c = 2L/c$ differentiates sudden vs. gradual closure.

### Cavitation
- ✅ Occurs when local pressure drops **below vapor pressure**.
- ✅ Causes pitting, vibration, noise, efficiency loss.
- ✅ Controlled by ensuring **NPSH available > NPSH required**.
- ✅ Thoma's cavitation number $\sigma$ quantifies cavitation risk.

### Open Channel Flow
- ✅ Has a **free surface**; gravity-driven (not pressure-driven).
- ✅ Energy equation uses **elevation head over free surface**, not pressure head.
- ✅ Hydraulic radius $R = A/P$.
- ✅ Hydraulic depth $D = A/T$.

### Types of Flow
- ✅ **Steady vs. Unsteady:** Time-dependence.
- ✅ **Uniform vs. Varied:** Spatial variation of depth.
- ✅ **Laminar ($Re < 500$) vs. Turbulent ($Re > 2000$):** Reynolds number criterion.
- ✅ **Subcritical ($Fr < 1$) vs. Critical ($Fr = 1$) vs. Supercritical ($Fr > 1$):** Wave behaviour.

### Velocity Distribution
- ✅ **Maximum velocity occurs below the free surface** — the **velocity dip**.
- ✅ Logarithmic profile (Prandtl): $u/u_* = (1/\kappa)\ln(y/y_0)$.
- ✅ Power-law profile: $u/u_{max} = (y/y_{max})^{1/n}$.

### Measurement Devices
- ✅ **Current Meter:** $V = a N_s + b$; rotating element, point velocity.
- ✅ **Parshall Flume:** Three sections (converging, throat, diverging); $Q = C H_a^n$.
- ✅ **Cut-Throat Flume:** Modified Parshall; flat-bottom, no parallel-throat section.
- ✅ **Venturi Flume:** Constricted section acts as broad-crested weir; based on Bernoulli's principle.

### Chezy's Equation
- ✅ $V = C \sqrt{R S}$.
- ✅ Derived from force balance: weight component = boundary shear.
- ✅ $C$ depends on roughness and $R$.

### Manning's Equation
- ✅ $V = (1/n) R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ (SI units).
- ✅ $V = (1.486/n) R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$ (FPS units).
- ✅ $C = R^{1/6}/n$ (Chezy–Manning relation).
- ✅ Manning's $n$ is **independent of $R$** — depends only on roughness.

### Critical Depth (Rectangular)
- ✅ $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3}$.
- ✅ $V_c = \sqrt{g y_c}$.
- ✅ $Fr = 1$ at critical flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aids:**
> - **"Mnemonic for $Fr$":** "**S**low **C**alm **F**r = $1$ **S**wift **S**hot" → Subcritical ($Fr<1$), Critical ($Fr=1$), Supercritical ($Fr>1$).
> - **Manning's $n$:** "**S**mooth = **S**mall $n$" — smoother surfaces have smaller $n$ values.
> - **Parshall Flume:** "**P**arshall = **P**arallel **T**hroat" — the throat has parallel walls.
> - **Siphon:** "**S**iphon = **S**ummit is the **S**ickest point" — lowest pressure, cavitation risk.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Most Economical Section of Channels: Rectangular, Trapezoidal, Triangular and Circular Channel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Most Economical Section of Channels: Rectangular, Trapezoidal, Triangular and Circular Channel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In open channel hydraulics, a channel section is termed **most economical** (also called a **hydraulically efficient section**) when it yields the **maximum discharge for a given cross-sectional area, bed slope, and roughness coefficient**, or equivalently, when the **wetted perimeter is minimum for a given cross-sectional area**.

Mathematically, for a uniform flow governed by **Chezy's formula** or **Manning's equation**:

$$Q = A \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S}$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} \cdot A \cdot R^{2/3} \cdot S^{1/2}$$

Since $R = A/P$, the discharge is maximum when $A/P$ is maximum. With $A$ held constant, this reduces to minimizing $P$. Therefore, the **fundamental condition** is:

$$\frac{dP}{dy} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d^2P}{dy^2} > 0$$

where $y$ is the depth of flow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A channel is most economical when the wetted perimeter is minimum for a given area. The most economical condition is obtained by differentiating the wetted perimeter $P$ with respect to the depth of flow $d$, and equating the result to zero.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are constructing a small irrigation channel in your college campus to carry a fixed volume of water (say, 100 litres per second). You have limited funds and want to use minimum construction material (concrete lining). 

The **area of cross-section** represents how much water the channel can hold, while the **wetted perimeter** represents the contact length of water with the channel boundary (which determines the construction cost and the frictional resistance).

If you build a very deep, narrow channel — even though the area is the same — the wetted perimeter is huge, meaning more material and more friction loss. If you make it perfectly proportioned (the most economical shape), you get the **maximum discharge with the least boundary contact** — saving material, money, and energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Definition:** A most economical channel is the *most efficient* shape that delivers maximum water using minimum boundary surface — like choosing the *most aerodynamic* shape for a car to use less fuel.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics Recalled

The following fundamental variables govern channel geometry:

- **A** = Cross-sectional area of flow ($m^2$)
- **P** = Wetted perimeter ($m$)
- **R** = Hydraulic radius ($m$), where $R = A/P$
- **d** or **y** = Depth of flow ($m$)
- **B** = Bed width ($m$)
- **b** = Side slope of channel (horizontal : 1 vertical)
- **T** = Top width of free water surface ($m$)
- **L** = Length of sloping side ($m$)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Minimum Wetted Perimeter?

From **Chezy's formula**:

$$Q = A \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S} = A \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{\frac{A}{P} \cdot S}$$

For a given area $A$, bed slope $S$, and Chezy's constant $C$:

$$Q \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}}$$

So $Q$ is **maximum** when $P$ is **minimum**. This is the central pillar of most-economical analysis.

## 2.2 Geometric Building Blocks

### 2.2.1 Trapezoidal Channel — The General Case

A trapezoidal channel is the most general and practically relevant shape:

- **Cross-sectional area:** $A = (B + by)d$
- **Wetted perimeter:** $P = B + 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$
- **Top width:** $T = B + 2by$
- **Length of sloping side:** $L = d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$

### 2.2.2 Rectangular Channel ($b = 0$)

- **Area:** $A = B \cdot d$
- **Wetted Perimeter:** $P = B + 2d$

### 2.2.3 Triangular Channel ($B = 0$)

- **Area:** $A = b \cdot d^2$
- **Wetted Perimeter:** $P = 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$

### 2.2.4 Circular Channel

- **Area:** $A = r^2 \left( \pi - \frac{\theta}{2} + \frac{\sin\theta}{2} \right)$
- **Wetted Perimeter:** $P = r(2\pi - \theta)$

where $\theta$ is the central angle subtended by the free water surface at the top, and $r$ is the radius.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Channel Type | Most Economical Condition | Hydraulic Radius $R$ | Key Geometric Relation |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rectangular** | $B = 2d$ | $R = d/2$ | Area $A = 2d^2$, $P = 4d$ |
| **Trapezoidal** | Top width $T = 2 \times$ Sloping side $L$ | $R = d/2$ | $A = d^2(2\sqrt{1+b^2} - b)$ |
| **Triangular** | $b = 1$ (half-angle $= 45^\circ$) | $R = d / (2\sqrt{2})$ | $A = d^2$, $P = 2\sqrt{2} \, d$ |
| **Circular (just full)** | $d = 2r$ (Depth $=$ Diameter) | $R = r/2 = D/4$ | $A = \pi r^2$, $P = 2\pi r$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Universal Result:** For any most economical channel (rectangular, trapezoidal, circular flowing just full), the **hydraulic radius $R = d/2$**. For triangular, $R = d/(2\sqrt{2})$.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The most economical section principle is used in:

1. **Irrigation canals** — to minimize lining cost (concrete or earthwork).
2. **Sewers and stormwater drains** — for efficient flow with smaller diameters.
3. **Power canals** — to reduce head loss and increase turbine input.
4. **Navigation channels** — to ensure minimum excavation with maximum carrying capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Most Economical Rectangular Channel

**Step 1:** Write the basic geometric relations for a rectangular channel ($b = 0$).

$$A = B \cdot d \quad \text{and} \quad P = B + 2d$$

**Step 2:** Express the bed width $B$ in terms of $A$ and $d$.

$$B = \frac{A}{d}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $B$ into the perimeter equation to express $P$ purely in terms of $d$ (with $A$ held constant).

$$P = \frac{A}{d} + 2d$$

**Step 4:** Apply the most economical condition $dP/dd = 0$.

$$\frac{dP}{dd} = -\frac{A}{d^2} + 2 = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for the depth $d$.

$$2 = \frac{A}{d^2} \implies d^2 = \frac{A}{2} \implies d = \sqrt{\frac{A}{2}}$$

**Step 6:** Find the bed width $B$.

$$B = \frac{A}{d} = \frac{A}{\sqrt{A/2}} = \sqrt{2A}$$

**Step 7:** Establish the relation $B = 2d$.

$$B = \sqrt{2A} = \sqrt{2 \cdot 2d^2} = 2d$$

**Step 8:** Compute the hydraulic radius and wetted perimeter.

$$A = B \cdot d = 2d^2$$

$$P = B + 2d = 2d + 2d = 4d$$

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{2d^2}{4d} = \frac{d}{2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Geometric Insight:** For a most economical rectangular channel, the bed width is exactly **twice the depth**, and the cross-section is inscribed in a half-circle of radius $d$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Most Economical Trapezoidal Channel

**Step 1:** Write the basic relations.

$$A = (B + by)d \quad \text{and} \quad P = B + 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$$

**Step 2:** From the area equation, isolate $B$.

$$B = \frac{A}{d} - by$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the perimeter equation.

$$P = \frac{A}{d} - by + 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$$

**Step 4:** Differentiate $P$ with respect to $d$ and equate to zero. Here $b$ is treated as a constant (fixed by soil/stability conditions), and $A$ is constant.

$$\frac{dP}{dd} = -\frac{A}{d^2} - b + 2\sqrt{1 + b^2} = 0$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $A/d^2$.

$$\frac{A}{d^2} = 2\sqrt{1 + b^2} - b$$

$$A = d^2 \left( 2\sqrt{1 + b^2} - b \right)$$

**Step 6:** Recall that $B + by = A/d$, so:

$$B + by = d\left( 2\sqrt{1 + b^2} - b \right)$$

**Step 7:** Solve for $B$ explicitly.

$$B = 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2} - 2bd$$

**Step 8:** Compute the top width $T$.

$$T = B + 2by = 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2} - 2bd + 2bd = 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$$

**Step 9:** Compare the length of sloping side $L = d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$ with the top width.

$$T = 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2} = 2L$$

**Step 10:** Compute the hydraulic radius.

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{d^2 \left(2\sqrt{1 + b^2} - b\right)}{4d\sqrt{1 + b^2} - 2bd} = \frac{d^2 \left(2\sqrt{1 + b^2} - b\right)}{2d \left(2\sqrt{1 + b^2} - b\right)} = \frac{d}{2}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Result (Most Tested):** For a most economical trapezoidal channel, the **length of each sloping side is exactly half the top width** ($L = T/2$). The trapezoid can be inscribed in a half-circle of radius $d$ (just like the rectangular case).

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Most Economical Triangular Channel

**Step 1:** Write the basic relations for a triangular channel ($B = 0$).

$$A = b \cdot d^2 \quad \text{and} \quad P = 2d\sqrt{1 + b^2}$$

**Step 2:** From the area equation, express $b$ in terms of $A$ and $d$.

$$b = \frac{A}{d^2}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute $b$ into the perimeter equation.

$$P = 2d \sqrt{1 + \frac{A^2}{d^4}}$$

**Step 4:** Apply $dP/dd = 0$. Using the chain rule:

$$\frac{dP}{dd} = 2\sqrt{1 + \frac{A^2}{d^4}} + 2d \cdot \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1 + A^2/d^4}} \cdot \left( -\frac{4A^2}{d^5} \right) = 0$$

**Step 5:** Simplify by letting $f = 1 + A^2/d^4$.

$$2\sqrt{f} - \frac{4A^2}{d^4 \sqrt{f}} = 0$$

**Step 6:** Multiply through by $\sqrt{f}$ and rearrange.

$$2f = \frac{4A^2}{d^4} \implies 1 + \frac{A^2}{d^4} = \frac{2A^2}{d^4}$$

**Step 7:** Solve for the relation.

$$\frac{A^2}{d^4} = 1 \implies A = d^2$$

**Step 8:** Since $A = bd^2$, equate to find $b$.

$$bd^2 = d^2 \implies b = 1$$

**Step 9:** Compute the other geometric parameters.

$$A = d^2, \quad P = 2d\sqrt{1+1} = 2\sqrt{2} \, d$$

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{d^2}{2\sqrt{2} \, d} = \frac{d}{2\sqrt{2}}$$

**Step 10:** Interpret geometrically.

Since $b = 1$ (1 horizontal : 1 vertical), the half-angle of the triangular section with the vertical is:

$$\tan \phi = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \implies \phi = 45^\circ$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The most economical triangular channel has its sides inclined at $45^\circ$ to the vertical (i.e., the angle at the apex is $90^\circ$).

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Most Economical Circular Channel

A circular channel is most economical (hydraulically efficient) when it flows **just full**, since at this stage the entire perimeter is wetted and utilized efficiently, and $R$ is maximum.

**Step 1:** When the circular channel flows full, depth $d = 2r = D$ (diameter).

**Step 2:** Compute the area and perimeter.

$$A = \pi r^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$P = 2\pi r = \pi D$$

**Step 3:** Compute the hydraulic radius.

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{\pi r^2}{2\pi r} = \frac{r}{2} = \frac{D}{4}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison Insight:** A circular section flowing just full has $R = D/4$, which is **twice** the hydraulic radius of a most-economical rectangular channel ($R = d/2$ where $d = D/2$ for the same area, giving $R = D/4$). This makes circular sections inherently efficient.

**Note on Partially Filled Circular Channels:** For partially filled circular sewers and drains:

- **Maximum velocity** occurs at depth $d \approx 0.81 \, D$
- **Maximum discharge** occurs at depth $d \approx 0.95 \, D$

These are practical design considerations for storm sewers (which are designed to flow at maximum discharge just below the full state to allow for surcharge).

---

## 3.5 Summary Analytical Matrix

| Section Type | $A$ | $P_{\min}$ | $R$ | Best Geometric Identifier |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Rectangular | $2d^2$ | $4d$ | $d/2$ | $B = 2d$ |
| Trapezoidal | $d^2(2\sqrt{1+b^2}-b)$ | $2d(2\sqrt{1+b^2}-b)$ | $d/2$ | $L = T/2$ |
| Triangular | $d^2$ | $2\sqrt{2} d$ | $d/(2\sqrt{2})$ | Half-angle $= 45^\circ$ |
| Circular (just full) | $\pi r^2$ | $2\pi r$ | $r/2$ | $d = 2r$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — Most Economical Section Analysis Procedure

The following Mermaid block renders a detailed functional flow diagram of the analysis procedure used to derive the most economical condition for any channel section.

```mermaid
graph TD
    startA[Start: Channel Section Type Given] --> startB[Express A and P Geometrically]
    startB --> startC[Eliminate One Variable Using A = constant]
    startC --> startD[Express P in Terms of Depth d Only]
    startD --> startE[Apply Condition: dP/dd = 0]
    startE --> startF[Obtain Relationship Among B, d, b]
    startF --> startG[Verify d2P/dd2 is Positive]
    startG --> startH{Section Type?}
    startH --> rec[Rectangular: B = 2d]
    startH --> trap[Trapezoidal: T = 2 times L, R = d/2]
    startH --> tri[Triangular: b = 1, half-angle 45 degrees]
    startH --> circ[Circular: d = 2r, R = r/2]
    rec --> result[Final Geometric Proportions]
    trap --> result
    tri --> result
    circ --> result
    result --> endA[Compute Discharge Using Chezy or Manning]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Every channel type follows the **same three-step method** — express $A$, eliminate variables, and minimize $P$. The only difference is the starting geometry.

## 4.2 Geometric Comparison of Most Economical Sections

The following Mermaid block renders a comparative block diagram showing the four channel types and their defining geometric conditions.

```mermaid
graph LR
    rect[Rectangular Channel] --> rectC[B equals 2d]
    rectC --> rectR[Hydraulic Radius R equals d/2]

    trap[Trapezoidal Channel] --> trapC[Sloping Side L equals T divided by 2]
    trapC --> trapR[Hydraulic Radius R equals d/2]

    tri[Triangular Channel] --> triC[Side Slope b equals 1]
    triC --> triR[Half Angle equals 45 degrees]

    circ[Circular Channel] --> circC[Depth d equals 2r]
    circC --> circR[Hydraulic Radius R equals r/2]

    rectR --> common[Common Theme: R proportional to d]
    trapR --> common
    triR --> common
    circR --> common
```

## 4.3 Cross-Section Schematic (Block Architecture View)

Since a physical drawing of cross-sections is complex, we present a **block-level functional architecture flow** mapping the geometric relationships:

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Cross Section A] --> B[Top Width T]
    A --> C[Depth d]
    A --> D[Wetted Perimeter P]
    B --> E[Free Water Surface]
    C --> F[Channel Bed]
    D --> G[Bed Portion B]
    D --> H[Sloping Sides: 2 times L]
    G --> I[Total P equals B plus 2L]
    H --> I
    F --> J[Most Economical: P Minimum]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Hydraulic Radius R equals A divided by P]
    K --> L[Discharge Q from Chezy or Manning]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Regardless of channel type, the most economical section is one where $P$ is minimum for a given $A$, leading to **maximum $R$** and **maximum $Q$**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define a most economical section of an open channel.**

**Model Answer:**

A channel section is called the **most economical** (or **hydraulically efficient**) when, for a given cross-sectional area $A$, bed slope $S$, and roughness coefficient, it **passes the maximum discharge**.

Equivalently, since $Q = AC\sqrt{RS}$ and $R = A/P$, the discharge is maximum when the **wetted perimeter $P$ is minimum** for a given $A$. The condition is:

$$\frac{dP}{dd} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{d^2P}{dd^2} > 0$$

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the conditions for a hydraulically efficient trapezoidal channel section.**

**Model Answer:**

For a most economical trapezoidal channel of bed width $B$, depth $d$, and side slope $b$ (horizontal : 1 vertical), the conditions are:

1. **Length of each sloping side is half the top width:**

$$L = \frac{T}{2} \implies d\sqrt{1 + b^2} = \frac{B + 2by}{2}$$

2. **The hydraulic radius equals half the depth:**

$$R = \frac{d}{2}$$

3. **The cross-section can be inscribed in a semicircle of radius $d$.**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with two sub-parts (a) and (b), typically 7 marks each.

### Choice A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

#### Part (a): Derive the conditions for the most economical rectangular channel section. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

For a rectangular channel:

$$A = B \cdot d, \quad P = B + 2d$$

From $A$: $B = A/d$. Substituting:

$$P = \frac{A}{d} + 2d$$

Differentiating $P$ with respect to $d$ and equating to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{dd} = -\frac{A}{d^2} + 2 = 0 \implies d = \sqrt{\frac{A}{2}}$$

Therefore:

$$B = 2d, \quad A = 2d^2, \quad P = 4d, \quad R = \frac{d}{2}$$

**Result:** A rectangular channel is most economical when its **bed width equals twice the depth**, and the section is inscribed in a semicircle of radius $d$.

#### Part (b): A rectangular channel has a bed width of 4 m and a depth of flow of 2 m. Calculate the discharge if the bed slope is 1 in 1500 and Manning's roughness coefficient $n = 0.025$. Check whether the section is most economical. (7 Marks)

**Given:**

- $B = 4$ m, $d = 2$ m, $S = 1/1500$, $n = 0.025$

**Step 1: Compute the area and wetted perimeter.**

$$A = B \cdot d = 4 \times 2 = 8 \, m^2$$

$$P = B + 2d = 4 + 4 = 8 \, m$$

**Step 2: Compute the hydraulic radius.**

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{8}{8} = 1 \, m$$

**Step 3: Apply Manning's equation.**

$$Q = \frac{1}{n} A R^{2/3} S^{1/2}$$

$$Q = \frac{1}{0.025} \times 8 \times (1)^{2/3} \times \left(\frac{1}{1500}\right)^{1/2}$$

$$Q = 40 \times 8 \times 1 \times 0.02582$$

$$Q = 8.262 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 4: Check the most economical condition.**

For a most economical rectangular channel, $B = 2d = 2 \times 2 = 4$ m.

Since the given $B = 4$ m **equals** $2d$, the section **is most economical**. ✓

---

### Choice B: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

#### Part (a): Derive the conditions for the most economical trapezoidal channel section and show that the sloping side equals half the top width. (7 Marks)

**Solution:**

For a trapezoidal channel:

$$A = (B + by)d, \quad P = B + 2d\sqrt{1+b^2}$$

From $A$: $B = A/d - by$. Substituting:

$$P = \frac{A}{d} - by + 2d\sqrt{1+b^2}$$

Differentiating:

$$\frac{dP}{dd} = -\frac{A}{d^2} - b + 2\sqrt{1+b^2} = 0$$

$$\implies A = d^2 \left(2\sqrt{1+b^2} - b\right)$$

The top width:

$$T = B + 2by = 2d\sqrt{1+b^2} - 2bd + 2bd = 2d\sqrt{1+b^2}$$

The length of the sloping side:

$$L = d\sqrt{1+b^2}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{L = \frac{T}{2}}$$

This proves that the sloping side equals half the top width. The hydraulic radius:

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{d}{2}$$

#### Part (b): A trapezoidal channel with side slopes 1.5 H : 1 V has a bed width of 6 m and a depth of 3 m. Compute the discharge for a bed slope of 1 in 2000 using Chezy's constant $C = 60$. Check if the section is most economical. (7 Marks)

**Given:**

- $B = 6$ m, $d = 3$ m, $b = 1.5$, $S = 1/2000$, $C = 60$

**Step 1: Compute the area.**

$$A = (B + by)d = (6 + 1.5 \times 3) \times 3 = (6 + 4.5) \times 3 = 31.5 \, m^2$$

**Step 2: Compute the wetted perimeter.**

$$P = B + 2d\sqrt{1+b^2} = 6 + 2 \times 3 \times \sqrt{1 + 1.5^2}$$

$$P = 6 + 6 \times \sqrt{1 + 2.25} = 6 + 6 \times \sqrt{3.25} = 6 + 6 \times 1.8028 = 6 + 10.817 = 16.817 \, m$$

**Step 3: Compute the hydraulic radius.**

$$R = \frac{A}{P} = \frac{31.5}{16.817} = 1.873 \, m$$

**Step 4: Compute the discharge using Chezy's formula.**

$$Q = A \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{R \cdot S}$$

$$Q = 31.5 \times 60 \times \sqrt{1.873 \times 0.0005}$$

$$Q = 31.5 \times 60 \times \sqrt{0.0009365}$$

$$Q = 31.5 \times 60 \times 0.0306 = 57.84 \, m^3/s$$

**Step 5: Check the most economical condition.**

For a most economical trapezoidal section: $L = T/2$.

- $L = d\sqrt{1+b^2} = 3 \times 1.8028 = 5.408$ m
- $T = B + 2by = 6 + 2 \times 1.5 \times 3 = 6 + 9 = 15$ m
- $T/2 = 7.5$ m

Since $L = 5.408$ m $\neq$ $T/2 = 7.5$ m, the section is **NOT most economical**. ✗

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $b$ with side slope notation:** Some students write $b$ as vertical/horizontal, but the KTU convention is **horizontal : 1 vertical**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the second derivative check:** When asked for *most economical* (not just stationary), KTU examiners often want verification that $d^2P/dd^2 > 0$ to confirm it's a minimum.
>
> 3. **Mismatched units:** Using $C$ in SI units and slope as percentage is a frequent error. Always ensure $S$ is a **dimensionless ratio** (e.g., 1/1500, not "1 in 1500 m").
>
> 4. **Skipping the wetted-perimeter-minimization step in trapezoidal derivation:** The differentiation must be with respect to $d$ (depth), treating $b$ as a **constant** fixed by soil/stability conditions. Treating $b$ as a variable is incorrect unless specifically asked to optimize $b$ too.
>
> 5. **Circular channel — stating "most economical only when full":** This is partially correct, but you should add that the **most economical condition for a circular section is when the cross-section is symmetric and flowing just full**, i.e., $d = 2r$. Practically, sewers are designed for max discharge at $d \approx 0.95D$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core principle:** Most economical section = **minimum wetted perimeter for a given cross-sectional area**.
- **Condition:** $dP/dd = 0$ and $d^2P/dd^2 > 0$.
- **Rectangular:** $B = 2d$, $R = d/2$, $A = 2d^2$.
- **Trapezoidal:** $L = T/2$, $R = d/2$, $A = d^2(2\sqrt{1+b^2} - b)$.
- **Triangular:** $b = 1$ (1H : 1V), $R = d/(2\sqrt{2})$, half-angle $= 45^\circ$.
- **Circular (just full):** $d = 2r$, $R = r/2 = D/4$.
- **Universal feature:** All most economical sections (except triangular) yield $R = d/2$.
- **Discharge formulas:** $Q = AC\sqrt{RS}$ (Chezy) or $Q = \frac{1}{n}AR^{2/3}S^{1/2}$ (Manning).
- **Best efficiency:** A circular section flowing just full has the **highest hydraulic efficiency** of all standard shapes.
- **Practical design:** Real channels must also satisfy **non-silting, non-scouring**, and **stability** conditions — pure hydraulic efficiency is only one criterion.
- **KTU Quick Check:** For any most economical section, the **hydraulic radius is always exactly half the depth** (rectangular, trapezoidal, circular). For triangular, it is $d/(2\sqrt{2})$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Energy, Critical Depth and Hydraulic Jump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Specific Energy, Critical Depth and Hydraulic Jump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Specific Energy (E)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Specific energy in an open channel is defined as the total energy per unit weight of the fluid measured with respect to the channel bed at a particular section.*

Mathematically, for any channel section carrying a discharge **Q**:

$$E = y + \alpha \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

where:
- **y** = depth of flow (m)
- **V** = mean velocity = Q/A (m/s)
- **α** = kinetic energy correction factor (Coriolis coefficient) — **assumed = 1** for turbulent flow in KTU problems
- **g** = 9.81 m/s²

For a **rectangular channel** of width **b**, using discharge per unit width **q = Q/b**, and noting **A = by**:

$$E = y + \frac{q^{2}}{2g\,y^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Roller Coaster of Channel"**
> Imagine a biker rolling down a hill into a valley. The total energy is *kinetic + potential*. If the biker descends further (depth **y** increases), they SLOW DOWN (kinetic energy reduces). At the bottom of the valley, the biker is at MINIMUM total energy (the curve dips down). That bottom point is exactly the **critical depth** in open channel flow! Specific energy tells us that for the SAME discharge **Q**, water can flow at two different depths — one shallow and fast (supercritical), one deep and slow (subcritical) — or at exactly one unique critical depth where the energy is minimum.

---

## 1.2 Critical Depth (yc)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Critical depth is the depth of flow at which the specific energy is minimum for a given discharge.* At critical depth, the **Froude number = 1**.

**Froude Number (Fr)** — a dimensionless number that classifies the flow regime:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gD}}$$

where **D = A/T** is the **hydraulic depth** (or mean hydraulic depth), and **T** is the top width.

| Flow Regime | Froude Number | Behaviour |
|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| **Subcritical** | Fr < 1 | Slow, deep; ripples move UPSTREAM |
| **Critical** | Fr = 1 | Flow at minimum specific energy |
| **Supercritical** | Fr > 1 | Fast, shallow; ripples move DOWNSTREAM |

---

## 1.3 Hydraulic Jump

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *A hydraulic jump is a rapidly varied flow phenomenon in open channels where a sudden and turbulent transition occurs from supercritical (Fr > 1) to subcritical (Fr < 1) flow, accompanied by significant turbulence, air entrainment, surface rollers, and energy dissipation.*

It is essentially a **stationary shock wave** in shallow water — analogous to a shockwave in compressible gas dynamics.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The "Breaking Wave"**
> Picture a surfer riding a fast, shallow wave toward a beach. As the water depth suddenly increases near the shore, the wave "trips" and crashes violently into white, churning foam — losing huge amounts of kinetic energy as heat and sound. That foamy, turbulent wall of water IS a hydraulic jump. Engineers deliberately build these jumps below spillways and sluice gates to **dissipate destructive energy** and protect downstream structures.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Specific Energy Curve (E–y Diagram)

For a constant discharge **Q** in a rectangular channel, plotting **E vs y** gives the famous **specific energy curve** (first plotted by Ludwig Prandtl-style analysis; studied extensively by Bakhmeteff in 1912):

- The curve has **two limbs** meeting at a single minimum point.
- **Upper limb (right side, deep water)** → subcritical flow (Fr < 1)
- **Lower limb (left side, shallow water)** → supercritical flow (Fr > 1)
- **Minimum point** → critical depth (Fr = 1)

### Mathematical Derivation of Minimum E

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^{2}}{g\,y^{3}}$$

Setting **dE/dy = 0**:

$$y_{c}^{3} = \frac{q^{2}}{g} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}$$

Since **d²E/dy² = 3q²/(gy⁴) > 0**, this critical depth is a **true minimum**.

### Minimum Specific Energy

Substituting back:

$$E_{c} = y_{c} + \frac{q^{2}}{2g\,y_{c}^{2}} = y_{c} + \frac{y_{c}}{2} = \frac{3}{2}y_{c}$$

$$\boxed{E_{c} = \frac{3}{2}y_{c} = \frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}}$$

---

## 2.2 Alternate Depths (Conjugate Depths)

For a given **E** and **Q**, there are **two possible depths** — one on each limb of the E–y curve. These are called **alternate depths**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Board Trick:** If a problem says *"alternate depth"*, the student must remember: **yc is the GEOMETRIC MEAN of alternate depths only when the two depths are equal (degenerate case).** For different alternate depths, **yc is NOT the arithmetic mean.**

---

## 2.3 Critical Depth for Non-Rectangular Sections

For any channel section:

$$\frac{Q^{2}\,T}{g\,A^{3}} = 1 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \frac{A^{3}}{T} = \frac{Q^{2}}{g}$$

For a **trapezoidal section** (bottom width b, side slope m horizontal:1 vertical):

$$A = (b + m y)\,y, \quad T = b + 2 m y$$

These yield a cubic equation in **yc** that is solved iteratively in KTU examinations.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — 90% of Part A & Part B problems need these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|----------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Specific Energy | $E = y + \dfrac{V^{2}}{2g}$ | m |
| 2 | Specific Energy (Rectangular) | $E = y + \dfrac{q^{2}}{2g\,y^{2}}$ | $q = Q/b$ in m²/s |
| 3 | Critical Depth (Rectangular) | $y_{c} = \left(\dfrac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}$ | m |
| 4 | Min Specific Energy | $E_{c} = \dfrac{3}{2}y_{c}$ | m |
| 5 | Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{V}{\sqrt{gD}},\ D = A/T$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Critical Condition (General) | $\dfrac{Q^{2}T}{gA^{3}} = 1$ | or $V^{2} = gA/T$ |
| 7 | Critical Slope | $S_{c} = \dfrac{g\,n^{2}}{R_{c}^{1/3}}\cdot\dfrac{A_{c}}{T_{c}}$ | m/m |
| 8 | Belanger Eq. (Sequent Depth) | $\dfrac{y_{2}}{y_{1}} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1+8Fr_{1}^{2}}-1\right)$ | Rectangular |
| 9 | Conjugate Depth Relation | $y_{1}y_{2}(y_{1}+y_{2}) = \dfrac{2q^{2}}{g}$ | Rectangular |
| 10 | Energy Loss in Jump | $\Delta E = \dfrac{(y_{2}-y_{1})^{3}}{4y_{1}y_{2}}$ | m |
| 11 | Jump Height | $h_{j} = y_{2} - y_{1}$ | m |
| 12 | Jump Length (Steady) | $L_{j} \approx 6.1\,h_{j}$ | m (empirical) |
| 13 | Power Dissipated | $P = \rho g Q \Delta E$ | W |
| 14 | Jump Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{2}}{E_{1}} = 1 - \dfrac{\Delta E}{E_{1}}$ | fraction (0–1) |

### Practical Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> - **Spillways & Weirs:** Hydraulic jumps are designed below dam spillways to dissipate the destructive energy of falling water before it can scour the riverbed.
> - **Sluice Gates:** Specific energy analysis predicts the depth downstream of a gate given the upstream depth and discharge.
> - **Stilling Basins:** Engineers (USBR Type I–IV, SAF, etc.) design basins that FORCE a hydraulic jump to occur over a short length, protecting the channel.
> - **Steep Channels:** Supercritical flow on mountain spillways must transition safely to subcritical flow in plain terrain.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Critical Depth in a Rectangular Channel

**Given:** Discharge per unit width **q** in a rectangular channel of width **b**.

The total energy per unit weight (specific energy) at a section:

$$E = y + \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

Substituting **V = q/y** (since **A = by**, so **V = Q/A = (qb)/(by) = q/y**):

$$E = y + \frac{q^{2}}{2g\,y^{2}}$$

**Step 1 — Differentiate E with respect to y** (treating q and g as constants):

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(y\right) + \frac{d}{dy}\left(\frac{q^{2}}{2g}y^{-2}\right) = 1 - \frac{2q^{2}}{2g\,y^{3}}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify:**

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^{2}}{g\,y^{3}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the condition for minimum E:** **dE/dy = 0**

$$1 - \frac{q^{2}}{g\,y_{c}^{3}} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for yc:**

$$\frac{q^{2}}{g\,y_{c}^{3}} = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{c}^{3} = \frac{q^{2}}{g}$$

$$\boxed{y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}}$$

**Step 5 — Confirm this is a minimum (not a maximum):**

$$\frac{d^{2}E}{dy^{2}} = \frac{d}{dy}\left(1 - \frac{q^{2}}{g}y^{-3}\right) = 0 + \frac{3q^{2}}{g\,y^{4}} = \frac{3q^{2}}{g\,y^{4}} > 0$$

Since the second derivative is **positive for all y > 0**, the stationary point is a **true minimum**, confirming **yc is the depth of minimum specific energy**.

**Step 6 — Compute the minimum specific energy Ec:**

$$E_{c} = y_{c} + \frac{q^{2}}{2g\,y_{c}^{2}}$$

Using $q^{2} = g\,y_{c}^{3}$:

$$E_{c} = y_{c} + \frac{g\,y_{c}^{3}}{2g\,y_{c}^{2}} = y_{c} + \frac{y_{c}}{2} = \frac{3y_{c}}{2}$$

$$\boxed{E_{c} = \frac{3}{2}y_{c} \approx 1.5\,y_{c}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Belanger Equation (Sequent Depth Ratio) in a Rectangular Channel

**Given:** A hydraulic jump occurs in a horizontal, rectangular, prismatic channel of width **b** with negligible friction. Upstream (supercritical) depth = **y₁**, downstream (subcritical) depth = **y₂**, discharge per unit width = **q**.

We apply the **momentum equation** (Newton's 2nd law) to a control volume enclosing the jump.

**Step 1 — Forces on the control volume (per unit width):**

The hydrostatic pressure force on a vertical face in a rectangular channel of width **b**:

$$F = \rho g \bar{y} A = \rho g \left(\frac{y}{2}\right)(by) = \frac{\rho g\,y^{2}}{2}\cdot\frac{1}{b}\cdot b = \frac{\rho g\,y^{2}}{2}$$

Per unit width: $F/b = \dfrac{\rho g y^{2}}{2}$

**Step 2 — Momentum flux per unit width:**

Rate of momentum entering = $\rho q V_{1}$  
Rate of momentum leaving = $\rho q V_{2}$

**Step 3 — Apply momentum equation** (steady, horizontal, no friction):

$$\frac{\rho g\,y_{1}^{2}}{2} - \frac{\rho g\,y_{2}^{2}}{2} = \rho q\,(V_{2} - V_{1})$$

Divide through by **ρ**:

$$\frac{g}{2}\left(y_{1}^{2} - y_{2}^{2}\right) = q(V_{2} - V_{1})$$

**Step 4 — Use continuity:** $V_{1} = q/y_{1}$ and $V_{2} = q/y_{2}$

$$\frac{g}{2}(y_{1}^{2} - y_{2}^{2}) = q\left(\frac{q}{y_{2}} - \frac{q}{y_{1}}\right) = q^{2}\left(\frac{1}{y_{2}} - \frac{1}{y_{1}}\right) = q^{2}\cdot\frac{y_{1}-y_{2}}{y_{1}y_{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Factor the LHS:** $y_{1}^{2} - y_{2}^{2} = (y_{1}-y_{2})(y_{1}+y_{2})$

$$\frac{g}{2}(y_{1}-y_{2})(y_{1}+y_{2}) = q^{2}\cdot\frac{y_{1}-y_{2}}{y_{1}y_{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel (y₁ − y₂)** (note: y₁ ≠ y₂ for a jump):

$$\frac{g}{2}(y_{1}+y_{2}) = \frac{q^{2}}{y_{1}y_{2}}$$

**Step 7 — Rearrange into the standard conjugate-depth relation:**

$$\boxed{y_{1}\,y_{2}\,(y_{1}+y_{2}) = \frac{2q^{2}}{g}}$$

**Step 8 — Solve for the ratio y₂/y₁.** Divide both sides by $y_{1}^{3}$:

$$\frac{y_{2}}{y_{1}}\left(1 + \frac{y_{2}}{y_{1}}\right) = \frac{2q^{2}}{g\,y_{1}^{3}}$$

**Step 9 — Recognize the Froude number:** $Fr_{1}^{2} = \dfrac{V_{1}^{2}}{g\,y_{1}} = \dfrac{q^{2}}{g\,y_{1}^{3}}$

So the RHS becomes $2/Fr_{1}^{2}$. Let $r = y_{2}/y_{1}$:

$$r^{2} + r = \frac{2}{Fr_{1}^{2}}$$

$$r^{2} + r - \frac{2}{Fr_{1}^{2}} = 0$$

**Step 10 — Solve the quadratic** (taking the positive root):

$$r = \frac{-1 + \sqrt{1 + 8/Fr_{1}^{2}}}{2} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 8\,Fr_{1}^{2}} - 1\right)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{y_{2}}{y_{1}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1+8Fr_{1}^{2}} - 1\right)}$$

This is the celebrated **Belanger Equation** (1828).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Loss Across a Hydraulic Jump

The energy loss is:

$$\Delta E = E_{1} - E_{2} = \left(y_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g}\right) - \left(y_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g}\right)$$

**Step 1 — Substitute V = q/y:**

$$\Delta E = (y_{1} - y_{2}) + \frac{1}{2g}\left(\frac{q^{2}}{y_{1}^{2}} - \frac{q^{2}}{y_{2}^{2}}\right) = (y_{1}-y_{2}) + \frac{q^{2}}{2g}\cdot\frac{y_{2}^{2}-y_{1}^{2}}{y_{1}^{2}y_{2}^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Factor the numerator:** $y_{2}^{2} - y_{1}^{2} = (y_{2}-y_{1})(y_{2}+y_{1})$

$$\Delta E = (y_{1}-y_{2}) + \frac{q^{2}(y_{2}-y_{1})(y_{2}+y_{1})}{2g\,y_{1}^{2}y_{2}^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Use the conjugate relation** $\dfrac{q^{2}}{g} = \dfrac{y_{1}y_{2}(y_{1}+y_{2})}{2}$ :

$$\frac{q^{2}(y_{1}+y_{2})}{2g\,y_{1}^{2}y_{2}^{2}} = \frac{y_{1}y_{2}(y_{1}+y_{2})^{2}}{4y_{1}^{2}y_{2}^{2}} = \frac{(y_{1}+y_{2})^{2}}{4y_{1}y_{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$\Delta E = (y_{1}-y_{2}) + (y_{2}-y_{1})\cdot\frac{(y_{1}+y_{2})^{2}}{4y_{1}y_{2}} = (y_{2}-y_{1})\left[\frac{(y_{1}+y_{2})^{2}}{4y_{1}y_{2}} - 1\right]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the bracket:**

$$\frac{(y_{1}+y_{2})^{2} - 4y_{1}y_{2}}{4y_{1}y_{2}} = \frac{(y_{1}-y_{2})^{2}}{4y_{1}y_{2}} = \frac{(y_{2}-y_{1})^{2}}{4y_{1}y_{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Final form:**

$$\boxed{\Delta E = \frac{(y_{2}-y_{1})^{3}}{4\,y_{1}\,y_{2}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification (limiting case):** When $y_{1} = y_{2}$ (no jump, Fr₁ = 1), we get $\Delta E = 0$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Hydraulic Jumps (USBR / Chow Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The U.S. Bureau of Reclamation (USBR) classification is the most-frequently-tested topic in KTU Module 3 viva & Part A.**

| Froude Number Fr₁ | Jump Type | Surface Characteristics | Energy Dissipated |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|
| **1.0 – 1.7** | **No jump / Undular** | Standing waves, smooth rise, no breaking | < 5% |
| **1.7 – 2.5** | **Weak jump** | Small rollers develop, smooth surface | 5% – 15% |
| **2.5 – 4.5** | **Oscillating jump** | Unstable, irregular jets at the base, pulsating | 15% – 45% |
| **4.5 – 9.0** | **Steady jump** | Stable, well-defined, best for energy dissipation | 45% – 70% |
| **> 9.0** | **Strong / Choppy jump** | Very rough, large rollers, high energy loss | 70% – 85% |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Examples

### **Example 1 — Critical Depth & Specific Energy**

A rectangular channel **3 m wide** carries **18 m³/s**. Compute the **critical depth** and **minimum specific energy**.

**Solution:**

$$q = \frac{Q}{b} = \frac{18}{3} = 6\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

$$y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{6^{2}}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{36}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (3.6697)^{1/3}$$

$$\boxed{y_{c} \approx 1.539\ \text{m}}$$

$$E_{c} = \frac{3}{2}y_{c} = 1.5 \times 1.539 = 2.308\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{E_{c} \approx 2.31\ \text{m}}$$

---

### **Example 2 — Hydraulic Jump Calculation**

In a **rectangular channel 5 m wide**, the upstream depth before a hydraulic jump is **y₁ = 0.6 m** and the discharge is **Q = 35 m³/s**. Determine: (a) Froude number Fr₁, (b) sequent depth y₂, (c) energy loss ΔE, (d) power dissipated, (e) jump efficiency.

**Solution:**

$$q = \frac{35}{5} = 7\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}, \quad V_{1} = \frac{q}{y_{1}} = \frac{7}{0.6} = 11.667\ \text{m}/\text{s}$$

**(a) Froude number:**

$$Fr_{1} = \frac{V_{1}}{\sqrt{g\,y_{1}}} = \frac{11.667}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.6}} = \frac{11.667}{2.426} \approx 4.81$$

So flow is **supercritical** (Fr > 1) — a jump is physically possible.

**(b) Sequent depth (Belanger):**

$$\frac{y_{2}}{y_{1}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1+8(4.81)^{2}}-1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1 + 185.1}-1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{186.1}-1\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}(13.642 - 1) = \frac{12.642}{2} = 6.321$$

$$y_{2} = 6.321 \times 0.6 = 3.793\ \text{m} \approx 3.79\ \text{m}$$

**(c) Energy loss:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{(y_{2}-y_{1})^{3}}{4y_{1}y_{2}} = \frac{(3.793-0.6)^{3}}{4 \times 0.6 \times 3.793} = \frac{(3.193)^{3}}{9.103} = \frac{32.55}{9.103}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E \approx 3.576\ \text{m}}$$

**(d) Power dissipated:**

$$P = \rho g Q \Delta E = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 35 \times 3.576 = 1.227 \times 10^{6}\ \text{W} \approx 1.23\ \text{MW}$$

**(e) Jump efficiency:**

$$E_{1} = y_{1} + \frac{V_{1}^{2}}{2g} = 0.6 + \frac{(11.667)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.6 + 6.937 = 7.537\ \text{m}$$

$$V_{2} = \frac{q}{y_{2}} = \frac{7}{3.793} = 1.846\ \text{m}/\text{s}$$

$$E_{2} = y_{2} + \frac{V_{2}^{2}}{2g} = 3.793 + \frac{(1.846)^{2}}{19.62} = 3.793 + 0.174 = 3.967\ \text{m}$$

$$\eta = \frac{E_{2}}{E_{1}} = \frac{3.967}{7.537} = 0.5263 \approx 52.6\%$$

This corresponds to a **Steady jump** (since Fr₁ ≈ 4.81) — well suited for stilling basin design.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Specific Energy Curve

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CONSTANT Q GIVEN"] --> B["Compute q equals Q by b"]
    B --> C["PLOT E versus y for y greater than 0"]
    C --> D["Find yc from yc cubed equals q squared over g"]
    D --> E["Ec minimum equals 1.5 yc"]
    C --> F["RIGHT LIMB y greater than yc SUBCRITICAL Fr less than 1"]
    C --> G["LEFT LIMB y less than yc SUPERCRITICAL Fr greater than 1"]
    C --> H["APEX POINT y equals yc CRITICAL Fr equals 1"]
    F --> I["DEEP SLOW FLOW"]
    G --> J["SHALLOW FAST FLOW"]
    H --> K["MINIMUM ENERGY STATE"]
    I --> L["Two alternate depths share same E and Q"]
    J --> L
    L --> M["Conjugate depths form on a horizontal M curve"]
    K --> N["y_c is unique for given Q"]
```

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydraulic Jump Phenomena

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SUPERCRITICAL INLET y1 Fr1 greater than 1"] --> B["V1 HIGH KINETIC ENERGY"]
    B --> C["TURBULENT ROLLER ZONE AIR ENTRAINMENT"]
    C --> D["MOMENTUM TRANSFER HYDRAULIC JUMP CORE"]
    D --> E["SUBCRITICAL OUTLET y2 Fr2 less than 1"]
    E --> F["V2 LOW KINETIC ENERGY"]
    F --> G["ENERGY LOSS Delta E dissipated as heat and sound"]
    A --> H["Sequent depth ratio from Belanger"]
    E --> H
    H --> I["Power P equals rho g Q Delta E"]
    G --> J["Efficiency eta equals E2 over E1"]
    I --> K["STILLING BASIN DESIGN USBR TYPE I TO IV"]
    J --> K
```

---

## 4.3 Classification Flowchart — USBR Jump Types

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["GIVEN Froude Number Fr1"] --> B{"Fr1 Range"}
    B -->|"1.0 to 1.7"| C["UNDULAR JUMP Standing waves"]
    B -->|"1.7 to 2.5"| D["WEAK JUMP Smooth transition"]
    B -->|"2.5 to 4.5"| E["OSCILLATING JUMP Pulsating"]
    B -->|"4.5 to 9.0"| F["STEADY JUMP Stable and best for design"]
    B -->|"Greater than 9.0"| G["STRONG JUMP Maximum dissipation"]
    C --> H["Energy loss under 5 percent"]
    D --> I["Energy loss 5 to 15 percent"]
    E --> J["Energy loss 15 to 45 percent"]
    F --> K["Energy loss 45 to 70 percent"]
    G --> L["Energy loss 70 to 85 percent"]
```
