<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define specific energy and critical depth. State the condition for critical flow in terms of Froude number.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Specific Energy (1.5 marks):** The specific energy of flowing fluid in an open channel per unit weight at a section is defined as the sum of the depth of flow (potential head) and the velocity head (kinetic head), measured above the channel bed:

$$E = y + \frac{V^{2}}{2g}$$

**Critical Depth (1 mark):** Critical depth is the depth of flow at which the specific energy is minimum for a given discharge. At critical depth, the Froude number equals unity:

$$Fr = \frac{V}{\sqrt{gD}} = 1 \quad \text{where } D = A/T$$

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is a hydraulic jump? State the Belanger equation for a hydraulic jump in a rectangular channel.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Hydraulic Jump (1.5 marks):** A hydraulic jump is a rapidly varied, turbulent transition of flow from supercritical (Fr > 1) to subcritical (Fr < 1) condition in an open channel, accompanied by a sudden rise in water surface, intense turbulence, air entrainment, and significant energy dissipation.

**Belanger Equation (1.5 marks):** For a rectangular channel, the ratio of sequent (conjugate) depths is given by:

$$\frac{y_{2}}{y_{1}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1+8Fr_{1}^{2}} - 1\right)$$

where $y_1$ = supercritical depth (pre-jump), $y_2$ = subcritical depth (post-jump), and $Fr_1$ = upstream Froude number.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the critical depth in a rectangular channel. (7 marks)

**(b)** A rectangular channel **6 m wide** carries a discharge of **24 m³/s** at a depth of **2.5 m**. Find: (i) the specific energy, (ii) the critical depth, (iii) whether the flow is subcritical or supercritical. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

Specific energy in a rectangular channel of width **b** carrying discharge per unit width **q = Q/b**:

$$E = y + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} = y + \frac{q^{2}}{2g\,y^{2}}$$

Differentiating with respect to **y** (q constant):

$$\frac{dE}{dy} = 1 - \frac{q^{2}}{g\,y^{3}}$$

For minimum E, **dE/dy = 0**:

$$1 = \frac{q^{2}}{g\,y_{c}^{3}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad y_{c}^{3} = \frac{q^{2}}{g}$$

$$\boxed{y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3}}$$

Second derivative $\dfrac{d^{2}E}{dy^{2}} = \dfrac{3q^{2}}{g\,y^{4}} > 0$, confirming minimum.

Minimum specific energy:

$$E_{c} = \frac{3}{2}y_{c}$$

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: b = 6 m, Q = 24 m³/s, y = 2.5 m.

$$q = \frac{Q}{b} = \frac{24}{6} = 4\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

**(i) Specific energy:**

$$V = \frac{Q}{by} = \frac{24}{6 \times 2.5} = 1.6\ \text{m/s}$$

$$E = y + \frac{V^{2}}{2g} = 2.5 + \frac{(1.6)^{2}}{2 \times 9.81} = 2.5 + 0.1305 = 2.6305\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{E \approx 2.63\ \text{m}}$$

**(ii) Critical depth:**

$$y_{c} = \left(\frac{q^{2}}{g}\right)^{1/3} = \left(\frac{16}{9.81}\right)^{1/3} = (1.630)^{1/3} \approx 1.176\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{c} \approx 1.176\ \text{m}}$$

**(iii) Flow regime:** Since actual depth y = 2.5 m > yc = 1.176 m, flow is **subcritical**.

**Verification via Froude number:** $Fr = \dfrac{1.6}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 2.5}} = \dfrac{1.6}{4.952} \approx 0.323 < 1$ ✓

---

### **OPTION A — Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the specific energy curve for a rectangular channel and discuss alternate depths. (7 marks)

**(b)** A hydraulic jump occurs in a rectangular channel **4 m wide** with upstream depth **y₁ = 0.5 m** and discharge **Q = 20 m³/s**. Compute: (i) the Froude number Fr₁, (ii) the sequent depth y₂, (iii) the energy loss ΔE, and (iv) the power dissipated. (7 marks)

#### **Model Solution**

**(a) Specific Energy Curve (7 marks):**

For constant **Q** in a rectangular channel, the specific energy is:

$$E = y + \frac{q^{2}}{2g\,y^{2}}$$

The E–y curve has two limbs (see flowchart in Section 4.1):

- **Upper limb (y > yc):** Subcritical flow (Fr < 1) — water deep, slow.
- **Lower limb (y < yc):** Supercritical flow (Fr > 1) — water shallow, fast.
- **Minimum point (y = yc):** Critical flow (Fr = 1).

**Alternate Depths (2 marks):** For a given specific energy E > Ec and discharge Q, there are two depths y₁ and y₂ (one on each limb) that produce the same E. These are called **alternate depths** and they always lie on opposite sides of yc. When the channel transitions from one depth to the alternate depth, a hydraulic jump may form (if the transition is supercritical → subcritical).

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

Given: b = 4 m, Q = 20 m³/s, y₁ = 0.5 m.

$$q = \frac{Q}{b} = \frac{20}{4} = 5\ \text{m}^{2}/\text{s}$$

$$V_{1} = \frac{q}{y_{1}} = \frac{5}{0.5} = 10\ \text{m/s}$$

**(i) Froude number:**

$$Fr_{1} = \frac{V_{1}}{\sqrt{g\,y_{1}}} = \frac{10}{\sqrt{9.81 \times 0.5}} = \frac{10}{2.215} = 4.514$$

$$\boxed{Fr_{1} \approx 4.51 \quad \text{(supercritical)}}$$

**(ii) Sequent depth (Belanger):**

$$\frac{y_{2}}{y_{1}} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1+8(4.514)^{2}}-1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{1+163.0}-1\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(\sqrt{164}-1\right)$$

$$= \frac{1}{2}(12.806 - 1) = \frac{11.806}{2} = 5.903$$

$$y_{2} = 5.903 \times 0.5 = 2.95\ \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{2} \approx 2.95\ \text{m}}$$

**(iii) Energy loss:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{(y_{2}-y_{1})^{3}}{4\,y_{1}\,y_{2}} = \frac{(2.95-0.5)^{3}}{4 \times 0.5 \times 2.95} = \frac{(2.45)^{3}}{5.90} = \frac{14.71}{5.90}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta E \approx 2.493\ \text{m}}$$

**(iv) Power dissipated:**

$$P = \rho g Q \Delta E = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 20 \times 2.493 = 4.891 \times 10^{5}\ \text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 489.1\ \text{kW}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (KTU 2024 Valuation Key):**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting to convert Q → q:** KTU problems are set in rectangular channels. Always compute **q = Q/b** FIRST. Forgetting this is the #1 reason students get **zero** in the numerical part.
> 
> 2. **Sign error in Belanger equation:** The correct root is the **POSITIVE** one: $r = \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{1+8Fr_1^2} - 1)$. Students sometimes use **+1** instead of **−1**, giving a ratio > 1+ ratio of jump, leading to wrong y₂.
> 
> 3. **Conjugate depth formula misuse:** $y_1 y_2 (y_1 + y_2) = 2q^2/g$ is for **rectangular** sections ONLY. For trapezoidal channels, the momentum equation must be integrated with full hydrostatic pressure.
> 
> 4. **Confusing alternate depths and conjugate depths:**
>    - **Alternate depths** lie on the **same E–y curve** for the **same Q** (related by SPECIFIC ENERGY equation).
>    - **Conjugate (sequent) depths** are **before and after a hydraulic jump** (related by MOMENTUM equation).
>    - These are TWO DIFFERENT concepts but students often interchange them. **Lose 2–3 marks per mistake.**
> 
> 5. **Units in power dissipation:** Power is in **Watts**, not kW, unless explicitly asked. Using $\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$ and $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$ is mandatory.
> 
> 6. **Skipping the second derivative check:** Examiner often awards **0.5 mark** explicitly for proving $d^2E/dy^2 > 0$ to confirm the minimum. Always include it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pin this to your wall the night before the exam.**

- [x] **Specific energy** is total mechanical energy per unit weight above the channel bed, **excluding** the bed elevation: $E = y + V^2/(2g)$.
- [x] The **E–y curve** has two limbs (subcritical upper, supercritical lower) meeting at the **minimum Ec**.
- [x] **Critical depth** in a rectangular channel: $y_c = (q^2/g)^{1/3}$.
- [x] **Minimum specific energy:** $E_c = 1.5\,y_c$.
- [x] **Froude number** classifies flow: $Fr = V/\sqrt{gD}$, where $D = A/T$.
- [x] $Fr < 1$: subcritical (deep/slow); $Fr = 1$: critical; $Fr > 1$: supercritical (shallow/fast).
- [x] **Critical slope** = slope that sustains critical flow at normal depth; $S_c$ depends on Manning's **n** and the section geometry.
- [x] **Alternate depths:** same Q, same E, but one subcritical + one supercritical. Solve using $E = y + q^2/(2gy^2)$ by iteration or cubic.
- [x] **Hydraulic jump:** supercritical → subcritical transition; characterized by rollers, air bubbles, energy loss.
- [x] **Belanger equation (rectangular):** $y_2/y_1 = 0.5\,(\sqrt{1+8Fr_1^2} - 1)$.
- [x] **Conjugate depth relation:** $y_1 y_2 (y_1+y_2) = 2q^2/g$.
- [x] **Energy loss in jump:** $\Delta E = (y_2 - y_1)^3 / (4 y_1 y_2)$.
- [x] **Power dissipated:** $P = \rho g Q \Delta E$ in Watts.
- [x] **Jump efficiency:** $\eta = E_2/E_1 = 1 - \Delta E/E_1$.
- [x] **USBR jump classification:** Undular (1–1.7) → Weak (1.7–2.5) → Oscillating (2.5–4.5) → Steady (4.5–9) → Strong (>9).
- [x] **Steady jump** is preferred for stilling basin design (best balance of stability and dissipation).
- [x] **Jump length** for steady jump: $L_j \approx 6.1\,h_j$ where $h_j = y_2 - y_1$.
- [x] **Engineering applications:** below spillways, sluice gates, drops, Parshall flumes, energy dissipators.
- [x] **Two memorize-by-heart equations for viva:** $E_c = 1.5 y_c$ and $\Delta E = (y_2-y_1)^3/(4y_1y_2)$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pumps: Water Lifting Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Pumps: Water Lifting Devices # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a Pump? (KTU Definition) A **pump** is a **mechanical hydraulic machine** that converts the **mechanical energy** (supplied by an external prime mover such as an electric motor, diesel engine, or turbine) into **hydraulic energy** (pressure energy, kinetic energy, and potential energy) in order to **lift water from a lower level to a higher level** or to push water against a resisting pressure. In the formal KTU 2024 syllabus vocabulary: > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition:** A pump is a turbo-machine that imparts energy to a fluid (typically water) to move it from a lower elevation/pressure region to a higher elevation/pressure region, thereby increasing the fluid's total head. The two principal categories used in the KTU syllabus are: 1. **Centrifugal Pump** (Rotodynamic / Impeller-type) 2. **Reciprocating Pump** (Positive-displacement / Piston-type) --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Bucket and the Engine" Imagine you have a **deep well** and a bucket. You want to lift water to the surface. - **You dipping the bucket by hand, pulling it up, and emptying it** → that is exactly how a **reciprocating pump** works: it pushes water out in **discrete strokes** (suction → delivery → suction → delivery). - **You spin a big fan-like wheel very fast at the top of the well so that the wheel flings water outward and upward through a pipe** → that is exactly how a **centrifugal pump** works: it uses **spinning motion** (centrifugal force) to fling water outward continuously, creating a low-pressure zone at the centre that draws more water in. > [!NOTE] > **Intuition Tip:** Centrifugal pump = "spinning-fling" type. Reciprocating pump = "piston-plunger" type. Your brain should immediately map a pump type to one of these two motion patterns. --- ## 1.3 Standard Key Terms (KTU Glossary) > [!IMPORTANT] > These terms are **must-know** for any 3-mark or 14-mark question. | Term | Meaning | |---|---| | **Suction Head ($H_s$)** | Vertical distance between the water source level and the **centre line of the pump** | | **Delivery Head ($H_d$)** | Vertical distance between the **pump centre line** and the **discharge point** (delivery tank/free outlet) | | **Static Head ($H_{stat}$)** | $H_s + H_d$ – the total vertical lift when the pump is **not running** | | **Manometric Head ($H_m$)** | Head developed by the pump **across its inlet and outlet flanges** | | **Total Head ($H$)** | Sum of static head, friction losses, and velocity head: $H = H_{stat} + h_f + \frac{V^2}{2g}$ | | **NPSH** | **Net Positive Suction Head** – the absolute pressure head available at pump suction above the water's vapour pressure | | **Manometric Efficiency ($\eta_{man}$)** | Ratio of **manometric head** to **head imparted by the impeller** to water | | **Mechanical Efficiency ($\eta_{mech}$)** | Ratio of **power transmitted to water** to **shaft power** | | **Overall Efficiency ($\eta_{overall}$)** | Ratio of **useful hydraulic power output** to **power supplied by the prime mover** | --- ## 1.4 The Physical Constants We Use - Acceleration due to gravity: **$g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$** - Density of water: **$\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$** - Specific weight of water: **$w = \rho g = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$** - 1 metre of water column ≈ **$9.81 \text{ kPa}$** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Centrifugal Pump – Theory of Operation ### 2.1.1 Construction (Major Parts) A centrifugal pump has the following main parts: 1. **Impeller** – A rotating wheel with curved (usually backward-curved) vanes. The impeller has two types: - **Open impeller** (vanes open on both sides) - **Closed/Enclosed impeller** (vanes enclosed between two shrouds – used for clean water) - **Semi-open impeller** (one shroud only) 2. **Casing (Volute)** – The outer stationary chamber. It is of two types: - **Volute casing** – progressively widening spiral shape that converts kinetic energy → pressure energy - **Vortex casing** – a circular chamber with a separate volute tongue (used in multi-stage pumps) 3. **Suction Pipe** – Connects the sump/water source to the **eye (centre)** of the impeller. Often fitted with a **foot valve** (one-way non-return valve) and a **strainer** (to prevent debris entering). 4. **Delivery Pipe** – Carries water from the casing to the delivery tank. 5. **Shaft & Bearings** – The impeller is mounted on a shaft supported by bearings. 6. **Sealing** – Mechanical seal or gland packing to prevent leakage along the shaft. 7. **Priming** – The initial filling of the casing with water before starting the pump (centrifugal pumps cannot suck air). ### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step) 1. The pump casing is first **primed** (filled with water so that no air remains). 2. The impeller is rotated at high speed by the prime mover. 3. Water at the **eye (centre)** of the impeller is thrown **outward** by the action of **centrifugal force**. 4. This outward motion creates a **low-pressure zone (vacuum)** at the centre of the impeller. 5. Atmospheric pressure acting on the **free water surface in the sump** forces water up the suction pipe into this low-pressure zone. 6. As water moves outward along the impeller vanes, it gains **kinetic energy**. 7. The water then enters the **volute casing**, where the cross-section gradually **expands** (area increases), causing **velocity to decrease**. By **Bernoulli's principle**, this drop in velocity converts into a **rise in pressure** (pressure energy). 8. The pressurised water is then discharged through the **delivery pipe** to the overhead tank. ### 2.1.3 The Two-Step Energy Conversion > [!IMPORTANT] > A centrifugal pump performs energy conversion in **two distinct stages**: > 1. **Impeller:** Mechanical energy → Kinetic energy (Centrifugal action) > 2. **Casing (Volute):** Kinetic energy → Pressure energy (Diffuser action) This is why it is classified as a **rotodynamic pump** and why it delivers a **continuous, smooth flow**. ### 2.1.4 Prime Mover Power Required $$P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{1000 \times \eta_{overall}} \quad \text{(in kW)}$$ Where $Q$ is discharge in m³/s and $H$ is total head in metres. --- ## 2.2 Reciprocating Pump – Theory of Operation ### 2.2.1 Construction (Major Parts) 1. **Cylinder** – Where the piston reciprocates (back-and-forth). 2. **Piston / Plunger** – Reciprocates inside the cylinder. 3. **Suction Valve** – One-way valve on the suction side, opens only during the suction stroke. 4. **Delivery Valve** – One-way valve on the delivery side, opens only during the delivery stroke. 5. **Suction Pipe** – Connects the sump to the suction valve. 6. **Delivery Pipe** – Connects the delivery valve to the delivery tank. 7. **Crank and Connecting Rod** – Convert rotary motion of the prime mover into reciprocating motion of the piston. 8. **Air Vessel** – A closed chamber containing compressed air, fitted on both suction and delivery sides. **Purpose:** to ensure a **continuous, smooth flow** by smoothing the pulsations caused by the reciprocating action. ### 2.2.2 Working Principle (Step-by-Step) **Suction Stroke:** 1. Piston moves from TDC (Top Dead Centre) to BDC (Bottom Dead Centre). 2. The cylinder volume above the piston **increases** → pressure inside cylinder **drops** below atmospheric. 3. The **suction valve opens** (because external atmospheric pressure pushes water up the suction pipe). 4. Water enters the cylinder. **Delivery Stroke:** 1. Piston moves from BDC to TDC. 2. The cylinder volume **decreases** → pressure inside cylinder **rises** above the delivery-side pressure. 3. The **delivery valve opens** and water is pushed up the delivery pipe. 4. The **suction valve closes** (because pressure inside cylinder > atmospheric). > [!NOTE] > **Single-acting vs. Double-acting:** > - **Single-acting:** only one side of the piston does work (suction + delivery per 2 strokes). > - **Double-acting:** both sides of the piston do work (2 suctions + 2 deliveries per 2 strokes → effectively continuous flow). ### 2.2.3 Slip in a Reciprocating Pump A small amount of water leaks back from the cylinder to the suction side through the delivery valve during the delivery stroke. This is called **slip**. $$\text{Slip} = Q_{theoretical} - Q_{actual}$$ **Percentage Slip:** $$S_{\%} = \dfrac{Q_{theoretical} - Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}} \times 100$$ > [!WARNING] > Slip is **positive** when $Q_{theoretical} > Q_{actual}$ (most common case). > Slip is **negative** (called *negative slip*) when the delivery pipe is very long or the speed is very high, and the water column cannot accelerate fast enough – the delivery valve closes *late*. --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Discharge (Centrifugal) | $Q = A \times V$ | m³/s | $A$ = impeller outlet area, $V$ = velocity of flow | | 2 | Work done per second (impeller) | $W_{impeller} = \dfrac{w Q}{g} V_w u_2$ | kW | $V_w$ = whirl velocity, $u_2$ = blade velocity | | 3 | Work done per second (manometric) | $W_{man} = \rho g Q H_m$ | W | $H_m$ = manometric head | | 4 | Manometric efficiency | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_m}{\dfrac{V_w u_2}{g}}$ | – | Ratio of manometric head to Euler head | | 5 | Mechanical efficiency | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}}$ | – | Bearings, friction losses | | 6 | Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$ | – | $P_{shaft} / P_{motor}$ | | 7 | Shaft power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{1000 \eta_{mech}}$ | kW | | | 8 | Power input to motor | $P_{motor} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{1000 \eta_{overall}}$ | kW | | | 9 | Theoretical discharge (single-acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60}$ | m³/s | $A$=area, $L$=stroke, $N$=rpm | | 10 | Theoretical discharge (double-acting) | $Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60}$ | m³/s | | | 11 | Slip % | $S_{\%} = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100$ | % | | | 12 | Coefficient of discharge | $C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}}$ | – | | | 13 | Velocity of reciprocating piston | $v = \omega r (\sin\theta + \dfrac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta)$ | m/s | **SHM approximation** | | 14 | Acceleration of piston | $a = \omega^2 r (\cos\theta + \dfrac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta)$ | m/s² | For finding inertia head | | 15 | Total head (centrifugal) | $H = H_s + H_d + \dfrac{V^2}{2g} + h_f$ | m | Static + velocity + friction | --- ## 2.4 Engineering Utility > [!IMPORTANT] > **Where do these pumps get used in the real world?** - **Centrifugal Pumps** → Water supply schemes (KTU Kerala water authority projects), fire-fighting systems, irrigation pumpsets (Krishna, Kaveri, Muvattupuzha lift-irrigation schemes), HVAC, boiler feed, swimming pools. They dominate **~80% of all pumping applications** because they handle large discharges with smooth flow. - **Reciprocating Pumps** → High-pressure, low-discharge applications: oil drilling, hydraulic presses, jetting, dosing pumps for chemicals, boiler feed in thermal plants, mud pumping in construction. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Work Done by the Impeller of a Centrifugal Pump (Euler's Turbomachine Equation applied to a Pump) We consider an **incompressible fluid** flowing through the impeller. Let us set up velocity triangles at the impeller **inlet (1)** and **outlet (2)**. **Velocity Triangle Notation:** - $u_1$, $u_2$ = blade/tangential velocities at inlet and outlet - $V_{r1}$, $V_{r2}$ = relative velocities (along the vane) - $V_{f1}$, $V_{f2}$ = flow velocities (axial components, perpendicular to $u$) - $V_{w1}$, $V_{w2}$ = whirl velocities (tangential components, along $u$) - $V_1$, $V_2$ = absolute velocities ### 3.1.1 Applying Angular Momentum Principle (Euler's Theorem) Consider the control volume as the fluid mass flowing through the impeller per unit time ($= \rho Q$). The torque exerted on the fluid by the impeller is: $$T = \text{Rate of change of angular momentum} = \rho Q (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1)$$ Since $u = \omega r$, we have $u_2 r_2 = u_2 \cdot \dfrac{u_2}{\omega} = \dfrac{u_2^2}{\omega}$ (or simply $u_2 = \omega r_2$). Thus torque is: $$T = \rho Q (V_{w2} \cdot \dfrac{u_2}{\omega} - V_{w1} \cdot \dfrac{u_1}{\omega})$$ $$T \cdot \omega = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$$ The work done per second (which equals power) on the fluid is $T \omega$: $$\boxed{W = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)}$$ This is the **Euler Head Equation for a pump**. ### 3.1.2 The Case of No Pre-Whirl (Radial Entry) If the flow enters the impeller **without any whirl** (radial entry, which is the design goal): $$V_{w1} = 0$$ Therefore the equation simplifies to: $$W = \rho Q V_{w2} u_2$$ ### 3.1.3 Head Imparted to Water (Euler Head) Dividing $W$ by $\rho g Q$ gives the head the impeller imparts: $$H_{impeller} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$ For radial entry: $$H_{impeller} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ ### 3.1.4 Derivation: Manometric Efficiency The actual manometric head ($H_m$) measured across the pump is less than $H_{impeller}$ because of **vane friction** and **circulation losses** inside the impeller passages. By definition: $$\eta_{man} = \dfrac{H_m}{H_{impeller}} = \dfrac{H_m g}{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}$$ Rearranging: $$\boxed{H_m = \dfrac{\eta_{man} (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)}{g}}$$ ### 3.1.5 Derivation: Overall Efficiency The chain of power transmission in a centrifugal pump is: $$\underbrace{P_{motor}}_{\text{electrical/mechanical input}} \;\xrightarrow{\eta_{mech}}\; \underbrace{P_{shaft}}_{\text{at impeller shaft}} \;\xrightarrow{\eta_{man}}\; \underbrace{P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m}_{\text{useful hydraulic power}}$$ Therefore: $$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{man}$$ And the power required to drive the pump (motor input): $$\boxed{P_{motor} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times Q \times H_m}{\eta_{overall}} \text{ W}}$$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Discharge, Slip and Coefficient of Discharge of a Reciprocating Pump ### 3.2.1 Theoretical Discharge of a Single-Acting Reciprocating Pump **Given:** - $A$ = piston/cylinder cross-sectional area (m²) - $L$ = length of stroke (m) - $N$ = speed of crank (rpm) In **one complete revolution** of the crank: - **One suction stroke** (piston moves forward) → draws a volume $A \times L$ - **One delivery stroke** (piston moves back) → pushes out a volume $A \times L$ Total volume handled per revolution = $A L$ At $N$ revolutions per **minute**, the volume handled per minute is: $$Q_{th} = A L N \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/min)}$$ Converting to per second: $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}}$$ ### 3.2.2 Theoretical Discharge of a Double-Acting Pump In a double-acting pump, both faces of the piston do useful work. So per revolution, **2A·L** of fluid is delivered: $$\boxed{Q_{th} = \dfrac{2 A L N}{60} \quad \text{(m}^3\text{/s)}}$$ ### 3.2.3 Derivation of Slip **Definition:** Slip is the volume of fluid that leaks back from the cylinder to the suction side per unit time. Mathematically: $$S = Q_{th} - Q_{actual}$$ For a reciprocating pump fitted with air vessels (which make the flow essentially steady), the actual discharge is measured at the delivery tank: $$Q_{actual} = \dfrac{\text{Volume collected in tank}}{\text{Time taken}}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{S_{\%} = \dfrac{Q_{th} - Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} \times 100}$$ ### 3.2.4 Derivation: Coefficient of Discharge This is the ratio of actual discharge to theoretical discharge: $$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$$ For a **pump with no air vessel** (pulsating flow), the slip can be large and the discharge is unsteady. With air vessels, slip is reduced significantly. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Which Formula | Situation | Use This Formula | Reason | |---|---|---| | Centrifugal pump power required | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{overall}}$ | Total head $H$ includes static + friction + velocity head | | Centrifugal pump work (Euler) | $W = \rho Q (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)$ | Velocity triangle analysis | | Manometric head | $H_m = \eta_{man} \cdot H_{impeller}$ | Loss of head inside impeller | | Reciprocating pump $Q_{th}$ (single) | $Q_{th} = ALN/60$ | 1 delivery per revolution | | Reciprocating pump $Q_{th}$ (double) | $Q_{th} = 2ALN/60$ | 2 deliveries per revolution | | Slip calculation | $S\% = (Q_{th} - Q_{act})/Q_{th} \times 100$ | Direct definition | | Piston velocity (SHM approx) | $v = \omega r (\sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta)$ | Slider-crank mechanism | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram – Centrifugal Pump Energy Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Prime Mover
Electric Motor or Diesel Engine] -->|Shaft Power PS| B[Impeller
Rotating Wheel] B -->|Mechanical to Kinetic Energy| C[Volute Casing
Expanding Cross Section] C -->|Kinetic to Pressure Energy| D[Delivery Pipe] D --> E[Overhead Tank] F[Suction Pipe with Foot Valve and Strainer] -->|Atmospheric Pressure Pushes Water In| B classDef source fill:#E0F2FE,stroke:#0369A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef convert fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#B45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef deliver fill:#DCFCE7,stroke:#166534,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class A,F source class B,C convert class D,E deliver ``` ## 4.2 Block Architecture – Working of a Reciprocating Pump ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph SUMP [Water Sump - Lower Reservoir] WS[Free Water Surface
Subject to Atmospheric Pressure] end subgraph PUMPUNIT [Reciprocating Pump Assembly] SP[Suction Pipe with Foot Valve] SV[Suction Valve One Way] CYL[Cylinder with Piston] DV[Delivery Valve One Way] CRANK[Crank and Connecting Rod
Converts Rotary to Reciprocating Motion] AV1[Air Vessel on Suction Side] AV2[Air Vessel on Delivery Side] end subgraph DELIVERY [Upper Reservoir] DT[Delivery Tank Free Outlet] end MOTOR[Prime Mover Motor] -->|Rotary Motion| CRANK CRANK -->|Reciprocating Motion| CYL WS --> SP --> SV --> CYL --> DV --> DT AV1 --- SP AV2 --- DV classDef sump fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E3A8A,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef pump fill:#FED7AA,stroke:#9A3412,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef dest fill:#BBF7D0,stroke:#14532D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef motor fill:#FECACA,stroke:#7F1D1D,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class WS sump class SP,SV,CYL,DV,AV1,AV2,CRANK pump class DT dest class MOTOR motor ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram – Power Transmission Chain in a Centrifugal Pump ```mermaid flowchart LR M[Input to Motor
Pmotor] -->|eta overall| H[Useful Hydraulic Power
rho g Q H] M -->|eta mech| S[Shaft Power
Pshaft] S -->|eta man| W[Water Power
rho g Q Hm] W --> H classDef inout fill:#E0E7FF,stroke:#3730A3,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef loss fill:#FECACA,stroke:#991B1B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class M,H inout class S,W loss ``` ## 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram – Centrifugal vs. Reciprocating Pump ```mermaid flowchart TB P[Pumps Used for Lifting Water] --> CP[Centrifugal Pump] P --> RP[Reciprocating Pump] CP --> CP1[Principle: Centrifugal Force] CP --> CP2[Continuous Smooth Flow] CP --> CP3[Requires Priming] CP --> CP4[Low Pressure High Discharge] CP --> CP5[Used in Water Supply and Irrigation] RP --> RP1[Principle: Piston Push Pull] RP --> RP2[Pulsating Flow unless air vessel fitted] RP --> RP3[Self Priming in wet cylinder] RP --> RP4[High Pressure Low Discharge] RP --> RP5[Used in Oil Drilling and Hydraulic Presses] classDef root fill:#FEF3C7,stroke:#92400E,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef cp fill:#DBEAFE,stroke:#1E40AF,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 classDef rp fill:#FCE7F3,stroke:#831843,stroke-width:2px,color:#000000 class P root class CP,CP1,CP2,CP3,CP4,CP5 cp class RP,RP1,RP2,RP3,RP4,RP5 rp ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Define priming of a centrifugal pump. Why is it necessary?** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** Priming of a centrifugal pump is the process of filling the suction pipe, casing, and a portion of the delivery pipe completely with water (or another liquid) before starting the pump, so that no air pocket is left inside the pump assembly. **Why necessary (2 marks):** 1. A centrifugal pump cannot develop suction to lift water if air is present inside the casing, because air is much lighter than water and the impeller cannot create the required pressure difference when there is air. 2. The mass density of water is **~1000 kg/m³** while air is **~1.2 kg/m³**; the centrifugal force acting on a low-density fluid column is insufficient to generate the required vacuum. 3. Priming ensures the impeller pumps water right from the start, so the suction pipe lifts water against atmospheric pressure without any air-lock interruption. --- ### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between a centrifugal pump and a reciprocating pump (any three points).** **Model Answer:** | Sl. No. | Centrifugal Pump | Reciprocating Pump | |---|---|---| | 1 | Continuous, smooth flow delivery | Pulsating flow (unless air vessels used) | | 2 | High discharge, low pressure head | Low discharge, high pressure head | | 3 | Requires priming before starting | Self-priming when cylinder is wet | | 4 | Works on the principle of centrifugal force | Works on the principle of piston reciprocation in a cylinder | | 5 | Mechanical efficiency is high (≈ 90%) | Mechanical efficiency is moderate (≈ 70–80%) | | 6 | Suitable for large-scale water supply and irrigation | Suitable for oil drilling, hydraulic presses, chemical dosing | --- ## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks) – Internal Choice Provided ### OPTION A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Question A: A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 30 m. The diameter and width of the impeller at outlet are 0.4 m and 0.05 m respectively. If the pump runs at 1450 rpm and the manometric efficiency is 90%, determine: (a) The vane angle at the outlet. (b) The power required to drive the pump, assuming an overall efficiency of 75%.** ### Solution: **Given Data:** - Discharge, $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ - Total head, $H = 30 \text{ m}$ - Impeller outlet diameter, $D_2 = 0.4 \text{ m}$ - Impeller outlet width, $B_2 = 0.05 \text{ m}$ - Speed, $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$ - Manometric efficiency, $\eta_{man} = 0.90$ - Overall efficiency, $\eta_{overall} = 0.75$ **Step 1: Calculate the blade/tangential velocity at the outlet ($u_2$)** $$u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.4 \times 1450}{60}$$ $$u_2 = \dfrac{3.1416 \times 0.4 \times 1450}{60} = \dfrac{1822.13}{60}$$ $$u_2 = 30.37 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2: Calculate the flow velocity at the outlet ($V_{f2}$)** Area at outlet: $A_2 = \pi D_2 B_2 = \pi \times 0.4 \times 0.05 = 0.06283 \text{ m}^2$ $$V_{f2} = \dfrac{Q}{A_2} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.06283} = 0.796 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 3: Calculate the manometric head ($H_m$)** Assuming the total head $H$ given in the problem is the manometric head across the pump (as the static head alone is 30 m but without further info we treat it as manometric head): $$H_m = 30 \text{ m}$$ (If static head is 30 m, then $H_m = H \cdot \eta_{man} = 30 \times 0.9 = 27 \text{ m}$; in KTU pattern, the *head against which the pump delivers* is usually taken as $H_m$. We proceed with $H_m = 30$ m.) **Part (a) — Vane Angle at Outlet** The Euler head for radial entry ($V_{w1} = 0$): $$H_{impeller} = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$ Using manometric efficiency: $$H_m = \eta_{man} \cdot H_{impeller}$$ $$H_{impeller} = \dfrac{H_m}{\eta_{man}} = \dfrac{30}{0.90} = 33.33 \text{ m}$$ Thus: $$V_{w2} = \dfrac{H_{impeller} \cdot g}{u_2} = \dfrac{33.33 \times 9.81}{30.37} = \dfrac{326.97}{30.37} = 10.77 \text{ m/s}$$ From the outlet velocity triangle: $$\tan \phi_2 = \dfrac{V_{f2}}{V_{w2} - u_2}$$ (Note: the vane angle $\beta_2$ is measured from the tangent. For a backward-curved vane, $V_{w2} < u_2$ in reality; however, here we use the formula for the **absolute angle** $\alpha_2$ of the absolute velocity or, more commonly, the vane angle as $\tan\phi = V_f / (u - V_w)$.) $$u_2 - V_{w2} = 30.37 - 10.77 = 19.60 \text{ m/s}$$ $$\tan \phi_2 = \dfrac{0.796}{19.60} = 0.0406$$ $$\phi_2 = \tan^{-1}(0.0406) = \boxed{2.32^\circ}$$ **Part (b) — Power Required** $$P_{motor} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_m}{\eta_{overall}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 30}{0.75}$$ $$P_{motor} = \dfrac{14715}{0.75} = \boxed{19620 \text{ W} = 19.62 \text{ kW}}$$ --- ### OPTION B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Question B: A single-acting reciprocating pump has a bore of 150 mm and a stroke of 300 mm. It runs at 60 rpm. The pump delivers water to a height of 20 m. (a) Calculate the theoretical discharge and the power required. (b) If the actual discharge is 5% less than the theoretical, calculate the slip and the coefficient of discharge.** ### Solution: **Given Data:** - Bore diameter, $D = 0.150 \text{ m}$ - Stroke length, $L = 0.300 \text{ m}$ - Speed, $N = 60 \text{ rpm}$ - Delivery head, $H = 20 \text{ m}$ - Single-acting **Step 1: Cross-sectional area of piston** $$A = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times (0.150)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 0.0225}{4}$$ $$A = 0.01767 \text{ m}^2$$ **Part (a) — Theoretical Discharge** $$Q_{th} = \dfrac{A L N}{60} = \dfrac{0.01767 \times 0.300 \times 60}{60} = 0.01767 \times 0.300$$ $$Q_{th} = 0.00530 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 5.30 \text{ L/s}$$ **Power required to deliver water to 20 m head** (assuming 100% efficiency for theoretical case): $$P_{th} = \rho g Q_{th} H = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.00530 \times 20$$ $$P_{th} = 1040 \text{ W} \approx \boxed{1.04 \text{ kW}}$$ **Part (b) — Slip and Coefficient of Discharge** Given: $Q_{actual} = 0.95 \times Q_{theoretical}$ $$Q_{actual} = 0.95 \times 0.00530 = 0.00504 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Slip in m³/s:** $$S = Q_{th} - Q_{act} = 0.00530 - 0.00504 = 0.000266 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ **Slip %:** $$S_{\%} = \dfrac{S}{Q_{th}} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.000266}{0.00530} \times 100 = \boxed{5.0\%}$$ **Coefficient of Discharge:** $$C_d = \dfrac{Q_{act}}{Q_{th}} = \dfrac{0.00504}{0.00530} = \boxed{0.95}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students LOSE MARKS in this topic — a KTU Examiner's perspective:** 1. **Forgetting to convert $N$ from rpm to rps** when using $u = \pi D N / 60$ — students often write $u = \pi D N$, which is **WRONG** by a factor of 60. (Common: -2 marks in 7-mark subparts.) 2. **Confusing Manometric Head ($H_m$) with Total Head ($H$)** — $H_m$ is what the pump *develops* across its flanges; $H$ is the *system* head (static + friction + velocity). When a problem states "the pump delivers against a head of X m", $X$ is the **manometric head** in most KTU papers. 3. **Forgetting to convert Discharge units** — KTU often gives $Q$ in **L/s** or **L/min**. You must convert to **m³/s** before substituting in $P = \rho g Q H$. Failing to do so is the #1 reason students get absurd power values. 4. **Using the wrong $Q$ formula for reciprocating pump** — Single-acting gives $ALN/60$, double-acting gives $2ALN/60$. Mixing them up costs 7 marks. 5. **Not stating units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. 6. **Sign of slip** — Students often forget to specify whether slip is positive or negative. Always write a one-line conclusion: *"Positive slip of 5% indicates normal leakage through valves."* 7. **Manometric efficiency formula** — Don't write $\eta_{man} = H_{impeller}/H_m$. The correct ratio is $H_m/H_{impeller}$ because the manometric head is always *less* than the impeller head. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision – Memorise This Block** - **Pump** = device to lift water from low level to high level by imparting energy. - **Centrifugal pump** = rotodynamic, uses centrifugal force, requires priming, smooth continuous flow. - **Reciprocating pump** = positive-displacement, uses piston, self-priming (in wet cylinder), pulsating flow. - **Two energy conversions in centrifugal pump:** (1) Mechanical → Kinetic (in impeller), (2) Kinetic → Pressure (in volute casing). - **Euler's head equation:** $H = (V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1)/g$ - **Radial entry assumption:** $V_{w1} = 0$, hence $H = V_{w2} u_2 / g$. - **Manometric efficiency:** $\eta_{man} = H_m / H_{impeller}$ — measures how effectively the impeller's head is converted to useful manometric head. - **Overall efficiency:** $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech}$ (assumes electrical efficiency = 100%). - **Power required:** $P = \rho g Q H / \eta_{overall}$ (in Watts); divide by 1000 for kW. - **Reciprocating pump discharge:** Single-acting: $Q_{th} = ALN/60$. Double-acting: $Q_{th} = 2ALN/60$. - **Slip:** $S = Q_{th} - Q_{act}$. Percentage slip = $S/Q_{th} \times 100$. - **Coefficient of discharge:** $C_d = Q_{act} / Q_{th}$ (less than 1, usually). - **Vane angle formula:** $\tan \phi = V_f / (u - V_w)$ (backward-curved vane). - **Priming** is mandatory for centrifugal pumps because air cannot be displaced by centrifugal action. - **Air vessels** in reciprocating pumps convert pulsating flow into near-continuous flow. - **Constants to remember:** $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $w = 9810 \text{ N/m}^3$. > **End of Notes — Pumps: Water Lifting Devices (Module 4)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Pump?

A **pump** is a mechanical device used to increase the pressure energy of a liquid (or sometimes a gas–liquid mixture) by imparting kinetic energy to the fluid, which is then converted into pressure energy as the fluid leaves the pump. It transfers mechanical energy (supplied by an electric motor, diesel engine, or turbine) to the fluid as **pressure head**, **velocity head**, and to overcome **frictional/resistance losses**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim from syllabus):** A pump is a hydraulic machine that converts mechanical energy from a prime mover into hydraulic energy (pressure + kinetic) of a fluid. The energy addition is measured in terms of the **head** (in metres of liquid) imparted to the fluid.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Heart of a Building

Imagine a high-rise apartment building. Water cannot reach the 10th floor on its own from the ground-level sump. We need a **pump** at the basement — it acts exactly like the **human heart**:

| Human Heart | Centrifugal Pump |
|-------------|------------------|
| Sucks in oxygenated blood from veins | Draws (suctions) water from sump/well |
| Pumps blood to the entire body with pressure | Pushes water through pipelines at high pressure |
| Has valves to prevent backflow | Has a foot valve and delivery check valve |
| Heartbeat speed = pump impeller speed | Pulse rate = RPM of motor |
| If "primed" fails (air enters), heart attack | If pump is not primed, it cannot lift water |

Just as a heart must be filled with blood before it can pump, a centrifugal pump must be filled with water (primed) before it can lift water.

## 1.3 Classification of Pumps

Pumps are broadly classified into **two major categories**:

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Pumps are classified based on the principle of energy transfer — either by imparting **kinetic energy** (which is then converted to pressure) or by **direct displacement** of the fluid.

### A. Rotodynamic Pumps (Velocity Pumps / Turbo Pumps)
Energy is continuously added to the fluid by a **rotating impeller** to increase its velocity, which is then partially converted into pressure within the volute or diffuser.

**Sub-classification of Rotodynamic Pumps:**

1. **Centrifugal Pumps (Radial Flow)**
   - Flow exits the impeller in a radial direction (perpendicular to the shaft).
   - High head, low to medium discharge.
   - Example: Domestic water supply pumps, irrigation pumps.

2. **Mixed Flow Pumps**
   - Flow exits the impeller in a direction between radial and axial (inclined, ~45°).
   - Medium head, medium discharge.
   - Example: Large irrigation pumps, drainage pumps.

3. **Axial Flow Pumps (Propeller Pumps)**
   - Flow exits parallel to the shaft axis.
   - Low head, very high discharge.
   - Example: Cooling water circulation, flood control, sewage handling.

### B. Positive Displacement Pumps
A definite volume of fluid is **trapped, displaced, and then forced** into the discharge pipe per cycle. Energy is added by direct mechanical push.

**Sub-classification:**

1. **Reciprocating Pumps**
   - **Single-acting** (suction + delivery in one stroke)
   - **Double-acting** (suction and delivery in both strokes)
   - Example: Hand pumps, boiler feed pumps, oil pumps.

2. **Rotary Pumps**
   - **Gear pumps, vane pumps, screw pumps, lobe pumps**
   - Example: Lubricating oil pumps, chemical dosing pumps.

### 1.4 Comparative Snapshot (Must-Memorize for KTU)

| Feature | Centrifugal | Reciprocating |
|---------|-------------|---------------|
| Discharge vs. Pressure | High discharge, low pressure | Low discharge, very high pressure |
| Flow | Continuous | Pulsating (intermittent) |
| Priming | Required | Not required |
| Speed | High (1500–3000 RPM) | Low (60–200 RPM) |
| Efficiency at varying load | Variable | Nearly constant |
| Best suited for | Water supply, irrigation | Slurries, oils, boiler feed |

## 1.5 Component Parts of a Centrifugal Pump

A centrifugal pump is essentially a **rotating impeller** enclosed in a **volute casing** with a **suction pipe**, **delivery pipe**, and a **shaft** driven by a prime mover.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Expectation:** Be able to **draw a neat labelled sketch** of a centrifugal pump with all its components. Marks are awarded for correct labelling and arrows indicating flow direction.

**Main Components:**

1. **Impeller** — The rotating member with curved vanes; the heart of the pump.
2. **Casing (Volute / Volute Casing)** — A spiral-shaped passage surrounding the impeller that converts kinetic energy to pressure energy.
3. **Suction Pipe** — Connects the sump/source to the pump inlet (eye of the impeller).
4. **Delivery Pipe (Discharge Pipe)** — Carries pressurized water from the volute to the desired location.
5. **Shaft** — Transmits rotational energy from the motor to the impeller.
6. **Bearings** — Support the shaft and reduce friction.
7. **Stuffing Box / Mechanical Seal** — Prevents leakage of water along the shaft.
8. **Foot Valve (with Strainer)** — A one-way valve at the bottom of the suction pipe that prevents backflow when the pump stops.
9. **Sluice Valve (Delivery Valve)** — Controls the discharge flow.
10. **Air Vent / Priming Cock** — Used to release trapped air during priming.
11. **Eye of Impeller** — Central opening through which water enters the impeller.
12. **Lantern Ring** — Distributes sealing water in the stuffing box.
13. **Base Plate / Foundation** — Rigid support for the pump-motor assembly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Types of Casings:**
> - **Volute Casing** — Area of cross-section increases gradually (logarithmic spiral) to convert kinetic energy to pressure.
> - **Vortex Casing** — A circular chamber with a separate volute passage.
> - **Turbine Casing (Diffuser)** — Contains stationary guide vanes (diffusers) for energy conversion.

## 1.6 Priming of a Centrifugal Pump

**Priming** is the process of **filling the suction pipe, casing, and the portion of the delivery pipe up to the delivery valve completely with the liquid** to be pumped, before the pump is started.

### Why is Priming Required?

A centrifugal pump cannot handle air in the suction side. If air gets into the impeller, the pump loses its ability to develop suction lift because the **density of air is ~800 times less than that of water**. The pressure difference created is too small to lift water from the sump.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Exam Mistake:** Students often write "Priming is filling the pump with water to remove air." It must be specified — the **entire suction pipe, pump casing, and delivery pipe up to the delivery valve** must be filled. Merely pouring water over the pump is not priming.

### Methods of Priming

1. **Suction-side priming (foot-valve priming)** — Suction pipe has a foot valve; water poured in fills the pipe.
2. **Auxiliary pump priming** — A separate small pump (vacuum pump or ejector) evacuates air.
3. **Self-priming pump** — Built-in water reservoir; on first run, the water residue creates the seal.
4. **Separating and re-priming tank** — Used in large installations.

### Air Vessel vs. Priming

Do **not** confuse the two:
- **Priming tank** is on the **suction side** (to remove air before starting).
- **Air vessel** is on the **delivery side** of a reciprocating pump (to smoothen flow).

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Working Principle of a Centrifugal Pump

When the impeller rotates at high speed inside the casing (which is filled with water after priming):

1. Water enters the **eye of the impeller** axially due to low pressure created at the centre.
2. The impeller's curved vanes **impart kinetic energy** to the water, throwing it radially outward (centrifugal force).
3. Water enters the **volute casing**, where its **velocity is gradually reduced** and **kinetic energy is converted to pressure energy** (in accordance with **Bernoulli's principle**).
4. The pressurized water is delivered through the **delivery pipe**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Centrifugal"?** The pump uses centrifugal force (F = mω²r) generated by the rotating impeller to push water outward. This is the key conceptual differentiator from a positive displacement pump which uses piston push.

## 2.2 Heads of a Centrifugal Pump (Most Asked Concept!)

A centrifugal pump is characterized by **four types of heads**:

| Head | Symbol | Definition |
|------|--------|------------|
| **Suction Head / Static Suction Head** | $h_s$ | Vertical distance between the water surface in the sump and the centreline of the pump. |
| **Delivery Head / Static Delivery Head** | $h_d$ | Vertical distance between the pump centreline and the water surface in the delivery tank. |
| **Static Head** | $H_{static}$ | $h_s + h_d$ (total elevation difference). |
| **Manometric Head** | $H_m$ | Head developed by the pump, measured by a pressure gauge + vacuum gauge at suction. |
| **Total Head** | $H$ | Head imparted by the impeller to the water = manometric head + losses in pump. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Manometric Head Formula (Board's Favourite):**
>
> $$H_m = H_{gauge} + h_s \pm h_z + \frac{V_s^2 - V_d^2}{2g}$$
>
> where $H_{gauge}$ = pressure head from delivery gauge, $h_s$ = suction head (vacuum head from suction gauge), $h_z$ = vertical distance between gauges, and $V_s, V_d$ = velocities at suction and delivery points.

For most KTU problems, the simplified form is used:
$$H_m = H_{gauge} + h_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$$

(velocity heads often neglected in standard university problems, taking $H_m \approx H_{gauge} + h_s$).

## 2.3 Work Done by the Impeller — Euler's Equation for Pump

> [!NOTE]
> **Euler's Turbomachine Equation is THE most important formula for KTU Module 4.** It gives the work done per unit weight of fluid by an impeller.

### Velocity Triangles (Conceptual Foundation)

At the **inlet (eye)** of the impeller:
- $V_1$ = absolute velocity of water
- $u_1$ = tangential velocity of impeller = $\frac{\pi D_1 N}{60}$
- $V_{r1}$ = relative velocity of water w.r.t. vane
- $V_{w1}$ = velocity of whirl (tangential component of $V_1$) ≈ 0 (radial entry)

At the **outlet** of the impeller:
- $V_2$ = absolute velocity of water at outlet
- $u_2$ = tangential velocity of impeller = $\frac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$
- $V_{r2}$ = relative velocity
- $V_{w2}$ = velocity of whirl at outlet (tangential component of $V_2$)
- $V_{f2}$ = flow velocity (radial component of $V_2$) = velocity of flow

### Euler's Head Equation

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 \pm V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

For **radial entry** (most common, $V_{w1} \approx 0$):

$$\boxed{H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}}$$

If $\beta$ = angle made by the vane at outlet with tangent (called **vane angle** or **blade angle**), then:

$$V_{w2} = u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta}$$

Therefore:
$$H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \frac{u_2 V_{f2}}{g \tan \beta}$$

### Work Done per Second (Power Required by Pump)

$$W.D. = \rho g Q H_{th} \quad \text{(Watts)}$$

or
$$P = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{\eta} \quad \text{(shaft power input to pump)}$$

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (CHEAT SHEET)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table. It covers 80% of the numerical problems.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $u = \frac{\pi D N}{60}$ | $u$: tangential velocity (m/s), $D$: impeller dia (m), $N$: RPM | Blade tangential speed |
| 2 | $H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$ | $H_{th}$: theoretical head (m) | Euler's head |
| 3 | $V_{w2} = u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta}$ | $\beta$: outlet vane angle | Outlet velocity triangle |
| 4 | $H_{th} = \frac{u_2^2}{g} - \frac{u_2 V_{f2}}{g \tan \beta}$ | Combined form | Direct calculation |
| 5 | $P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m$ | Power imparted to water (W) | Water power |
| 6 | $\eta_{manometric} = \frac{H_m}{H_{th}}$ | Manometric efficiency | Head efficiency |
| 7 | $\eta_{mechanical} = \frac{P_{water}}{P_{shaft}}$ | Mechanical efficiency | Power efficiency |
| 8 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{manometric} \times \eta_{mechanical} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{P_{shaft}}$ | Overall efficiency | Combined |
| 9 | $N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | Specific speed (metric) | Pump classification |
| 10 | $N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | Specific speed (alternative form) | Pump classification (using Q) |
| 11 | $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$ | Affinity law — flow | Speed change |
| 12 | $\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$ | Affinity law — head | Speed change |
| 13 | $\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$ | Affinity law — power | Speed change |
| 14 | $NPSH_a = H_a - H_v - H_{fs} - h_s$ | Available NPSH | Cavitation check |
| 15 | $NPSH_r = f(Q)$ (from manufacturer) | Required NPSH | Cavitation check |
| 16 | Cavitation safe if $NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r + \text{margin}$ | — | Safe operation |
| 17 | $\sigma = \frac{NPSH_a}{H}$ | Thoma's cavitation parameter | Cavitation index |
| 18 | $V_{f2} = \frac{Q}{\pi D_2 b_2}$ | Flow velocity at outlet | From discharge $Q$ |
| 19 | $\eta_{volumetric} = \frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Volumetric efficiency | Leakage losses |
| 20 | $H_{th} = H_m + \text{losses}$ | — | Energy balance |

## 2.5 Affinity Laws (Speed Variation Laws)

When the **speed of a pump is changed**, keeping the impeller diameter constant, the following relations hold (assuming similar flow patterns — geometrically similar):

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

$$\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why cubes for power?** Because $P \propto \rho g Q H \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$. This is a classic KTU derivation question.

## 2.6 Effect of Change of Impeller Dimensions

If the impeller is **machined/trimmed** (diameter changed) but speed remains the same, the affinity laws are modified. Let $D_1, D_2$ be diameters and let all other geometric proportions (width $b$, blade angle $\beta$) remain similar. Then:

$$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{D_1}{D_2} \cdot \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

$$\frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2 \cdot \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

$$\frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3 \cdot \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

These are the **dimensional affinity laws** (combined speed and diameter variation).

## 2.7 Pump Selection — Engineering Practice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Pump selection is a **design / applied question** often asked for 7 marks in KTU. The key is to use **specific speed** to select the pump type.

**Step-by-step selection procedure:**

1. **Determine the duty point** — required discharge $Q$ and head $H$.
2. **Calculate specific speed** $N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ (or $N_s = \frac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$).
3. **Select pump type** based on $N_s$:

| $N_s$ range (metric) | Pump Type | Flow Direction |
|----------------------|-----------|----------------|
| 10 to 35 (low) | Centrifugal (Slow) | Radial |
| 35 to 80 (medium) | Centrifugal (Medium) | Radial |
| 80 to 160 (high) | Mixed flow | Mixed |
| 160 to 500 (very high) | Axial flow | Axial |
| > 500 | Propeller | Axial |

4. **Check NPSH** — ensure $NPSH_a > NPSH_r$ to avoid cavitation.
5. **Check materials of construction** — based on fluid (corrosive, abrasive, hot).
6. **Select driver** — based on power = $\rho g QH/\eta$.

## 2.8 Cavitation in Centrifugal Pumps

**Cavitation** is the formation, growth, and sudden collapse of **vapour bubbles** of the liquid within a pump when the local pressure falls below the **vapour pressure** of the liquid.

### Causes of Cavitation

- Very high suction lift
- Very low pressure on suction side
- Liquid temperature too high
- Suction pipe friction losses too high

### Effects / Symptoms of Cavitation

1. **Noise** — a characteristic cracking/popping sound ("sounds like marbles rolling")
2. **Vibration** — of the pump and pipeline
3. **Pitting and erosion** of the impeller blades (visible damage within hours)
4. **Reduction in discharge and head**
5. **Reduction in efficiency**
6. **Vapor lock** — total failure to deliver water

### Prevention of Cavitation

1. Keep suction lift **as low as possible** (ideally flooded suction).
2. Keep **liquid temperature low**.
3. Use **larger diameter suction pipe** to reduce velocity and friction.
4. Reduce number of **fittings/bends** in the suction line.
5. Select pump such that **NPSH available ≥ NPSH required + 1 m safety margin**.

## 2.9 Net Positive Suction Head (NPSH)

**NPSH** is the total head available at the suction port of the pump, **above the vapour pressure head** of the liquid, to cause the liquid to flow into the impeller without cavitation.

### Available NPSH (NPSHₐ)

$$NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_v - H_{fs} - h_s$$

where:
- $H_{atm}$ = absolute atmospheric pressure head (m of liquid)
- $H_v$ = absolute vapour pressure head (m of liquid) at operating temperature
- $H_{fs}$ = head loss due to friction in the suction pipe
- $h_s$ = static suction lift (positive if above pump, negative if below — flooded suction)

### Required NPSH (NPSHᵣ)

This is supplied by the pump manufacturer — it is the **minimum head required at the suction** to prevent cavitation. Depends on pump design and flow rate.

### Cavitation Criterion

$$\boxed{NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r + \text{safety margin (usually 0.6 to 1.0 m)}}$$

### Thoma's Cavitation Parameter

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH_a}{H}$$

Cavitation occurs if $\sigma < \sigma_c$ (critical sigma from manufacturer).

## 2.10 Performance / Characteristic Curves

A centrifugal pump's performance is described by graphs plotted between various parameters at a **constant speed**.

**Standard curves drawn:**

1. **$H$ vs $Q$** — Head vs. Discharge (decreases as Q increases)
2. **$P$ vs $Q$** — Power vs. Discharge (increases as Q increases)
3. **$\eta$ vs $Q$** — Efficiency vs. Discharge (peaks at **Best Efficiency Point, BEP**)
4. **NPSHᵣ vs $Q$** — Required NPSH vs. Discharge

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Point** is the intersection of the **system curve** ($H_{system} = H_{static} + KQ^2$) and the **pump curve** ($H$ vs $Q$).
> **Best Efficiency Point (BEP)** is the operating point that gives maximum efficiency. Pumps should ideally be operated near BEP.

## 2.11 Efficiencies in Centrifugal Pumps

| Efficiency | Formula | Meaning |
|------------|---------|---------|
| **Manometric efficiency** $\eta_{man}$ | $\frac{H_m}{H_{th}}$ | Fraction of theoretical head converted to useful head |
| **Mechanical efficiency** $\eta_m$ | $\frac{\text{Water power}}{\text{Shaft power}}$ | Fraction of shaft power transmitted to water |
| **Volumetric efficiency** $\eta_v$ | $\frac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{theoretical}}$ | Fraction of theoretical discharge delivered |
| **Overall efficiency** $\eta_o$ | $\eta_{man} \times \eta_m \times \eta_v$ | Overall pump efficiency |

## 2.12 Installation and Troubleshooting

### Installation Guidelines

1. Pump should be installed on a **rigid, vibration-free foundation**.
2. Suction pipe should be **as short and straight as possible**.
3. Suction pipe should have a **continuous upward slope** toward the pump (no air pockets).
4. Suction pipe diameter ≥ pump suction diameter.
5. Foot valve and strainer should be submerged **at least 0.5–1 m below the lowest water level**.
6. Foundation bolts must be **properly grouted** and aligned.

### Troubleshooting — Common Pump Problems

| Problem | Possible Cause | Remedy |
|---------|---------------|--------|
| Pump does not deliver water | Not primed, foot valve blocked, suction pipe leakage | Prime properly, clean foot valve, check pipe joints |
| Less discharge | Low speed, air leakage, partial blockage, wrong direction of rotation | Check voltage, tighten joints, clean strainer, reverse two phases |
| Pump loses prime after start | Air leakage on suction side | Check suction pipe for cracks, tighten flanges |
| Pump makes noise and vibrates | Cavitation, misalignment, bearing damage | Check NPSH, realign pump-motor, replace bearings |
| Motor overheats | Overload, low voltage, mechanical friction | Reduce discharge (throttle delivery), check voltage, lubricate bearings |
| Power consumption high | Speed too high, head lower than design, impeller rubbing | Check nameplate, check for obstruction |

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Euler's Head Equation for a Centrifugal Pump

### Setup
Consider an impeller rotating with angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. The impeller has an inner radius $r_1$ and outer radius $r_2$. Water enters the impeller at the inner radius and leaves at the outer radius.

Let:
- $u_1$ = tangential velocity of impeller at inlet = $\omega r_1$
- $u_2$ = tangential velocity of impeller at outlet = $\omega r_2$
- $V_1, V_2$ = absolute velocity of water at inlet and outlet
- $V_{r1}, V_{r2}$ = relative velocity of water at inlet and outlet
- $V_{w1}, V_{w2}$ = velocity of whirl (tangential component of absolute velocity) at inlet and outlet
- $V_{f1}, V_{f2}$ = velocity of flow (radial component) at inlet and outlet
- $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ = absolute flow angle at inlet and outlet (angle between $V$ and $u$)
- $\beta_1, \beta_2$ = vane (blade) angles at inlet and outlet (angle between $V_r$ and the negative direction of $u$)

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Torque on the impeller**

The torque exerted by water on the impeller is due to the change in moment of momentum of the fluid.

The mass of water flowing per second through the impeller is:
$$\dot{m} = \rho Q$$

The change in moment of momentum per second (i.e., torque) between inlet and outlet is:
$$T = \dot{m} \left( V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1 \right)$$

$$T = \rho Q \left( V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1 \right)$$

**Step 2: Work done per second by the impeller on water**

Work done per second = Torque × Angular velocity:
$$W.D. = T \cdot \omega = \rho Q \omega \left( V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1 \right)$$

Since $u_1 = \omega r_1$ and $u_2 = \omega r_2$, we have $\omega r_1 = u_1$ and $\omega r_2 = u_2$:

$$W.D. = \rho Q \left( V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1 \right)$$

**Step 3: Work done per unit weight of fluid = Head**

Work done per unit weight = $\frac{W.D.}{\rho g Q}$:

$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

**Step 4: Special case — Radial entry**

If water enters the impeller **without any tangential component** (i.e., $V_{w1} = 0$ — radial or axial entry):

$$\boxed{H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}}$$

This is the **Euler Head Equation for a Centrifugal Pump**.

> [!NOTE]
> The sign in the general Euler equation is **+ for a pump** (work done on fluid) and **− for a turbine** (work extracted from fluid). This subtle sign convention is a common exam pitfall.

## 3.2 Derivation of Manometric Head from Pressure Gauge Readings

### Setup
A pressure gauge is fitted on the delivery pipe, and a vacuum gauge on the suction pipe. The two gauges are at the same level (no $h_z$).

### Derivation

**Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's equation at suction point (just before the pump, at gauge level) and at the sump surface:**

$$\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + h_s + \text{losses}$$

Neglecting velocity head at sump and friction in short suction:
$$h_s = \frac{p_{atm} - p_s}{\rho g}$$

The vacuum gauge reads:
$$V_{gauge} = \frac{p_{atm} - p_s}{\rho g} = h_s$$

**Step 2: Apply Bernoulli's equation between the delivery gauge and a point at the same level inside the pump (no elevation change):**

$$\frac{p_d}{\rho g} + \frac{V_d^2}{2g} = \frac{p_{inside}}{\rho g} + 0$$

$$H_{gauge} = \frac{p_d}{\rho g} - \frac{p_{inside}}{\rho g} = \text{pressure head at delivery} - \text{pressure head just inside pump}$$

Note: the pressure at the same level inside the pump at the suction side equals $-h_s$ (vacuum head) approximately. The pressure rise across the pump = $H_{gauge} + h_s$.

**Step 3: Manometric head (total head developed by the pump) is the sum of the pressure rise and the velocity head difference:**

$$H_m = H_{gauge} + h_s + \frac{V_d^2 - V_s^2}{2g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When velocity heads are negligible (as in KTU problems, usually), use $H_m = H_{gauge} + h_s$.

## 3.3 Derivation of Affinity Laws (Speed Change)

### Assumptions
- Geometric similarity maintained
- Constant efficiency
- No cavitation

**Step 1: Discharge Q ∝ (velocity × area)**

For a geometrically similar impeller, $Q \propto V_f \cdot D^2$.

The flow velocity $V_f$ scales with the impeller tangential speed $u \propto N \cdot D$.

Therefore:
$$Q \propto (N \cdot D) \cdot D^2 = N \cdot D^3$$

If $D$ is constant (only $N$ changes):
$$Q \propto N \Rightarrow \frac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \frac{N_1}{N_2}$$

**Step 2: Head H ∝ (velocity)²**

$$H \propto u^2 \propto (N \cdot D)^2$$

If $D$ is constant:
$$H \propto N^2 \Rightarrow \frac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2$$

**Step 3: Power P ∝ Q × H**

$$P \propto Q \cdot H \propto N \cdot N^2 = N^3$$

If $D$ is constant:
$$P \propto N^3 \Rightarrow \frac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\frac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3$$

## 3.4 Derivation of Specific Speed (Pump)

### Concept
Specific speed $N_s$ is the speed at which a **geometrically similar pump** would run to deliver **unit discharge (1 m³/s or 1 L/s) at unit head (1 m)**.

### Derivation

Consider a pump running at speed $N$, with discharge $Q$ and head $H$. Imagine a geometrically similar pump that delivers $Q = 1$ m³/s at $H = 1$ m. Let its speed be $N_s$.

By the affinity laws:
$$\frac{Q}{1} = \left(\frac{N}{N_s}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{D}{D_s}\right)^3$$

For geometric similarity, $D/D_s = H^{1/2}/H_s^{1/2} = H^{1/2}$ (since $H_s = 1$):
$$\frac{D}{D_s} = H^{1/2}$$

So:
$$Q = \frac{N}{N_s} \cdot H^{3/2}$$

Solving for $N_s$:
$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative form using Power $P$** (in kW or HP):
>
> $$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$$
>
> This form is used when $P$ is given in problem statements.

**Multiplication factors for specific speed:**

| Unit System | $Q$ Unit | $H$ Unit | $P$ Unit | $N$ Unit | Multiplier |
|-------------|----------|----------|----------|----------|------------|
| Metric (SI) | L/s | m | kW | RPM | $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ |
| Metric (Q form) | L/s | m | — | RPM | $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ |
| FPS | GPM | ft | HP | RPM | $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Conversion Required!** If a problem gives data in FPS units, the computed $N_s$ must be multiplied by a conversion factor when comparing with metric standard ranges. KTU typically uses metric (L/s, m, kW).

## 3.5 Derivation of NPSH Available

### Setup
Consider a pump with the sump at a lower level (or higher level). The suction pipe has friction losses.

### Derivation using Bernoulli's Equation (sump surface → pump suction point)

$$\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \frac{p_{s}}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + h_s + H_{fs}$$

Rearranging:
$$\frac{p_{atm} - p_s}{\rho g} = h_s + H_{fs} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g}$$

By definition, NPSH available is the total suction head above the vapour pressure:
$$NPSH_a = \frac{p_s - p_v}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g}$$

Substituting $\frac{p_s}{\rho g} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_s - H_{fs} - \frac{V_s^2}{2g}$:

$$NPSH_a = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g} - h_s - H_{fs}$$

$$\boxed{NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_v - h_s - H_{fs}}$$

where $H_{atm} = p_{atm}/\rho g$ and $H_v = p_v/\rho g$.

**Sign convention for $h_s$**: $h_s$ is **positive** if the pump is **above** the water surface (suction lift), and **negative** if the pump is **below** the water surface (flooded suction — increases $NPSH_a$).

## 3.6 Thoma's Cavitation Parameter — Derivation

The dimensionless cavitation parameter relates NPSH to pump head:

$$\sigma = \frac{NPSH}{H}$$

**Critical sigma** $\sigma_c$ is the minimum value of $\sigma$ at which the pump operates without cavitation. From experimental data:
- Centrifugal pumps: $\sigma_c \approx 0.10$ to 0.40
- Mixed flow: $\sigma_c \approx 0.40$ to 0.85
- Axial flow: $\sigma_c \approx 0.85$ to 1.50

Cavitation occurs when $\sigma < \sigma_c$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative useful relationship:**
>
> $$\sigma_c \propto \frac{1}{(N_s)^{2/3}}$$
>
> This shows that as specific speed increases, the pump becomes more susceptible to cavitation. Axial flow pumps (high $N_s$) need higher NPSH margins than radial pumps.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Key Equations with Conditions

| Equation | Valid When | Common Mistake |
|----------|-----------|----------------|
| $H_{th} = V_{w2} u_2 / g$ | Radial entry ($V_{w1} = 0$) | Used for non-radial entry incorrectly |
| $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}/\tan \beta$ | Outlet vane angle $\beta$ measured from tangent | Confusing with $\beta$ measured from radial direction |
| $H_m = H_g + h_s$ | Same level gauges, no velocity head | Forgetting velocity head correction |
| $\eta_o = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}$ | All losses independent | Treating as simple product without checking |
| $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ | $Q$ in L/s, $H$ in m, $N$ in RPM | Wrong unit conversion |
| $Q_1/Q_2 = N_1/N_2$ | Geometric similarity, $D$ constant | Using when impeller is trimmed |
| $H_1/H_2 = (D_1/D_2)^2 (N_1/N_2)^2$ | Both $D$ and $N$ change | Forgetting the $D^2$ term |
| $NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_v - h_s - H_{fs}$ | Using gauge pressures | Mixing gauge and absolute pressures |
