# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "priming" as applied to centrifugal pumps. Why is it necessary?**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Priming is the process of filling the suction pipe, the pump casing, and the portion of the delivery pipe up to the delivery valve completely with the liquid to be pumped, before starting the pump.

**Necessity (1.5 marks):** Priming is necessary because:
1. A centrifugal pump cannot develop suction if air is present in the casing.
2. Air has very low density (~1.2 kg/m³) compared to water (~1000 kg/m³). The pressure difference created by the impeller on air is insufficient to lift water.
3. Without priming, the impeller simply churns the air–water mixture and no significant suction lift is created.
4. Once primed, the pump can continuously lift water by creating low pressure at the eye of the impeller.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define specific speed of a centrifugal pump. What is its significance?**

### Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Specific speed ($N_s$) of a pump is defined as the speed at which a geometrically similar pump would run to deliver a unit discharge (1 m³/s or 1 L/s) at a unit head (1 m) of liquid.

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

**Significance (1.5 marks):**
1. It helps to **classify the type of pump** (radial / mixed / axial flow) based on the duty requirement.
2. It allows **comparison of geometrically similar pumps** of different sizes.
3. It is used for **predicting the shape of the impeller** — low $N_s$ → radial vane; high $N_s$ → axial vane.
4. It helps in **selecting the appropriate pump** for a given application.

---

## PART B — LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the work done per second by the impeller of a centrifugal pump on water. Assume water enters radially (no pre-whirl). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers 0.05 m³/s of water against a head of 20 m. The impeller has an outer diameter of 30 cm, runs at 1450 RPM, and the vane angle at outlet is 30°. The manometric and overall efficiencies are 90% and 70% respectively. Calculate: (i) the outlet whirl velocity, (ii) the theoretical head developed, (iii) the shaft power required, and (iv) the power lost in bearings. **[7 Marks]**

### Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Work Done by Impeller (7 Marks)

**Given:** Water enters radially at the impeller eye.

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Consider a centrifugal pump with impeller rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ rad/s. Let the inner and outer radii of the impeller be $r_1$ and $r_2$ respectively. The mass of water handled per second by the pump is $\dot{m} = \rho Q$.

**Step 2 (1.5 Marks):** The velocity of water entering the impeller ($V_1$) is purely radial (no whirl), so the tangential component $V_{w1} = 0$. The water leaves the impeller at outlet with absolute velocity $V_2$ which has a tangential component $V_{w2}$.

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Applying the **principle of angular momentum** (moment of momentum theorem), the torque on the impeller is:
$$T = \rho Q (V_{w2} r_2 - V_{w1} r_1) = \rho Q V_{w2} r_2 \quad (\text{since } V_{w1} = 0)$$

**Step 4 (1.5 Marks):** Work done per second = Torque × Angular velocity:
$$W.D. = T \cdot \omega = \rho Q V_{w2} r_2 \cdot \omega = \rho Q V_{w2} u_2$$
where $u_2 = \omega r_2$ is the tangential velocity of the impeller at outlet.

**Step 5 (1.5 Marks):** Therefore, the head developed by the impeller (work done per unit weight):
$$\boxed{H_{th} = \frac{W.D.}{\rho g Q} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}}$$

This is **Euler's head equation** for a centrifugal pump with radial entry.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $Q = 0.05$ m³/s
- $H_m = 20$ m
- $D_2 = 0.30$ m
- $N = 1450$ RPM
- $\beta = 30°$ (outlet vane angle)
- $\eta_{man} = 90\% = 0.90$
- $\eta_{overall} = 70\% = 0.70$

**Step 1 (1 Mark): Calculate tangential velocity $u_2$ at outlet:**
$$u_2 = \frac{\pi D_2 N}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 0.30 \times 1450}{60}$$
$$u_2 = \frac{1366.5}{60} = 22.78 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark): Calculate flow velocity $V_{f2}$ at outlet:**

Assuming the width of impeller $b_2 = $ unknown, we use the manometric efficiency to get $V_{f2}$ from the theoretical head.

From the velocity triangle, theoretical head:
$$H_{th} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} \quad \text{where} \quad V_{w2} = u_2 - \frac{V_{f2}}{\tan \beta}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 Marks):** Manometric efficiency:
$$\eta_{man} = \frac{H_m}{H_{th}} = 0.90$$
$$H_{th} = \frac{H_m}{\eta_{man}} = \frac{20}{0.90} = 22.22 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (1 Mark): Compute $V_{w2}$:**
$$V_{w2} = \frac{H_{th} \cdot g}{u_2} = \frac{22.22 \times 9.81}{22.78} = \frac{217.99}{22.78} = 9.57 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 5 (0.5 Mark): Compute $V_{f2}$ from velocity triangle:**
$$V_{f2} = (u_2 - V_{w2}) \tan \beta = (22.78 - 9.57) \tan 30° = 13.21 \times 0.5774 = 7.63 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 6 (1 Mark): Shaft power required:**
$$P_{shaft} = \frac{\rho g Q H_m}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 20}{0.70}$$
$$P_{shaft} = \frac{9810}{0.70} = 14014 \text{ W} = 14.01 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7 (1 Mark): Power lost in bearings:**

Water power (power actually transferred to water) = Manometric power:
$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_m = 9810 \text{ W} = 9.81 \text{ kW}$$

Power lost in bearings = $P_{shaft} - P_{water} = 14.01 - 9.81 = 4.20$ kW

**Final Results:**
| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Outlet whirl velocity $V_{w2}$ | 9.57 m/s |
| Theoretical head $H_{th}$ | 22.22 m |
| Shaft power | 14.01 kW |
| Power lost in bearings | 4.20 kW |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do **not** confuse manometric power with shaft power. The shaft power is what the motor supplies; the water power (or manometric power) is what is delivered to the fluid. The ratio gives overall efficiency. Many students write $P = \rho g Q H_m$ for the "power required" — this is only the water power.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]`

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of cavitation in centrifugal pumps. What is NPSH? Derive an expression for the available NPSH for a pump with suction lift. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A centrifugal pump running at 1200 RPM delivers 0.10 m³/s against a head of 25 m. If the pump speed is increased to 1500 RPM, determine the new discharge, head, and power. Assume the manometric efficiency remains 80% and the overall efficiency remains 75%. **[7 Marks]**

### Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Cavitation and NPSH (7 Marks)

**Cavitation Definition (1.5 Marks):** Cavitation is the phenomenon of formation, growth, and sudden collapse of vapour bubbles of the liquid in a region where the local pressure falls below the vapour pressure of the liquid.

**Causes (1 Mark):**
- Excessive suction lift
- High fluid temperature
- High friction losses in suction line
- Pump operating beyond its NPSH requirement

**Effects (1 Mark):**
- Noise and vibration
- Pitting and erosion of impeller blades
- Drop in head, discharge, and efficiency
- Possible total failure of the pump

**NPSH Definition (1 Mark):** Net Positive Suction Head is the total head available at the suction port of the pump, above the vapour pressure head of the liquid, that causes the liquid to flow into the impeller without cavitation.

**Derivation of NPSHₐ (2.5 Marks):**

Apply Bernoulli's equation from the sump surface (1) to the pump suction point (2):

$$\frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 = \frac{p_s}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g} + h_s + H_{fs}$$

Rearranging:
$$\frac{p_s}{\rho g} = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - h_s - H_{fs} - \frac{V_s^2}{2g}$$

Available NPSH = total head at pump suction − vapour pressure head:

$$NPSH_a = \frac{p_s}{\rho g} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g} + \frac{V_s^2}{2g}$$

Substituting:

$$NPSH_a = \frac{p_{atm}}{\rho g} - \frac{p_v}{\rho g} - h_s - H_{fs}$$

$$\boxed{NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_v - h_s - H_{fs}}$$

For safe operation: $NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r + \text{safety margin}$

#### Part (b) — Affinity Law Application (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**
- $N_1 = 1200$ RPM, $Q_1 = 0.10$ m³/s, $H_1 = 25$ m
- $N_2 = 1500$ RPM
- $\eta_{man} = 80\%$ = 0.80, $\eta_{overall} = 75\%$ = 0.75

**Step 1 (1 Mark): Initial shaft power (water power = manometric power):**
$$P_1 = \frac{\rho g Q_1 H_1}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.10 \times 25}{0.75}$$
$$P_1 = \frac{24525}{0.75} = 32700 \text{ W} = 32.70 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks): New discharge $Q_2$ using affinity law:**

Speed ratio: $\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{1500}{1200} = 1.25$

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 1.25$$
$$Q_2 = 1.25 \times 0.10 = 0.125 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks): New head $H_2$:**
$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = (1.25)^2 = 1.5625$$
$$H_2 = 1.5625 \times 25 = 39.06 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks): New shaft power $P_2$:**
$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (1.25)^3 = 1.9531$$
$$P_2 = 1.9531 \times 32.70 = 63.86 \text{ kW}$$

**Final Results:**
| Parameter | Value at 1200 RPM | Value at 1500 RPM |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Discharge $Q$ | 0.10 m³/s | **0.125 m³/s** |
| Head $H$ | 25 m | **39.06 m** |
| Shaft Power $P$ | 32.70 kW | **63.86 kW** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** The affinity laws apply to the **performance of the same pump** at different speeds (geometric similarity maintained). They are **not** valid for comparing two physically different pumps. Also, ensure that the speed increase does not lead to cavitation (always check NPSHₐ ≥ NPSHᵣ at the new operating point).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 4):**

### A. Definitions to Memorize
- ✅ **Pump** — Machine that converts mechanical energy to hydraulic energy.
- ✅ **Priming** — Filling suction pipe, casing, and delivery pipe up to the delivery valve with the liquid.
- ✅ **Manometric Head ($H_m$)** — Actual head developed by the pump, measured by pressure and vacuum gauges.
- ✅ **Specific Speed ($N_s$)** — Speed of a geometrically similar pump to deliver unit discharge at unit head.
- ✅ **NPSH** — Net Positive Suction Head; total suction head above vapour pressure.
- ✅ **Cavitation** — Vapour bubble formation and collapse due to local pressure falling below vapour pressure.
- ✅ **Affinity Laws** — Relations between $Q$, $H$, $P$ and $N$ for the same pump at different speeds.

### B. Critical Numerical Formulas
- ✅ Tangential velocity: $u = \pi D N / 60$
- ✅ Euler's head (radial entry): $H_{th} = V_{w2} u_2 / g$
- ✅ Outlet whirl velocity: $V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{f2}/\tan \beta$
- ✅ Manometric head: $H_m = H_g + h_s + (V_d^2 - V_s^2)/(2g)$
- ✅ Water power: $P_w = \rho g Q H_m$
- ✅ Shaft power: $P_s = \rho g Q H_m / \eta_o$
- ✅ Specific speed: $N_s = N\sqrt{Q}/H^{3/4}$ (L/s, m) or $N_s = N\sqrt{P}/H^{5/4}$ (kW, m)
- ✅ NPSH available: $NPSH_a = H_{atm} - H_v - h_s - H_{fs}$
- ✅ Affinity laws: $Q \propto N$, $H \propto N^2$, $P \propto N^3$

### C. Classification Snapshot
- ✅ Rotodynamic (Centrifugal, Mixed, Axial) vs. Positive Displacement (Reciprocating, Rotary)
- ✅ Specific speed ranges: Radial (10–80), Mixed (80–160), Axial (160–500+)

### D. Cavitation — Symptoms & Prevention
- ✅ Symptoms: Noise, vibration, pitting, drop in $H$ and $Q$, drop in efficiency.
- ✅ Prevention: Reduce $h_s$, reduce $H_{fs}$, increase suction pipe dia, lower liquid temperature, ensure $NPSH_a \geq NPSH_r$.

### E. Common Mistake Zones (Examiner Traps)
- ❌ Confusing manometric power with shaft power.
- ❌ Forgetting to subtract vapour pressure in NPSHₐ formula.
- ❌ Using the wrong form of specific speed (with $Q$ vs. with $P$).
- ❌ Applying affinity laws across two different pumps (only valid for the same pump at varying $N$).
- ❌ Sign convention in $h_s$ (positive for suction lift, negative for flooded suction).
- ❌ Confusing **air vessel** (reciprocating pump, delivery side) with **priming** (centrifugal pump, suction side).

### F. Key Diagram to Practice
- ✅ Labelled centrifugal pump with all 13 components (impeller, casing, shaft, bearings, foot valve, strainer, etc.).
- ✅ Velocity triangles at inlet and outlet of impeller.
- ✅ Performance curve graph ($H$, $P$, $\eta$ vs $Q$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Word:** The KTU Module 4 on Centrifugal Pumps is heavily **numerical + conceptual**. Master the velocity triangles, Euler's equation, affinity laws, and NPSH formula. The specific speed concept is the bridge between theory and pump selection — know it cold. Always draw velocity triangles for any outlet-vane-angle problem, and always check $NPSH_a$ before declaring any pump installation safe.

# Module 4 | Centrifugal & Special Pump Classification

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Three Hydraulic Machine Archetypes

In the previous sections, we studied the basic **centrifugal pump** (radial flow). The KTU 2024 scheme now extends the classification along the *Specific Speed* axis, recognizing that engineering demands cannot always be served by the simple radial-flow impeller. Three distinct pump families emerge from the trade-off between **head (H)** and **discharge (Q)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4):**
> Turbomachines handling liquids are classified as **(a) Centrifugal (Radial) Pumps**, **(b) Mixed Flow Pumps**, and **(c) Axial Flow (Propeller) Pumps**, based on the direction in which the fluid leaves the impeller relative to the shaft axis. The selection between them is governed by the dimensionless **Specific Speed (Ns)** of the required duty point.

### 1.2 The Multistage Pump — Stacking Pressure Stages

A **multistage pump** is a centrifugal pump that contains **two or more identical impellers mounted on the same shaft** within a single casing. The fluid passes sequentially from the discharge of one impeller (stage) into the suction of the next stage. Because each impeller adds an increment of head, multistage pumps are the natural choice when **very high heads** are required from a horizontally-driven pump (a single-stage centrifugal impeller is mechanically limited to heads of roughly 100–150 m per stage).

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (Staircase vs. Elevator):**
> Think of a single-stage centrifugal pump as climbing one floor of a building at a time using a staircase — each step adds a fixed height of head. A multistage pump is like installing multiple staircases end-to-end inside the same shaft: the head developed is the *sum* of the head from every staircase. A propeller/axial pump, by contrast, is a giant elevator moving huge volumes but only changing the height slightly.

### 1.3 The Axial Flow (Propeller) Pump — High Volume, Low Head

In an **axial flow pump**, the fluid enters and leaves the impeller **parallel to the axis of rotation**. The impeller resembles a marine propeller (hence the synonym *propeller pump*). The impeller blades are profiled aerofoil sections that act on the fluid by lift, propelling it axially.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical Application Domain:**
> - Drainage of low-lying areas
> - Large-scale irrigation lift schemes
> - Condenser cooling water circulation in thermal power stations
> - Stormwater and flood pumping stations
> - Circulating water in industrial cooling towers

### 1.4 The Mixed Flow Pump — The Compromise Design

In a **mixed flow pump**, the fluid leaves the impeller in a direction that is **neither purely radial nor purely axial**, but at an angle (typically 30°–60°) to the shaft axis. The impeller combines the high-pressure capability of the radial design with the high-flow capability of the axial design. It is the natural "middle child" — used where neither pure radial nor pure axial geometry is optimal.

### 1.5 Standard Reference Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants Used in This Module:**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Specific weight of water: **γ = ρg = 9810 N/m³**
> - Specific speed reference units (SI): **N in rpm, Q in m³/s, H in m**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Anatomy of a Multistage Centrifugal Pump

A multistage centrifugal pump consists of **n** identical stages. Each stage contains:
1. An **impeller** (radial, Francis-type, or mixed-flow type)
2. A **diffuser / volute casing** (or guide vanes) that converts kinetic energy to pressure
3. A **return guide** (external guide vane ring) that directs the flow from one stage to the next with minimum loss
4. A **common shaft** driven by a single motor

**Two arrangement philosophies exist:**

| Arrangement | Function | Head vs. Flow |
|---|---|---|
| **Series** | Impellers mounted coaxially; discharge of stage *i* feeds suction of stage *i+1* | Head adds: H_total = n·H_stage. Flow remains Q |
| **Parallel** | Identical pumps fed from a common manifold, discharging to a common manifold | Flow adds: Q_total = n·Q_pump. Head remains H |

> [!NOTE]
> **Series vs. Parallel — Intuitive Analogy:**
> Series pumps are like *batteries connected end-to-end* — voltage (head) increases. Parallel pumps are like *batteries connected side-by-side* — current (discharge) increases.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus specifically focuses on the *series multistage centrifugal pump* — impellers in a single casing, each adding its share of head, with a single suction and a single discharge.

### 2.2 The Concept of Specific Speed (Ns)

The most important unifying concept for this entire module is **Specific Speed (Ns)**. It is the speed (in rpm) at which a *geometrically similar* pump would have to run to deliver **1 m³/s against a head of 1 m**.

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{(H)^{3/4}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> - N is in **rpm**
> - Q is in **m³/s**
> - H is in **m** (per stage for multistage)

The specific speed *embeds the entire design philosophy* of a pump:

- **Low Ns (8–80 SI units):** Radial / Centrifugal pumps — high head, low discharge
- **Medium Ns (80–400 SI units):** Mixed flow pumps — moderate head, moderate discharge
- **High Ns (400–1500+ SI units):** Axial / Propeller pumps — low head, very high discharge

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Specific Speed (SI units)** | $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$ | N in rpm, Q in m³/s, H in m |
| 2 | **Multistage Head (Series)** | $H_{total} = n \cdot H_{stage}$ | n = number of stages |
| 3 | **Multistage Discharge (Parallel)** | $Q_{total} = n \cdot Q_{pump}$ | Identical pumps in parallel |
| 4 | **Shaft Power Input** | $P = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta}$ | P in Watts |
| 5 | **Pump Affinity Law — Discharge** | $\dfrac{Q_1}{Q_2} = \dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^3$ | Geometrically similar pumps |
| 6 | **Pump Affinity Law — Head** | $\dfrac{H_1}{H_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^2\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^2$ | — |
| 7 | **Pump Affinity Law — Power** | $\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \left(\dfrac{N_1}{N_2}\right)^3\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)^5$ | — |
| 8 | **Manometric Efficiency** | $\eta_{man} = \dfrac{gH}{V_{w2} u_2}$ | Euler's turbine equation derived |
| 9 | **Overall Efficiency** | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{man} \times \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{vol}$ | Product of all component efficiencies |
| 10 | **Shut-off Head (Axial/Propeller)** | $H_{so} \approx 1.5\, H_{design}$ to $2.0\, H_{design}$ | Steep H-Q curve |
| 11 | **Shut-off Head (Mixed Flow)** | $H_{so} \approx 1.2\, H_{design}$ to $1.5\, H_{design}$ | Moderate slope |
| 12 | **Thoma's Cavitation Parameter** | $\sigma = \dfrac{H_{sv} - H_{v}}{H}$ | Cavitation safety criterion |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The specific-speed classification is **not academic** — it drives the entire procurement, manufacturing, and installation strategy for any pumping station in Kerala. For example:
> - The **Idukki Hydro-Electric Project** uses *Francis turbines* (the turbine analog of mixed-flow pumps) precisely because the specific speed falls in the 80–400 range.
> - The **Kochi Metro stormwater drainage system** uses axial flow pumps (Ns ≈ 800) to move massive volumes of water against low heads.
> - Multistage pumps are the workhorses of **high-rise building water supply** and **boiler feed applications** in Kerala's industrial estates.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: How a Multistage Head is the Sum of Individual Stage Heads

We begin with Euler's equation for a single stage, applied between the inlet (suffix 1) and outlet (suffix 2) of one impeller:

$$\left(\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z\right)_{stage} = \left(\frac{p}{\rho g} + \frac{V^2}{2g} + z\right)_{inlet} + H_{stage}$$

For an *ideal* impeller with no losses, the head developed by a single impeller, by Euler's turbomachinery equation, is:

$$H_{stage} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2 - V_{w1} u_1}{g}$$

where:
- $V_{w2}$ is the **whirl (tangential) component** of absolute velocity at impeller outlet (m/s)
- $u_2$ is the **blade tip speed** at outlet (m/s)
- $V_{w1}$ is the whirl component at impeller inlet
- $u_1$ is the blade tip speed at inlet

If we assume **no pre-whirl** (a typical design choice — radial inlet, $V_{w1} = 0$), the equation reduces to:

$$H_{stage} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

**Step 1:** Define the work done per unit weight of fluid by a single impeller.

From Euler's turbomachinery equation, with no pre-whirl:

$$W_{stage} = V_{w2} u_2 \quad \text{(in J/kg)}$$

**Step 2:** For n identical impellers mounted in series on the same shaft, the total energy added to the fluid is:

$$W_{total} = n \cdot W_{stage} = n \cdot V_{w2} u_2$$

**Step 3:** Convert this energy per unit mass to head per unit weight by dividing by *g*:

$$H_{total} = \frac{W_{total}}{g} = \frac{n \cdot V_{w2} u_2}{g} = n \cdot H_{stage}$$

**Result:**

$$\boxed{H_{total} = n \cdot H_{stage} = \frac{n \cdot V_{w2} u_2}{g}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the result is intuitive:** Each impeller receives fluid from the previous stage at *low pressure* and discharges it at *higher pressure*. The pressure rise is additive because the discharge pipe of stage *i* becomes the suction pipe of stage *i+1*. The fluid undergoes *n* sequential pressure transformations, so the total head is the sum of the per-stage increments.

### 3.2 Derivation: The Specific Speed Formula from Dimensional Analysis

The specific speed is *not* an arbitrary definition — it emerges from a formal dimensional analysis. The head H developed by a geometrically similar pump is governed by three independent variables: rotational speed N, impeller diameter D, and discharge Q (or equivalently, the flow velocity).

**Step 1:** Write the functional relationship.

A geometrically similar pump obeys:

$$H = f(N, D, Q)$$

**Step 2:** Assign dimensions to each variable.

| Variable | Symbol | Dimensions [M L T] |
|---|---|---|
| Head | H | $[L]$ |
| Rotational speed | N | $[T^{-1}]$ |
| Impeller diameter | D | $[L]$ |
| Discharge | Q | $[L^3 T^{-1}]$ |

**Step 3:** Express H as a product of powers of the independent variables.

$$H = K \cdot N^a \cdot D^b \cdot Q^c$$

**Step 4:** Substitute dimensions.

$$[L] = [T^{-1}]^a \cdot [L]^b \cdot [L^3 T^{-1}]^c$$

**Step 5:** Equate exponents of M, L, T (Buckingham Π theorem).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Mass (M):} &\quad 0 = 0 \\
\text{Length (L):} &\quad 1 = b + 3c \\
\text{Time (T):} &\quad 0 = -a - c
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6:** Solve the system.

We have two equations and three unknowns. Choose $a$ as the free variable.

From the time equation: $c = -a$.

From the length equation: $b = 1 - 3c = 1 + 3a$.

**Step 7:** Substitute back into the dimensional equation.

$$H = K \cdot N^a \cdot D^{(1+3a)} \cdot Q^{-a}$$

**Step 8:** Rearrange to make *a* appear as an isolated group.

$$\frac{H}{D} = K \cdot \left(\frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{D^{3/2} H^{3/4}}\right)^a \cdot H^{1/4}$$

> After simplification (the standard route), we arrive at the well-known result:

$$\boxed{N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Specific speed is *dimensionless* when expressed in the MKS (or "metric") reference units. It is a **figure of merit** — a low value means "high head, low flow" (radial pump), and a high value means "low head, high flow" (axial pump).

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Performance Characteristics Side-by-Side

| Characteristic | Radial (Centrifugal) | Mixed Flow | Axial (Propeller) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Specific Speed Ns (SI)** | 8 – 80 | 80 – 400 | 400 – 1500+ |
| **Head Range (m)** | 10 – 150+ (per stage) | 5 – 60 | 1 – 20 |
| **Discharge Range** | Low – Moderate | Moderate – High | Very High |
| **H–Q Curve Shape** | Flat / Stable drop | Steeper drop | Very steep, almost vertical at shut-off |
| **Shut-off Head / Design Head** | ≈ 1.1 – 1.25 | ≈ 1.2 – 1.5 | ≈ 1.5 – 2.0 |
| **Starting Torque** | Low | Moderate | Very High (motor must be sized carefully) |
| **Power Curve (P vs Q)** | Rises with Q | Roughly constant | Falls with Q |
| **Efficiency Range** | 60–90% | 70–90% | 75–90% |
| **NPSH Requirement** | Lowest | Moderate | Highest (most cavitation-prone) |
| **Application Example** | Boiler feed, high-rise supply | Irrigation mains, circulating pumps | Drainage, condenser cooling |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: H-Q Curve Generation Procedure

1. **Run the pump** at constant rated speed N.
2. **Vary the discharge** Q by throttling the delivery valve.
3. **Record the head H** at the corresponding Q using a Bourdon pressure gauge (discharge) and vacuum gauge (suction).
4. **Compute shaft power** P using a dynamometer or by P = 2πNT/60.
5. **Compute efficiency** η = ρgQH/P.
6. **Plot** H on Y-axis, Q on X-axis. Connect points to get the H-Q curve.
7. **Repeat** for P-Q and η-Q curves on the same graph.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Multistage Centrifugal Pump — Series Flow Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Suction Pipe] --> B[Stage 1 Impeller]
    B --> C[Return Guide Vane 1]
    C --> D[Stage 2 Impeller]
    D --> E[Return Guide Vane 2]
    E --> F[Stage 3 Impeller]
    F --> G[Stage 4 Impeller]
    G --> H[Return Guide Vane 4]
    H --> I[Volute Casing]
    I --> J[Delivery Pipe]

    K[Common Drive Shaft] -.couples.-> B
    K -.couples.-> D
    K -.couples.-> F
    K -.couples.-> G

    L[Electric Motor / Drive] --> K
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The fluid enters the suction pipe, picks up head $H_1$ at stage 1, is redirected by the return guide vane, picks up another $H_2$ at stage 2, and so on. The return guide vanes prevent the swirl imparted by one impeller from destroying the inlet conditions of the next. The shaft shown as a dotted line is a *common* shaft — one motor drives all impellers at the same angular velocity.

### 4.2 Classification Tree by Specific Speed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydraulic Pump Selection] --> B[Compute Specific Speed Ns]
    B --> C{Ns Range}
    C -->|8 to 80| D[Radial / Centrifugal Pump]
    C -->|80 to 400| E[Mixed Flow Pump]
    C -->|400 to 1500| F[Axial / Propeller Pump]
    C -->|Below 8 or Above 1500| G[Re-evaluate Operating Point]

    D --> D1[High Head, Low Discharge]
    D --> D2[Boiler Feed, High-Rise Building]
    E --> E1[Moderate Head, Moderate Discharge]
    E --> E2[Irrigation Mains, Industrial Circulation]
    F --> F1[Low Head, Very High Discharge]
    F --> F2[Stormwater, Condenser Cooling]
```

### 4.3 Performance Characteristic Curves (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Plot1 [H vs Q Curve]
        A1[Flow Q increases] --> A2[Head H decreases]
        A2 --> A3[Steepness: Radial Flat, Axial Steep]
    end

    subgraph Plot2 [Eta vs Q Curve]
        B1[Flow Q increases] --> B2[Efficiency peaks at BEP]
        B2 --> B3[Drops on either side of BEP]
    end

    subgraph Plot3 [P vs Q Curve]
        C1[Flow Q increases] --> C2{Type}
        C2 -->|Radial| C3[Power rises with Q]
        C2 -->|Axial| C4[Power falls with Q]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Functional Architecture Note (in lieu of a hand-drawn multi-curve chart):**
> The H-Q curve *falls* from left to right; the η-Q curve *peaks* at the Best Efficiency Point (BEP); the P-Q curve *rises* (for radial pumps) or *falls* (for axial pumps) depending on the geometry. KTU examiners frequently test the *qualitative* shape of these curves — a written description earns full marks if drawn well in words.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Define specific speed of a centrifugal pump and state its significance.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The specific speed of a centrifugal pump is defined as the speed in rpm at which a geometrically similar pump would run to deliver a discharge of 1 m³/s against a head of 1 m. Mathematically:

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H^{3/4}}$$

where N is in rpm, Q is in m³/s, and H is in m. Its significance is that it serves as a **design classification parameter** — a low Ns indicates a radial flow pump (high head, low discharge), a medium Ns indicates a mixed flow pump, and a high Ns indicates an axial / propeller pump (low head, high discharge). It allows engineers to select the *most efficient pump type* for a given duty point before any detailed design.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023]**
> **Distinguish between a single-stage and a multistage centrifugal pump. Give one application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **single-stage centrifugal pump** has only one impeller and therefore can develop a head limited by the centrifugal design (typically up to 100–150 m in practice). It is used where moderate heads are required, e.g., domestic water supply, small irrigation schemes.

A **multistage centrifugal pump** has two or more impellers mounted on the same shaft within a single casing, with the discharge of one impeller feeding the suction of the next. The total head developed is the *sum* of the per-stage heads:

$$H_{total} = n \cdot H_{stage}$$

It is used where very high heads are needed, e.g., boiler feed pumps in thermal power stations and high-rise building water supply systems.

---

### Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout #1 (Multistage Pumps):**
> A *very common* mistake is to confuse **multistage series** (which adds head, keeps Q constant) with **multistage parallel** (which adds Q, keeps H constant). Marks are routinely lost when students state "multistage pumps increase the discharge" without specifying the configuration. Always mention whether the impellers/pumps are arranged in **series or parallel**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout #2 (Specific Speed Units):**
> Specific speed Ns is *dimensionless only when SI units (N in rpm, Q in m³/s, H in m) are used*. Substituting Q in L/s or H in cm without conversion will result in a numerically correct but **physically meaningless** answer. KTU examiners award **0 marks** for the final numerical value if the units are not explicitly stated. Always state the unit system before the calculation.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**(a) (7 marks)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a multistage centrifugal pump. Derive an expression for the total head developed.

**(b) (7 marks)** A 4-stage centrifugal pump runs at 1500 rpm. Each stage develops a manometric head of 30 m when delivering 0.05 m³/s. If the overall efficiency is 75%, determine (i) the total head developed, (ii) the shaft power input, and (iii) the specific speed.

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:**
A multistage centrifugal pump consists of *n* impellers (typically identical) mounted on a **common shaft** within a single casing. The shaft is driven by an electric motor. Each impeller is followed by a **return guide vane ring**, which removes the whirl component from the fluid leaving one impeller before it enters the next. The pump has a single suction pipe (connected to the first stage only) and a single delivery pipe (connected to the volute of the last stage).

**Working:**
Liquid enters the suction pipe and is admitted to the eye of the first impeller. Centrifugal force throws the liquid outward at high pressure into the diffuser, where kinetic energy is partially converted to pressure. The high-pressure liquid then passes through the return guide vanes (which remove the residual swirl), enters the second impeller at its eye, and the cycle repeats. Each impeller adds an equal increment of head, and the fluid leaves the last stage at the cumulative head.

**Derivation of total head:**

By Euler's turbomachinery equation, the head developed by a single impeller (assuming no pre-whirl) is:

$$H_{stage} = \frac{V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

For *n* identical impellers in series on the same shaft, the total energy added per unit weight is:

$$H_{total} = n \cdot H_{stage} = \frac{n \cdot V_{w2} u_2}{g}$$

For the same impeller geometry, the per-stage whirl velocity $V_{w2}$ and blade tip speed $u_2$ are constant. Hence the head is additive across stages. **(7 marks — 3 for sketch explanation, 2 for working, 2 for derivation)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Number of stages: n = 4
- Speed: N = 1500 rpm
- Per-stage manometric head: H_stage = 30 m
- Discharge: Q = 0.05 m³/s
- Overall efficiency: η_overall = 0.75

**(i) Total head developed:**

$$H_{total} = n \cdot H_{stage} = 4 \times 30 = \boxed{120 \text{ m}}$$

**(ii) Shaft power input:**

Water power output:

$$P_{water} = \rho g Q H_{total} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 120 = 58{,}860 \text{ W} = 58.86 \text{ kW}$$

Shaft power input:

$$P_{shaft} = \frac{P_{water}}{\eta_{overall}} = \frac{58.86}{0.75} = \boxed{78.48 \text{ kW}}$$

**(iii) Specific speed:**

$$N_s = \frac{N \sqrt{Q}}{H_{total}^{3/4}} = \frac{1500 \times \sqrt{0.05}}{(120)^{0.75}}$$

Compute the pieces:
- $\sqrt{0.05} = 0.2236$
- $120^{0.75} = 120^{3/4} = (120^3)^{1/4} = (1{,}728{,}000)^{1/4} \approx 36.34$

Therefore:

$$N_s = \frac{1500 \times 0.2236}{36.34} = \frac{335.4}{36.34} \approx \boxed{9.23 \text{ (SI units, dimensionless)}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Ns ≈ 9 places this pump firmly in the **radial / centrifugal pump** category (range 8–80), confirming that a multistage configuration was correctly chosen to meet the *high head* requirement of 120 m without resorting to a mixed-flow design. **(7 marks — 2 for (i), 3 for (ii), 2 for (iii))**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — December 2023]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the construction and working of an axial flow (propeller) pump. State any three of its applications. Draw the typical H–Q and η–Q characteristic curves and explain the shape.

**(b) (7 marks)** A propeller pump delivers 1.2 m³/s against a head of 6 m when running at 720 rpm. If the pump is now re-run at 900 rpm on the same pipeline (assume pipeline resistance is negligible compared to the pump head), estimate the new discharge, new head, and the new power required (assume overall efficiency remains 80%).

**Model Solution (a):**

**Construction:**
An axial flow pump has a **propeller-type impeller** (typically 3 to 6 blades) with profiled aerofoil cross-sections, mounted inside a **cylindrical casing**. A set of **stationary guide vanes** (also called *stay vanes* or *diffuser vanes*) surrounds the impeller at the discharge side. The impeller is suspended by the shaft, and a suction bell-mouth ensures smooth entry. The fluid flows **axially** through the impeller (parallel to the shaft), unlike in a centrifugal pump where flow is radial.

**Working:**
As the impeller rotates, the aerofoil blades exert a *lift* force on the fluid, propelling it forward along the axis. The fluid enters axially through the bell-mouth, gains energy in the impeller, and leaves axially through the guide vanes. The guide vanes remove the residual whirl, converting the tangential kinetic energy into useful pressure rise.

**Three Applications:**
1. Drainage of low-lying areas and flood-control pumping stations
2. Condenser cooling water circulation in thermal power plants
3. Large-scale irrigation lift schemes

**Typical H–Q curve shape:**
The H–Q curve of a propeller pump is **very steep** — the head falls dramatically as discharge increases. At the shut-off condition (Q = 0), the head is typically **1.5 to 2.0 times the design head**, which is a critical design concern for the motor and delivery pipe.

**Typical η–Q curve shape:**
The η–Q curve rises *sharply* from zero at Q = 0 to a peak at the Best Efficiency Point (BEP), and then falls *gradually* on the higher-Q side. The peak efficiency of a propeller pump is comparable to that of a centrifugal pump, but the *flatness* of the curve is much narrower, meaning the pump is highly sensitive to off-design operation.

**(7 marks — 2 for construction, 2 for working + applications, 3 for curves + interpretation)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- Initial duty: Q₁ = 1.2 m³/s, H₁ = 6 m, N₁ = 720 rpm
- New speed: N₂ = 900 rpm
- Overall efficiency: η = 0.80 (constant)
- Pipeline resistance negligible → system head ≈ 0, so the *pump* head H ≈ the manometric head at the duty point.

**Apply the pump affinity laws:**

**(i) New discharge:**

$$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1} = \frac{N_2}{N_1} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q_2 = Q_1 \cdot \frac{N_2}{N_1} = 1.2 \times \frac{900}{720}$$

$$Q_2 = 1.2 \times 1.25 = \boxed{1.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) New head:**

$$\frac{H_2}{H_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_2 = H_1 \cdot \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^2 = 6 \times (1.25)^2$$

$$H_2 = 6 \times 1.5625 = \boxed{9.375 \text{ m}}$$

**(iii) New shaft power required:**

Water power at the new duty:

$$P_{water,2} = \rho g Q_2 H_2 = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 1.5 \times 9.375$$

$$P_{water,2} = 137{,}953 \text{ W} \approx 137.95 \text{ kW}$$

Shaft power input (with η = 0.80):

$$P_{shaft,2} = \frac{P_{water,2}}{\eta} = \frac{137.95}{0.80} = \boxed{172.44 \text{ kW}}$$

> **Alternative check using the power affinity law:**

$$\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\frac{N_2}{N_1}\right)^3 = (1.25)^3 = 1.953$$

Original shaft power: $P_1 = \rho g Q_1 H_1 / \eta = (1000)(9.81)(1.2)(6)/0.80 = 88.29$ kW. Then $P_2 = 88.29 \times 1.953 \approx 172.44$ kW ✓ **(7 marks — 2 each for (i), (ii), (iii), with 1 mark for the alternative check)**

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**
>
> 1. **Multistage pumps** add *head* in series; **parallel pumps** add *discharge*. Never confuse the two.
> 2. The head per stage of a multistage pump is identical only if the impellers are *geometrically identical* and operating at the *same speed*.
> 3. **Specific speed Ns = N√Q / H^(3/4)** uses N in rpm, Q in m³/s, H in m (SI units).
> 4. The three pump families are: **Radial (low Ns)**, **Mixed (medium Ns)**, **Axial (high Ns)**.
> 5. The **H–Q curve of an axial flow pump is steep** (head is highly sensitive to Q); the H–Q curve of a radial pump is *flat* (head is stable across a wide Q range).
> 6. The **shut-off head** of an axial pump is 1.5×–2.0× the design head — this is critical for motor and pipe design.
> 7. The **P–Q curve of an axial pump falls** with increasing Q (opposite of radial pumps).
> 8. The **three pump affinity laws** relate Q, H, and P to changes in N and D for geometrically similar pumps.
> 9. **Cavitation (NPSH)** is the most limiting factor in axial flow pumps — they are the most cavitation-prone of the three families.
> 10. The **overall efficiency** is the *product* of manometric, mechanical, and volumetric efficiencies — not the average.
> 11. Always state the **configuration (series or parallel)** explicitly when discussing multistage pumps in an exam — this is the #1 reason for lost marks.
> 12. Always state the **unit system (SI)** explicitly when computing specific speed — this is the #2 reason for lost marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deep Well Turbine Pump and Submersible Pump
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCAGT303 - FLUID MECHANICS & OPEN CHANNEL HYDRAULICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deep Well Turbine Pump and Submersible Pump

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Deep Well Turbine Pump (DWTP)

### Formal Definition
A **Deep Well Turbine Pump** is a multi-stage **vertical-axis centrifugal pump** designed specifically to lift water from deep bore wells (typically depths greater than **7 to 10 meters**, the practical suction limit of ordinary centrifugal pumps). It consists of a series of impellers (bowls) stacked vertically on a common vertical shaft, enclosed within a casing (bowl assembly) submerged below the water level in the well. The driving motor is mounted on the ground surface and connected to the pump through a long drive shaft running inside a column pipe.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Deep well turbine pumps are also known as **"Vertical Turbine Pumps"** or **"Borehole Pumps."** They essentially overcome the suction head limitation (theoretical max ≈ **10.33 m of water**) by placing the pump itself *inside* the water source.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine trying to drink a thick milkshake through a long straw from the bottom of a tall glass. You can only suck up a limited height before the vacuum fails. Now imagine instead sticking a small motorized propeller pump *inside* the milkshake at the bottom of the straw, with the motor on top connected by a long rod. The fluid is pushed up mechanically — no suction limit! That is precisely how a Deep Well Turbine Pump works. The impellers push water **upward in stages**, with each impeller adding a fixed increment of head.

## 1.2 Submersible Pump

### Formal Definition
A **Submersible Pump** is a hermetically sealed, motor-and-pump unit that operates entirely **submerged in the water** being pumped. The electric motor and the multi-stage pump assembly are coupled together and lowered into the well as a single compact unit. The motor is specially designed with water-tight windings (often filled with water or oil) and is cooled by the surrounding fluid itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction from DWTP:** In a **DWTP**, the motor is on the *surface* and connected via a long shaft. In a **Submersible Pump**, the motor and pump together go *underwater* — eliminating the long drive shaft altogether.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Picture a small electric fan sealed inside a waterproof capsule, dropped into a fish tank, with the power cable coming out the top. The fan blades (impellers) push water up through an outlet pipe at the top of the capsule, while the capsule is fully underwater. That is a submersible pump in essence — a self-contained, sealed pumping unit living inside its own working fluid.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
- Specific weight of water: **γ = 9810 N/m³**
- Maximum theoretical suction lift for ordinary centrifugal pumps: **10.33 m** (at sea level, 0°C)
- Practical suction limit for centrifugal pumps: **7 to 8 m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Deep Well Pumps?** When the water table is deeper than ~7 m, ordinary centrifugal pumps fail to draw water due to **cavitation** and the inability to create sufficient vacuum. Deep well pumps overcome this by placing the pumping element inside the water itself, ensuring the impeller eye is always flooded under positive suction head (submergence).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Components of a Deep Well Turbine Pump

A DWTP is structurally divided into **three major assemblies**, arranged vertically from top to bottom:

### A. Discharge Head Assembly (Top — Above Ground)
- **Discharge head / motor bracket:** Supports the entire pump assembly and the motor.
- **Discharge elbow:** Redirects the vertical flow to a horizontal discharge pipe.
- **Stuffing box / mechanical seal:** Prevents leakage around the shaft.
- **Right-angle gear drive** (optional): Used when motor speed (e.g., 1450 rpm) must be changed.

### B. Column Assembly (Middle — Inside Well Casing)
- **Column pipe (delivery pipe):** Carries water upward from the bowls to the discharge head.
- **Line shaft:** Transmits torque from the surface motor to the impellers.
- **Line shaft bearings (oil-lubricated or water-lubricated):** Support the long shaft against buckling and vibration.
- **Shaft coupling:** Connects successive shaft sections.

### C. Bowl Assembly (Bottom — Submerged in Water)
- **Impellers (multi-stage, enclosed type):** Convert mechanical energy into kinetic energy and then into pressure energy.
- **Bowl casings (diffusers / volutes):** Convert kinetic energy into pressure energy.
- **Suction case / suction bell:** Houses the suction strainer and foot valve.
- **Strainer:** Filters out debris before water enters the impeller.
- **Foot valve (non-return valve):** Prevents back-flow when the pump is stopped, keeping the bowls primed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Number of Stages:** A DWTP may have anywhere from **2 to over 30 stages**, depending on the total dynamic head required. Each stage typically adds **5 to 25 m of head**.

## 2.2 Components of a Submersible Pump

A submersible pump is more compact and consists of:

- **Submersible electric motor** (water-filled, oil-filled, or dry-type hermetically sealed).
- **Pump section:** Multi-stage centrifugal impellers with diffusers (radial or mixed-flow type).
- **Suction screen / strainer** at the bottom intake.
- **Check valve (non-return valve):** Prevents back-flow / water hammer.
- **Discharge outlet** with cable entry gland.
- **Power and control cables:** Watertight cable connects motor to surface starter panel.

## 2.3 Operational Principle (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Priming:** Both pumps are inherently self-primed since the pumping element is **always submerged** below the water surface.

**Step 2 — Energy Transfer:** The motor delivers rotational energy to the shaft, which spins the impellers at design speed **N** (rpm).

**Step 3 — Velocity Generation:** Water enters the impeller eye at the suction and is flung outward by centrifugal force, gaining kinetic energy. Absolute velocity of water leaving the impeller:
$$V_{w2} = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

**Step 4 — Pressure Conversion:** In the diffuser (bowl casing), the high-velocity water decelerates and kinetic energy converts to **pressure head (H)**.

**Step 5 — Series Staging:** Water exits the first bowl, enters the next impeller through a return channel, and the process repeats, **adding head cumulatively** stage after stage.

**Step 6 — Discharge:** Water is delivered through the column pipe (DWTP) or outlet (submersible) to the surface.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Remarks** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Discharge per stage (theoretical) | $Q = \pi D_2 B_2 V_{f2}$ | m³/s | $D_2$ = outer dia, $B_2$ = width, $V_{f2}$ = flow velocity |
| Velocity of water at impeller outlet | $V_{w2} = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$ | m/s | $N$ = rpm, $D_2$ in m |
| Velocity of flow (Manometric head relation) | $V_f = C_v \sqrt{2gH_m}$ | m/s | $C_v$ = velocity coefficient |
| Manometric head (single stage) | $H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}$ | m | Euler's equation for radial flow |
| Total head (multi-stage) | $H_{total} = n \times H_{stage}$ | m | $n$ = number of stages |
| Power input to impeller | $P_{impeller} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H_{total}}{\eta_{manometric}}$ | W | Shaft power basis |
| Shaft power | $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{P_{impeller}}{\eta_{mechanical}}$ | W | Includes bearing losses |
| Electrical power input | $P_{electrical} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$ | W | Includes motor efficiency |
| Overall efficiency | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{manometric} \times \eta_{mechanical} \times \eta_{motor}$ | dimensionless | Multiplicative product |
| Specific speed | $N_s = \dfrac{N \sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ | — | Used for pump classification |
| Minimum submergence to avoid vortex | $S_{min} = C \cdot V_f \cdot \sqrt{D}$ | m | Empirical (Fahlbusch / Knauss) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize Euler's head equation $H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}$ — it is the single most-tested formula in pump problems. Note that for a *radial vane*, $V_{w1} = 0$, simplifying the equation.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply:** Deep well pumps supply water to towns, villages, and high-rise buildings from borewells.
- **Agricultural irrigation:** Submersible pumps are widely used in farms for groundwater extraction.
- **Industrial process water:** Power plants, refineries, and cooling towers use DWTP for high-head, high-capacity applications.
- **Dewatering:** Mining and construction sites use submersible pumps to remove accumulated groundwater.
- **Geothermal systems:** High-temperature submersible pumps are used for hot water extraction.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Discharge Through a Centrifugal Pump Impeller

### Statement
Derive an expression for the **discharge (Q)** flowing through a centrifugal pump impeller, treating the flow through the impeller channels as flow between two concentric cylinders.

### Given
- Outer diameter of impeller at outlet: $D_2$
- Width of impeller at outlet: $B_2$
- Velocity of flow at outlet: $V_{f2}$ (radial component, treated as constant across outlet)

### Assumption
Flow is assumed to be **steady, incompressible**, and uniformly distributed across the outlet periphery. The impeller channels act like passages between two coaxial cylinders.

### Step 1 — Geometry of Outlet Area
Consider the outer cylindrical surface at the impeller outlet. Its circumferential length is:
$$L = \pi D_2$$

The outlet area of flow (annular cylindrical surface) is:
$$A = \pi D_2 \cdot B_2$$

### Step 2 — Apply Continuity Equation
By the principle of continuity (conservation of mass), the discharge equals the area of flow multiplied by the velocity of flow at that section:
$$Q = A \times V_{f2}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Area
$$Q = \pi D_2 \cdot B_2 \cdot V_{f2}$$

### Step 4 — Express Velocity of Flow Using Manometric Head
The velocity of flow $V_{f2}$ at the impeller outlet is related to the manometric head $H_m$ by:
$$V_{f2} = C_v \sqrt{2gH_m}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute into Discharge Equation
$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \pi D_2 \cdot B_2 \cdot C_v \sqrt{2gH_m}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 6 — Final Expression
$$\boxed{Q = \pi D_2 B_2 C_v \sqrt{2gH_m}}$$

### Interpretation
This equation is the **working formula** used by pump designers to size impellers. A larger impeller diameter, wider blade, higher manometric head, or higher velocity coefficient directly increases the discharge.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Euler's Equation for Head Developed by Centrifugal Pump

### Statement
Derive the **manometric head** developed by a centrifugal pump impeller, using Euler's velocity-triangle approach.

### Step 1 — Velocity Triangle at Inlet and Outlet
At impeller inlet (radius $r_1$) and outlet (radius $r_2$), we draw velocity triangles with:
- $u$ = tangential velocity of blade (m/s)
- $V_r$ = relative velocity of water w.r.t. blade
- $V$ = absolute velocity of water
- $V_w$ = whirling (tangential) component of absolute velocity
- $V_f$ = flow (radial) component of absolute velocity

Tangential blade velocities:
$$u_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1 N}{60}, \quad u_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

### Step 2 — Apply Euler's Turbomachine Equation
Euler's equation for head transferred to the fluid by an impeller is:
$$H_m = \dfrac{1}{g}\left[(V_{w2} u_2) - (V_{w1} u_1)\right]$$

### Step 3 — Simplification for Radial Vane (Inlet Whirl = 0)
If the inlet flow has no pre-whirl (radial entry, which is the common design assumption for centrifugal pumps), then:
$$V_{w1} = 0$$

So Euler's equation reduces to:
$$H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} \cdot u_2}{g}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute $u_2$
$$H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2}}{g} \cdot \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}$$

### Step 5 — Express $V_{w2}$ Using Velocity Coefficient
From the outlet velocity triangle:
$$V_{w2} = u_2 - V_{r2} \cos\beta_2$$

For backward-curved vanes (typical pump design), $\beta_2 < 90°$. If we assume no friction losses and radial exit ($\beta_2 = 90°$), then $\cos\beta_2 = 0$ and $V_{w2} = u_2$:
$$H_m = \dfrac{u_2^2}{g} = \dfrac{1}{g}\left(\dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}\right)^2$$

### Step 6 — General Head Equation
$$\boxed{H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \dfrac{1}{g}\left(\dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60}\right)^2 \quad \text{(for radial exit)}}$$

### Interpretation
This is the **fundamental head equation** of a centrifugal pump. It shows that the head developed is proportional to the **square of the impeller diameter** and the **square of the rotational speed**. Hence, doubling the speed quadruples the head.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: DWTP vs. Submersible Pump Comparison

| **Parameter** | **Deep Well Turbine Pump** | **Submersible Pump** |
|---|---|---|
| Motor location | On the surface (dry) | Submerged (underwater) |
| Drive mechanism | Long vertical drive shaft | Direct-coupled (short shaft) |
| Installation | Complex — alignment of long shaft critical | Simple — lower the unit, connect cable & pipe |
| Maintenance of motor | Easy (motor is accessible) | Difficult (motor must be pulled out) |
| Maintenance of pump | Difficult (must lift entire pump) | Difficult |
| Shaft alignment issues | Common (long shaft = critical) | Negligible |
| Cavitation risk | Lower (impeller deeply submerged) | Negligible (deeply submerged) |
| Borehole diameter requirement | Smaller (compact pump) | Larger (motor diameter adds) |
| Cost of installation | Higher (long shaft, bearings) | Moderate |
| Operating efficiency | Slightly lower (shaft losses) | Higher (direct drive, no shaft friction) |
| Typical depth range | 7 m to 300+ m | Up to 500+ m |
| Noise level | Low (motor on surface, enclosed) | Very low (fully submerged) |
| Cooling of motor | Self-cooled by air | Cooled by surrounding water |
| Application preference | High-flow, large municipal schemes | Domestic, agricultural, small industrial |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Deep Well Turbine Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    surfaceMotor["SURFACE MOTOR 1450 or 2900 rpm"]
    dischargeHead["DISCHARGE HEAD ASSEMBLY"]
    columnPipe1["COLUMN PIPE STAGE 1 Delivery tube"]
    lineShaft1["LINE SHAFT SECTION 1 with bearings"]
    columnPipe2["COLUMN PIPE STAGE 2 Delivery tube"]
    lineShaft2["LINE SHAFT SECTION 2 with bearings"]
    columnPipeN["COLUMN PIPE STAGE N Delivery tube"]
    lineShaftN["LINE SHAFT SECTION N"]
    bowlStage1["BOWL STAGE 1 Impeller plus Diffuser"]
    bowlStage2["BOWL STAGE 2 Impeller plus Diffuser"]
    bowlStageN["BOWL STAGE N Impeller plus Diffuser"]
    suctionBell["SUCTION BELL with Strainer"]
    waterTable["WATER TABLE Static level in well"]
    dischargeOutlet["DISCHARGE OUTLET To pipeline or tank"]
    powerSupply["3 PHASE POWER SUPPLY"]
    starterPanel["STARTER PANEL with protection"]

    surfaceMotor --> dischargeHead
    dischargeHead --> dischargeOutlet
    dischargeHead --> columnPipe1
    columnPipe1 --> lineShaft1
    lineShaft1 --> columnPipe2
    columnPipe2 --> lineShaft2
    lineShaft2 --> columnPipeN
    columnPipeN --> lineShaftN
    lineShaftN --> bowlStage1
    bowlStage1 --> bowlStage2
    bowlStage2 --> bowlStageN
    bowlStageN --> suctionBell
    suctionBell -.Enters from.-> waterTable
    powerSupply --> starterPanel
    starterPanel --> surfaceMotor
```

### Reading the Diagram
The vertical flow of energy is: **Power Supply → Starter Panel → Surface Motor → Discharge Head → Line Shaft (long, multi-section) → Multi-Stage Bowls → Suction Bell (from water table)**. Water travels upward in the *opposite* direction: **Water Table → Suction Bell → Bowls (N stages, each adding head) → Column Pipe → Discharge Head → Outlet**.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Submersible Pump

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    powerSupply["3 PHASE POWER SUPPLY Surface"]
    starterPanel["STARTER PANEL DOL or Star Delta"]
    cable["WATERPROOF POWER CABLE Through well casing"]
    checkValveTop["TOP CHECK VALVE Prevents back flow"]
    stageN["PUMP STAGE N Impeller and Diffuser"]
    stageN1["PUMP STAGE N-1 Impeller and Diffuser"]
    stage2["PUMP STAGE 2 Impeller and Diffuser"]
    stage1["PUMP STAGE 1 Impeller and Diffuser"]
    suctionScreen["SUCTION SCREEN Bottom intake"]
    submersibleMotor["SUBMERSIBLE MOTOR Hermetically sealed"]
    waterTable["WATER TABLE Fluid reservoir"]
    dischargeOutlet["DISCHARGE RISER PIPE To surface"]

    powerSupply --> starterPanel
    starterPanel --> cable
    cable --> submersibleMotor
    submersibleMotor --> stage1
    stage1 --> stage2
    stage2 --> stageN1
    stageN1 --> stageN
    stageN --> checkValveTop
    checkValveTop --> dischargeOutlet
    stage1 --> suctionScreen
    suctionScreen -.Draws from.-> waterTable
```

### Reading the Diagram
The motor and pump form an **integrated single unit** lowered into the well. The motor (bottom) drives the impeller stages stacked on top, water enters through the suction screen, passes through multiple stages of pressure-boost, exits through the top check valve, and rises through the discharge riser pipe to the surface.

---

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional Schematic (Text-Block Description)

### Deep Well Turbine Pump — Top to Bottom Layout

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph AboveGround["ABOVE GROUND"]
        A1["Electric Motor (1)"]
        A2["Motor Coupling (2)"]
        A3["Discharge Head Body (3)"]
        A4["Right-Angle Gear Drive (4) optional"]
    end

    subgraph InsideWell["INSIDE WELL CASING"]
        B1["Column Pipe Assembly (5)"]
        B2["Line Shaft Bearings (6)"]
        B3["Drive Shaft (7)"]
        B4["Bowl Assembly Multi-Stage (8)"]
        B5["Suction Case and Strainer (9)"]
        B6["Foot Valve (10)"]
    end

    subgraph WaterLevel["WATER SOURCE"]
        C1["Static Water Level Line (11)"]
        C2["Dynamic Water Level During Pumping (12)"]
    end

    A1 --> A2 --> A3 --> B1
    B1 --> B3 --> B4 --> B5 --> B6
    A4 -.Gear reduction.-> A3
    B4 -.Rotates.-> B3
    B5 -.Draws from.-> C1
    C1 --> C2
```

### Annotations Legend
1. **Electric Motor** — Surface-mounted, typically TEFC type.
2. **Motor Coupling** — Flexible coupling allowing minor misalignment.
3. **Discharge Head Body** — Houses stuffing box and supports column.
4. **Right-Angle Gear Drive** — Used when pump speed ≠ motor speed.
5. **Column Pipe Assembly** — Flanged steel pipes carrying water upward.
6. **Line Shaft Bearings** — Rubber or bronze bearings at every joint.
7. **Drive Shaft** — High-tensile steel, transmits torque to impellers.
8. **Bowl Assembly (Multi-Stage)** — 2 to 30+ stages, each adding head.
9. **Suction Case and Strainer** — Filters debris, ensures smooth inflow.
10. **Foot Valve** — Non-return valve retaining prime.
11. **Static Water Level** — Water table when pump is off.
12. **Dynamic Water Level** — Lowered level during pumping (drawdown).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a Deep Well Turbine Pump. Why is it preferred over an ordinary centrifugal pump for deep bore wells?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks)
A **Deep Well Turbine Pump (DWTP)** is a vertical, multi-stage centrifugal pump in which the impeller assembly (bowl) is submerged below the water level in the well, while the driving motor is mounted on the ground surface and connected via a long drive shaft running inside a column pipe. **(1 mark)**

An ordinary centrifugal pump can lift water only up to a maximum of about **7 to 10.33 m** due to atmospheric pressure limitation and cavitation risk. **(1 mark)**

In a DWTP, the pump is placed inside the water, so the impeller eye is always under positive pressure (no suction lift needed). This allows water to be lifted from depths of **30 m to over 300 m**, making it suitable for deep bore wells. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**List any three differences between a deep well turbine pump and a submersible pump.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each for any 3 valid differences)

| **S.No.** | **Deep Well Turbine Pump** | **Submersible Pump** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Motor is on the surface; pump is in the well. | Motor and pump are together submerged in water. |
| 2 | Long drive shaft with bearings connects motor to pump. | Direct-coupled, no long drive shaft. |
| 3 | Motor is air-cooled (TEFC). | Motor is water-cooled (surrounding water). |
| 4 | Shaft alignment is critical and troublesome. | Shaft alignment is automatic; no issue. |
| 5 | Suitable for large municipal / industrial schemes. | Suitable for domestic, agricultural use. |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working of a Deep Well Turbine Pump**. List its main components. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A multi-stage deep well turbine pump has **8 stages**, each developing a head of **12 m** at a discharge of **50 L/s**. If the overall efficiency is **65%**, calculate: **(7 marks)**
1. The total head developed.
2. The power input to the motor (in kW).
3. The diameter of the impeller at outlet if the speed is 1450 rpm, assuming radial flow (no pre-whirl) and velocity coefficient $C_v = 0.95$.

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 marks)

**Construction (4 marks):**
A Deep Well Turbine Pump consists of three main assemblies:

1. **Surface Assembly (Discharge Head + Motor):** Located above the ground. It contains the electric motor, motor coupling, discharge head body, and a discharge elbow. The discharge head supports the entire weight of the pump assembly suspended below.

2. **Column Assembly:** Located inside the well casing between the surface and the bowl assembly. It contains:
   - **Column pipe** — carries water upward from bowls to discharge head.
   - **Line shaft** — transmits torque from motor to impellers.
   - **Line shaft bearings** — supports shaft at every joint (rubber/bronze).
   - **Shaft couplings** — connect shaft sections.

3. **Bowl Assembly:** Submerged in water at the bottom of the well. Contains:
   - **Multi-stage impellers** (enclosed type) — convert mechanical energy to fluid energy.
   - **Bowl casings (diffusers)** — convert kinetic energy to pressure energy.
   - **Suction case / strainer** — filters water entering impeller.
   - **Foot valve** — non-return valve for priming retention.

**Working (3 marks):**
The surface-mounted motor rotates the vertical line shaft at design speed **N**. The impellers at the bottom spin and draw water through the strainer. Each impeller accelerates the water centrifugally, and the diffuser converts this kinetic energy to pressure energy (head). Water passes from one stage to the next through return channels, with each stage adding a fixed increment of head. Cumulative head from all stages lifts water up through the column pipe to the discharge head, where it is delivered to the surface pipeline.

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Number of stages: $n = 8$
- Head per stage: $H_{stage} = 12$ m
- Discharge: $Q = 50$ L/s $= 50 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s $= 0.05$ m³/s
- Overall efficiency: $\eta_{overall} = 65\% = 0.65$
- Speed: $N = 1450$ rpm
- $C_v = 0.95$
- Radial flow assumption: $V_{w1} = 0$

#### Step 1 — Total Head Developed
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{total} &= n \times H_{stage} \\
H_{total} &= 8 \times 12 \\
H_{total} &= 96 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks for this sub-part)**

#### Step 2 — Power Input to Motor
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{electrical} &= \dfrac{\rho g Q H_{total}}{\eta_{overall}} \\
P_{electrical} &= \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.05 \times 96}{0.65} \\
P_{electrical} &= \dfrac{47088}{0.65} \\
P_{electrical} &= 72{,}443 \text{ W} \\
P_{electrical} &\approx 72.44 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks for this sub-part)**

#### Step 3 — Impeller Outlet Diameter
From Euler's equation (radial exit, no pre-whirl):
$$H_{stage} = \dfrac{u_2^2}{g}$$

Solving for $u_2$:
$$\begin{aligned}
u_2 &= \sqrt{g \cdot H_{stage}} \\
u_2 &= \sqrt{9.81 \times 12} \\
u_2 &= \sqrt{117.72} \\
u_2 &= 10.85 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Relating tangential velocity to diameter:
$$\begin{aligned}
u_2 &= \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} \\
D_2 &= \dfrac{60 \cdot u_2}{\pi N} \\
D_2 &= \dfrac{60 \times 10.85}{\pi \times 1450} \\
D_2 &= \dfrac{651}{4555.31} \\
D_2 &= 0.143 \text{ m} \\
D_2 &= 143 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}$$

**(3 marks for this sub-part)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students often confuse whether to use **manometric head per stage** or **total head** when applying Euler's equation. Recall: **Euler's equation gives head per stage**, not total. Multiply by number of stages only for total head / total power calculations.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Explain the **construction, working, and applications of a Submersible Pump**. Compare it briefly with a Deep Well Turbine Pump. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A submersible pump delivers **1800 L/min** against a total head of **45 m**. The pump runs at **2900 rpm** with a manometric efficiency of **85%** and overall efficiency of **70%**. Calculate: **(7 marks)**
1. The power input to the electric motor (in kW).
2. The input power to the impeller (shaft power at impeller).
3. If the impeller outlet diameter is **200 mm** and velocity coefficient is **0.92**, determine the manometric head developed per stage (assume single equivalent stage and radial exit).

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Submersible Pump Description (7 marks)

**Construction (3 marks):**
A submersible pump is a sealed, integrated unit consisting of:
- **Submersible electric motor:** Hermetically sealed, water/oil-filled stator, water-cooled.
- **Multi-stage pump section:** Radial or mixed-flow impellers with diffusers, stacked on a common short shaft.
- **Suction screen:** Filters water at the bottom intake.
- **Check valve (NRV):** Prevents back-flow during shutdown.
- **Discharge outlet:** Top-mounted, connects to the rising main.
- **Waterproof power cable:** Sealed gland entry, runs to surface starter panel.

**Working (2 marks):**
The pump-motor assembly is lowered fully submerged into the well below the water level. Power is supplied through the waterproof cable. The motor drives the impellers directly. Water enters through the suction screen, gains energy at each impeller stage, passes through diffusers (pressure conversion), and exits through the top discharge to the rising main. The motor is cooled by the surrounding well water, ensuring long life.

**Applications (1 mark):**
- Domestic water supply from borewells.
- Agricultural irrigation.
- Industrial dewatering and mine drainage.
- Fountains and water features.

**Comparison with DWTP (1 mark):**
Unlike a DWTP, the submersible pump has no long drive shaft — the motor and pump form one compact unit. This eliminates shaft alignment problems, reduces mechanical losses, and simplifies installation. However, motor maintenance requires pulling the entire unit out of the well.

### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Discharge: $Q = 1800$ L/min $= \dfrac{1800}{60}$ L/s $= 30$ L/s $= 0.03$ m³/s
- Total head: $H = 45$ m
- Speed: $N = 2900$ rpm
- Manometric efficiency: $\eta_{man} = 0.85$
- Overall efficiency: $\eta_{overall} = 0.70$
- Impeller diameter: $D_2 = 200$ mm $= 0.20$ m
- Velocity coefficient: $C_v = 0.92$

#### Step 1 — Power Input to Electric Motor
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{electrical} &= \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{overall}} \\
P_{electrical} &= \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03 \times 45}{0.70} \\
P_{electrical} &= \dfrac{13243.5}{0.70} \\
P_{electrical} &= 18{,}919 \text{ W} \\
P_{electrical} &\approx 18.92 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)**

#### Step 2 — Input Power to Impeller (Shaft Power at Impeller Coupling)
Using manometric efficiency (which converts hydraulic power at impeller to shaft input at impeller):
$$\begin{aligned}
P_{shaft, impeller} &= \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{manometric}} \\
P_{shaft, impeller} &= \dfrac{1000 \times 9.81 \times 0.03 \times 45}{0.85} \\
P_{shaft, impeller} &= \dfrac{13243.5}{0.85} \\
P_{shaft, impeller} &= 15{,}580 \text{ W} \\
P_{shaft, impeller} &\approx 15.58 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**(2 marks)**

#### Step 3 — Manometric Head Per Stage (Single Equivalent Stage)
First, compute tangential blade velocity:
$$\begin{aligned}
u_2 &= \dfrac{\pi D_2 N}{60} \\
u_2 &= \dfrac{\pi \times 0.20 \times 2900}{60} \\
u_2 &= \dfrac{1822.12}{60} \\
u_2 &= 30.37 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

Velocity of flow at outlet:
$$\begin{aligned}
V_{f2} &= C_v \sqrt{2gH_{man}} \\
V_{f2} &= 0.92 \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 45} \\
V_{f2} &= 0.92 \sqrt{882.9} \\
V_{f2} &= 0.92 \times 29.71 \\
V_{f2} &= 27.34 \text{ m/s}
\end{aligned}$$

For radial exit (no pre-whirl), Euler's equation gives:
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{man, ideal} &= \dfrac{u_2^2}{g} \\
H_{man, ideal} &= \dfrac{(30.37)^2}{9.81} \\
H_{man, ideal} &= \dfrac{922.34}{9.81} \\
H_{man, ideal} &\approx 94.02 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}$$

With velocity coefficient correction (the actual manometric head considering velocity losses):
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{man, actual} &= C_v^2 \cdot \dfrac{u_2^2}{g} \quad \text{or use Euler's actual form}
\end{aligned}$$

Using $V_{f2} = C_v \sqrt{2gH_{man}}$ and $V_{w2} = u_2$ (radial exit):
$$\begin{aligned}
H_{man} &= \dfrac{u_2^2}{g} \approx 94 \text{ m per stage (ideal)}
\end{aligned}$$

Acceptable KTU answer: **94.0 m per stage** (assuming single stage), with note that actual multi-stage pumps would have several such stages summing to required total head.

**(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A very common error is using the **wrong efficiency** at the wrong stage:
> - **Manometric efficiency** = Hydraulic power at impeller → Shaft power at impeller.
> - **Overall efficiency** = Hydraulic water power → Electrical input.
> Mixing them up will yield a wrong answer and cost 2 marks minimum.
> Also: Do not forget to **convert L/min to m³/s** (divide by 60000) or **L/s to m³/s** (divide by 1000). Many students lose marks on this unit conversion.

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **Deep Well Turbine Pump (DWTP)** is a multi-stage vertical centrifugal pump with the bowl assembly submerged in water and the motor on the surface, connected by a long drive shaft.
- A **Submersible Pump** has the motor and pump as one integrated, hermetically sealed unit submerged in water.
- The **suction lift limit** of an ordinary centrifugal pump is **7 to 10.33 m**, depending on altitude and temperature.
- **Three main assemblies of DWTP:** Surface assembly (motor + discharge head), Column assembly (pipe + shaft + bearings), Bowl assembly (impellers + diffusers + suction case).
- **Key components of submersible pump:** Submersible motor, multi-stage pump, suction screen, check valve, waterproof cable.
- **Euler's head equation** (radial exit, no pre-whirl): $H_m = \dfrac{V_{w2} u_2}{g} = \dfrac{u_2^2}{g}$
- **Discharge equation:** $Q = \pi D_2 B_2 V_{f2} = \pi D_2 B_2 C_v \sqrt{2gH_m}$
- **Power equations:** $P_{electrical} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{overall}}$ and $P_{shaft} = \dfrac{\rho g Q H}{\eta_{manometric}}$
- **Multi-stage pumps** develop total head = (number of stages) × (head per stage).
- **Submersible pumps** are preferred for **domestic and agricultural** use due to ease of installation.
- **DWTP** is preferred for **large municipal and industrial** schemes due to high capacity and easy motor maintenance.
- **Velocity coefficient** $C_v$ accounts for frictional losses in impeller passages (typical $C_v = 0.9$ to $0.98$).
- **Specific speed** $N_s = \dfrac{N\sqrt{P}}{H^{5/4}}$ helps classify pump type (radial / mixed / axial flow).
- Always convert units consistently: **mm → m** (divide by 1000), **L/s → m³/s** (divide by 1000), **L/min → m³/s** (divide by 60000).
- **Drawdown** = Static water level − Dynamic water level during pumping; pump bowls must always remain submerged below the dynamic water level to avoid cavitation.
